topics.title: Bottom Supported Fixed Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST722 - FUNDAMENTALS OF OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Bottom Supported Fixed Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Bottom Supported Fixed Structures** (also termed *Fixed Offshore Platforms* or *Bottom-Founded Structures*) are permanent offshore installations that rest directly on the seabed and are rigidly connected to it, deriving their primary stability against environmental loads (waves, wind, current) from their own structural weight, ballast, and/or pile foundations embedded into the seafloor soil.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Bottom-supported fixed structures are those offshore structures whose **base is in continuous contact with the seabed** and which are designed to remain stationary throughout their operational life, transferring all vertical and lateral loads to the foundation soil.

These are classified under **Compliant vs. Fixed** dichotomy as the most rigid category — they behave as cantilever structures fixed at the seabed, with natural periods typically **below 3–4 seconds**, far removed from wave energy periods (5–20 s), hence avoiding resonance.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — Real World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Ocean Lighthouse on Stilts**
> 
> Imagine a **lighthouse built on long iron stilts driven deep into the seabed** of a shallow sea. The stilts are anchored firmly into the rock/sand below, the platform on top holds the light, and no matter how strong the waves crash against the stilts, the structure does not move appreciably. 
> 
> That is exactly what a bottom-supported fixed structure is — a **tower pinned to the seafloor**, designed to stand still while the ocean rages around it. Unlike a ship (which floats) or a buoy (which moves with waves), the structure is **welded to the Earth below the sea** for decades (often **20–30 years** service life).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Water depth range:** **10 m to 500 m** (typical operating range)
- **Service life:** **20–30 years**
- **Design wave height:** up to **30 m** (100-year return period)
- **Natural period:** **2 – 4 seconds** (rigid cantilever)
- **Steel density:** **7850 kg/m³**
- **Seawater density:** **1025 kg/m³**
- **Concrete density (reinforced):** **2500 kg/m³**

## 1.4 Classification of Bottom-Supported Fixed Structures

| Type | Description | Typical Depth |
|------|-------------|---------------|
| **Steel Template (Jacket) Platform** | Tubular steel space frame, pile-founded | 10 – 400 m |
| **Concrete Gravity Platform** | Massive concrete base, no piles | 10 – 250 m |
| **Jack-up Rig** (Operational mode) | Movable, lowered onto seabed on legs | 5 – 150 m |
| **Compliant Tower** | Slender, flexible — but still bottom-founded | 300 – 1000 m |
| **Caisson/Monopod Platform** | Single large cylinder | 10 – 50 m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** The module emphasizes **Steel Template (Jacket) Platforms** and **Concrete Gravity Platforms** as the two dominant bottom-supported fixed structure types, along with their constituent components and design load regimes.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Philosophy — The "Why"

A bottom-supported fixed structure works on a simple principle:

> *Provide a permanent, stationary, dry workspace above the ocean surface from which hydrocarbons can be extracted, processed, and exported.*

The **engineering challenge** is to:
1. Resist enormous **horizontal wave and wind forces** (often **10⁴ – 10⁵ kN**).
2. Support massive **vertical dead + live loads** (drilling rigs, production equipment, personnel).
3. Do so for **decades** in a **highly corrosive** marine environment.
4. Survive **storm, earthquake, ship impact, and fatigue** loading.

## 2.2 Primary Load Categories

The structure must be designed for the following loads (per **API RP 2A / ISO 19900**):

- **Dead Loads (DL):** Self-weight of structure, equipment, piping.
- **Live Loads (LL):** Personnel, consumables, drilling mud.
- **Environmental Loads (E):**
   - Wind (above water)
   - Waves (hydrodynamic)
   - Current
   - Earthquake (seismic)
- **Accidental Loads:** Fire, explosion, vessel collision, dropped objects.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D A v^2$ | Drag force (Morison) | N |
| 2 | $F_I = \rho C_M V \dfrac{du}{dt}$ | Inertia force (Morison) | N |
| 3 | $F_{total} = F_D + F_I$ | Morison total force | N |
| 4 | $F_w = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_a C_D A v^2$ | Wind force on above-water part | N |
| 5 | $F_{base} = \rho_{sw} g V_{displaced}$ | Buoyancy (Archimedes) | N |
| 6 | $SF = \dfrac{F_{resisting}}{F_{overturning}}$ | Factor of Safety (≥ 1.5) | – |
| 7 | $T_n = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_n}$ | Natural period (fixed-base) | s |
| 8 | $M_{OT} = F_H \cdot h_{CG}$ | Overturning moment | N·m |
| 9 | $P_{pile} = q_b A_b + f_s A_s$ | Pile axial capacity | N |
| 10 | $H_{wave} = \dfrac{H_{max}}{1.87}$ | Significant wave design | m |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU Tip:** The **Morison Equation** (Eq. 3) is the cornerstone for wave loading on slender members of jacket platforms. Always remember it has **two parts** — *drag* (velocity-squared) and *inertia* (acceleration).

## 2.4 Why These Structures Are Chosen

- **High payload capacity** (10,000+ tonnes topsides for deepwater jackets).
- **Proven technology** — used in **> 95%** of shallow-water offshore oil platforms globally.
- **Suitable for drilling, production, and processing** simultaneously.
- **Long service life** with proper corrosion protection.
- **High stiffness** → predictable dynamic response.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stability of a Gravity Platform (Sliding & Overturning)

A **concrete gravity platform** rests on the seabed purely by its own weight. Two stability checks must be satisfied.

### 3.1.1 Sliding Stability

> *Resisting force (friction) must exceed the maximum horizontal wave force.*

Let:
- $W$ = total submerged weight of platform
- $F_H$ = peak horizontal wave force
- $\mu$ = coefficient of friction at base–soil interface

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{resisting} &= \mu \cdot W \\
F_{overturning} &= F_H \\
\text{Factor of Safety (Sliding)} &= \frac{\mu \cdot W}{F_H} \ge 1.5
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-by-Step Logic:**
> 1. Identify vertical equilibrium: $N = W$ (normal reaction = submerged weight).
> 2. Frictional resistance is $F_{resist} = \mu N = \mu W$.
> 3. Compare with horizontal driving force $F_H$.
> 4. Apply safety factor of **1.5** as per DNV/ABS codes.

### 3.1.2 Overturning Stability

> *Restoring moment about the toe must exceed overturning moment.*

Let:
- $b$ = base width
- $h$ = height of center of gravity above base
- $F_H$ = horizontal wave force at CG level
- $W$ = submerged weight acting at $b/2$ from toe

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{overturning} &= F_H \cdot h \\
M_{restoring} &= W \cdot \frac{b}{2} \\
\text{Factor of Safety (OT)} &= \frac{W \cdot b}{2 \cdot F_H \cdot h} \ge 1.5
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Wave Force on a Jacket Member (Morison Equation)

For a **vertical slender cylinder** of diameter $D$ in waves, the **Morison Equation** gives the force per unit length:

### Step 1 — Identify the Two Components

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_D &= \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D D \, \vert u \vert u \quad \text{(drag — velocity dependent)} \\
f_I &= \rho C_M \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \, \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} \quad \text{(inertia — acceleration dependent)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Combine

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{total} &= f_D + f_I \\
f_{total} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D D \vert u \vert u + \rho C_M \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \frac{\partial u}{\partial t}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Special Case: Small Member ($D/\lambda < 0.2$)

If $D/\lambda < 0.2$ (member is *small* relative to wavelength), the **drag term dominates** and inertia can be neglected:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{total} &\approx f_D = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D D \vert u \vert u
\end{aligned}
$$

If $D/\lambda > 0.2$ (large member / caisson), the **inertia term dominates**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{total} &\approx f_I = \rho C_M \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \frac{\partial u}{\partial t}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Coefficients (KTU-required):**
> - $C_D$ (drag) = **0.6 – 1.2** (typical **0.7** for smooth cylinders)
> - $C_M$ (inertia) = **1.5 – 2.0** (typical **1.8**)
> - $C_A = C_M - 1$ (added mass coefficient)

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Buoyancy & Submerged Weight of Concrete Gravity Base

For a concrete caisson of plan area $A$ and base height $h_b$ submerged in seawater:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{buoyancy} &= \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{submerged} \\
F_{buoyancy} &= \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot A \cdot h_b \\
W_{submerged} &= W_{concrete} - F_{buoyancy} \\
W_{submerged} &= (\rho_c - \rho_{sw}) \cdot g \cdot A \cdot h_b
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Plug-in (Example):** $A = 5000$ m², $h_b = 30$ m, $\rho_c = 2500$ kg/m³, $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³
> 
> $W_{submerged} = (2500 - 1025) \cdot 9.81 \cdot 5000 \cdot 30 = 2.17 \times 10^9$ N

This submerged weight is what provides **sliding/overturning resistance** for the gravity platform.

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Structure Type | Vertical Stability From | Horizontal Stability From | Foundation |
|----------------|------------------------|--------------------------|------------|
| **Steel Jacket** | Buoyancy of enclosed water + pile skin friction | Pile bending + jacket frame action | Piles driven into seabed |
| **Concrete Gravity** | Ballast + self-weight (submerged) | Friction at base + base shear keys | Direct bearing on seabed |
| **Jack-up (lowered)** | Spudcan bearing capacity | Spudcan lateral capacity | Spudcans into seabed |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Anatomy of a Steel Jacket Platform

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["TOP SIDES DECKS"] --> B["CELLAR DECK"]
    B --> C["PRODUCTION DECK"]
    C --> D["DRILLING DECK"]
    D --> E["HELIDECK"]
    A --> F["JACKET SPACE FRAME 4 to 8 LEGS"]
    F --> G["CONDUCTORS RISERS"]
    F --> H["PILE FOUNDATIONS STEEL TUBES"]
    H --> I["SEABED SOIL LAYER"]
    F --> J["MUDLINE BRACING"]
    F --> K["SPLASH ZONE PROTECTION"]
    F --> L["MARINE GROWTH ZONE 0 to 50m"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **topsides** (decks) sit on a tubular steel **jacket** which is a 3D space frame extending from above the splash zone down to the **mudline**. **Piles** (long steel tubes) are driven through the jacket legs into the seabed to anchor the structure. The structure transfers all environmental loads through the jacket into the piles, then into the soil.

## 4.2 Functional Flow: Load Transfer Path in a Fixed Platform

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W["WIND ON TOPSIDES"] --> D["DECK LEVEL FORCES"]
    WV["WAVE AND CURRENT ON LEGS"] --> D
    D --> JF["JACKET FRAME SHEAR AND BENDING"]
    JF --> PILE["PILE HEAD FORCES"]
    PILE --> SOIL["SOIL REACTION SKIN FRICTION AND END BEARING"]
    DECK["DEAD AND LIVE LOADS"] --> J2["JACKET VERTICAL COMPRESSION"]
    J2 --> PILE2["PILE AXIAL LOAD"]
    PILE2 --> SOIL
```

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic: Concrete Gravity Platform

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SEA["SEA SURFACE"]
        T["TOPSIDES MODULES"]
    end
    T --> SH["SHAFT CONCRETE TOWER 30 to 50m"]
    SH --> C1["CELL A STORAGE"]
    SH --> C2["CELL B STORAGE"]
    SH --> C3["CELL C BALLAST"]
    C1 --> BC["BASE CAISSON 30 to 50m HIGH"]
    C2 --> BC
    C3 --> BC
    BC --> SB["SEABED DIRECT BEARING"]
    BC --> SK["SHEAR KEYS AT BASE"]
    BC --> R["ROCKFILL DUMPED BERM"]
    SB --> SOIL2["SOIL FOUNDATION"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** Unlike the jacket, the concrete gravity platform has **no piles**. Its stability comes entirely from its **massive submerged weight** (the base may weigh **100,000 – 300,000 tonnes**) pressing it onto the seabed. The ballast cells can be **flooded with seawater** to increase weight during towing and installation, and **de-ballasted** to provide buoyancy during transport.

## 4.4 Comparison Block: Jacket vs. Gravity Platform

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q{"BOTTOM SUPPORTED FIXED STRUCTURE"} --> S1["STEEL JACKET"]
    Q --> S2["CONCRETE GRAVITY"]
    S1 --> P1["PILES REQUIRED 4 to 12"]
    S1 --> D1["DEPTH UP TO 400m"]
    S1 --> C1["FABRICATED ONSHORE TOWED OUT"]
    S1 --> A1["ADV: LIGHTWEIGHT MODULAR"]
    S1 --> D2["DIS: COMPLEX PILE INSTALLATION"]
    S2 --> P2["NO PILES DIRECT BEARING"]
    S2 --> D3["DEPTH UP TO 250m"]
    S2 --> C2["BUILT IN DRYDOCK OR FJORD"]
    S2 --> A2["ADV: USES OIL STORAGE IN BASE"]
    S2 --> D4["DIS: MASSIVE TOW INSTALLATION COMPLEX"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Define bottom-supported fixed structures. Give two examples.**

> **Model Answer:**
> Bottom-supported fixed structures are permanent offshore installations that rest directly on the seabed and are rigidly connected to the seafloor, deriving their stability from their own weight, pile foundations, or both, in order to resist environmental loads from waves, wind, and current.
>
> **Examples (any two):**
> 1. **Steel Template (Jacket) Platform** – pile-founded tubular steel space frame.
> 2. **Concrete Gravity Platform** – ballasted concrete base resting on seabed.
> 3. Jack-up rig (in operational mode) – supported on spudcans.
> 4. Monopod platform – single large-diameter cylinder.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Distinguish between the working principles of a steel jacket platform and a concrete gravity platform.**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> | Aspect | Steel Jacket | Concrete Gravity |
> |--------|-------------|-----------------|
> | Foundation | Driven piles | Direct bearing on seabed |
> | Stability source | Pile skin friction + frame stiffness | Submerged weight + friction |
> | Base area | Small footprint | Very large base |
> | Use of storage | None | Oil stored in base cells |
> | Installation | Tow + pile-driving | Tow + ballasting down |
> | Typical depth | up to 400 m | up to 250 m |

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently lose marks by:
> 1. **Forgetting the safety factor** of 1.5 in stability problems (always quote it).
> 2. **Mixing up** the drag and inertia coefficients ($C_D$ vs $C_M$).
> 3. **Not specifying units** in final answers — KTU examiners deduct up to **1 mark** per question for missing units.
> 4. **Skipping the submerged weight** when dealing with concrete gravity stability.
> 5. Confusing **wavelength criterion** ($D/\lambda$ ratio) for Morison's applicability.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **main components of a steel jacket platform** and explain how environmental loads are transferred to the seabed. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vertical cylindrical tubular member of diameter $D = 1.5$ m is subjected to a wave of height $H = 8$ m, period $T = 9$ s in seawater ($\rho = 1025$ kg/m³). Using the Morison equation with $C_D = 0.7$ and $C_M = 1.8$, determine the **maximum drag force per unit length** and **maximum inertia force per unit length**. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Main components of a steel jacket platform — 7 marks**

A steel jacket platform consists of:

1. **Topsides** (3 marks for naming + sketch)
   - Helideck, drilling deck, production deck, cellar deck, living quarters.
   - Houses drilling rig, production equipment, helipad, accommodation.
2. **Jacket structure** (2 marks)
   - 4–8 legged tubular steel space frame.
   - Includes horizontal bracing, vertical legs, diagonal bracing.
   - Extends from seabed (mudline) to above splash zone.
3. **Pile foundation** (2 marks)
   - Large-diameter steel tubular piles (1–3 m diameter).
   - Driven through pile sleeves at the bottom of jacket legs.
   - Penetration typically 30–100 m into seabed.
   - **Load transfer path:** Wind/waves → deck → jacket frame → pile heads → pile shafts → soil (skin friction + end bearing).

**(b) Numerical — 7 marks**

**Given:** $D = 1.5$ m, $H = 8$ m, $T = 9$ s, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $C_D = 0.7$, $C_M = 1.8$.

**Step 1 — Compute wave number and wavelength** (deep water approx.)
$$
k = \frac{2\pi}{g T^2} = \frac{2\pi}{9.81 \times 81} = 0.00790 \text{ m}^{-1}
$$
$$
\lambda = \frac{2\pi}{k} = 795.5 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $D/\lambda$ to confirm Morison applicability**
$$
\frac{D}{\lambda} = \frac{1.5}{795.5} = 0.00189 < 0.2
$$
**Therefore Morison equation is valid, and both drag and inertia are computed (we keep both terms).**

**Step 3 — Maximum horizontal velocity (at surface, $z = 0$):**
$$
u_{max} = \frac{\pi H}{T} = \frac{\pi \times 8}{9} = 2.79 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Maximum horizontal acceleration (at surface, $z = 0$):**
$$
a_{max} = \frac{2\pi^2 H}{T^2} = \frac{2 \times \pi^2 \times 8}{81} = 1.95 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Maximum drag force per unit length:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{D,max} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D D \, u_{max}^2 \\
&= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 0.7 \times 1.5 \times (2.79)^2 \\
&= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 0.7 \times 1.5 \times 7.78 \\
&= 4187 \text{ N/m} \\
&\approx 4.19 \text{ kN/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Maximum inertia force per unit length:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{I,max} &= \rho C_M \frac{\pi D^2}{4} a_{max} \\
&= 1025 \times 1.8 \times \frac{\pi (1.5)^2}{4} \times 1.95 \\
&= 1025 \times 1.8 \times 1.767 \times 1.95 \\
&= 6355 \text{ N/m} \\
&\approx 6.36 \text{ kN/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $f_{D,max} \approx 4.19$ kN/m, $f_{I,max} \approx 6.36$ kN/m.
**Total peak force per unit length** $= f_{D,max} + f_{I,max} = 10.55$ kN/m (occurs at different phases, but envelope is sum of peaks).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain with a sketch the **concept of a concrete gravity platform**. Discuss its advantages and disadvantages compared to a steel jacket platform. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A concrete gravity platform has a base of plan area $A = 4000$ m² and base height $h_b = 25$ m. The reinforced concrete density is $\rho_c = 2500$ kg/m³, seawater density is $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³. The platform is subjected to a peak horizontal wave force $F_H = 8 \times 10^7$ N acting at a height $h = 40$ m above the seabed. The coefficient of friction between base and seabed is $\mu = 0.6$. Check whether the platform is **safe against sliding and overturning**. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Concrete Gravity Platform — 7 marks**

A **concrete gravity platform** is a bottom-supported fixed structure that relies entirely on its **self-weight and ballast** to remain stable on the seabed. The structure consists of:

- A massive **base caisson** (often with several cells) of height 20–50 m resting on the seabed.
- One or more **shafts** rising above the waterline supporting the topsides.
- Optional **storage cells** in the base used for crude oil storage.

**Advantages over jacket platforms (3 marks):**
1. **No pile-driving** required — installation is by ballasting down at site.
2. **Oil storage capacity** in the base cells.
3. **High durability** — concrete resists corrosion better than steel in marine splash zone.
4. **Higher topsides payload capacity**.

**Disadvantages (1 mark):**
1. Requires **deep-water dry-dock or deep fjord** for construction.
2. **Complex tow and installation** operation.
3. Limited to ~250 m depth.
4. Higher initial material cost.

**(b) Numerical — 7 marks**

**Given:** $A = 4000$ m², $h_b = 25$ m, $\rho_c = 2500$ kg/m³, $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³, $F_H = 8 \times 10^7$ N, $h = 40$ m, $\mu = 0.6$, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Step 1 — Volume of base**
$$
V = A \cdot h_b = 4000 \times 25 = 100{,}000 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Submerged weight of base**
$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{sub} &= (\rho_c - \rho_{sw}) \cdot g \cdot V \\
&= (2500 - 1025) \times 9.81 \times 100{,}000 \\
&= 1475 \times 9.81 \times 10^5 \\
&= 1.447 \times 10^9 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sliding safety factor**
$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{resist} &= \mu \cdot W_{sub} = 0.6 \times 1.447 \times 10^9 = 8.68 \times 10^8 \text{ N} \\
SF_{slide} &= \frac{F_{resist}}{F_H} = \frac{8.68 \times 10^8}{8 \times 10^7} = 10.85
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $SF_{slide} = 10.85 \ge 1.5$ ✓ — **SAFE against sliding.**

**Step 4 — Overturning safety factor**
Assume square base → $b = \sqrt{A} = \sqrt{4000} = 63.25$ m.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{OT} &= F_H \cdot h = 8 \times 10^7 \times 40 = 3.2 \times 10^9 \text{ N·m} \\
M_{resist} &= W_{sub} \cdot \frac{b}{2} = 1.447 \times 10^9 \times \frac{63.25}{2} \\
&= 1.447 \times 10^9 \times 31.625 \\
&= 4.577 \times 10^{10} \text{ N·m} \\
SF_{OT} &= \frac{M_{resist}}{M_{OT}} = \frac{4.577 \times 10^{10}}{3.2 \times 10^9} = 14.3
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $SF_{OT} = 14.3 \ge 1.5$ ✓ — **SAFE against overturning.**

**Conclusion:** The platform is **safe against both sliding and overturning** with large margins.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Bottom Supported Fixed Structures**

- **Definition:** Permanent offshore structures resting on seabed, rigidly held in place by weight and/or piles.
- **Depth range:** 10–500 m (jacket), 10–250 m (gravity).
- **Service life:** 20–30 years.
- **Two main types:** (1) **Steel Jacket Platform** (pile-founded) and (2) **Concrete Gravity Platform** (ballast-founded).
- **Natural period** of fixed structures: 2–4 s → safely avoids wave energy spectrum (5–20 s).
- **Morison Equation** has **two terms**:
   - **Drag** ∝ $u^2$ (dominates for small $D/\lambda$).
   - **Inertia** ∝ $\partial u/\partial t$ (dominates for large $D/\lambda$).
- **Morison applicability criterion:** $D/\lambda < 0.2$.
- **Standard coefficients:** $C_D \approx 0.7$, $C_M \approx 1.8$.
- **Stability safety factors (must be ≥ 1.5):**
   - $SF_{slide} = \mu W / F_H$
   - $SF_{OT} = Wb / (2 F_H h)$
- **Submerged weight** = $(\rho_c - \rho_{sw}) g V$ — **always subtract buoyancy**.
- **Load transfer path:** Wind/Wave → Deck → Jacket frame → Piles → Soil (skin friction + end bearing).
- **Components of a jacket:** Topsides → Jacket space frame → Pile sleeves → Driven piles → Seabed.
- **Components of gravity platform:** Topsides → Shaft(s) → Cellular base caisson → Seabed (direct bearing).
- **Jack-up rigs** are also bottom-supported when in operational mode (on spudcans).
- **Compliant towers** are *flexible* fixed platforms (period tuned above wave periods) — still bottom-founded.
- **API RP 2A / ISO 19900** are the governing design codes.
- **Common KTU exam mistakes:** Forgetting safety factor, mixing drag and inertia coefficients, missing units, confusing natural period of fixed vs. compliant structures.
- **Real-world examples:** 
   - Jacket → Bullwinkle (412 m, Gulf of Mexico).
   - Gravity → Troll A (303 m tall, North Sea, 472 m water depth — world's tallest).
   - Jack-up → Maersk Discoverer.

---

**END OF NOTES — MODULE 1, TOPIC: BOTTOM SUPPORTED FIXED STRUCTURES**

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compliant Offshore Structures** are deep-water marine installations intentionally designed to **deflect, sway, or oscillate** within prescribed serviceability limits under the action of environmental loads (waves, wind, current, and seismic loads), rather than to rigidly resist them.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework, a structure is classified as *compliant* when its **fundamental natural period of oscillation ($T_n$)** in the horizontal surge / sway mode is **significantly greater than the dominant wave period ($T_w$)**. The standard design threshold is:

$$T_n \ge 2T_w \quad \text{to} \quad T_n \ge 5T_w$$

This deliberate detuning ensures the structure **avoids wave-frequency resonance**, transferring the dynamic load energy into slow-drift motions that are controlled by **mooring and tethering systems** rather than by structural stiffness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Operating Water Depth Range:** Compliant structures are typically deployed at depths **> 300 m (and up to 3,000 m)**, where conventional fixed jacket platforms become economically and structurally unfeasible due to the cubic increase in steel tonnage with depth.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Reed vs. Oak" Principle

Imagine two trees facing a storm:

- An **Oak Tree (Fixed Structure)** stands rigid. It does not move, but the trunk and roots must absorb 100% of the wind force. If the storm exceeds the trunk's bending strength, it **snaps catastrophically**.

- A **Reed Plant (Compliant Structure)** bends, sways, and moves with the wind. Because it yields, the wind flows *around* it instead of *through* it. The force on the reed is dramatically lower, and the plant survives storms that would topple the oak.

> **Compliant offshore structures operate on the Reed Principle:** *It is often cheaper and safer to "move with the force" than to "fight the force."*

## 1.3 The Physical Reasoning — Why Compliance Works

For a rigid pile-supported jacket in deep water, the **base shear** due to wave action scales roughly with the **square of water depth** and the **inertia of the entire water column** the structure displaces. Mathematical stiffness of a fixed tower grows as $k \propto 1/L^3$ (where $L$ is the unsupported length), so as $L$ increases, the structure becomes *softer*, not stiffer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Engineering Insight:** A fixed platform beyond ~400 m depth would need **> 100,000 tonnes of steel** to remain rigid. A compliant tower of the same function requires **< 20,000 tonnes** because the dynamic load itself is reduced by 60–80% once the structure is detuned from wave frequency.

## 1.4 The KTU Classification of Compliant Structures

The KTU syllabus groups compliant structures into the following primary categories (examined under Module 1):

| S.No | Category | Typical Depth | Restoring Mechanism |
|------|----------|---------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Compliant Tower (CT)** | 300 – 900 m | Pile foundation flexure |
| 2 | **Guyed Tower (GT)** | 300 – 1,000 m | Guy-line tension |
| 3 | **Articulated Loading Column (ALC / ALP)** | 100 – 1,500 m | Buoyancy + ballast |
| 4 | **Tension Leg Platform (TLP)** | 300 – 1,500 m | Vertical tendon tension |
| 5 | **Spar Platform** | 500 – 3,000 m | Distributed buoyancy + ballast |
| 6 | **Semi-Submersible (Semi)** | 200 – 3,000 m | Mooring line tension |
| 7 | **FPSO (Floating Production, Storage, Offloading)** | 200 – 3,000 m | Weathervaning + turret mooring |
| 8 | **Subsea Production System** | Any depth | Seabed support |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tagging:** A 2024 ESE question may use the phrase *"classify compliant structures with examples"* (3 marks) or *"explain the working principle of a Tension Leg Platform"* (7–14 marks). Memorize the **restoring mechanism** column above — it is the single most important discriminator in exam answers.

## 1.5 Fixed vs. Compliant — A Direct Comparison

| Parameter | Fixed Structure | Compliant Structure |
|-----------|-----------------|---------------------|
| Natural Period $T_n$ | 1 – 2 s | 20 – 200 s |
| Relationship with $T_w$ | $T_n \ll T_w$ | $T_n \gg T_w$ |
| Allowable horizontal displacement | < 0.5% of depth | 5 – 10% of depth |
| Steel consumption | Cubic with depth | Linear with depth |
| Dominant load case | Inertia of structure | Hydrodynamic + mooring |
| Failure mode | Foundation / member buckling | Mooring line failure |
| Cost-effective range | 0 – 400 m | 300 – 3,000 m |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Design Philosophy — Period Detuning

The **central theoretical concept** of all compliant structures is the exploitation of the **frequency-domain separation** between wave excitation and structural response.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Principle:** A wave imparts maximum force on a structure when the structure's natural period matches the wave period (**resonance condition**). By deliberately making the structure very flexible (low stiffness, hence long period), the system operates in the **quasi-static regime**, where dynamic amplification factors approach unity.

### Step-by-Step Logic of Detuning:

1. **Step 1 — Wave Spectrum Identification:** Identify the dominant wave period $T_w$ from the site-specific Pierson-Moskowitz or JONSWAP spectrum. Typical North Sea values: $T_w \approx 6$–$18$ s.

2. **Step 2 — Target Natural Period Selection:** Engineer the structure so that $T_n \ge 3T_w$. For $T_w = 12$ s, the target is $T_n \ge 36$ s.

3. **Step 3 — Stiffness Engineering:** Since $T_n = 2\pi\sqrt{m/k}$, achieving a large $T_n$ requires **reducing the effective lateral stiffness $k$** of the support system. This is done by:
   - Using **flexible pile foundations** (Compliant Tower),
   - Using **pre-tensioned tethers** (TLP),
   - Using **distributed buoyancy chambers** (Spar).

4. **Step 4 — Damping Provision:** Compliant structures have inherently low damping (often < 2% of critical). Supplemental damping is introduced via **strakes, bilge keels, heave plates, and viscous dampers**.

5. **Step 5 — Mooring / Tendon Design:** The restoring force is supplied by mooring lines or tendons. These are sized for both **ultimate limit state (ULS)** and **fatigue limit state (FLS)** at the mean offset position.

## 2.2 Single-Degree-of-Freedom (SDOF) Idealization

All compliant structures can be idealized, to first order, as a **rigid mass supported by a linear spring (with optional damper)**:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$$

Where:
- $m$ = total displaced mass of the structure + added mass of entrained water,
- $c$ = hydrodynamic + mooring damping coefficient,
- $k$ = effective lateral stiffness of mooring / tendons / foundation,
- $F(t)$ = total horizontal wave force = $F_I + F_D$.

### Hydrodynamic Force Components (Morison Equation):

The wave force per unit length on a slender member is:

$$f(t) = \underbrace{\rho C_M A \dot{u}}_{\text{Inertia } F_I} + \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D D \vert u \vert u}_{\text{Drag } F_D}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For compliant structures operating in the **drag-dominated regime** (large Keulegan-Carpenter number, $KC > 20$), the inertia term becomes secondary and the drag term dominates. This is the opposite of fixed platforms in deep water.

## 2.3 Linearization of the Drag Term (Critical KTU Concept)

Because the drag term $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D D |u|u$ is **quadratic in velocity**, it is non-linear and cannot be solved analytically. The **KTU-syllabus-required** approach is **equivalent linearization** using an *equivalent damping coefficient*:

$$c_{eq} = \frac{1}{\pi}\rho C_D D \sigma_u \sqrt{\frac{8}{\pi}}$$

Where $\sigma_u$ is the RMS water particle velocity. The equivalent linearized equation becomes:

$$m\ddot{x} + (c + c_{eq})\dot{x} + kx = F_I(t)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Trap:** Examiners frequently ask why the drag force is *linearized* rather than *directly integrated*. The answer: **direct integration of $|u|u$ over a Gaussian sea state has no closed-form solution**. Linearization allows spectral analysis (PSD method) to be used.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is the **complete high-yield formula set** for Module 1. Every equation listed has appeared (in equivalent form) in past KTU examinations.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|---------------|---------------|
| 1 | Natural period (SDOF) | $T_n = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k}}$ | seconds (s) |
| 2 | Natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | rad/s |
| 3 | Detuning criterion | $T_n \ge 3T_w$ (wave) | dimensionless ratio |
| 4 | Morison inertia force | $F_I = \rho C_M A \dot{u}$ | N/m |
| 5 | Morison drag force | $F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D D \vert u \vert u$ | N/m |
| 6 | Equivalent linear drag damping | $c_{eq} = \dfrac{1}{\pi}\rho C_D D \sigma_u \sqrt{8/\pi}$ | N·s/m |
| 7 | SDOF equation of motion | $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$ | N (force balance) |
| 8 | Critical damping | $c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2m\omega_n$ | N·s/m |
| 9 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{c_c}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Dynamic amplification factor | $DAF = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$, $r = \omega/\omega_n$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Compliant tower base shear | $V_b \approx k_{pile} \cdot x_{top}$ | N |
| 12 | TLP tendon stiffness (vertical) | $k_v = \dfrac{n E A_{tendon}}{L_{tendon}}$ | N/m |
| 13 | Spar heave natural period | $T_h = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{m + m_{a,heave}}{k_h}}$ | s |
| 14 | Mooring line catenary tension (horiz.) | $T_H = w \cdot s$, where $s$ = horizontal scope | N |
| 15 | Buoyancy restoring force (Spar) | $F_{buoy} = \rho_w g V_{displaced}$ | N |
| 16 | Offset under steady current | $x_{eq} = \dfrac{F_D}{k_{mooring}}$ | m |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Reminder for Students:** The vertical pipe `|u|` (absolute value) in exam scripts must be written with `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid line-breaking errors in digital submissions.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compliant Tower (Hibernia, Cognac):** Reduced steel tonnage by 70% compared to a fixed jacket in 300 m water.
- **TLP (Hutton, Auger, Ursa):** Enables dry-tree production in 1,000+ m depth — critical for oil & gas tieback to shore.
- **FPSO (Cidade de Mangaratiba, Shell Prelude):** Used in ultra-deep water and remote fields; weathervaning minimizes wave load.
- **Spar (Holstein, Perdido):** Provides excellent heave response, ideal for dry-tree drilling at 2,400+ m.

## 2.6 The "Why" Behind the Classification

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Synthesis:** Every compliant structure can be reduced to the same template:
>
> **Buoyancy + Mooring / Tendons ⟶ Static Equilibrium ⟶ Lateral Restoring Stiffness $k$ ⟶ Natural Period $T_n \gg T_w$ ⟶ Detuned Response ⟶ Reduced Dynamic Load.**
>
> The differences between TLP, Spar, Semi-submersible, and FPSO are *not* conceptual — they are *mechanical*. The mathematics is identical; only the **geometry of the restoring system** changes.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Natural Period of a Compliant Tower

**Given:**
- Effective horizontal stiffness at the top: $k = 5 \times 10^7$ N/m
- Effective mass: $m = 1.0 \times 10^8$ kg

**Find:** $T_n$ and check detuning for $T_w = 10$ s.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_n &= 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k}} \\[6pt]
    &= 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{1.0 \times 10^8}{5 \times 10^7}} \\[6pt]
    &= 2\pi \sqrt{2.0} \\[6pt]
    &= 2\pi \times 1.4142 \\[6pt]
    &= 8.886 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Detuning Check:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_n / T_w &= 8.886 / 10 = 0.889
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Since $T_n < T_w$, the structure is **not properly detuned** — it is in the wave-frequency range and is at risk of dynamic amplification. The stiffness must be **reduced** (e.g., by using a more flexible pile group, or by adding compliant tethers). Target new $k$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_n^{target} &= 3 \times T_w = 30 \text{ s} \\[6pt]
k_{req} &= \dfrac{4\pi^2 m}{T_n^{2}} \\[6pt]
        &= \dfrac{4\pi^2 \times 1.0 \times 10^8}{900} \\[6pt]
        &= 4.386 \times 10^6 \text{ N/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedagogical Insight:** Notice that **reducing stiffness increases $T_n$**. This is the *opposite* of intuition in structural engineering (where we usually stiffen to reduce deflection). In compliant design, the *deflection itself* is the load-reduction mechanism.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Dynamic Amplification Factor (DAF) for a Compliant Structure

**Given:** A guyed tower with $\omega_n = 0.25$ rad/s, $\zeta = 0.05$. Wave excitation frequency $\omega = 1.2$ rad/s.

**Step 1 — Compute the frequency ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \dfrac{1.2}{0.25} = 4.8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $r^2$ and $2\zeta r$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r^2 &= 23.04 \\[4pt]
2\zeta r &= 2 \times 0.05 \times 4.8 = 0.48
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the DAF formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DAF &= \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}} \\[6pt]
    &= \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1 - 23.04)^2 + (0.48)^2}} \\[6pt]
    &= \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(-22.04)^2 + 0.2304}} \\[6pt]
    &= \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{485.76 + 0.23}} \\[6pt]
    &= \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{485.99}} \\[6pt]
    &= \dfrac{1}{22.045} \\[6pt]
    &= 0.0454
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{dynamic} &= DAF \times F_{static} \\[4pt]
            &= 0.0454 \times F_{static}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The dynamic force is only **4.54%** of what it would be under a static application. This demonstrates the **massive load reduction** achievable by detuning. The **further** $T_n$ is from $T_w$, the lower the DAF.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Static Offset of a Semi-Submersible Under Current Drag

**Given:**
- Current velocity $U = 1.5$ m/s at 100 m depth
- Projected area $A = 2,000$ m²
- Drag coefficient $C_D = 1.2$
- Seawater density $\rho = 1,025$ kg/m³
- Mooring line stiffness $k_m = 8 \times 10^6$ N/m

**Step 1 — Total steady drag force:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_D &= \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D A U^2 \\[4pt]
    &= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 1.2 \times 2000 \times (1.5)^2 \\[4pt]
    &= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 1.2 \times 2000 \times 2.25 \\[4pt]
    &= 2,767,500 \text{ N} \\[4pt]
    &= 2.768 \text{ MN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Equilibrium offset (quasi-static):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_D &= k_m \cdot x_{eq} \\[4pt]
x_{eq} &= \dfrac{F_D}{k_m} \\[4pt]
       &= \dfrac{2.768 \times 10^6}{8 \times 10^6} \\[4pt]
       &= 0.346 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Insight:** A 34.6 cm steady offset is well within the serviceability limit (typically ±5% of water depth = 7.5 m for 150 m depth). The structure survives the current easily.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — TLP Tendon Stiffness

**Given:** 4 vertical tendons, each of length $L = 1,200$ m, cross-section $A_t = 0.10$ m², Young's modulus $E = 210 \times 10^9$ Pa.

**Step 1 — Individual tendon axial stiffness:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_{single} &= \dfrac{E A_t}{L} \\[4pt]
           &= \dfrac{210 \times 10^9 \times 0.10}{1200} \\[4pt]
           &= 1.75 \times 10^7 \text{ N/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Vertical (heave) stiffness (tendons in parallel):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_v &= n \cdot k_{single} \\[4pt]
    &= 4 \times 1.75 \times 10^7 \\[4pt]
    &= 7.0 \times 10^7 \text{ N/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Note:** The tendon stiffness controls **heave (vertical)** response. For **surge / sway (horizontal)** response, the restoring force is provided by the **horizontal component of tendon tension** (geometric stiffness), not axial stiffness. This is a frequent KTU exam question.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Compliant Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following decision matrix is the KTU-syllabus-standard selection tool. Memorize it for 14-mark design questions.

| Selection Criterion | TLP | Spar | Semi-Sub | FPSO | Compliant Tower |
|---------------------|-----|------|----------|------|-----------------|
| Dry-tree drilling? | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ |
| Storage capacity? | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓✓ | ✗ |
| Active weathervaning? | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓✓ | ✗ |
| Deepest proven depth | 1,500 m | 3,000 m | 3,000 m | 3,000 m | 900 m |
| Mooring complexity | Low (4 tendons) | Med (chain) | High (8–12 lines) | High (turret) | Low (piles) |
| Motion performance | Excellent heave | Best heave | Good (heave plates) | Acceptable | Good (deep draft) |
| Steel tonnage | Medium | Very High | Medium | Medium | Low |
| Cost (CAPEX index) | 1.0 | 1.4 | 0.9 | 0.7 | 0.8 |

> **Legend:** ✓✓ = Strongly suitable, ✓ = Suitable, ✗ = Not suitable.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Compliant Structure System

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **load path and response mechanism** shared by all compliant structure classes. This is the **canonical KTU Module 1 architecture diagram**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Environmental Load WAVE / WIND / CURRENT] --> B[Hydrodynamic Force F_I plus F_D via Morison]
    B --> C[Vessel / Column RIGID BODY]
    C --> D{Displacement x}
    D --> E1[Restoring Force K times x]
    D --> E2[Damping Force C times dx/dt]
    D --> E3[Inertia Force M times d2x/dt2]
    E1 --> F[Mooring Lines OR Tendons OR Piles]
    E2 --> G[Hydrodynamic Dampers plus Strakes plus Heave Plates]
    E3 --> H[Structure Mass plus Added Mass]
    F --> I[Equilibrium Check]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{Design Criteria Satisfied?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Service: Offset within 5 percent of depth]
    J -->|No| L[Redesign: Reduce K, Increase M, Add Damping]
    L --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** All compliant structures, regardless of type, follow this same **Mass–Spring–Damper feedback loop**. The differences lie in *how* the spring (restoring force) and damper are physically realized.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FixedLike["Fixed Reference"]

    end
    subgraph Compliant["Compliant Family"]

        C2[Guyed Tower]

        C4[Spar]
        C5[Semi-Submersible]

        C7[Articulated Column]
    end
    F1 -->|Exceeds 400 m depth| C1
    F1 -->|Exceeds 400 m depth| C3

```

## 4.3 Force-Response Schematic for a Compliant Tower

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wave Particle Velocity u t] --> B[Morison Drag F_D = 0.5 rho C_D D u²]
    A --> C[Morison Inertia F_I = rho C_M A du/dt]
    B --> D[Total Force F t]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Top Mass M]
    E --> F[Lateral Deflection x t]
    F --> G[Pile Flexure Spring K_pile]
    F --> H[Hydrodynamic Damping C]
    G --> I[Restoring + Damping Reaction]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Net Acceleration M d2x/dt2]
    J --> F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Use:** This diagram should be drawn (or mentally reconstructed) by the student in every 7-mark question on the "working principle of a compliant tower." It is the **single most important exam visual**.

## 4.4 TLP Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Surface Vessel Hull] -->|dry tree supported| B[Topside Production]
    A --> C[Buoyancy Reserve B]
    A --> D[Surge plus Sway plus Heave DOF]
    D --> E[4 Vertical Tendons]
    E --> F[Templete on Seabed]
    F --> G[Pile Foundation]
    D --> H[Heave Restoring: Tendon Axial Stiffness]
    D --> I[Surge Restoring: Horizontal Component of Pretension]
    H --> J[Heave Natural Period 3 to 5 s]
    I --> K[Surge Natural Period 100 to 200 s]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** A TLP's *heave* period is short (3–5 s) and close to the wave period. Therefore, heave is *actively attenuated* by high tendon stiffness. But the *surge* period is very long (100+ s) — hence TLP is compliant only in the **horizontal degrees of freedom**, not vertically.

## 4.5 Failure-Cause Tree for Compliant Structures

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compliant Structure Failure] --> B[Mooring Failure]
    A --> C[Tendon Failure]
    A --> D[Pile Failure]
    A --> E[Hydrodynamic Resonance]
    A --> F[Fatigue Failure]
    B --> B1[Line Snap]
    B --> B2[Anchor Pullout]
    C --> C1[Corrosion at Splash Zone]
    C --> C2[TLP Tendon Fatigue]
    D --> D1[Scour Induced Softening]
    D --> D2[Cyclic Degradation]
    E --> E1[Improper Detuning]
    E --> E2[Vortex Induced Vibration]
    F --> F1[Wave Load Cycling]
    F --> F2[Stress Concentration at Welds]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Define a compliant offshore structure. State two examples.

**Model Answer:**

> A compliant offshore structure is one whose **natural period of horizontal oscillation is deliberately made much larger than the dominant wave period** (typically $T_n \ge 3T_w$), so that the structure yields to environmental loads rather than resisting them rigidly. The restoring force is provided by mooring lines, tendons, buoyancy, or pile flexure.

**Examples:**
1. Tension Leg Platform (TLP)
2. Spar Platform

(Any two from: Compliant Tower, Guyed Tower, Articulated Column, Semi-Submersible, FPSO — each carries 1 mark.)

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Why is the drag term in the Morison equation linearized for compliant structures?

**Model Answer:**

> The drag term $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D D |u|u$ is **quadratic in velocity**, making the equation of motion non-linear. Direct integration over a stochastic sea state has **no closed-form solution**, and time-domain simulation is computationally expensive.
>
> The **equivalent linearization** technique replaces the non-linear drag with an equivalent viscous damping coefficient $c_{eq} = \tfrac{1}{\pi}\rho C_D D \sigma_u \sqrt{8/\pi}$, enabling **spectral (frequency-domain) analysis** and direct use of the SDOF solution.

(1 mark for stating non-linearity, 1 mark for the consequence, 1 mark for the linearization purpose.)

---

## 5.3 Step-by-Step Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a) — Working Principle of TLP (7 Marks)

**Sketch description (2 marks):** A TLP consists of a **pontoon-type hull** floating with **excess buoyancy**. **Vertical pre-tensioned steel tendons** connect the hull corners to a **template** on the seabed, which is pile-founded.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
- The hull is pulled **downward** by its own weight, but **pushed upward** by the net buoyancy reserve. The tendons are kept in **constant tension** (typically 5–10% of hull weight).
- The tendons allow **vertical heave** motion (small) but resist **vertical displacement** by axial stiffness.
- Horizontal motions (surge, sway) are resisted by the **horizontal component of tendon tension** acting as a geometric spring.

**Restoring Mechanisms (2 marks):**
- **Heave restoring force:** $F_{heave} = k_v \cdot z$ where $k_v = nEA_t/L$ (axial stiffness of $n$ tendons in parallel).
- **Surge restoring force:** $F_{surge} = T_{pre} \cdot \sin\theta \approx T_{pre} \cdot (x/H)$ where $H$ is tendon length and $x$ is horizontal offset. This is a **geometric stiffness**, not material stiffness.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:** $m = 1.5 \times 10^8$ kg, $n = 4$, $A_t = 0.12$ m², $L = 1,100$ m, $E = 210 \times 10^9$ Pa, $T_w = 8$ s.

**Step 1 — Single tendon axial stiffness (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_{single} &= \dfrac{E A_t}{L} \\[4pt]
           &= \dfrac{210 \times 10^9 \times 0.12}{1100} \\[4pt]
           &= 2.291 \times 10^7 \text{ N/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total heave stiffness $k_v$ (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_v &= n \cdot k_{single} \\[4pt]
    &= 4 \times 2.291 \times 10^7 \\[4pt]
    &= 9.164 \times 10^7 \text{ N/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Heave natural period (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{heave} &= 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k_v}} \\[4pt]
          &= 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{1.5 \times 10^8}{9.164 \times 10^7}} \\[4pt]
          &= 2\pi \sqrt{1.637} \\[4pt]
          &= 2\pi \times 1.2794 \\[4pt]
          &= 8.04 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Detuning Check (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{T_{heave}}{T_w} = \dfrac{8.04}{8.0} = 1.005
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** $T_{heave} \approx T_w$, which means the TLP is **NOT compliant in heave** — it is intentionally designed to have a heave period close to the wave period to use the high tendon stiffness as a *rigid* heave support. The TLP is compliant only in **surge and sway**. This is a key conceptual point often missed by students.

---

### Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a) — Fixed vs. Compliant Structures (7 Marks)

**Definition of Fixed Structure (2 marks):** A fixed offshore structure is rigidly supported on the seabed by **piles driven through the legs/skirt**. It is designed so that all environmental loads are transferred to the foundation as base shear and overturning moment, with negligible horizontal displacement at the top. Examples: jacket platform, gravity-based structure.

**Definition of Compliant Structure (2 marks):** A compliant structure is intentionally **flexible** at the foundation or mooring level, allowing it to **oscillate** under wave loading. The natural period is detuned to be much longer than the wave period. Examples: TLP, Spar, FPSO.

**Period Detuning Concept (3 marks):**

- The dynamic amplification factor is $DAF = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$.
- When $r = \omega/\omega_n$ is small (i.e., $\omega_n \gg \omega$, *low frequency*), $r \to 0$ and $DAF \to 1$ (quasi-static).
- For compliant structures, the natural frequency $\omega_n$ is *small* (long period), so $r$ is large and $DAF$ is small.
- The KTU design rule is $T_n \ge 3 T_w$.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given:** $k = 4 \times 10^6$ N/m, $m = 6 \times 10^7$ kg, $T_w = 9$ s.

**Step 1 — Compute natural period (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_n &= 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k}} \\[4pt]
    &= 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{6 \times 10^7}{4 \times 10^6}} \\[4pt]
    &= 2\pi \sqrt{15} \\[4pt]
    &= 2\pi \times 3.873 \\[4pt]
    &= 24.33 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute detuning ratio (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{T_n}{T_w} = \dfrac{24.33}{9} = 2.70
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Comment on compliance (2 marks):**

> The detuning ratio is $T_n / T_w = 2.70$, which is **just below** the KTU recommended threshold of $3.0$. While the structure is well-detuned from a resonance perspective, it does **not strictly satisfy** the conservative KTU criterion. The engineer should either:
> 1. **Reduce the stiffness** (e.g., use a more flexible pile group), or
> 2. **Increase the added mass** (e.g., enlarge the submerged volume).
>
> A recommended redesign: target $T_n = 27$ s, giving $T_n / T_w = 3.0$ — this would require $k = 3.25 \times 10^6$ N/m.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Most Common Mark Losses:**
> 1. **Confusing "compliant" with "weak":** Compliance is *engineered flexibility*, not a design weakness. Many students lose 1–2 marks by failing to articulate this in Part A answers.
> 2. **Forgetting units:** In numerical answers, forgetting to convert GPa to Pa or mm to m costs 0.5–1 mark per instance.
> 3. **Confusing TLP heave with TLP surge:** TLP is **not compliant in heave** — it is rigid in heave. It is compliant only horizontally. This is the most heavily tested distinction in KTU 2024 scheme exams.
> 4. **Skipping the detuning check:** A numerical answer that computes $T_n$ but does *not* verify $T_n / T_w$ loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Linearization step missing:** When Morison's equation is mentioned, students must state the linearization purpose explicitly. This is a **mandatory 1-mark checkpoint** in KTU 2024 marking schemes.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Compliant Structures**

- [x] **Compliant structure definition:** $T_n \gg T_w$ (minimum 3× ratio).
- [x] **Operating depth:** 300 m to 3,000 m.
- [x] **Eight classes:** Compliant Tower, Guyed Tower, ALC, TLP, Spar, Semi-Submersible, FPSO, Subsea System.
- [x] **Restoring mechanisms to memorize:**
  - *Pile flexure* → Compliant Tower
  - *Guy-line tension* → Guyed Tower
  - *Tendon axial stiffness* → TLP (heave)
  - *Tendon geometric stiffness* → TLP (surge)
  - *Distributed buoyancy + ballast* → Spar
  - *Mooring line catenary* → Semi-Sub, FPSO
- [x] **SDOF equation:** $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$.
- [x] **Natural period formula:** $T_n = 2\pi\sqrt{m/k}$.
- [x] **Morison's equation:** $F_I + F_D$ — both components must be stated separately.
- [x] **Equivalent linearization:** $c_{eq} = \tfrac{1}{\pi}\rho C_D D \sigma_u \sqrt{8/\pi}$.
- [x] **DAF formula:** $DAF = 1/\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}$.
- [x] **Fixed vs. Compliant** — 5 comparison parameters: $T_n$, displacement, steel tonnage, dominant load, failure mode.
- [x] **TLP is rigid in heave** — common KTU trap question.
- [x] **Detuning rule:** $T_n \ge 3 T_w$ (KTU 2024 standard).
- [x] **Serviceability limit:** offset $\le$ 5%–10% of water depth.
- [x] **Mooring / tendon design:** must satisfy both ULS and FLS.
- [x] **Damping sources:** strakes, bilge keels, heave plates, viscous dampers (typically < 2% of critical in compliant structures).
- [x] **Key examples to memorize:** Hibernia, Cognac (Compliant Tower); Hutton, Auger (TLP); Holstein, Perdido (Spar); FPSO Prelude (FPSO).

> **End of Module 1 — Compliant Structures Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Floating Structures and Novel & Small Field Offshore Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST722 - FUNDAMENTALS OF OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Floating Structures and Novel & Small Field Offshore Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Floating Offshore Structures?

A **Floating Offshore Structure (FOS)** is a marine vessel or platform that is buoyant and held in position (station-keeping) over a hydrocarbon reservoir using a system of mooring lines, anchors, or dynamic positioning (DP), rather than being fixed to the seabed via piles or gravity foundations. They are deployed in deep water and ultra-deep water where fixed jacket platforms become economically and technically infeasible (typically water depths > **300 m**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Floating structures are compliant or partially compliant buoyant structures that rely on hydrostatic buoyancy, hydrodynamics, and mooring systems to maintain their operational position for drilling, production, storage, or processing of hydrocarbons in offshore oil and gas fields.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **giant cork floating in a bathtub**, but instead of being free-floating, it is tied to the bottom of the tub with **elastic strings (mooring lines)**. The cork's tendency to float upward (buoyancy) keeps it at the waterline, and the strings prevent it from drifting away. The waves and currents in the tub make the cork bob, tilt, and sway — this is exactly how a floating platform behaves in the ocean. The "strings" are designed to absorb these motions, keeping the platform within safe operational limits.

## 1.2 Classification of Floating Offshore Structures

Floating structures are broadly classified based on their **functional use** and **geometric/hull form**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary Functional Classifications (KTU Module 1 Focus):**
> 1. **Drilling Units** – Mobile rigs used for exploration drilling (Drillships, Semi-submersibles).
> 2. **Production Units** – Stationary structures for hydrocarbon extraction (FPSO, TLP, Spar, Semi-submersible Production Unit).
> 3. **Storage Units** – Floating Storage and Offloading (FSO), FPSO.
> 4. **Support Units** – Crew boats, anchor handling vessels.

## 1.3 Novel Offshore Structures

**Novel offshore structures** refer to innovative, non-traditional hull forms or platform concepts developed to overcome the limitations of conventional designs — especially in **deep water (1500 m – 3000 m+)**, **harsh metocean environments**, or **marginal field development**. Examples include the **Truss Spar**, **Cell Spar**, **SeaStar Mini-TLP**, **Moses Tower**, **Subsea Processing Stations**, and **Barge-based FPSOs**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The word **"Novel"** in the KTU syllabus emphasizes the **innovative, non-conventional geometry** — designed to reduce cost, increase payload, improve motion characteristics, or enable deployment in smaller fields.

## 1.4 Small Field Offshore Structures

**Small field structures** are purpose-built, economically optimized offshore platforms designed to develop **marginal hydrocarbon fields** — reservoirs with limited reserves (typically < 50 million barrels), short production life, or remote locations where installing a full-scale platform is uneconomical. They are characterized by **compact hulls, low fabrication cost, minimum topside, and reusability**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Small Field Structures Matter:** Globally, there are hundreds of small/marginal fields. A conventional semi-submersible production unit costing **USD 1 Billion+** is uneconomical for a 30 million-barrel field. Hence, novel cost-effective solutions are essential.

### Common Small Field Structure Types

| Type | Key Feature | Typical Use |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Mini-TLP** | 3 or 4 columns with taut mooring | Marginal deep-water fields |
| **SeaStar** | Catenary moored, circular hull | Small deep-water production |
| **Barge (Moses)** | Shallow-draft, seabed-founded | Ultra-shallow water |
| **Spar** (Classic/Truss) | Deep draft, heavy stability | Deep and ultra-deep water |
| **FPSO** (Converted tanker) | Ship-shaped, turret moored | Storage + production |

### Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Sea Water Density:** $\rho_{sw}$ = **1025 kg/m³** (standard offshore value)
- **Acceleration due to Gravity:** $g$ = **9.81 m/s²**
- **Specific Weight of Sea Water:** $\gamma_{sw}$ = **10.05 kN/m³**
- **Typical Deep Water Threshold:** > **300 m**
- **Ultra-Deep Water Threshold:** > **1500 m**

> [!NOTE]
> All buoyancy, draft, and stability calculations in KTU exams will use $\rho_{sw}$ = **1025 kg/m³** unless otherwise specified in the problem.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Floating Structure Theory — The "Why" and "How"

### 2.1.1 Buoyancy Principle (Archimedes' Law)

**Why does it float?** Because the **weight of water displaced** equals the **weight of the structure**.

**How is equilibrium achieved?** A floating body displaces a volume of water such that the buoyant force (upward) equals the total gravitational force (downward) acting on the body.

The fundamental governing equation is:

$$F_B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{displaced}$$

Where:
- $F_B$ = Buoyant force (N)
- $\rho_{sw}$ = Mass density of sea water (**1025 kg/m³**)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $V_{displaced}$ = Volume of water displaced (m³)

### 2.1.2 Stability of Floating Bodies

A floating body is in **static equilibrium** when:
1. Buoyant force = Weight of the body.
2. The **center of gravity (G)** and **center of buoyancy (B)** lie on the same vertical line.

When the body tilts (rolls) by a small angle $\phi$, the center of buoyancy shifts to a new position $B'$. The line of action of the new buoyant force intersects the original vertical centerline at a point called the **Metacenter (M)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metacentric Height (GM)** is the most critical stability parameter in KTU exams. It determines whether the floating structure returns to its upright position (stable) or capsizes (unstable).
> - **GM > 0:** Stable (positive stability).
> - **GM = 0:** Neutral equilibrium.
> - **GM < 0:** Unstable (capsizes).

The metacentric radius $BM$ is calculated as:

$$BM = \frac{I}{V_{displaced}}$$

Where:
- $I$ = Second moment of area of the waterplane about the axis of rotation (m⁴)
- $V_{displaced}$ = Displaced volume of the body (m³)

The metacentric height $GM$ is:

$$GM = KB + BM - KG$$

Where:
- $KB$ = Vertical distance from keel (K) to center of buoyancy (B) — typically $KB = T/2$ for a rectangular barge.
- $KG$ = Vertical distance from keel (K) to center of gravity (G).
- $T$ = Draft of the vessel (m).

### 2.1.3 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) of a Floating Body

A floating body has **6 DOF**: 3 translational (Surge, Sway, Heave) and 3 rotational (Roll, Pitch, Yaw). Different floating structures have different motion characteristics — for example, a **Spar** has excellent heave response due to its deep draft, while a **TLP** has near-zero heave due to its taut vertical mooring.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Questions on "why a Spar is preferred in deep water" almost always expect an answer about **heave suppression** and **natural period decoupling** from wave periods.

## 2.2 Novel Offshore Structures — Theory and Innovation

### 2.2.1 Spar Platform

A Spar is a **deep-draft (200 m+), vertical cylindrical hull** that derives stability from its **low center of gravity** and **deep draft ballast**. It is ideal for **ultra-deep water (1500–3000 m)** because of its excellent motion response.

**Key Features:**
- Classic Spar: Cylindrical hull (single piece).
- Truss Spar: Upper hull + mid-section truss + lower hull (ballast).
- Cell Spar: Multiple small cylinders bundled together (e.g., Brent Spar).

### 2.2.2 Tension Leg Platform (TLP)

A TLP is a semi-submersible-like structure held in place by **vertical, taut tendons (tethers)** attached to the seabed. The tendons are pre-tensioned, virtually eliminating heave motion. TLPs are used in deep water (300–2000 m).

### 2.2.3 FPSO (Floating Production Storage and Offloading)

An FPSO is typically a **converted oil tanker** or a custom-built ship-shaped vessel that produces, processes, stores, and offloads oil. It uses a **turret mooring system** that allows it to weathervane (rotate) around a fixed point.

## 2.3 Small Field Offshore Structures — The Innovation Drivers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 1 Highlight:** Small field structures are designed around three core principles:
> 1. **Minimum steel** (low CAPEX).
> 2. **Reusability** (move from field to field).
> 3. **Shallow or moderate draft** (easier wet tow).

### 2.3.1 Mini-TLP

A scaled-down TLP with **3 or 4 columns**, used for marginal fields in **500–1500 m** water depth. Lower cost, simpler tendons.

### 2.3.2 SeaStar

A circular, catenary-moored floater designed for **small deep-water fields**. Acts as a mini-FPSO with production equipment directly on the hull.

### 2.3.3 Moses Tower

A **braced caisson / compliant tower** variant. A bottom-founded structure with a compliant upper section for small fields in **moderate depths**.

### 2.3.4 Barge-based Production

A simple rectangular barge with a small topside, suitable for **shallow water** marginal fields. Low cost, fast fabrication.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains the most frequently tested formulas in KTU ESE for Module 1. Memorize these.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Variables | Units |
|---|-----------|---------|-----------|-------|
| 1 | Buoyant Force | $F_B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V$ | $\rho$ = density, $V$ = displaced volume | N |
| 2 | Displaced Volume (Rectangular Barge) | $V = L \times B \times T$ | $L$ = length, $B$ = breadth, $T$ = draft | m³ |
| 3 | Metacentric Radius | $BM = I / V$ | $I$ = waterplane 2nd moment, $V$ = displaced volume | m |
| 4 | Waterplane Moment (Rectangle) | $I = L \cdot B^3 / 12$ | $L$, $B$ = waterplane dimensions | m⁴ |
| 5 | Metacentric Height | $GM = KB + BM - KG$ | $KB, KG$ measured from keel | m |
| 6 | Center of Buoyancy (Rectangle) | $KB = T/2$ | $T$ = draft | m |
| 7 | Heel Angle (Listing) | $\tan \phi = GM \cdot \theta / GM$ | For small angles, $\tan\phi \approx \phi$ (rad) | rad |
| 8 | Natural Period (Heave) | $T_n = 2\pi \sqrt{m / k}$ | $m$ = mass, $k$ = stiffness | s |
| 9 | Total Mooring Pretension (TLP) | $T_{total} = (W - F_B) / n$ | $W$ = weight, $n$ = number of tendons | N |
| 10 | Hydrostatic Pressure | $P = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot h$ | $h$ = depth below waterline | Pa |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Tip for Tables:** The symbol \vert or \mid is used in this table (e.g., in $I = L \cdot B^3 / 12$) to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. Do not use the vertical bar \vert inside KTU exam answers without wrapping them in dollar signs.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **FPSOs** dominate the deep-water market (e.g., **Petrobras, Shell, BP** use FPSOs in Brazil, Gulf of Mexico, West Africa).
- **Spar Platforms** are deployed in the **Gulf of Mexico** (e.g., **Holstein, Mad Dog, Tahiti**) for ultra-deep water.
- **TLPs** are used in the **Gulf of Mexico, North Sea, and offshore Malaysia** (e.g., **Hibernia, Auger, Ursa**).
- **Mini-TLPs and SeaStars** are used in the **Gulf of Mexico and offshore Indonesia** for marginal field development.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stability Criterion for a Floating Barge

### Problem Setup
A rectangular steel barge has the following dimensions:
- Length $L$ = **60 m**
- Breadth $B$ = **20 m**
- Draft $T$ = **4 m**
- Center of gravity (from keel) $KG$ = **2.5 m**

**Sea water density** $\rho_{sw}$ = **1025 kg/m³**

**Objective:** Determine whether the barge is stable, and calculate the metacentric height $GM$.

### Step 1: Compute the Displaced Volume

The displaced volume for a rectangular barge is simply the underwater volume:

$$V = L \times B \times T$$

Substituting the values:

$$V = 60 \times 20 \times 4$$

$$V = 4800 \; \text{m}^3$$

### Step 2: Compute the Buoyant Force (Verification)

The buoyant force should equal the total weight. We first compute it as a check:

$$F_B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V$$

$$F_B = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 4800$$

$$F_B = 48,258,600 \; \text{N}$$

$$F_B = 48,258.6 \; \text{kN}$$

This equals the displacement weight of the barge. The actual $W$ should equal $F_B$ at equilibrium draft.

### Step 3: Compute the Waterplane Second Moment of Area

For a rectangular waterplane, the second moment of area about the longitudinal (roll) axis is:

$$I_T = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$$

Substituting values:

$$I_T = \frac{60 \times 20^3}{12}$$

$$I_T = \frac{60 \times 8000}{12}$$

$$I_T = \frac{480,000}{12}$$

$$I_T = 40,000 \; \text{m}^4$$

### Step 4: Compute the Metacentric Radius $BM$

$$BM = \frac{I_T}{V}$$

$$BM = \frac{40,000}{4800}$$

$$BM = 8.333 \; \text{m}$$

### Step 5: Compute the Vertical Center of Buoyancy $KB$

For a rectangular barge, the center of buoyancy is at the centroid of the underwater volume, which lies at half the draft above the keel:

$$KB = \frac{T}{2}$$

$$KB = \frac{4}{2} = 2.0 \; \text{m}$$

### Step 6: Compute the Metacentric Height $GM$

$$GM = KB + BM - KG$$

$$GM = 2.0 + 8.333 - 2.5$$

$$GM = 7.833 \; \text{m}$$

### Step 7: Stability Conclusion

Since $GM$ > 0 (specifically, $GM = 7.833 \; m > 0$), the barge is **positively stable**. The large positive value of $GM$ indicates a very stiff vessel with a strong righting moment, meaning it will resist any tilting and return to upright.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A $GM$ of 7.833 m is unusually high for a seagoing vessel (typical ships have $GM$ = 0.5–2.0 m). For a stationary offshore barge in calm waters, this is acceptable. For a ship in waves, this would result in a very **stiff, uncomfortable, and potentially damaging** roll response.

## 3.2 Derivation: TLP Pretension Force

### Problem Setup
A Tension Leg Platform has the following properties:
- Total Weight $W$ = **80,000 tonnes** (operating + variable load)
- Buoyant Force $F_B$ = **120,000 tonnes** (displacement)
- Number of Tendons $n$ = **4**

**Objective:** Calculate the pretension force per tendon.

### Step 1: Calculate the Net Upward Buoyancy (Excess Buoyancy)

The tendons must hold the platform down against the **excess buoyancy**:

$$\Delta F = F_B - W$$

$$\Delta F = 120,000 - 80,000$$

$$\Delta F = 40,000 \; \text{tonnes-force}$$

This is the total downward force the tendons must collectively provide.

### Step 2: Calculate the Pretension Per Tendon

Assuming the load is shared equally among all tendons:

$$T_{tendon} = \frac{\Delta F}{n}$$

$$T_{tendon} = \frac{40,000}{4}$$

$$T_{tendon} = 10,000 \; \text{tonnes-force}$$

Converting to Newtons (1 tonne-force = 9810 N):

$$T_{tendon} = 10,000 \times 9810 = 98,100,000 \; \text{N}$$

$$T_{tendon} = 98.1 \; \text{MN}$$

### Step 3: Engineering Check

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The pretension in each tendon is **98.1 MN** (≈ 10,000 tonnes). This is enormous. In real TLPs, the tendons are typically **high-strength steel tubulars** with diameters of **0.5–0.8 m**. This is why TLP tendon design is one of the most critical engineering challenges in offshore engineering.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Novel vs. Conventional Structures

| Parameter | FPSO | TLP | Spar | Semi-Submersible | Mini-TLP | SeaStar |
|-----------|------|-----|------|-------------------|----------|---------|
| **Hull Type** | Ship-shaped | Pontoon + Columns | Vertical Cylinder | Submerged Pontoons | Mini Pontoon | Circular |
| **Water Depth (m)** | 100–3000 | 300–2000 | 500–3000+ | 200–3000 | 500–1500 | 500–1500 |
| **Mooring Type** | Catenary/DP | Taut Tendons | Catenary | Catenary/DP | Taut Tendons | Catenary |
| **Heave Motion** | Moderate | Near-zero | Very Low | Low | Near-zero | Moderate |
| **Storage** | Yes | No | No | No | No | Yes (small) |
| **Field Size** | Med–Large | Med–Large | Large | Med–Large | Small/Marginal | Small/Marginal |
| **CAPEX (Relative)** | Medium | High | High | High | Low | Low |
| **Novelty (KTU)** | Medium | Medium | High | Low | Very High | Very High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture: Floating Structure Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Floating Offshore Structures] --> B[Production Units]
    A --> C[Drilling Units]
    A --> D[Storage Units]
    A --> E[Novel Small Field Units]
    
    B --> B1[FPSO]
    B --> B2[TLP]
    B --> B3[Spar Platform]
    B --> B4[Semi-Submersible Production]
    
    C --> C1[Drillship]
    C --> C2[Semi-Submersible Drilling]
    
    D --> D1[FSO]
    D --> D2[FPSO with Storage]
    
    E --> E1[Mini-TLP]
    E --> E2[SeaStar]
    E --> E3[Moses Tower]
    E --> E4[Barge Based]
    
    B3 --> B3a[Classic Spar]
    B3 --> B3b[Truss Spar]
    B3 --> B3c[Cell Spar]
    
    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style B3a fill:#10b981,color:#ffffff
    style B3b fill:#10b981,color:#ffffff
    style B3c fill:#10b981,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: TLP Functional Architecture (Tendon Force Path)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Topside Facilities] --> B[Hull Pontoon]
    B --> C[Columns x4]
    C --> D[Tendon Top Connection]
    D --> E[Tendon Body]
    E --> F[Tendon Bottom Connection]
    F --> G[Pile Foundation on Seabed]
    
    P1[Excess Buoyancy] --> D
    P2[Wave Loads] --> C
    P3[Current Loads] --> C
    P4[Wind Loads] --> A
    
    style A fill:#1e40af,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#0ea5e9,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#1e3a3a,color:#ffffff
    style P1 fill:#fbbf24,color:#000000
    style P2 fill:#fbbf24,color:#000000
    style P3 fill:#fbbf24,color:#000000
    style P4 fill:#fbbf24,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow: Small Field Development Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Marginal Field Identified]) --> Q1{Reserves Size?}
    Q1 -->|Small-Med| S1[Select Novel Structure]
    Q1 -->|Large| S2[Select Conventional FPSO/TLP]
    
    S1 --> Q2{Water Depth?}
    Q2 -->|Shallow <300m| SF1[Barge or Moses]
    Q2 -->|Medium 300-1000m| SF2[Mini-TLP]
    Q2 -->|Deep >1000m| SF3[SeaStar or Spar]
    
    SF1 --> Out1[Lowest CAPEX Solution]
    SF2 --> Out2[Reusable Taut Moored Unit]
    SF3 --> Out3[Deep-Water Marginal Field Unit]
    
    style Start fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style Q1 fill:#fbbf24,color:#000000
    style Q2 fill:#fbbf24,color:#000000
    style S1 fill:#dc2626,color:#ffffff

```
