<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — READ FIRST**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** Marks are deducted for mixing *m* and *mm* in the same calculation. **Always convert D, t to metres** before plugging into SI formulas.
> 2. **Forgetting the ovality correction:** Many students compute $p_{el}$ for a "perfect" pipe and stop — examiners *expect* the $0.65\,\Delta_0$ reduction.
> 3. **Wrong collapse regime:** A 14-mark problem with $D/t = 80$ demands the **elastic** formula, not the plastic one. Read the slenderness first.
> 4. **Combined loading interaction:** Writing $\dfrac{p}{p_c} + \dfrac{M}{M_p}$ without defining both $p_c$ and $M_p$ explicitly loses **2 marks**.

---

## 📘 Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Distinguish between small-diameter and large-diameter offshore structures. Why is the classification based on D/t ratio more meaningful than D alone?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Small-diameter structures (D < 16 inches):** Used as flowlines, umbilicals, small-bore risers; failure dominated by **elastic hydrostatic collapse** governed by $E$ and $(t/D)^3$. **1 mark**
- **Large-diameter structures (D ≥ 16 inches):** Used as trunklines, export pipelines, large caissons, monopile foundations; failure dominated by **propagation buckling and combined loading**, governed by $\sigma_y$ and interaction equations. **1 mark**
- The D/t ratio classifies the *failure mode* (elastic/elastic-plastic/plastic), whereas D alone only classifies *capacity* — so D/t is the **mechanically meaningful** index. **1 mark**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Define propagation buckling in an offshore pipeline. State the formula and explain why it is critical for large-diameter pipes."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Once a pipe wall locally collapses, the buckle can travel *axially* along the pipe like a zipper, flattening large lengths rapidly and catastrophically.
- **Formula (1 mark):** $p_{pr} = 24\,\sigma_y \cdot (t/D)^{5/2}$ (elastic–plastic regime).
- **Criticality (1 mark):** For large-D pipes the propagation pressure is often *much lower* than the hydrostatic head, so the entire pipeline can flatten in minutes unless **buckle arrestors** are welded at regular intervals.

---

## 📕 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module 2 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **7 + 7 marks**. The student must answer **EITHER** Option A **OR** Option B.

---

### **🅰️ Option A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]**

**A small-diameter offshore flowline has the following data:**
- Outer diameter, $D = 250 \text{ mm} = 0.25 \text{ m}$
- Wall thickness, $t = 12 \text{ mm} = 0.012 \text{ m}$
- Yield strength, $\sigma_y = 415 \text{ MPa}$
- Elastic modulus, $E = 207 \text{ GPa}$
- Poisson's ratio, $\nu = 0.3$
- Initial ovality, $\Delta_0 = 0.015$
- Operating depth, $h = 800 \text{ m}$ (seawater)

#### **(a) [7 marks]** Determine the **elastic collapse pressure** of the pipe. Apply the ovality correction and compute the **factor of safety** against collapse at the operating depth.

#### **(b) [7 marks]** Check whether the pipe is safe under **combined loading** when it experiences a bending moment $M = 12 \text{ kN·m/m}$ in addition to the hydrostatic pressure.

---

#### **🔹 Solution to A(a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Slenderness ratio:**

$$\dfrac{D}{t} = \dfrac{0.25}{0.012} \approx 20.83$$

This is in the **elastic collapse regime** ($D/t > 20$ for elastic behaviour is borderline; here we use elastic formula as the question specifies). **1 mark**

**Step 2 — Elastic collapse pressure (perfect pipe):**

$$p_{el} = \dfrac{2E}{1-\nu^2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^{3}$$

$$= \dfrac{2 \times 207 \times 10^{9}}{1 - 0.3^2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{0.012}{0.25}\right)^{3}$$

$$= \dfrac{414 \times 10^{9}}{0.91} \times (0.048)^{3}$$

$$= 4.549 \times 10^{11} \times 1.105 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{p_{el} = 5.03 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} = 50.3 \text{ MPa}}$$

**2 marks**

**Step 3 — Apply ovality correction:**

$$p_{c,real} = p_{el} \cdot (1 - 0.65 \cdot \Delta_0)$$

$$= 50.3 \times (1 - 0.65 \times 0.015)$$

$$= 50.3 \times (1 - 0.00975)$$

$$= 50.3 \times 0.99025$$

$$\boxed{p_{c,real} = 49.81 \text{ MPa}}$$

**2 marks**

**Step 4 — Hydrostatic pressure at operating depth:**

$$p_e = \rho \cdot g \cdot h = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 800 = 8.04 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 8.04 \text{ MPa}$$

**1 mark**

**Step 5 — Factor of Safety:**

$$SF = \dfrac{p_{c,real}}{p_e} = \dfrac{49.81}{8.04} \approx 6.20$$

**1 mark** — **Pipe is SAFE (SF >> 2).**

---

#### **🔹 Solution to A(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Plastic collapse pressure:**

$$p_p = \dfrac{2 \sigma_y t}{D} = \dfrac{2 \times 415 \times 10^{6} \times 0.012}{0.25} = 3.984 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} = 39.84 \text{ MPa}$$

**2 marks**

**Step 2 — Plastic moment capacity per unit length:**

$$M_p = \sigma_y \cdot t^2 = 415 \times 10^{6} \times (0.012)^{2} = 59{,}760 \text{ N·m/m} \approx 59.76 \text{ kN·m/m}$$

**2 marks**

**Step 3 — Interaction equation:**

$$\dfrac{p_e}{p_c} + \dfrac{M}{M_p} = \dfrac{8.04}{39.84} + \dfrac{12}{59.76}$$

$$= 0.2018 + 0.2008 = 0.4026$$

$$\boxed{= 0.403 \le 1.0}$$

**3 marks** — **Pipe is SAFE under combined loading** (40% of yield capacity used).

---

### **🅱️ Option B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question — Alternative]**

**A large-diameter offshore export pipeline has:**
- $D = 1.0 \text{ m}$, $t = 0.02 \text{ m}$, $\sigma_y = 450 \text{ MPa}$, $E = 210 \text{ GPa}$
- Operating depth $h = 1500 \text{ m}$ (seawater, $\rho = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$)

#### **(a) [7 marks]** Compute the **propagation buckling pressure** and compare it to the **hydrostatic external pressure** at operating depth. Comment on whether **buckle arrestors are needed**.

#### **(b) [7 marks]** Determine the **elastic collapse pressure** of this pipe and the **factor of safety** against collapse. State whether the design is acceptable per DNV guidelines (SF ≥ 2).

---

#### **🔹 Solution to B(a) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Propagation buckling pressure (elastic–plastic):**

$$p_{pr} = 24 \cdot \sigma_y \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^{5/2}$$

$$= 24 \times 450 \times 10^{6} \times (0.02)^{5/2}$$

$$= 1.08 \times 10^{10} \times 5.657 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\boxed{p_{pr} = 61{,}090 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.61 \text{ bar}}$$

**3 marks**

**Step 2 — Hydrostatic pressure at 1500 m:**

$$p_e = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 1500 = 1.508 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} = 15.08 \text{ MPa} = 150.8 \text{ bar}$$

**2 marks**

**Step 3 — Comparison:**

$$\dfrac{p_e}{p_{pr}} = \dfrac{15.08 \text{ MPa}}{0.061 \text{ MPa}} \approx 247$$

**1 mark** — Hydrostatic pressure is **~247 times** the propagation pressure. The buckle **WILL propagate catastrophically** if it initiates.

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** **Buckle arrestors are MANDATORY** for this large-diameter pipe. Recommended spacing: every **100–200 m** along the trunkline. **1 mark**

---

#### **🔹 Solution to B(b) — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — D/t classification:**

$$\dfrac{D}{t} = \dfrac{1.0}{0.02} = 50$$

→ **Elastic / elastic–plastic transition** regime. **1 mark**

**Step 2 — Elastic collapse pressure (perfect pipe):**

$$p_{el} = \dfrac{2E}{1-\nu^2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^{3}$$

$$= \dfrac{2 \times 210 \times 10^{9}}{1 - 0.09} \times (0.02)^{3}$$

$$= 4.615 \times 10^{11} \times 8.0 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\boxed{p_{el} = 3.69 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 3.69 \text{ MPa}}$$

**3 marks**

**Step 3 — Factor of safety (without ovality correction, conservative assumption $\Delta_0 = 0$):**

$$SF = \dfrac{p_{el}}{p_e} = \dfrac{3.69 \text{ MPa}}{15.08 \text{ MPa}} = 0.245$$

**2 marks**

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** **SF = 0.245 < 2.0** → **NOT acceptable per DNV-OS-F101.** The wall thickness must be increased, or a different installation method (e.g. trenching) used to reduce the effective hydrostatic head. **1 mark**

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam!**

- ✅ **D < 16" → Small-diameter** (flowlines, umbilicals) | **D ≥ 16" → Large-diameter** (trunklines, caissons, monopiles).
- ✅ **D/t ratio selects the failure mode:** > 50 elastic, 30–50 elastic-plastic, < 30 plastic.
- ✅ **Elastic collapse formula:** $p_{el} = \dfrac{2E}{1-\nu^2}(t/D)^3$ — cubic in $(t/D)$.
- ✅ **Plastic collapse formula:** $p_p = 2\sigma_y(t/D)$ — linear in $(t/D)$.
- ✅ **Propagation buckling formula:** $p_{pr} = 24\sigma_y(t/D)^{5/2}$ — controls *large-D* design.
- ✅ **Ovality correction:** multiply $p_{el}$ by $(1 - 0.65\,\Delta_0)$.
- ✅ **Combined loading check:** $\dfrac{p}{p_c} + \dfrac{M}{M_p} \le 1.0$ (Langer interaction).
- ✅ **Standard values to memorise:** $E = 207$ GPa (steel), $\nu = 0.3$, $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².
- ✅ **Buckle arrestors:** required on *all* large-D deep-water lines where $p_e \gg p_{pr}$.
- ✅ **Unit discipline:** convert D, t to **metres** before SI formula insertion.
- ✅ **Codes referenced in KTU 2024:** DNV-OS-F101, API 1111, ASME B31.8.
- ✅ **Examination trap:** A 14-mark question on "propagation" *always* wants the $24\sigma_y(t/D)^{5/2}$ form, not the elastic $(t/D)^{5/3}$ form. Read the slenderness first.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vertical and Arbitrarily Oriented Cylindrical Members
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST722 - FUNDAMENTALS OF OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Vertical and Arbitrarily Oriented Cylindrical Members

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **cylindrical member** in offshore engineering is any slender, prismatic structural element of circular cross-section used in subsea and marine installations — including jacket legs, braces, risers, mooring lines, and conductor pipes. In the context of wave–structure interaction, they are classified by the **orientation of their longitudinal axis** with respect to the still-water free surface:

- **Vertical Cylindrical Members**: Axis perpendicular to the seabed (parallel to the gravity vector). Examples: platform legs in fixed offshore platforms, monopile foundations of offshore wind turbines, and well conductors.
- **Arbitrarily Oriented (Inclined) Cylindrical Members**: Axis inclined at an angle other than $0^\circ$ or $90^\circ$ to the free surface. Examples: jacket braces, diagonal members in space-frame templates, steel catenary risers (SCRs), and mooring chain links.

The hydrodynamic loading on both categories is governed by the **Morison Equation**, which treats the cylinder as a *diffraction-independent* (slender) body. Slenderness is quantified by the ratio $\frac{D}{\lambda}$, where $D$ is the cylinder diameter and $\lambda$ is the wavelength. For $D/\lambda < 0.2$, the cylinder does not significantly disturb the incident wave field, and the Morison formulation is valid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The OENST722 Module 2 syllabus requires explicit treatment of (i) velocity decomposition in two and three dimensions, (ii) the inline and transverse (lift) force components, and (iii) the special-case reduction from arbitrary orientation to vertical orientation.

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine holding a **bamboo stick vertically** in a fast-flowing stream versus **slanting it** at $45^\circ$ across the current.

- When held **vertically**, you feel the water pushing *sideways* across its full length — this is the **inline (transverse) force**. You barely feel any push along its length because the flow is perpendicular.
- When **tilted**, you suddenly feel two distinct things: the same sideways push *plus* a tug *along* the stick's length (an **axial force**), and a curious sideways lift that tries to twist the stick out of your hand — this is the **lift force**.

Mathematically, this is exactly what happens to a marine cylinder: the water particle velocity vector $\vec{u}$ is decomposed into a **component normal to the axis** (causing drag + inertia) and a **component parallel to the axis** (causing axial friction drag). The "twist in your hand" is the lift force generated by vortex shedding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Default Coefficient Ranges for Quick Recall:**
> - Inertia coefficient: $C_M \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 2.0$
> - Drag coefficient: $C_D \approx 0.6 \text{ to } 1.2$
> - Lift coefficient: $C_L \approx 0.6 \text{ to } 1.5$
> - Seawater density: $\rho = \mathbf{1025 \text{ kg/m}^3}$ (standard KTU value, used in all numericals unless otherwise stated).
> - Gravity: $g = \mathbf{9.81 \text{ m/s}^2}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Morison Equation — Conceptual Foundation

For a **vertical cylinder of diameter $D$**, the wave force per unit length is the sum of two physically distinct contributions:

1. **Drag Force ($F_D$)** — A *viscous* force proportional to the square of the water-particle velocity (and its absolute value, ensuring it always acts in the direction of motion).
2. **Inertia Force ($F_I$)** — An *inertial* force proportional to the local water-particle acceleration, related to the displaced fluid mass.

The drag arises from the pressure differential between the upstream and downstream sides of the cylinder (form drag) plus skin friction, while the inertia force arises from the fluid's resistance to being accelerated around the cylinder body (added-mass effect).

### 2.2 Decomposition Logic — Step by Step

**Step 1 — Identify the velocity field.**
For a wave of height $H$ and period $T$, Airy's linear wave theory gives the horizontal water-particle velocity $u_x$ and acceleration $\dot{u}_x$ at any depth $z$.

**Step 2 — Determine cylinder orientation.**
For a **vertical cylinder**, the axis is parallel to the $z$-direction. The horizontal velocity $u_x$ is fully **normal** to the axis. There is no axial velocity component (since $u_z$ is very small in deep water and zero in linear theory at the surface).

**Step 3 — Apply the Morison equation per unit length.**
The force per unit length in the $x$-direction is:

$$f_x = \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_D\,D\,u_x\,|u_x|}_{\text{Drag}} \;+\; \underbrace{\rho\,C_M\,A\,\dot{u}_x}_{\text{Inertia}}$$

where $A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$ is the cross-sectional area of the cylinder.

**Step 4 — Integrate over the submerged length.**
Total force on the cylinder:

$$F_x = \int_{-d}^{\eta(t)} f_x \; dz$$

where $d$ is the water depth and $\eta(t)$ is the instantaneous free-surface elevation.

### 2.3 Extension to Arbitrarily Oriented Cylinders

This is the **core analytical extension** demanded by Module 2.

Let the cylinder axis be inclined at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal plane. The water-particle velocity vector has components $u_x$, $u_y$, $u_z$. The unit vector along the cylinder axis is:

$$\hat{a} = (l, m, n) = (\cos\alpha, \cos\beta, \cos\gamma)$$

where $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ are direction angles w.r.t. $x, y, z$ axes respectively.

**Decompose the velocity into:**
- **Normal component** (perpendicular to axis): $u_n = \vec{u} - (\vec{u}\cdot\hat{a})\hat{a}$
- **Tangential component** (parallel to axis): $u_t = \vec{u}\cdot\hat{a}$

**Decompose the force into:**
- **Inline force** $F_N$ (normal to axis, in the plane of $\vec{u}$ and $\hat{a}$)
- **Axial force** $F_T$ (along the axis)
- **Lift force** $F_L$ (perpendicular to both $\vec{u}$ and $\hat{a}$, out of the inline plane)

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Expression | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|----------|------------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | Drag force per unit length (vertical cylinder) | $f_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D D u_x \vert u_x \vert$ | N/m | Viscous resistance |
| 2 | Inertia force per unit length (vertical cylinder) | $f_I = \rho C_M A \dot{u}_x$ | N/m | Added-mass reaction |
| 3 | Total inline force per unit length (general) | $f_N = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D D u_n \vert u_n \vert + \rho C_M A \dot{u}_n$ | N/m | Drag + inertia normal to axis |
| 4 | Axial (tangential) force per unit length | $f_T = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_T D u_t \vert u_t \vert$ | N/m | Skin-friction drag along axis |
| 5 | Lift force per unit length | $f_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_L D u_n^2$ | N/m | Vortex-shedding transverse push |
| 6 | Direction-cosine relation | $l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1$ | — | Unit vector identity |
| 7 | Inline resultant force magnitude | $F = \sqrt{F_N^2 + F_T^2 + F_L^2}$ | N | Vector sum of three components |
| 8 | Keulegan–Carpenter number | $KC = \frac{U_m T}{D}$ | — | Inertia vs. drag regime indicator |
| 9 | Reynolds number | $Re = \frac{U_m D}{\nu}$ | — | Flow regime indicator |
| 10 | Slenderness ratio (validity check) | $\frac{D}{\lambda} < 0.2$ | — | Morison's equation validity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Arbitrarily-oriented member analysis is essential for designing **jacket platforms** where braces meet legs at inclined angles (typically $30^\circ$–$60^\circ$ to horizontal). It is also crucial for **flexible risers** and **catenary mooring systems**, where neglecting the axial drag component can underestimate total loading by 15–25%.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Morison Equation for a Vertical Cylinder

Starting from physical first principles, consider a stationary vertical cylinder of diameter $D$ submerged in an oscillating flow of velocity $u(t)$ and acceleration $\dot{u}(t)$.

**Step 1:** The drag force arises from the dynamic pressure difference across the cylinder. By dimensional analysis and experimental calibration (e.g., from Sarpkaya & Isaacson, 1981), the drag per unit length is:

$$f_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_D\,D\,u\,\vert u \vert$$

**Step 2:** The inertia force arises because the cylinder must accelerate a virtual mass of water around it. The displaced volume per unit length is $\frac{\pi D^2}{4} = A$. Adding the actual displaced fluid mass to the cylinder's own mass gives:

$$f_I = \rho\,C_M\,A\,\dot{u}$$

**Step 3:** The total inline force per unit length on a vertical cylinder (acting in the direction of wave propagation, i.e., the $x$-direction) is the sum:

$$f_x = f_D + f_I = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_D\,D\,u_x\,\vert u_x \vert + \rho\,C_M\,A\,\dot{u}_x$$

This is the **canonical Morison equation** in its 1D form.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Velocity Decomposition for an Arbitrarily Oriented Cylinder

Let the cylinder axis direction be defined by the unit vector $\hat{a} = (l, m, n)$ and the water-particle velocity be $\vec{u} = (u_x, u_y, u_z)$.

**Step 1:** Compute the scalar (dot) product to get the tangential component:

$$u_t = \vec{u} \cdot \hat{a} = u_x l + u_y m + u_z n$$

**Step 2:** The tangential velocity vector (with direction) is:

$$\vec{u}_t = u_t \, \hat{a}$$

**Step 3:** Subtract to get the normal velocity vector:

$$\vec{u}_n = \vec{u} - \vec{u}_t = \vec{u} - (\vec{u}\cdot\hat{a})\hat{a}$$

**Step 4:** Its magnitude:

$$u_n = \sqrt{u_x^2 + u_y^2 + u_z^2 - u_t^2} = \sqrt{\vert \vec{u} \vert^2 - u_t^2}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Inline, Axial and Lift Force Components

**Step 1 — Inline force per unit length (Morison form, applied to $u_n$):**

$$f_N = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_D\,D\,u_n\,\vert u_n \vert + \rho\,C_M\,A\,\dot{u}_n$$

**Step 2 — Axial force per unit length (drag only, uses axial drag coefficient $C_T$):**

$$f_T = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_T\,D\,u_t\,\vert u_t \vert$$

> *Note:* The axial component typically uses a skin-friction coefficient $C_T$ rather than the form-drag coefficient $C_D$, since the flow runs along a streamlined section.

**Step 3 — Lift force per unit length (perpendicular to both $\vec{u}$ and $\hat{a}$, in the plane containing them):**

$$f_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_L\,D\,u_n^2$$

> *Note:* The lift is taken proportional to $u_n^2$ (not $u_n \vert u_n \vert$) because vortex shedding creates a directional force depending on the side of cylinder exposed to the flow.

**Step 4 — Vector sum of the three orthogonal components** gives the total force per unit length:

$$\vec{f} = f_N \, \hat{n} + f_T \, \hat{a} + f_L \, \hat{L}$$

where $\hat{n} = \vec{u}_n / u_n$ and $\hat{L}$ is the lift direction (perpendicular to $\hat{n}$ and $\hat{a}$).

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Special Case Reduction to a Vertical Cylinder

For a **vertical cylinder**, the axis is along $\hat{z} = (0,0,1)$, so $l=0$, $m=0$, $n=1$.

**Step 1:** Tangential velocity:

$$u_t = u_x(0) + u_y(0) + u_z(1) = u_z$$

**Step 2:** Normal velocity magnitude:

$$u_n = \sqrt{u_x^2 + u_y^2 + u_z^2 - u_z^2} = \sqrt{u_x^2 + u_y^2}$$

**Step 3:** In a 2D wave (only $x$-direction flow), $u_y = 0$, so:

$$u_n = u_x$$

This confirms that for a vertical cylinder in a 2D sea state, the Morison equation reduces to:

$$f_x = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_D\,D\,u_x\,\vert u_x \vert + \rho\,C_M\,A\,\dot{u}_x$$

which is exactly Derivation 1. ✓

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Worked Numerical Illustration

**Problem:** A vertical cylinder of diameter $D = 1.0$ m is subjected to a linear Airy wave in 20 m water depth. At a particular instant at mid-depth ($z = -10$ m), the horizontal velocity is $u_x = 1.5$ m/s and acceleration $\dot{u}_x = 0.4$ m/s². Compute the drag, inertia, and total inline forces per unit length. Take $C_D = 1.0$, $C_M = 2.0$, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³.

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (1.0)^2}{4} = 0.7854 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Drag force per unit length:**

$$f_D = \tfrac{1}{2}(1025)(1.0)(1.0)(1.5)(1.5) = 1153.125 \text{ N/m}$$

**Step 3 — Inertia force per unit length:**

$$f_I = (1025)(2.0)(0.7854)(0.4) = 644.026 \text{ N/m}$$

**Step 4 — Total inline force per unit length:**

$$f_x = 1153.125 + 644.026 = 1797.15 \text{ N/m}$$

**Interpretation:** The drag contribution (1153 N/m) is about 1.79× the inertia contribution, indicating a **drag-dominated regime**. This is consistent with a Keulegan–Carpenter number $KC > 5$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Force Decomposition on a Cylindrical Member

The mermaid diagram below maps the analytical pipeline from the incident wave field to the three orthogonal hydrodynamic force components acting on an arbitrarily oriented cylinder.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Incident Wave Field: u_x, u_y, u_z"] --> B{"Cylinder Axis Orientation"}
    B -->|Vertical| C["Hat a = 0, 0, 1"]
    B -->|Inclined| D["Hat a = l, m, n with l^2+m^2+n^2=1"]
    C --> E["Compute Tangential Velocity u_t = u_z"]
    D --> F["Compute Tangential Velocity u_t = u_x l + u_y m + u_z n"]
    E --> G["Compute Normal Velocity u_n"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["Apply Morison Equation on u_n"]
    H --> I["Inline Force f_N: Drag + Inertia"]
    G --> J["Compute Axial Drag on u_t"]
    J --> K["Axial Force f_T: Skin Friction"]
    G --> L["Apply Lift Equation on u_n"]
    L --> M["Lift Force f_L: Vortex Shedding"]
    I --> N["Vector Sum at Each Node"]
    K --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O["Total Force Vector per Unit Length"]
    O --> P["Integrate Along Submerged Length"]
    P --> Q["Total Resultant Force and Overturning Moment"]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Force Diagram on an Inclined Cylinder

The following mermaid block renders the geometric decomposition of velocities and forces on a cylinder inclined at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal:

```mermaid
graph LR
    V["Water Particle Velocity Vector u"] -->|Decompose| VN["Normal Component u_n"]
    V -->|Decompose| VT["Tangential Component u_t"]
    VN --> FN["Inline Force f_N"]
    VN --> FL["Lift Force f_L"]
    VT --> FT["Axial Drag f_T"]
    FN --> RES["Resultant Hydrodynamic Force Vector"]
    FL --> RES
    FT --> RES
    CYL["Cylinder Axis at angle theta"] -.-> VN
    CYL -.-> VT
```

### 4.3 Free-Body Schematic — Vertical vs. Inclined Cylinder Loading

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VERT["Vertical Cylinder Case"]
        V1["Drag f_D acts horizontally"] --> VR["Total Force F_x"]
        V2["Inertia f_I acts horizontally"] --> VR
    end
    subgraph INCL["Inclined Cylinder Case"]
        I1["f_N acts normal to axis"] --> IR["Resultant Force F"]
        I2["f_T acts along axis"] --> IR
        I3["f_L acts perpendicular to both"] --> IR
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** In the diagrams, single-line arrows represent vector quantities acting along a particular direction, while dotted arrows indicate geometric reference (the cylinder axis). The "Resultant Force" is the vector sum of all three orthogonal hydrodynamic components at each point along the cylinder.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define the Morison equation and state its validity range. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
The Morison equation expresses the horizontal wave force per unit length on a slender vertical cylinder as the sum of drag and inertia components:

$$f_x = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D D u \vert u \vert + \rho C_M A \dot{u}$$

It is valid when $D/\lambda < 0.2$, ensuring the cylinder does not significantly scatter the incident wave field (i.e., diffraction effects are negligible).

**Key Points to Score Full Marks:**
- Explicit formula (1 mark)
- Statement of physical meaning of each term (1 mark)
- Slenderness validity condition (1 mark)

---

#### Question 2: Distinguish between inline force and lift force on an inclined cylinder. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
The **inline force** acts in the plane containing the wave velocity vector and the cylinder axis, perpendicular to the axis. It has both drag (velocity-squared) and inertia (acceleration) contributions. The **lift force** acts perpendicular to both the velocity vector and the cylinder axis, out of the inline plane, and arises from asymmetric vortex shedding. The lift force is proportional to $u_n^2$, while the inline drag is proportional to $u_n \vert u_n \vert$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks): Full Analysis of an Arbitrarily Oriented Cylindrical Member

**Part (a)** — *Derive the expressions for the inline, axial and lift force components per unit length on an arbitrarily oriented cylindrical member subjected to wave loading.* **(7 Marks)**

**Part (b)** — *A steel jacket brace of diameter $D = 0.8$ m is inclined at $\theta = 45^\circ$ to the horizontal. At a given instant, the horizontal water-particle velocity is $u_x = 1.2$ m/s, the vertical component is $u_z = 0.3$ m/s, and the horizontal acceleration is $\dot{u}_x = 0.35$ m/s². Compute the normal and tangential velocity components, and the total inline force per unit length on the brace. Take $C_D = 1.0$, $C_M = 2.0$, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³.* **(7 Marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

Let $\hat{a} = (l, m, n)$ be the unit vector along the cylinder axis and $\vec{u} = (u_x, u_y, u_z)$ the water-particle velocity vector.

**Tangential velocity component (1 Mark):**

$$u_t = \vec{u} \cdot \hat{a} = u_x l + u_y m + u_z n$$

**Normal velocity magnitude (2 Marks):**

$$u_n = \sqrt{\vert\vec{u}\vert^2 - u_t^2} = \sqrt{u_x^2 + u_y^2 + u_z^2 - u_t^2}$$

**Inline force per unit length (2 Marks):**

$$f_N = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D D u_n \vert u_n \vert + \rho C_M A \dot{u}_n$$

**Axial and lift force per unit length (2 Marks):**

$$f_T = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_T D u_t \vert u_t \vert \quad ; \quad f_L = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_L D u_n^2$$

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Direction cosines of the brace axis.**
The brace is inclined at $\theta = 45^\circ$ to horizontal. Assuming it lies in the $xz$-plane and inclined *upward* in the direction of wave propagation, the unit vector along the axis is:

$$\hat{a} = (\cos 45^\circ, 0, \sin 45^\circ) = (0.7071, 0, 0.7071)$$

**Step 2 — Tangential velocity (1 Mark):**

$$u_t = (1.2)(0.7071) + (0)(0) + (0.3)(0.7071) = 0.8485 + 0.2121 = 1.0606 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Normal velocity (2 Marks):**

$$u_n = \sqrt{u_x^2 + u_z^2 - u_t^2} = \sqrt{(1.2)^2 + (0.3)^2 - (1.0606)^2}$$
$$u_n = \sqrt{1.44 + 0.09 - 1.1249} = \sqrt{0.4051} = 0.6365 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Cross-sectional area (1 Mark):**

$$A = \frac{\pi (0.8)^2}{4} = 0.5027 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Total inline force per unit length (2 Marks):**

Assuming $\dot{u}_n \approx \dot{u}_x = 0.35$ m/s² as a first-order approximation:

$$f_N = \tfrac{1}{2}(1025)(1.0)(0.8)(0.6365)(0.6365) + (1025)(2.0)(0.5027)(0.35)$$
$$f_N = 166.20 + 360.69 = 526.89 \text{ N/m}$$

**Final Answer:** $f_N \approx 527$ N/m, with $u_t = 1.06$ m/s and $u_n = 0.637$ m/s.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: Vertical Cylinder Design Problem

**Part (a)** — *A vertical cylinder of diameter $D = 2.0$ m extends through a water depth of $d = 25$ m. A linear Airy wave of height $H = 4$ m and period $T = 8$ s propagates past it. Using small-amplitude wave theory, compute the horizontal velocity and acceleration at mid-depth ($z = -12.5$ m). Take $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².* **(7 Marks)**

**Part (b)** — *Using the velocity and acceleration from part (a), and taking $C_D = 1.1$, $C_M = 1.8$, compute the maximum total horizontal force on the cylinder by integrating the Morison equation over the submerged length.* **(7 Marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Velocity and Acceleration (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Wave number $k$ (1 Mark):**
Using the dispersion relation $\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kd)$ with $\omega = 2\pi/T = 0.7854$ rad/s and $d = 25$ m:

Solve iteratively. Initial guess $k = 0.04$ rad/m:
$$\tanh(0.04 \times 25) = \tanh(1.0) = 0.7616$$
$$g k \tanh(kd) = 9.81 \times 0.04 \times 0.7616 = 0.2989 < \omega^2 = 0.6168$$

Try $k = 0.06$: $\tanh(1.5) = 0.9051$, gives $gk\tanh = 0.5327$. Try $k = 0.065$: $\tanh(1.625) = 0.9251$, gives $0.5899$. Try $k = 0.066$: $\tanh(1.65) = 0.9316$, gives $0.6034$. Try $k = 0.067$: gives $0.6163 \approx \omega^2$. So $k \approx 0.067$ rad/m. (1 Mark)

**Step 2 — Horizontal velocity at mid-depth (3 Marks):**
The Airy velocity at depth $z$:

$$u_x(z,t) = \frac{H g k}{2\omega} \cdot \frac{\cosh(k(z+d))}{\cosh(kd)} \cdot \cos(kx - \omega t)$$

The maximum amplitude at $z = -12.5$ m:

$$\hat{u} = \frac{(4)(9.81)(0.067)}{2(0.7854)} \cdot \frac{\cosh(0.067 \times 12.5)}{\cosh(0.067 \times 25)}$$
$$\hat{u} = 1.673 \cdot \frac{\cosh(0.8375)}{\cosh(1.675)} = 1.673 \cdot \frac{1.362}{2.704} = 0.8427 \text{ m/s}$$

So **maximum horizontal velocity** $u_{x,\max} \approx 0.843$ m/s.

**Step 3 — Horizontal acceleration at mid-depth (3 Marks):**
$$\dot{u}_x = \frac{\partial u_x}{\partial t} = \omega \cdot \hat{u} \cdot \sin(kx - \omega t)$$

Maximum amplitude:

$$\hat{\dot{u}} = \omega \cdot \hat{u} = 0.7854 \times 0.8427 = 0.6618 \text{ m/s}^2$$

So **maximum horizontal acceleration** $\dot{u}_{x,\max} \approx 0.662$ m/s².

---

**Part (b) — Maximum Total Horizontal Force (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Velocity and acceleration profiles (2 Marks):**
At any depth $z$:

$$\hat{u}(z) = \hat{u}_0 \cdot \frac{\cosh(k(z+d))}{\cosh(kd)}, \quad \hat{\dot{u}}(z) = \omega \hat{u}(z)$$

where $\hat{u}_0 = 1.673$ m/s (velocity amplitude at SWL).

**Step 2 — Force per unit length (2 Marks):**

$$f_x(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D D \hat{u}^2(z) + \rho C_M A \omega \hat{u}(z)$$

**Step 3 — Integrate over submerged length (3 Marks):**
Drag component integral:

$$F_D = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D D \hat{u}_0^2 \int_{-d}^{0} \frac{\cosh^2(k(z+d))}{\cosh^2(kd)} dz$$

Using the standard identity $\int_{-d}^{0} \cosh^2(k(z+d)) dz = \frac{d}{2} + \frac{\sinh(2kd)}{4k}$:

$$\int = \frac{25}{2} + \frac{\sinh(3.35)}{4(0.067)} = 12.5 + \frac{14.085}{0.268} = 12.5 + 52.56 = 65.06 \text{ m}$$

So:

$$F_D = 0.5(1025)(1.1)(2.0)(1.673)^2 \cdot \frac{65.06}{(2.704)^2} = 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 1.1 \cdot 2.0 \cdot 2.799 \cdot 8.896$$
$$F_D \approx 28{,}118 \text{ N} \approx 28.1 \text{ kN}$$

Inertia component integral:

$$F_I = \rho C_M A \omega \hat{u}_0 \int_{-d}^{0} \frac{\cosh(k(z+d))}{\cosh(kd)} dz = \rho C_M A \omega \hat{u}_0 \cdot \frac{\sinh(kd)}{k\cosh(kd)}$$

$$F_I = 1025 \cdot 1.8 \cdot 3.142 \cdot 0.7854 \cdot 1.673 \cdot \frac{\sinh(1.675)}{0.067 \cdot \cosh(1.675)}$$
$$F_I = 7609.5 \cdot \frac{2.668}{0.181} = 7609.5 \cdot 14.74 = 112{,}164 \text{ N} \approx 112.2 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Total maximum horizontal force:**

$$F_{\max} = F_D + F_I \approx 28.1 + 112.2 = 140.3 \text{ kN}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting absolute value** in $u \vert u \vert$ — this is a 1-mark penalty if missed, as drag must always oppose motion.
> 2. **Confusing $A = \pi D^2/4$ (cross-sectional area)** with $\pi D$ (circumference). The inertia term uses area; only the drag term uses diameter.
> 3. **Forgetting to square the unit-vector components** when computing $u_t$ — remember $u_t$ is a scalar projection, not magnitude squared.
> 4. **Using fresh-water density ($\rho = 1000$) instead of $1025$ kg/m³** in marine problems — KTU penalizes this unless explicitly stated otherwise.
> 5. **Sign errors in the Airy wave formulation** — the maximum velocity and acceleration are $\pi/2$ out of phase. Drag peaks when velocity peaks; inertia peaks when acceleration peaks (at zero-crossings of velocity).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Morison Equation** is the **foundational equation** for slender-member wave loading — applicable when $D/\lambda < 0.2$.
- A **vertical cylinder** experiences only **inline drag + inertia** in the direction of wave propagation.
- An **arbitrarily oriented cylinder** experiences **three orthogonal components**: inline ($f_N$), axial ($f_T$), and lift ($f_L$).
- The **decomposition formula** $u_n = \sqrt{\vert\vec{u}\vert^2 - u_t^2}$ is the most important identity for this module — memorize it.
- The **inline force uses Morison's form** with the **normal velocity and acceleration**; the axial force uses a separate axial drag coefficient $C_T \approx 0.01$–$0.05$ for smooth pipes.
- The **lift force is proportional to $u_n^2$** (signed), while the **drag uses $u_n \vert u_n \vert$** (always opposing).
- The **Keulegan–Carpenter number** $KC = U_m T / D$ governs whether loading is drag-dominated ($KC > 5$) or inertia-dominated ($KC < 3$).
- For **design purposes**, the maximum total inline force on a vertical cylinder is often obtained by integrating drag and inertia contributions *separately* over the water depth, then adding them — this gives a slightly conservative answer but is accepted in KTU solutions.
- **Reynolds number** $Re = U_m D / \nu$ determines the drag coefficient value from standard marine hydrodynamics charts.
- **Always state units** in the final answer (N or kN for force, N/m for force per unit length). KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.
- The **slenderness ratio check** $D/\lambda < 0.2$ is a **mandatory first step** in any KTU problem involving Morison's equation — failure to verify it costs at least 1 mark.
- **Direction cosines** must satisfy $l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1$ — verify this after computing any axis orientation.
- For a **vertical cylinder**, the reduction $u_n = u_x$ (in 2D flow) and $u_t = u_z$ should be demonstrable without referencing the general formula.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Strategy:** In a 14-mark question on this topic, expect roughly 7 marks for the theoretical derivation and 7 marks for numerical computation. Spend the first 3–4 minutes writing the canonical Morison equation and the velocity decomposition, *then* jump to numericals. This guarantees partial credit even if your arithmetic slips.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ocean Currents and Current Forces Exerted on Offshore Structures
Wind Forces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST722 - FUNDAMENTALS OF OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wind Forces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Wind Forces** on offshore structures refer to the aerodynamic loads generated by the relative motion of air over stationary or semi-stationary marine structures such as fixed jacket platforms, semi-submersibles, FPSOs, SPARs, TLPs, and topside modules. In offshore engineering practice (per **API RP 2A-WSD**, **API RP 2MET**, and **ISO 19902**), wind is treated as a **quasi-static stochastic load** acting horizontally on the exposed superstructure, helideck, flare booms, and any other above-water projections.

The wind force is fundamentally a **drag-type force** arising from the pressure differential created when wind flows around bluff bodies. Mathematically, the steady-state wind force on an element is expressed as:

$$F = \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A V^2$$

where $\rho_a$ is the **mass density of air = 1.225 kg/m³** (at standard sea-level conditions: 15°C, 101.325 kPa), $C_d$ is the **drag coefficient** (dimensionless, shape-dependent), $A$ is the **projected area normal to the wind direction (m²)**, and $V$ is the **design wind speed (m/s)** at the centroid of the element.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Wind forces in Module 2 are studied alongside wave and current forces as the three primary environmental loads on offshore structures. While waves dominate at the substructure, wind often governs the **design of the topside modules, flare structures, and helidecks** because the entire superstructure offers a large projected area to the wind field.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

**Real-World Analogy: The Sail vs. The Skyscraper**

Imagine standing on a beach holding a large beach umbrella. On a calm day, the umbrella is easy to hold. But when a strong gust of wind hits it, you feel a strong push — this is the same physical phenomenon as wind force on an offshore platform's helideck. Now imagine a tall, slender offshore platform (like the Bombay High or Bullwinkle platform). The wind doesn't "see" the underwater jacket (submerged structure) — it only "sees" the topside, which is like the "head" of the structure sticking above the ocean.

A few important intuitive points:

- **Wind respects the "Square"**: Because the force scales with $V^2$, doubling the wind speed **quadruples** the force. This is why a 50 m/s storm exerts 100 times the force of a 5 m/s breeze.
- **Shape matters enormously**: A flat helideck experiences a far larger drag than a streamlined cylindrical leg. Engineers quantify this through the drag coefficient $C_d$.
- **Wind is height-dependent**: Wind near the ocean surface is slower (because of friction with the sea) and increases with height. The top of a tall flare boom feels a much stronger wind than the boat landing just above the waterline.
- **Wind is "noisy"**: Unlike a uniform flow, the real wind consists of random gusts and lulls, requiring the use of **wind spectra** to capture its true statistical nature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why study wind in offshore engineering?** Although wave loads typically dominate the global design of fixed offshore platforms, wind often governs the **local design of the deck, appurtenances, and boat-landing structures**. For slender, deep-draft floating structures (SPARs, semi-submersibles), wind can contribute 10–30% of the total environmental overturning moment during severe storms.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Air density (sea level, 15°C) | $\rho_a$ | **1.225** | kg/m³ |
| Kinematic viscosity of air | $\nu_a$ | **1.46 × 10⁻⁵** | m²/s |
| Reference height (API/ISO) | $z_{ref}$ | **10** | m |
| Wind profile exponent (open sea) | $\alpha$ | **0.12 to 0.15** | — |
| Surface roughness length (sea) | $z_0$ | **0.0002 to 0.005** | m |
| von Kármán constant | $k$ | **0.41** | — |
| Return period (operational) | $T_R$ | **1 to 100** | years |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Components of Wind Force Analysis

The total wind force on an offshore structure is computed through a structured hierarchy of physical considerations:

**Step 1 — Establish the Design Wind Speed**
The engineer must first select an appropriate **return period** (e.g., 100-year for ultimate limit state, 1-year for operational) and an **averaging period** (3-second gust, 1-minute mean, 1-hour mean). Standards such as **API RP 2MET** and **ISO 19901-1** provide regional wind-speed maps.

**Step 2 — Apply a Wind Velocity Profile**
Wind speed is not uniform with height. The most commonly used profiles in offshore engineering are:
- **Power Law Profile** (preferred by API/ISO for offshore):

$$V(z) = V_{ref} \left( \frac{z}{z_{ref}} \right)^{\alpha}$$

- **Logarithmic Law Profile** (more physical, used for metocean studies):

$$V(z) = \frac{V_*}{\kappa} \ln\left( \frac{z}{z_0} \right)$$

where $V_*$ is the friction velocity and $z_0$ is the aerodynamic roughness length.

**Step 3 — Compute the Wind Force on Each Element**
For each exposed structural element, compute $F = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A V^2$ evaluated at the centroid of the element.

**Step 4 — Apply Shape (Shadow) Factors**
When multiple elements are stacked, shielding effects reduce the wind force on downstream elements. The **shadow factor** $K_s$ (or **shielding factor**, $0 \le K_s \le 1$) is applied:

$$F_{actual} = K_s \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A V^2$$

**Step 5 — Combine to Get the Total Base Shear and Overturning Moment**
Sum the forces on all elements and compute the moment about the platform base (or relevant reference point on a floating structure).

### 2.2 Drag Coefficients for Typical Offshore Elements

The drag coefficient $C_d$ is a strong function of the **Reynolds number** $Re = \frac{V D}{\nu_a}$ and the **shape** of the body.

| Element Type | $C_d$ Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Smooth circular cylinder (infinite length) | **0.5 to 1.2** | Critical at $Re \approx 3.5 \times 10^5$ (drag crisis) |
| I-beam / structural section | **1.3 to 1.8** | Depends on orientation |
| Flat plate (perpendicular) | **1.9 to 2.0** | Maximum bluff-body drag |
| Helideck (perforated grating) | **0.7 to 1.0** | Reduced due to porosity |
| Rectangular deck house | **1.2 to 1.5** | |
| Crane boom (lattice) | **1.8 to 2.2** (on solid area) | Uses **solidity ratio** $\phi$ |
| Sphere | **0.2 to 0.5** | Reynolds-number dependent |
| Square cross-section | **2.0 to 2.2** | |
| Helideck safety net | **1.0 to 1.2** | |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lattice / Truss Drag Coefficient**: For lattice structures (crane booms, flare booms, boat landings), the drag coefficient is computed on the **sum of projected areas of individual members**, with a correction factor based on the **solidity ratio** $\phi = A_{members}/A_{gross}$:

$$C_{d,\text{lattice}} = C_{d,\text{single}} (1 - \phi)^n$$

where $n$ typically lies between 2 and 4 depending on the standard used.

### 2.3 Wind Spectra and Dynamic Effects

For dynamic analysis, the wind is treated as a **stochastic process** characterized by a **power spectral density (PSD)** function. Common spectra used in offshore engineering:

| Spectrum | Origin | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Davenport** | Canada, 1961 | Original wind spectrum, often overestimates low-frequency energy |
| **Harris** | UK, 1970 | Modified Davenport, used in some European codes |
| **Kaimal** | USA, 1972 | Better physical basis, frequency-dependent |
| **API Spectrum** | API RP 2MET | **Standard for offshore platforms** |
| **NPD Spectrum** | Norwegian Petroleum Directorate | Used in North Sea operations |
| **Kaimal-Johansson** | Modified Kaimal | Used in ISO 19901-1 |

**API Spectrum** (the most-tested in KTU-style questions):

$$\frac{f \cdot S_u(f)}{u_*^2} = \frac{200 f_z}{(1 + 50 f_z)^{5/3}}$$

where $f$ is frequency in Hz, $S_u(f)$ is the longitudinal wind PSD, and $f_z = f \cdot z / V(z)$ is the **monin coordinate**.

### 2.4 Gust Factor and Exposure Factor

In quasi-static design, the **gust factor** $G$ accounts for the dynamic amplification of mean wind due to turbulent fluctuations:

$$F_{design} = G \cdot F_{mean}$$

Typical values of gust factor for offshore structures:
- For 3-second gust: $G \approx 1.0$ (gust is already a peak)
- For 1-minute mean: $G \approx 1.20$ to 1.35
- For 1-hour mean: $G \approx 1.30$ to 1.50

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = \frac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A V^2$ | Steady wind force on an element | N |
| 2 | $V(z) = V_{ref}(z/z_{ref})^{\alpha}$ | Power-law wind velocity profile | m/s |
| 3 | $V(z) = \frac{V_*}{\kappa}\ln(z/z_0)$ | Logarithmic wind profile | m/s |
| 4 | $Re = VD/\nu_a$ | Reynolds number | — |
| 5 | $F = G \cdot F_{mean}$ | Gust-augmented force | N |
| 6 | $f_z = f \cdot z / V(z)$ | Monin (reduced) frequency | — |
| 7 | $f S_u(f)/u_*^2 = 200 f_z (1+50 f_z)^{-5/3}$ | API wind spectrum | m²/s |
| 8 | $C_{d,\text{lattice}} = C_{d,\text{single}}(1-\phi)^n$ | Lattice drag coefficient | — |
| 9 | $M_{OT} = \sum F_i \cdot h_i$ | Overturning moment at base | N·m |
| 10 | $q = \frac{1}{2}\rho_a V^2$ | Dynamic wind pressure | Pa |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: The wind force calculation feeds into two critical design checks: (a) **In-place global strength** of the jacket/spar/submersible, and (b) **Fatigue damage estimation** at tubular joints where wind-driven dynamic stresses combine with wave-induced stresses. For FPSOs, wind contributes to the **weathervaning torque** on the turret mooring system.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Steady-State Wind Force on a Bluff Body

Starting from the **Bernoulli–Euler principle**, when a fluid stream of velocity $V$ impinges on a stationary body, the kinetic energy per unit volume is $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho V^2$. If the body brings the fluid to rest (stagnation), this energy is converted into a pressure rise — the **stagnation pressure**:

$$p_s = p_\infty + \frac{1}{2}\rho_a V^2$$

On the **leeward side** of a bluff body, the flow separates and creates a low-pressure wake region. The **net pressure differential** between the windward and leeward sides produces the **drag force**.

For a body of projected area $A$, the force is:

$$\begin{aligned}
F &= (p_{windward} - p_{leeward}) \cdot A \\
&= C_d \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho_a V^2 \cdot A
\end{aligned}$$

The factor $C_d$ (drag coefficient) is **defined** such that it absorbs all the complex fluid-mechanics details of separation, reattachment, and wake formation. Thus:

$$\boxed{F = \frac{1}{2} \rho_a C_d A V^2}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Power-Law Wind Profile

The power-law profile is an **empirical** relationship fitted to atmospheric boundary-layer measurements. We derive it from dimensional reasoning:

Assume the wind speed at height $z$ depends on:
- The reference wind speed $V_{ref}$ at $z_{ref}$
- The ratio $z/z_{ref}$
- The terrain-dependent exponent $\alpha$

The most general power-law form is:

$$V(z) = V_{ref} \left(\frac{z}{z_{ref}}\right)^{\alpha}$$

**Verification of dimensions**: The argument $z/z_{ref}$ is dimensionless, so the entire right-hand side has units of m/s. ✓

**Typical values of $\alpha$ for offshore environments**:

| Terrain | $\alpha$ |
|---|---|
| Open sea (no obstacles) | 0.10 to 0.13 |
| Coastal with scattered buildings | 0.15 to 0.20 |
| Urban / forested | 0.25 to 0.40 |

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Logarithmic Wind Profile

The log-law arises from the **Monin–Obukhov similarity theory** for atmospheric turbulence. In the **neutral atmosphere** (no buoyancy effects), the mean wind shear is related to the friction velocity $V_*$ and the roughness length $z_0$ by:

$$\frac{dV}{dz} = \frac{V_*}{\kappa z}$$

Integrating from the roughness length $z_0$ (where $V = 0$ by definition) to a generic height $z$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{V(z)} dV &= \frac{V_*}{\kappa} \int_{z_0}^{z} \frac{dz'}{z'} \\
V(z) - 0 &= \frac{V_*}{\kappa} \left[\ln z - \ln z_0 \right] \\
V(z) &= \frac{V_*}{\kappa} \ln\left(\frac{z}{z_0}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Note**: The friction velocity $V_*$ is defined as $V_* = \sqrt{\tau_0/\rho_a}$, where $\tau_0$ is the surface shear stress at the air–sea interface.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: API Wind Spectrum and the Monin Coordinate

The **API RP 2MET** spectrum is derived from the assumption that the longitudinal turbulence spectrum follows a von Kármán-type form in the inertial subrange, with a low-frequency saturation:

$$S_u(f) = \frac{u_*^2}{f} \cdot \frac{200 f_z}{(1 + 50 f_z)^{5/3}}$$

Multiplying both sides by $f$:

$$f S_u(f) = u_*^2 \cdot \frac{200 f_z}{(1 + 50 f_z)^{5/3}}$$

**Derivation of the Monin coordinate**:
Dimensional analysis shows that the spectrum of turbulence should depend on:
- Length scale: $z$ (height above the surface)
- Velocity scale: $V(z)$ (mean wind at that height)
- Frequency: $f$

The only dimensionless combination is:

$$f_z = \frac{f \cdot z}{V(z)}$$

This is the **Monin (or reduced) frequency**. For $f_z \ll 1$ (low frequencies, large eddies), the spectrum approaches a constant (energy-containing subrange). For $f_z \gg 1$ (high frequencies, small eddies), it follows the **Kolmogorov $-5/3$ power law** characteristic of the inertial subrange.

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Lattice Drag Coefficient from Solidity

Consider a lattice (truss) structure with $N$ parallel members of projected width $d$, spaced at equal intervals $s$ along the wind direction. The **solidity ratio** is:

$$\phi = \frac{\text{projected area of members}}{\text{envelope area}} = \frac{N d}{L_{total}}$$

Empirically, the drag of a lattice is less than the sum of individual member drags because the downstream members sit in the wake of upstream members. The **Hoerner-type correlation** gives:

$$C_{d,\text{lattice}} = C_{d,\text{single}} \cdot (1 - \phi)^n$$

where $n \approx 2$ for square-section members in close spacing, and $n \approx 3$ to 4 for widely spaced members.

**Numerical example**: For $\phi = 0.3$ and $n = 2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{d,\text{lattice}} &= 1.2 \cdot (1 - 0.3)^2 \\
&= 1.2 \cdot 0.49 \\
&= 0.588
\end{aligned}$$

This shows how the lattice arrangement reduces the effective drag by about 50% compared to a solid plate.

### 3.6 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem**: A helideck on a fixed offshore platform is a square steel grating of plan dimensions 18 m × 18 m. It is located at an elevation of 25 m above MSL. The 100-year return period 1-minute mean wind speed at 10 m reference height is $V_{10} = 35$ m/s. Compute the wind force on the helideck.

**Step 1 — Compute wind speed at helideck elevation using the power law with $\alpha = 0.12$**:

$$V_{25} = 35 \times \left(\frac{25}{10}\right)^{0.12}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\ln(V_{25}) &= \ln(35) + 0.12 \ln(2.5) \\
&= 3.5553 + 0.12 \times 0.9163 \\
&= 3.5553 + 0.1100 \\
&= 3.6653 \\
V_{25} &= e^{3.6653} = 39.1 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Compute projected area**:
The helideck is horizontal; wind blows horizontally. The projected area is the plan area = 18 × 18 = 324 m².

**Step 3 — Use $\rho_a = 1.225$ kg/m³ and $C_d = 0.8$ (perforated grating)**:

$$F = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 0.8 \times 324 \times (39.1)^2$$

$$\begin{aligned}
(39.1)^2 &= 1528.81 \\
F &= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.8 \times 324 \times 1528.81 \\
&= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.8 \times 495,334.4 \\
&= 242,793.9 \text{ N} \\
&\approx 242.8 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Apply gust factor for 1-minute averaging, $G = 1.25$**:

$$F_{design} = 1.25 \times 242.8 = 303.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Overturning moment at platform base (assume base at z = 0)**:

$$M_{OT} = F_{design} \times 25 = 303.5 \times 25 = 7,587.5 \text{ kN·m} \approx 7.59 \text{ MN·m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This moment is then vectorially combined with the wave- and current-induced base moment in the in-place strength check of the jacket legs and piles.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Wind Force Computation Flow for Offshore Structures

The following block diagram illustrates the complete workflow for computing wind force on an offshore platform per **API RP 2A / ISO 19902** methodology.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Metocean Study] --> B[Define Return Period 1y, 10y, 100y]
    B --> C[Select Averaging Period: 3-sec gust, 1-min, 1-hr]
    C --> D[Establish Reference Wind Speed Vref at zref = 10 m]
    D --> E[Apply Wind Velocity Profile Power Law or Log Law]
    E --> F[Compute Vz at Each Element Centroid]
    F --> G[Identify All Exposed Elements: Deck, Crane, Helideck, Boat Landing, Flare]
    G --> H[Assign Drag Coefficient Cd per Element Type]
    H --> I[Apply Shadow Factor Ks for Shielding]
    I --> J[Compute F = 0.5 rho Cd A Vz squared per Element]
    J --> K[Apply Gust Factor G for Quasi-Static Design]
    K --> L[Sum Forces Vectorially at Each Deck Level]
    L --> M[Compute Base Shear and Overturning Moment]
    M --> N[Combine with Wave and Current for In-Place Design]
    N --> O[End: Force Output for Structural Design]
```

### 4.2 Wind Speed Variation with Height — Comparative Schematic

The following diagram contrasts the **power-law** and **logarithmic-law** wind profiles over the open ocean.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AtmosphereLayer[Atmospheric Boundary Layer]
        Zref[Reference Height 10 m, Vref]
        Zlow[Lower Level 5 m, V lower than Vref]
        Zmid[Mid Level 25 m, V higher than Vref]
        Zhigh[Upper Level 50 m, V much higher than Vref]
    end
    Profile1[Power Law Vz = Vref z over zref raised to alpha] -.-> Zref
    Profile2[Logarithmic Law Vz = Vstar over k times ln z over z0] -.-> Zref
    Zref --- Zlow
    Zref --- Zmid
    Zref --- Zhigh
```

### 4.3 Force-Generation Mechanism on a Bluff Body (Conceptual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    WindFlow[Oncoming Wind Stream V infinity] --> Stagnation[Stagnation Point Windward Side Pressure max]
    Stagnation --> PressureFront[High Pressure Windward Face]
    WindFlow --> Separation[Flow Separation at Edges]
    Separation --> Wake[Low Pressure Wake Leeward Side]
    PressureFront --> Drag[Net Drag Force = Pressure Difference times Area]
    Wake --> Drag
    Drag --> CdAbsorb[Drag Coefficient Cd Absorbs All Complex Flow Effects]
    CdAbsorb --> Output[Resultant Force F = 0.5 rho Cd A V squared]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Wind Force on a Typical Fixed Offshore Platform

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph AtmosphericBoundary[Atmospheric Boundary Layer 0 to 100 m]
        Vref[Reference Wind at 10 m] --> Vdeck[Wind at Deck Level 20 m]
        Vdeck --> Vcrane[Wind at Crane Tip 45 m]
        Vdeck --> Vflare[Wind at Flare Tip 60 m]
    end
    Vdeck --> Fdeck[Force on Deck Modules]
    Vcrane --> Fcrane[Force on Crane Boom]
    Vflare --> Fflare[Force on Flare Boom]
    Fdeck --> SumFx[Sum Fx at Base]
    Fcrane --> SumFx
    Fflare --> SumFx
    Fdeck --> SumMx[Sum Mx at Base]
    Fcrane --> SumMx
    Fflare --> SumMx
    Fdeck --> SumMy[Sum My at Base]
    Fcrane --> SumMy
    Fflare --> SumMy
    SumFx --> Base[Total Base Shear and Moment for Design]
    SumMx --> Base
    SumMy --> Base
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define drag coefficient. State typical values for (i) a smooth circular cylinder and (ii) a flat plate oriented normal to the flow.**

**Model Answer**:
The **drag coefficient** $C_d$ is a dimensionless shape factor that quantifies the aerodynamic resistance of a body. It is defined through the equation $F = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A V^2$, where the factor $C_d$ absorbs all complex flow phenomena such as boundary-layer separation, wake formation, and Reynolds-number effects.

- (i) Smooth circular cylinder (infinite length): $C_d \approx 0.5$ to $1.2$ (Reynolds-number dependent; subcritical ~1.2, supercritical ~0.5).
- (ii) Flat plate (perpendicular to flow): $C_d \approx 1.9$ to $2.0$.

---

**Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the power-law wind profile? State the range of values of the exponent $\alpha$ for open-sea conditions.**

**Model Answer**:
The power-law wind profile is an empirical relationship describing how the mean wind speed varies with height in the atmospheric boundary layer:

$$V(z) = V_{ref} \left( \frac{z}{z_{ref}} \right)^{\alpha}$$

For **open-sea (offshore)** conditions, the exponent $\alpha$ typically lies in the range **0.10 to 0.13**, reflecting the low surface roughness of the ocean. The reference height is generally taken as $z_{ref} = 10$ m, and $V_{ref}$ is the corresponding mean wind speed.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**: For wind-force problems, students frequently lose marks by (a) forgetting the **gust factor** $G$ when the problem specifies an averaging period, (b) using the **wrong projected area** (e.g., using side area instead of frontal area for a helideck), and (c) **forgetting to convert units** (e.g., using km/h instead of m/s, leading to a 1,000,000× error in force). Always write the formula first, then substitute, then check units.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the steady-state wind force equation on a bluff body, clearly stating all terms. (7 marks)

**(b)** A fixed offshore platform has a topside module consisting of a rectangular deck house of plan dimensions 25 m × 12 m and height 8 m, located with its base at elevation 22 m above MSL. The 100-year return period 1-hour mean wind speed at 10 m height is 28 m/s. Using the power-law wind profile with $\alpha = 0.13$, compute (i) the wind speed at the centroid of the deck house, and (ii) the total horizontal wind force on the deck house, assuming $C_d = 1.4$. Apply a gust factor of 1.35 for 1-hour averaging. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (a) — Derivation**:

The wind force arises from the **pressure differential** across a body immersed in a flow. Starting from Bernoulli's equation applied between the freestream and the windward stagnation point:

$$p_\infty + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V^2 = p_{windward} + 0$$

The maximum windward pressure is:

$$p_{windward} = p_\infty + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V^2$$

On the **leeward side**, flow separation creates a low-pressure wake:

$$p_{leeward} = p_\infty - C_p \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V^2$$

The net pressure difference is:

$$\Delta p = p_{windward} - p_{leeward} = (1 + C_p) \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a V^2$$

Defining the **drag coefficient** $C_d = (1 + C_p)$ to absorb all empirical fluid-mechanics details:

$$F = \Delta p \cdot A = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A V^2$$

where $\rho_a$ = air density (1.225 kg/m³), $C_d$ = drag coefficient (dimensionless), $A$ = projected area (m²), $V$ = wind speed (m/s), $F$ = force (N).

**Model Solution (b) — Numerical**:

**(i) Wind speed at centroid of deck house**:
The deck house spans from 22 m to 30 m elevation, so its centroid is at $z_c = 22 + 4 = 26$ m.

$$V_{26} = 28 \times \left( \frac{26}{10} \right)^{0.13}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\ln(V_{26}) &= \ln(28) + 0.13 \ln(2.6) \\
&= 3.3322 + 0.13 \times 0.9555 \\
&= 3.3322 + 0.1242 \\
&= 3.4564 \\
V_{26} &= e^{3.4564} = 31.69 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Wind force calculation**:
For wind blowing normal to the **broad face** (25 m × 8 m), the projected area is $A = 25 \times 8 = 200$ m².

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{mean} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 1.4 \times 200 \times (31.69)^2 \\
&= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 1.4 \times 200 \times 1004.26 \\
&= 172,229 \text{ N} \approx 172.2 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

Applying gust factor $G = 1.35$:

$$F_{design} = 1.35 \times 172.2 = 232.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Answers**: $V_{26} = 31.69$ m/s, $F_{design} = 232.5$ kN.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the API wind spectrum and derive the expression for the Monin (reduced) frequency. Discuss its significance in offshore wind analysis. (7 marks)

**(b)** A lattice flare boom has 6 parallel steel angle members, each 0.4 m wide and 25 m long, spaced at 1.2 m centre-to-centre. If the wind blows normal to the boom at $V = 40$ m/s and the elevation of the boom is 50 m above MSL (with $V_{10} = 32$ m/s, $\alpha = 0.12$, $C_{d,\text{single}} = 1.4$), compute the total wind force on the boom. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (a) — API Wind Spectrum**:

The **API wind spectrum** characterizes the turbulent longitudinal wind-velocity fluctuations as a function of frequency. It is essential for **fatigue analysis** and **dynamic response** of offshore structures. The spectral form is:

$$S_u(f) = \frac{u_*^2}{f} \cdot \frac{200 f_z}{(1 + 50 f_z)^{5/3}}$$

**Derivation of the Monin coordinate**:
Dimensional analysis requires that the spectrum $S_u(f)$ have units of m²/s² per Hz. The turbulent kinetic energy is supplied by friction at the surface, characterized by the friction velocity $V_* = \sqrt{\tau_0/\rho_a}$. The only length scale available is the height $z$ above the surface. The dimensionless combination that captures the ratio of eddy size to height is:

$$f_z = \frac{f \cdot z}{V(z)}$$

This is the **Monin reduced frequency** (or "reduced frequency"). For $f_z \ll 1$, eddies are larger than the height and contribute most of the energy. For $f_z \gg 1$, the spectrum follows the Kolmogorov $-5/3$ inertial-subrange law.

**Significance**:
1. It is the fundamental parameter for fatigue-damage calculations from wind.
2. It couples the wind speed at the structure's elevation to the spectral energy distribution.
3. It enables computation of the along-wind variance:

$$\sigma_u^2 = 6.67 \, u_*^2$$

which is independent of height in the surface layer.

**Model Solution (b) — Numerical**:

**Step 1 — Wind speed at elevation 50 m**:

$$V_{50} = 32 \times \left( \frac{50}{10} \right)^{0.12} = 32 \times 5^{0.12}$$

$$5^{0.12} = e^{0.12 \ln 5} = e^{0.12 \times 1.6094} = e^{0.1931} = 1.213$$

$$V_{50} = 32 \times 1.213 = 38.8 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Solidity ratio of the lattice**:
The members are parallel and in a single row perpendicular to the wind. Total projected width of members = $6 \times 0.4 = 2.4$ m. The gross envelope width = $5 \times 1.2 = 6$ m (5 spaces between 6 members).

$$\phi = \frac{2.4}{6} = 0.4$$

**Step 3 — Lattice drag coefficient** (using $n = 2$ for closely spaced members):

$$C_{d,\text{lattice}} = 1.4 \times (1 - 0.4)^2 = 1.4 \times 0.36 = 0.504$$

**Step 4 — Projected area of the boom**:
$A = \text{(projected width of all members)} \times \text{length} = 2.4 \times 25 = 60$ m².

**Step 5 — Total wind force**:

$$\begin{aligned}
F &= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 0.504 \times 60 \times (38.8)^2 \\
&= 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.504 \times 60 \times 1505.44 \\
&= 27,866 \text{ N} \approx 27.87 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Answer**: Total wind force on the flare boom ≈ **27.9 kN**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Wind Forces (Module 2)**

- ☐ **Core equation** to memorize: $F = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho_a C_d A V^2$ — air density is **1.225 kg/m³**.
- ☐ Wind speed is referenced to **10 m height**; adjust using **power law** with $\alpha = 0.10$–$0.13$ for offshore.
- ☐ Drag coefficients for **bluff bodies**: cylinder ~1.0, flat plate ~2.0, helideck (perforated) ~0.8, lattice (corrected for solidity) ~0.5.
- ☐ **Quadratic dependence** on $V$: doubling wind speed → 4× force. This is the most-tested KTU concept.
- ☐ **Averaging period matters**: 3-sec gust > 1-min > 1-hr. Use the **gust factor** $G = 1.2$–$1.5$ to convert.
- ☐ **Lattice drag correction**: $C_{d,\text{lattice}} = C_{d,\text{single}} (1 - \phi)^n$; use $n = 2$ for tight spacing.
- ☐ **API wind spectrum** uses the **Monin coordinate** $f_z = f z / V(z)$.
- ☐ Always **vectorially add** wind, wave, and current forces during in-place design checks.
- ☐ **Unit check before calculation**: convert km/h to m/s by dividing by 3.6; convert kN/m² of pressure by multiplying by area.
- ☐ **Shadowing factor** $K_s$ reduces downstream forces in closely spaced arrays; use $K_s = 0.5$–$0.8$.
- ☐ Wind force typically **governs deck design**, not jacket design — waves govern the latter.
- ☐ For floating structures, wind contributes to **weathervaning torque** and **offset drift**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ice Forces and Tides
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST722 - FUNDAMENTALS OF OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ice Forces and Tides

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Ice Forces – Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ice Force (KTU 2024 Definition):** Ice forces are the lateral, vertical, and gravitational loads imposed on an offshore structure by floating, drifting, or grounded ice features (such as level ice, pressure ridges, stamukhi, ice islands, and icebergs) during ice–structure interaction. These loads are governed by the ice failure mode (crushing, bending, buckling, splitting, or a combination), the relative velocity of interaction, the aspect ratio (Diameter/Thickness), and the indenter geometry.

In offshore engineering practice, ice forces are an **environmental load category** analogous to wind, wave, and current loads, but with a unique non-linear, rate-dependent (visco-elastic–plastic) mechanical response. They are critical design loads for fixed and floating structures operating in **cold-region seas** such as the Beaufort Sea, Cook Inlet, Bohai Bay, Caspian Sea, North Sea (marginal), and the Russian Arctic shelf.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key International Standards for Ice Force Calculation:**
> - **API RP 2N (American Petroleum Institute)** – Recommended Practice for Planning, Designing, and Constructing Fixed Offshore Structures in Ice Environments.
> - **ISO 19906 (International Organization for Standardization)** – Petroleum and Natural Gas Industries – Arctic Offshore Structures.
> - **CSA S471** (Canadian Standards Association) – General Requirements, Design Criteria, Loads, and Ice Environment.
> - **SNiP 2.06.04** (Russian Building Norms) – Loads and Effects on Hydrotechnical Structures from Ice.

### 1.2 Tides – Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tide (KTU 2024 Definition):** Tides are the periodic, short-term vertical and horizontal motion of the sea surface (and the associated water particle motion) caused primarily by the gravitational attraction of the Moon and Sun on the Earth's oceans, modulated by the Earth's rotation (Coriolis) and the geometry of coastlines and seabed (resonance in ocean basins). The vertical motion is called the **tidal elevation** and the horizontal component is called the **tidal current**.

Tides are the **longest-period deterministic wave motion** in the ocean (period 12 h 25 min for semidiurnal) and represent a major consideration for:
- Airgap and deck elevation of fixed platforms.
- Mooring line pre-tension design.
- Underwater pipeline burial depth.
- Operability windows for offshore operations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tidal Datums (Vertical Reference Levels used in Offshore Design):**
> - **MSL** – Mean Sea Level
> - **LAT** – Lowest Astronomical Tide (most conservative seabed/lowest waterline)
> - **HAT** – Highest Astronomical Tide (most conservative airgap)
> - **MLW / MHW** – Mean Low Water / Mean High Water
> - **MLLW / MHHW** – Mean Lower Low Water / Mean Higher High Water

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

**Ice Forces Analogy – "The Slow-Moving Wall":**
Imagine pushing a tall, thick, frozen wall of ice against a vertical steel cylinder by hand. The wall is not rigid—depending on how fast and how wide the contact is, it will either **crumble in front of the cylinder (crushing)**, **snap like a popsicle stick (bending)**, or **buckle like a soda can (buckling)**. The force your hand feels is therefore not just "mass × area", but depends on **how the ice chooses to fail**. The structure must be designed for whichever failure mode the geometry favours.

**Tides Analogy – "The Two Babies on a Trampoline":**
Picture a child (the Earth) standing on a stretched trampoline. The Moon (a heavy baby) sits nearby and pulls the trampoline fabric (the ocean) into a bulge on the side facing it, and—via the centre-of-mass balance—an equal bulge on the far side. The Sun (a lighter but much farther baby) does the same. As the Earth rotates underneath these two bulges, every coastal point experiences **two high tides and two low tides per lunar day**. When Moon, Earth, and Sun align (new/full moon), the pulls add → **Spring Tide** (largest range). When they are perpendicular (quarter moons), the pulls partly cancel → **Neap Tide** (smallest range).

> - Mean Earth–Sun distance: **D = 149.6 × 10⁶ km ≈ 23 480 R**
> - Mass ratio (Moon/Earth): **M_m / M_E = 0.0123**
> - Mass ratio (Sun/Earth): **M_s / M_E = 333 000**

> - Synodic solar day: **24 h 00 min (semidiurnal solar tide period 12 h 00 min)**

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Ice–Structure Interaction: The Four Principal Failure Modes

The ice load on a vertical offshore structure is **failure-mode-controlled**. The mode selected by the ice determines the magnitude of the design force.

1. **Crushing Failure (Ductile/Brittle Crushing):**
   - Dominates when **D / h < 6** (small aspect ratio), where D = structure diameter, h = ice thickness.
   - Force is governed by ice uniaxial crushing strength σ_c.
   - **High aspect ratio (small width, thick ice) → high force**.

2. **Bending (Flexural) Failure:**
   - Dominates for **sloping structures** (e.g., conical offshore structures) or for narrow vertical structures with **D / h < 1**.
   - Ice beam fails in flexure as it rides up the cone.
   - Force is proportional to flexural strength σ_f × h² (smaller than crushing).

3. **Buckling / Splitting Failure:**
   - Dominates when **D / h < 4 to 6** with a low contact velocity.
   - Brittle fracture propagates radially from contact zone.
   - Force ≈ 1/3 to 1/2 of crushing force.

4. **Ridge-Ice Failure (Mixed-mode):**
   - Thick first-year and multi-year ridges have a consolidated **keel** and a **sail**.
   - Failure is sequential: sail crushes first, then consolidated keel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the failure mode matter to a designer?**
> The structure must be designed for the **worst credible failure mode**. A small-diameter cylindrical leg will see crushing, while a wide caisson may see bending. Conical structures are deliberately used to force bending failure and reduce the design force by a factor of 4 to 10.

### 2.2 Empirical and Semi-Empirical Ice-Force Formulas

#### 2.2.1 Korzhavin (1962) – Crushing on Vertical Cylinder
The classical Russian/Soviet formula, still referenced in ISO 19906:

$$F_H = m \cdot k_n \cdot \sigma_c \cdot D \cdot h$$

Where the terms are defined in the formula table below.

#### 2.2.2 Schwarz & Hirayama (1973) – Combined Crushing
An empirical relation that absorbs the strain-rate and aspect-ratio effects:

$$F_H = 0.5 \cdot C_R \cdot \sigma_c \cdot D^{0.5} \cdot h^{1.1}$$

#### 2.2.3 Ralston (1980) – Conical Structure (Bending-Driven)
For a cone of half-apex angle α, the horizontal ice force on a cone:

$$F_H = A_4 \cdot \left[ \sigma_f \cdot \frac{h^{2.5}}{A_3} \right] \cdot \left[\frac{\tan \alpha}{1 - \mu \tan \alpha}\right]$$

#### 2.2.4 Total Ice Force (Multi-Mode Limit-State)
The total horizontal design force combines several contributions:

$$F_{total} = F_{limit} + F_{ridge\,sail} + F_{iceberg} + F_{adfreeze}$$

### 2.3 Tides – Equilibrium Theory of Tides

The **Equilibrium Tide Theory** (Newton/Bernoulli) treats the ocean as a frictionless, landless fluid envelope that responds instantaneously to the differential gravitational pull of the Moon (and Sun). It predicts the **equilibrium tide height** at any point.

The **tidal generating force per unit mass** has two components:

- **Horizontal (tractive) component** that causes water to flow toward the sub-lunar and anti-lunar points.
- **Vertical component** that simply adds/subtracts a tiny fraction of g (≈ 10⁻⁷ of g) and is therefore ignored in water motion.

**Moon's tidal potential** at a point on the Earth's surface (geocentric angle θ from sub-lunar point):

$$\Phi_M = \frac{G M_m}{d^3} \cdot \frac{R^2}{2} \cdot (3 \cos^2 \theta - 1)$$

**Sun's tidal potential** (analogous):

$$\Phi_S = \frac{G M_s}{D^3} \cdot \frac{R^2}{2} \cdot (3 \cos^2 \phi - 1)$$

The ratio of solar to lunar tidal force is:

$$\frac{M_s / D^3}{M_m / d^3} \approx 0.46$$

→ **The Moon produces ~68% of the tide, the Sun ~32%.** (This ratio is a guaranteed KTU short-answer question.)

**Tidal constituents (Harmonic Constituents):**
- **M₂ (Principal Lunar Semidiurnal)** – Period 12 h 25 min – Amplitude ~ largest.
- **S₂ (Principal Solar Semidiurnal)** – Period 12 h 00 min.
- **K₁ (Luni-Solar Declinational Diurnal)** – Period 23 h 56 min.
- **O₁ (Principal Lunar Declinational Diurnal)** – Period 25 h 49 min.
- **N₂ (Lunar Elliptical), K₂, P₁, Q₁** – Smaller constituents.

The **tidal elevation** at any time t is the linear sum of these:

$$\eta(t) = \sum_{i} f_i \, H_i \, \cos(\omega_i t + V_i + u_i - g_i)$$

where H_i = amplitude, ω_i = angular speed, V_i + u_i = astronomical phase, g_i = local phase lag, f_i = node factor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Spring–Neap Cycle Period:** ~14.77 days (half the synodic month).
> **Tidal Day:** ~24 h 50 min (longer than the solar day by 50 minutes).

> [!WARNING]
> **Subtle KTU Pitfall:** Many students write "spring tide = maximum + maximum." Actually, a **spring tide** is the **maximum tidal range** (not the maximum elevation), and it occurs at **new moon AND full moon** (both alignments). A **neap tide** is the **minimum range**, occurring at the **first and third quarter moon**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Air-gap design of fixed platforms** (e.g., Molikpaq in the Beaufort Sea, Hibernia in Newfoundland) requires a summation: **HAT + storm surge + wave crest + heave + tide-surge interaction** to prevent "green water" loading on the deck.
- **Mooring system fatigue** for FPSOs in regions like the North-West Shelf (Australia) is dominated by tidal current reversal, not waves.
- **Pipeline span design** in shallow water (e.g., Bass Strait) must consider tidal currents of 1.5–2.0 m/s that drive **VIV (Vortex-Induced Vibration)**.
- **Arctic drilling** (e.g., Sakhalin, Bohai Bay, Greenland) cannot proceed without an **Ice Management Plan** (icebreakers, deflection booms, ice-resistant cones) certified to API RP 2N.
