# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define corrosion in the context of offshore structures. Why is the splash zone considered the most aggressive corrosion zone?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Corrosion** is the electrochemical deterioration of a metal through reaction with its environment. In offshore structures, it refers specifically to the **electrochemical attack of seawater** on submerged and exposed structural steel. **(1 mark)**

> The **splash zone** is the most aggressive because it is subject to: **(2 marks)**
> 1. **Continuous wet-dry cycling** — never stays wet (no protective biofilm) and never stays dry (no drying-induced passivation).
> 2. **Constant oxygen replenishment** — splashing waves continuously refresh dissolved $O_2$, accelerating the cathodic reduction reaction.
> 3. **Mechanical impact** from waves erodes any incipient protective film.
> 4. **Highest biofilm activity** and highest chloride concentration on the structure.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between Sacrificial Anode Cathodic Protection (SACP) and Impressed Current Cathodic Protection (ICCP).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | SACP | ICCP |
|---|---|---|
| Power source | None (galvanic) | External DC rectifier **(1 mark)** |
| Anode material | Active metal (Zn, Al, Mg) | Inert (MMO, Pb-Ag, graphite) **(1 mark)** |
| Current output | Low (limited by anode potential) | High and adjustable **(1 mark)** |
| Application | Small/medium structures, fixed platforms | Large structures, pipelines, ships |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Always show units in the final answer.** Marks are deducted when a numerical result is given without proper units (e.g., writing 93 instead of 93 mm/yr).
> - **Never confuse corrosion rate with corrosion current.** They are *related* but not the same quantity.
> - **Write Faraday's constant with the unit C/mol** — writing just "96500" without units loses 0.5 mark.
> - In CP design problems, students often forget the **utilization factor** — this costs up to 2 marks.

### ❓ QUESTION A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **electrochemical mechanism of corrosion of steel in seawater**, including the anodic and cathodic reactions, with a neat schematic. State the conditions under which **oxygen differential cell** and **concentration cell** corrosion occurs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sacrificial **aluminum anode** of mass **$8.5$ kg** is used to protect a subsea pipeline. The electrochemical equivalent of Al is $Z = 0.335 \; g/Ah$ and the utilization factor is $u = 0.85$. If the design current output is $I = 0.45$ A, calculate: **(7 marks)**
   (i) The **theoretical life** of the anode in years.
   (ii) The **actual life** accounting for the utilization factor.
   (iii) Comment on whether this is sufficient for a 20-year design life.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

### Part (a) — Electrochemical Mechanism (7 marks)

**1. The Four Components of a Corrosion Cell (2 marks):**

For corrosion of steel in seawater to occur, four elements must be present simultaneously:
- **Anode** — region where metal dissolves.
- **Cathode** — region where reduction occurs.
- **Electrolyte** — seawater.
- **Metallic path** — the steel itself.

**2. Anodic Reaction (1.5 marks):**

At the anodic site, iron atoms lose electrons and enter the solution as ferrous ions:

$$Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^- \quad (E^\circ = -0.44 \; V \text{ vs SHE})$$

**3. Cathodic Reaction (1.5 marks):**

In aerated, neutral seawater, the dominant cathodic reaction is oxygen reduction:

$$O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^- \quad (E^\circ = +0.40 \; V \text{ vs SHE})$$

**4. Oxygen Differential Cell (1 mark):**

When one part of the steel is exposed to *high* oxygen (e.g., near the waterline) and another to *low* oxygen (e.g., under marine growth or in a crevice), a differential aeration cell forms. The **low-oxygen area becomes the anode** and corrodes preferentially. The **high-oxygen area becomes the cathode**.

**5. Concentration Cell (1 mark):**

Differences in **salt concentration** or **pH** between two regions of the same metal in the same electrolyte can drive a galvanic current. The region in *dilute* or *low-pH* electrolyte becomes the anode.

### Part (b) — Anode Life Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:** $m = 8.5 \; \text{kg} = 8500 \; \text{g}$, $Z = 0.335 \; \text{g/Ah}$, $I = 0.45 \; \text{A}$, $u = 0.85$, Design life = 20 years.

**(i) Theoretical life (3 marks):**

Total theoretical charge capacity:

$$Q_{theoretical} = \frac{m}{Z} = \frac{8500}{0.335} = 25{,}373 \; \text{Ah}$$

Time in hours at design current:

$$t_{hours} = \frac{Q_{theoretical}}{I} = \frac{25{,}373}{0.45} = 56{,}385 \; \text{hours}$$

Convert to years:

$$L_{theoretical} = \frac{56{,}385}{8760} = 6.44 \; \text{years}$$

**(ii) Actual life (3 marks):**

Apply the utilization factor:

$$L_{actual} = u \times L_{theoretical} = 0.85 \times 6.44 = 5.47 \; \text{years}$$

**(iii) Comment (1 mark):**

Since $L_{actual} = 5.47$ years $\ll$ 20 years, the **anode is grossly undersized**. The engineer must either:
- Use **multiple anodes** (at least $\lceil 20/5.47 \rceil = 4$ anodes in parallel), OR
- Specify **larger anodes** with proportional mass scaling.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative) — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With neat sketches, describe the **various forms of corrosion** encountered in offshore structures. For each form, give **one example** of its occurrence in a typical fixed jacket platform. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **corrosion rate equation** from Faraday's First Law. A carbon steel specimen of exposed area $50 \; cm^2$ and density $7.85 \; g/cm^3$ was exposed to seawater for **60 days**, losing **2400 mg** of mass. Calculate: **(7 marks)**
   (i) The **corrosion rate** in mm/year.
   (ii) The **classification** of severity as per NACE standards.
   (iii) The **corrosion current** in amperes, given $n = 2$ and $M = 56 \; g/mol$ for iron.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

### Part (a) — Forms of Corrosion (7 marks — 1 mark per form × 7 forms)

| # | Form | Description | Offshore Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Uniform** | Even metal loss across surface | Atmospheric zone of jacket |
| 2 | **Pitting** | Localized deep cavities | Submerged leg of platform near weld |
| 3 | **Crevice** | Attack in shielded gaps | Underneath bolted flange connections |
| 4 | **Galvanic** | Dissimilar metals coupled | Bronze valve on steel riser |
| 5 | **Erosion** | Mechanical + chemical combo | At T-junction of high-velocity flowline |
| 6 | **SCC** | Cracking under stress + corrosion | Susceptible stainless steel piping |
| 7 | **MIC** | Bacterial action (SRB) | Subsea mud zone on buried piles |

### Part (b) — Corrosion Rate Derivation & Numerical (7 marks)

**Derivation (3 marks):** Refer to the full derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The student must:
1. Start with Faraday's First Law $m = \dfrac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$.
2. Substitute $m = A \cdot d \cdot D$.
3. Rearrange to $CR = \dfrac{K \cdot W}{A \cdot T \cdot D}$ with $K = 8.76 \times 10^4$.

**Given:** $A = 50 \; cm^2$, $D = 7.85 \; g/cm^3$, $T = 60 \times 24 = 1440 \; \text{hours}$, $W = 2400 \; \text{mg}$.

**(i) Corrosion rate (2 marks):**

$$CR = \frac{8.76 \times 10^4 \times 2400}{50 \times 1440 \times 7.85} = \frac{2.1024 \times 10^8}{5.652 \times 10^5}$$

$$CR \approx 372 \; \text{mm/year}$$

**(ii) Classification (1 mark):**

Per NACE TM0169, $CR > 0.6$ mm/yr → **SEVERE corrosion**. The student should also comment that this rate is unrealistically high for unprotected mild steel, suggesting experimental conditions (e.g., accelerated test, high temperature, acidic).

**(iii) Corrosion current (1 mark):**

From Faraday's Law, $I = \dfrac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{M \cdot t}$. Convert $m = 2400 \; \text{mg} = 2.4 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{kg} = 2.4 \; \text{g}$ and $t = 1440 \; \text{h} = 1440 \times 3600 = 5.184 \times 10^6 \; \text{s}$:

$$I = \frac{2.4 \times 2 \times 96485}{56 \times 5.184 \times 10^6}$$

$$I = \frac{463{,}128}{2.903 \times 10^8} \approx 1.595 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{A} = 1.595 \; \text{mA}$$

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — The 15-Point High-Yield List**

- [x] **Corrosion is electrochemical** — always requires anode + cathode + electrolyte + metallic path.
- [x] **Splash zone = most aggressive** marine zone (40% of platform damage).
- [x] **Anode reaction:** $Fe \rightarrow Fe^{2+} + 2e^-$ ($E^\circ = -0.44$ V).
- [x] **Cathode reaction in aerated seawater:** $O_2 + 2H_2O + 4e^- \rightarrow 4OH^-$ ($E^\circ = +0.40$ V).
- [x] **Driving force** for Fe corrosion in seawater: $\Delta E^\circ \approx 0.84$ V.
- [x] **8 forms of corrosion** to memorize: Uniform, Pitting, Crevice, Galvanic, Erosion, SCC, MIC, Cavitation.
- [x] **CP potential range** for steel: **−800 mV to −1100 mV** vs Ag/AgCl reference.
- [x] **SACP anodes** = Zn, Al, Mg (more active than Fe).
- [x] **ICCP anodes** = MMO, Pb-Ag, graphite (inert).
- [x] **Faraday's constant** = $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol.
- [x] **Corrosion rate formula:** $CR = \dfrac{K \cdot W}{A \cdot T \cdot D}$ with $K = 8.76 \times 10^4$ → mm/year.
- [x] **NACE severity threshold:** $CR > 0.6$ mm/yr = **Severe**.
- [x] **CP design current density:** $100-150 \; mA/m^2$ (tropical seawater).
- [x] **Anode utilization factor** $u$ is **always ≤ 1** (typically 0.80–0.90 for Al, 0.90–0.95 for Zn).
- [x] **Three-layer protection philosophy:** Coatings (1st line) + Cathodic Protection (2nd line) + Corrosion Allowance (3rd line — extra steel thickness in design).

> **End of Notes — Best Wishes for your KTU 2024 Examinations! 🎓⚓**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sizing Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST722 - FUNDAMENTALS OF OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sizing Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Sizing Considerations** in offshore structural engineering refer to the systematic, multi-criteria process of determining the principal geometric dimensions, displacement, draft, mass distribution, and load-carrying capacities of an offshore unit (fixed or floating) such that it safely performs its intended functions—production, drilling, storage, or habitation—throughout its design life under the combined action of operational loads, environmental loads (wind, wave, current), and accidental loads.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the sizing problem is treated as a **preliminary design synthesis** task in which the design variables are:

- **Principal dimensions**: Length ($L$), Breadth ($B$), Depth ($D$), Draft ($T$).
- **Mass groups**: Light Ship Weight ($LSW$), Topside Operating Weight ($OW$), Variable Load ($VL$).
- **Geometric coefficients**: Block coefficient ($C_B$), Waterplane area coefficient ($C_WP$), Midship section coefficient ($C_M$).
- **Stability & motion parameters**: Metacentric height ($GM$), Natural periods ($T_{heave}, T_{roll}, T_{pitch}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight**: Sizing is *not* a single optimization; it is an **iterative convergence** between weight-growth, displacement-growth, and stability constraints. The output of sizing is the **General Arrangement (GA)** drawing input set.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Floating Apartment Block"

Imagine you are designing a **floating apartment block** that must:
1. Carry a fixed weight of furniture, people, and water tanks (the **Dead/Operating Weight**).
2. Have enough space on the roof for a helipad and AC units (**Deck Space / Topside Layout**).
3. Sit high enough above the water so waves do not crash over it (**Freeboard**).
4. Stay upright even when tenants move from one side to the other (**Stability**).
5. Be reasonably cheap to build using steel plates of known thickness (**Steel Weight Estimation**).

Each of these "real-world" demands translates directly into a sizing equation. The art of sizing is balancing all these demands so the floating block (offshore unit) is **safe, stable, functional, and economical**—no single constraint is allowed to dominate.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Board Standard)

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| Sea water density | $\rho_{sw} = \mathbf{1.025\ t/m^3}$ |
| Gravitational acceleration | $g = \mathbf{9.81\ m/s^2}$ |
| Typical FPSO storage capacity | $\mathbf{2 \times 10^5}$ to $\mathbf{3 \times 10^5}$ m³ |
| Typical Semisubmersible draft (operating) | $\mathbf{20}$ to $\mathbf{35}$ m |
| Typical Spar draft (operating) | $\mathbf{150}$ to $\mathbf{200}$ m |
| Minimum freeboard (Drillships/Semis) | $\mathbf{\geq 10}$ m above MWL |
| Design wave height (100-yr storm) | $\mathbf{15}$ to $\mathbf{30}$ m Gulf of Mexico |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip**: Examiners expect students to **memorize** the sea water density value $\rho_{sw} = 1.025\ t/m^3$ and $g = 9.81\ m/s^2$. Questions frequently begin with these constants given in the stem; missing units cost marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of the Sizing Problem (Stepwise Logic)

The KTU 2024 syllabus breaks "Sizing Considerations" into **five interlinked decision blocks**. The order is important—earlier blocks feed later blocks.

### Block A — Functional Requirement Definition
- Define mission: Drilling? Production? Storage? FPSO? Semisubmersible? TLP? Spar?
- Define payload: Number of wells, oil processing capacity, crew complement (typically **120–180** persons for a deepwater drillship).
- Define water depth ($d_w$) at the installation site—this dictates platform type.

### Block B — Topside Sizing
The topside is sized to accommodate:
1. **Drilling equipment** (derrick, drawworks, mud pumps): occupies a fixed footprint.
2. **Production equipment** (separators, manifolds, compressors): footprint depends on throughput.
3. **Accommodation block**: typical rule-of-thumb **25–35 m²/person** (KTU standard).
4. **Cranes, helideck, lifeboat stations**: regulated by **API 4F / IMO MODU Code**.

### Block C — Hull / Substructure Sizing (for Floaters)
- **Buoyancy must equal total weight** at the design draft.
- **Reserve buoyancy** must be sufficient to keep the unit afloat with any one compartment flooded (damaged stability).
- **Waterplane area** must be large enough to provide acceptable roll/pitch periods (typically **$T_{roll} \geq 25$ s** for semis to avoid wave resonance).

### Block D — Stability Sizing
- **GM (metacentric height)** must satisfy classification society rules:
  - Intact: $GM \geq 0.15$ m.
  - Damaged: $GM_{damaged} \geq 0.05$ m with one compartment flooded.
- **GZ curve** area (a measure of righting energy) must be $\geq 0.055$ m·rad up to 40° heel.

### Block E — Structural Sizing (Steel Weight)
- Steel weight drives cost more than any other variable.
- Empirical formulas relate steel weight to the principal dimensions.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Block | Why it matters |
|---|---|
| A — Functional | Wrong mission = wrong platform type = total redesign. |
| B — Topside | Topside grows *downward* (more equipment) and *upward* (crane boom length)—both affect stability. |
| C — Hull | Under-sized hull = insufficient buoyancy = sinking risk or excess draft. |
| D — Stability | An unstable platform capsizes—the single most catastrophic failure mode. |
| E — Steel | Steel is 30–40% of total CAPEX; over-sizing inflates cost dramatically. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Memorize This Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **complete KTU 2024 high-yield formula set** for the Sizing Considerations module. Every numerical problem in the university exam will reduce to one or more of these.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ | Archimedes: Displacement equals buoyancy | kN (or tonnes-force) |
| 2 | $W_{total} = LSW + DW + OW + VL$ | Total weight of floating unit | tonnes |
| 3 | $T = \dfrac{\Delta}{\rho_{sw} \cdot A_{WP}}$ | Draft from displacement & waterplane area | m |
| 4 | $RB = V_{total} - V_{submerged} = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla - W_{total}/g$ | Reserve buoyancy (volume basis) | m³ |
| 5 | $f = D - T$ | Freeboard = Depth − Draft | m |
| 6 | $C_B = \dfrac{\nabla}{L \cdot B \cdot T}$ | Block coefficient (fullness of hull) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $C_{WP} = \dfrac{A_{WP}}{L \cdot B}$ | Waterplane area coefficient | dimensionless |
| 8 | $C_M = \dfrac{A_M}{B \cdot T}$ | Midship section coefficient | dimensionless |
| 9 | $BM = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla}$ | Metacentric radius | m |
| 10 | $GM = KB + BM - KG$ | Metacentric height (intact stability) | m |
| 11 | $T_{roll} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot k}{\sqrt{g \cdot GM}}$ | Natural roll period (semi-empirical) | s |
| 12 | $W_{steel} = k_s \cdot (L \cdot B \cdot D)^{n_s}$ | Empirical steel weight (typical $k_s = 0.035$, $n_s = 1.1$) | tonnes |
| 13 | $A_{deck} = A_{topside} \cdot f_{util}$ | Deck area with utilization factor (typical $f_{util} = 1.3$) | m² |
| 14 | $VL = V_{crew} + V_{supplies} + V_{fluids} + V_{drill}$ | Variable load breakdown | tonnes |
| 15 | $SF = \dfrac{\Delta_{max}}{\Delta_{operating}}$ | Safety factor on displacement | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Substitution Rule (Mandatory)**: In KTU Formula Sheet tables, never use the `|` character for absolute value (e.g., write $\vert x \vert$ instead of $\vert x \vert$ directly). Use `\vert` or `\mid` to keep the markdown table parser happy.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Sizing considerations are the **first 30% of the design effort** but determine **70% of the lifecycle cost**. Major offshore projects (e.g., Shell's *Prelude* FLNG, Total's *CLOV* FPSO) spend 12–18 months in the sizing phase alone. A 5% error in displacement estimation can translate to a **\$50–100 million** CAPEX overrun because it cascades into hull steel, mooring line size, and even dry-dock selection.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Draft from Displacement and Waterplane Area

**Statement (KTU Standard)**: For a floating unit in static equilibrium at a small trim angle, derive the relationship between draft, displacement, and waterplane area.

### Given
- $\Delta$ = displacement (mass of water displaced, in tonnes)
- $\rho_{sw}$ = sea water density (t/m³)
- $A_{WP}$ = waterplane area (m²)
- $T$ = mean draft (m)

### Assumptions
- Calm water, static equilibrium.
- Hull form is such that $A_{WP}$ is approximately constant near the design waterline.
- Trim is zero (we use mean draft).

### Derivation

**Step 1**: Buoyancy force equals the weight of displaced water (Archimedes' Principle):

$$
F_B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla
$$

where $\nabla$ is the displaced volume in m³.

**Step 2**: In static equilibrium, the buoyancy force equals the total weight of the unit ($W_{total}$):

$$
F_B = W_{total} = \Delta \cdot g
$$

where $\Delta$ is the displacement in tonnes (mass units).

**Step 3**: Equating the two expressions for $F_B$ and cancelling $g$:

$$
\Delta \cdot g = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla
$$

$$
\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla
$$

**Step 4**: For a small change in draft $dT$ around the design waterline, the change in displaced volume is:

$$
d\nabla = A_{WP} \cdot dT
$$

This is the fundamental relationship from naval architecture—the waterplane area is the *derivative* of volume with respect to draft.

**Step 5**: Integrating from $T = 0$ (bottom of hull) to the mean draft $T$:

$$
\nabla(T) = \int_{0}^{T} A_{WP}(z) \, dz
$$

**Step 6**: For a *prismatic* hull where $A_{WP}$ is approximately constant (a KTU-acceptable simplification for box-shaped or semi-submersible pontoons):

$$
\nabla \approx A_{WP} \cdot T
$$

**Step 7**: Substituting back into $\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot \nabla$:

$$
\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot A_{WP} \cdot T
$$

**Step 8**: Solving for the mean draft $T$:

$$
\boxed{T = \dfrac{\Delta}{\rho_{sw} \cdot A_{WP}}}
$$

**Dimensional Check**:
- Numerator: tonnes.
- Denominator: $(\text{t/m}^3) \cdot (\text{m}^2) = \text{t/m}$.
- Result: m ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Metacentric Height $GM$

**Statement**: Derive the metacentric height of a floating unit using the standard naval architecture formulation.

### Given
- $KB$ = height of centre of buoyancy above keel (m)
- $BM$ = metacentric radius (m)
- $KG$ = height of centre of gravity above keel (m)
- $I_T$ = transverse second moment of waterplane area about the centreline (m⁴)
- $\nabla$ = displaced volume (m³)

### Derivation

**Step 1**: When a floating unit is heeled by a small angle $\phi$, the centre of buoyancy $B$ shifts to a new position $B'$. The line of action of the buoyancy force (now through $B'$) intersects the original vertical centreline at a point $M$ called the **metacentre**.

**Step 2**: By the geometry of the wedge-shaped displaced/emerge volume (Wallis's formula, 1762):

$$
BM = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla}
$$

This is the classical metacentric radius formula. The derivation involves integrating the moment of the wedge volumes about the centreline, leading to the second moment of the waterplane area.

**Step 3**: The righting lever arm $GZ$ for small angles of heel ($\phi$ in radians) is:

$$
GZ = GM \cdot \sin(\phi) \approx GM \cdot \phi
$$

**Step 4**: From the geometry of the buoyancy-centre-gravity triangle:

$$
GM = KB + BM - KG
$$

**Step 5**: Combining Steps 2 and 4:

$$
\boxed{GM = KB + \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla} - KG}
$$

**Physical Interpretation**:
- $KB$ is set by the hull form (usually $KB \approx 0.5T$ for a rectangular pontoon).
- $BM$ depends on waterplane stiffness (wide hulls have large $BM$).
- $KG$ is set by the mass distribution (tall/heavy topsides push $G$ up, reducing $GM$).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Pattern**: 14-mark derivation questions on $GM$ frequently test whether students can (a) state the assumption (small angle), (b) write the formula, and (c) compute the three components $KB$, $BM$, $KG$ from a numerical problem.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Steel Weight Empirical Relationship

**Statement**: Show how the steel weight of an offshore unit scales with the principal dimensions.

### Derivation Outline

**Step 1**: Steel weight $W_s$ of a hull is proportional to the surface area of the structural shell times the average plate thickness $\bar{t}$:

$$
W_s = \rho_{steel} \cdot \bar{t} \cdot A_{wetted}
$$

**Step 2**: For a rectangular approximation, the wetted surface scales as:

$$
A_{wetted} \approx 2(LB + BT + LT) \approx k_1 \cdot (LBD)^{2/3}
$$

using geometric similarity arguments.

**Step 3**: The average plate thickness scales with the design hydrostatic pressure, which scales with depth ($D$) of the hull:

$$
\bar{t} \propto D
$$

**Step 4**: Combining:

$$
W_s \propto D \cdot (LBD)^{2/3} = (LBD)^{1} \cdot (LBD)^{-1/3} \cdot D = (LBD)^{2/3} \cdot D
$$

**Step 5**: Re-exponentiating and absorbing constants into a single coefficient $k_s$, we obtain the well-known **Moss Rosenberg** form:

$$
\boxed{W_{steel} = k_s \cdot (L \cdot B \cdot D)^{n_s}}
$$

with typical values $k_s \approx 0.025$ to $0.045$ and $n_s \approx 1.0$ to $1.2$ for offshore hulls.

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem**: A semi-submersible has the following characteristics:
> - Length $L = 110$ m, Breadth $B = 80$ m, Depth $D = 40$ m.
> - Operating displacement $\Delta = 48{,}000$ tonnes.
> - Waterplane area $A_{WP} = 2{,}400$ m².
> - Transverse second moment $I_T = 1.2 \times 10^6$ m⁴.
> - $KB = 12$ m, $KG = 18$ m.

**Find**: (a) The operating draft. (b) The metacentric height $GM$. (c) The natural roll period. (d) Comment on stability.

### Solution

**(a) Operating Draft**:
Using $T = \dfrac{\Delta}{\rho_{sw} \cdot A_{WP}}$:

$$
T = \dfrac{48{,}000}{1.025 \times 2{,}400} = \dfrac{48{,}000}{2{,}460}
$$

$$
\boxed{T = 19.51\ \text{m}}
$$

**(b) Metacentric Height $GM$**:
First compute $BM$:

$$
BM = \dfrac{I_T}{\nabla} = \dfrac{I_T \cdot \rho_{sw}}{\Delta} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^6 \times 1.025}{48{,}000}
$$

$$
BM = \dfrac{1{,}230{,}000}{48{,}000} = 25.625\ \text{m}
$$

Then:

$$
GM = KB + BM - KG = 12 + 25.625 - 18 = 19.625\ \text{m}
$$

$$
\boxed{GM = 19.625\ \text{m}}
$$

**(c) Natural Roll Period**:
Using the semi-empirical formula $T_{roll} = \dfrac{2\pi \cdot k}{\sqrt{g \cdot GM}}$ with $k \approx 0.45$ (typical for semis):

$$
T_{roll} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 0.45}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 19.625}} = \dfrac{2.827}{\sqrt{192.52}} = \dfrac{2.827}{13.875}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{roll} = 0.204\ \text{s}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Valuation Error**: In (c), students frequently write $T_{roll} \approx 20$ s, which is the correct order of magnitude for a *ship* but **not** for a semi-submersible. The semi's wide pontoons make $k \approx 0.45$ to $0.55$, and a roll period of $\sim 20$ s is a sign of *inadequate* stability, not adequate. Always state the assumption on $k$.

**(d) Stability Comment**:
- $GM = 19.625$ m is **extremely high** (typical safe range: 1–5 m for a semi). This is over-stiff, leading to jerky, uncomfortable, high-acceleration motions.
- $T_{roll} = 0.2$ s is too small (target: 25–35 s). The semi would behave more like a buoy than a stable platform.
- **Conclusion**: The numerical values are inconsistent with the assumed dimensions; this suggests the $I_T$ value is unrealistically large, or the unit is incorrectly modelled. In practice, designers would *reduce* $I_T$ by adding bracing, or *increase* $A_{WP}$ by widening the pontoons.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied**:

> - No reserved keywords (`end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, `style`) used as node names.
> - Labels contain clean uppercase text only—no bold, no italics, no inline tables.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stpA1[BLOCK A - DEFINE FUNCTIONAL REQUIREMENTS] --> stpA2[Identify mission: drilling production storage FPSO semis TLP spar]
    stpA2 --> stpA3[Determine water depth and metocean criteria]

    blkC2 --> blkC3[Compute required displacement Delta from buoyancy]
    blkC3 --> blkC4[Iterate principal dimensions L B D T for C_B and A_WP]
    blkC4 --> blkD1[BLOCK D - STABILITY SIZING]

    blkD2 --> blkD3[Compute KB and BM from hull form]

    blkD4 --> blkE1[BLOCK E - STRUCTURAL AND STEEL SIZING]

    blkF1 --> blkF2{All constraints satisfied?}

    blkF2 -->|No| stpA1
```

flowchart LR
