# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** *Define bracing system in a fixed offshore steel jacket platform. List any four functions it serves.* `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A bracing system in a fixed offshore steel jacket platform is the secondary load-resisting framework composed of inclined tubular members (diagonals, horizontals, knee braces) that interconnect the vertical legs of the jacket. It converts the jacket into a stiff vertical cantilever truss.

**Four functions of a bracing system (½ mark each):**

1. **Lateral load transfer** – Transmits horizontal wave, current, and wind loads from the deck to the pile foundation.
2. **Lateral stiffness** – Limits horizontal sway of the platform under operational and storm conditions.
3. **Reduction of leg unbraced length** – Horizontal bracing reduces the effective length of legs, improving their buckling capacity.
4. **Provision of redundancy** – Multiple load paths ensure structural integrity even if one member is damaged.

*(Any four correct functions – full marks.)*

---

### **Q2.** *Differentiate between X-bracing and K-bracing in offshore jacket structures.* `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | X-Bracing | K-Bracing |
|---|---|---|
| **Geometry** | Two diagonals crossing at the centre of the bay | Diagonals meet at a point on a vertical sub-member |
| **Stiffness** | Maximum lateral stiffness | Moderate stiffness |
| **Joint Type** | X-joint or XX-joint | K-joint (more complex) |
| **Fatigue** | Better, since X-joints have lower SCF | Poorer, K-joints have high SCF |
| **Use** | Storm regions, upper bays | Deep water where leg slenderness is critical |
| **Steel Weight** | Heavier | Lighter in some configurations |

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **🔵 QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**The diagonal brace in a jacket platform has the following properties:**
- Bay width: B = 8 m
- Bay height: h = 10 m
- Brace diameter: D = 600 mm
- Brace thickness: t = 20 mm
- Horizontal shear at that elevation: V = 1200 kN
- Yield strength: f_y = 360 MPa
- E = 2.0 × 10⁵ MPa

#### **(a)** *Compute the axial force in the diagonal brace and the axial stress developed. Verify whether the brace is safe in tension. (7 marks)*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Diagonal length (½ mark)**

$$L_d = \sqrt{B^2 + h^2} = \sqrt{8^2 + 10^2} = \sqrt{164} = 12.81 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Inclination angles (1 mark)**

$$\sin\theta = \frac{10}{12.81} = 0.7806, \quad \cos\theta = \frac{8}{12.81} = 0.6245$$

**Step 3 — Axial force in brace (1.5 marks)**

Equilibrium of the bay:

$$F_{brace} = \frac{V}{\cos\theta} = \frac{1200}{0.6245} = 1921.6 \text{ kN (Tension)}$$

**Step 4 — Cross-sectional area (1 mark)**

$$A_g = \pi t (D - t) = \pi \times 0.020 \times (0.600 - 0.020)$$

$$A_g = \pi \times 0.020 \times 0.580 = 0.03644 \text{ m}^2 = 36{,}442 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 5 — Axial stress (1 mark)**

$$\sigma_a = \frac{F_{brace}}{A_g} = \frac{1921.6 \times 10^3}{36{,}442} = 52.73 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6 — Safety check (1 mark — final)**

$$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{f_y}{SF} = \frac{360}{1.67} = 215.6 \text{ MPa}$$

Since $\sigma_a = 52.73 \text{ MPa} \ll 215.6 \text{ MPa}$, the brace is **SAFE in tension** with a utilization of **24.5%**.

**Utilization Ratio:** $UR = 52.73/215.6 = 0.245$ → **24.5% utilized**

---

#### **(b)** *Check whether the brace is safe in compression using the API RP 2A inelastic buckling formula. Assume K = 0.8 (joint can effect). (7 marks)*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Radius of gyration (½ mark)**

$$r = \frac{D}{4} = \frac{600}{4} = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.150 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Effective length (½ mark)**

$$L_e = K \cdot L_d = 0.8 \times 12.81 = 10.25 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Slenderness ratio (1 mark)**

$$\lambda = \frac{L_e}{r} = \frac{10.25}{0.150} = 68.3$$

**Step 4 — Limiting slenderness (1 mark)**

$$\lambda_c = \pi \sqrt{\frac{2E}{f_y}} = \pi \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 2.0 \times 10^5}{360}} = 104.7$$

Since $\lambda = 68.3 < \lambda_c = 104.7$ → **inelastic buckling** governs.

**Step 5 — Critical buckling stress (1.5 marks)**

$$F_{cr} = \left[ 1 - 0.25 \left(\frac{\lambda}{\lambda_c}\right)^2 \right] f_y$$

$$F_{cr} = \left[ 1 - 0.25 \times \left(\frac{68.3}{104.7}\right)^2 \right] \times 360$$

$$F_{cr} = \left[ 1 - 0.25 \times 0.4256 \right] \times 360 = [1 - 0.1064] \times 360$$

$$F_{cr} = 0.8936 \times 360 = 321.7 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6 — Buckling capacity (1 mark)**

$$P_{cr} = F_{cr} \times A_g = 321.7 \times 36{,}442 = 11{,}723{,}400 \text{ N} = 11{,}723 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 7 — Safety check (1.5 marks)**

Applied compression (worst case = same as tension magnitude): $F_a = 1921.6$ kN

$$\text{Utilization Ratio} = \frac{F_a}{P_{cr}} = \frac{1921.6}{11723} = 0.164 = 16.4\%$$

Since $UR < 1.0$, the brace is **SAFE in compression as well**, with 16.4% utilization.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to use effective length factor K** — many students use $L$ instead of $KL$, leading to a 25% error in slenderness.
> 2. **Using the wrong formula** — students often apply Euler's elastic formula even when $\lambda < \lambda_c$. Always check the regime first!
> 3. **Mixing units** — keep $L$ in mm when $r$ is in mm; convert carefully to MPa and N consistently.
> 4. **Neglecting joint can effect** — K = 0.8 is standard for tubular joints, not K = 1.0.

---

### **🟢 QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**A jacket platform has three bays along its height in one face, with the following data:**

| Bay No. | Height h (m) | Width B (m) | Shear V (kN) | Brace D × t (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (Top) | 8 | 10 | 600 | 500 × 16 |
| 2 (Mid) | 10 | 10 | 900 | 600 × 20 |
| 3 (Bot) | 12 | 10 | 1200 | 700 × 22 |

Take $f_y$ = 360 MPa, E = 2.0 × 10⁵ MPa, K = 0.8.

#### **(a)** *For each bay, determine the axial force in the diagonal brace. Comment on the trend. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

For each bay, the diagonal axial force is:

$$F_{brace,i} = \frac{V_i}{\cos\theta_i} = \frac{V_i \cdot L_{d,i}}{B}$$

**Bay 1 (Top):**
- $L_{d,1} = \sqrt{8^2 + 10^2} = \sqrt{164} = 12.81$ m
- $\cos\theta_1 = 10/12.81 = 0.7806$
- $F_{brace,1} = 600 / 0.7806 = 768.7$ kN (Tension)

**Bay 2 (Mid):**
- $L_{d,2} = \sqrt{10^2 + 10^2} = \sqrt{200} = 14.14$ m
- $\cos\theta_2 = 10/14.14 = 0.7071$
- $F_{brace,2} = 900 / 0.7071 = 1272.8$ kN (Tension)

**Bay 3 (Bottom):**
- $L_{d,3} = \sqrt{12^2 + 10^2} = \sqrt{244} = 15.62$ m
- $\cos\theta_3 = 10/15.62 = 0.6402$
- $F_{brace,3} = 1200 / 0.6402 = 1874.4$ kN (Tension)

**Trend (½ mark for comment):**
The axial force **increases from top to bottom** because:
1. **Base shear accumulates** — total horizontal force at the base is the sum of all environmental forces, so the bottom braces carry the largest share.
2. **Brace angle becomes shallower** at the bottom (lower $\cos\theta$) → same horizontal force requires larger axial force.
3. The cross-section also increases toward the base, which is **good design practice**.

---

#### **(b)** *Determine the axial stress in each brace and identify the most critically loaded brace. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

Cross-sectional areas:

$$A_{g,1} = \pi \times 16 \times (500-16) = \pi \times 16 \times 484 = 24{,}325 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_{g,2} = \pi \times 20 \times (600-20) = \pi \times 20 \times 580 = 36{,}442 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_{g,3} = \pi \times 22 \times (700-22) = \pi \times 22 \times 678 = 46{,}852 \text{ mm}^2$$

Axial stresses:

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{768.7 \times 10^3}{24{,}325} = 31.60 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_2 = \frac{1272.8 \times 10^3}{36{,}442} = 34.93 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_3 = \frac{1874.4 \times 10^3}{46{,}852} = 40.01 \text{ MPa}$$

**Utilization Ratios** (with $\sigma_{allow} = 360/1.67 = 215.6$ MPa):

$$UR_1 = 31.60/215.6 = 0.147 = 14.7\%$$

$$UR_2 = 34.93/215.6 = 0.162 = 16.2\%$$

$$UR_3 = 40.01/215.6 = 0.186 = 18.6\%$$

**Conclusion:** The **bottom brace (Bay 3)** is the **most critically loaded** with a utilization of **18.6%**, although all braces are well within safe limits. The designer has appropriately increased the brace cross-section to compensate for higher base shear.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall — What costs marks here:**
> 1. **Forgetting to compute $\cos\theta$** — students often use $V$ directly as the brace force, ignoring the angle. This underestimates the force by 30–60%.
> 2. **Sign convention confusion** — clearly state **Tension** or **Compression** in the answer.
> 3. **Not commenting on the trend** — Part (a) carries a comment mark; without it, you lose 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting SF** — directly comparing with $f_y$ instead of $f_y/SF$ loses the safety-check mark.

---

## 🎯 KTU Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Bracing system = secondary framework** of inclined/horizontal tubular members in a jacket.
- ✅ **Main function:** Transfer horizontal environmental loads to the pile foundation via truss action.
- ✅ **Four key bracing patterns:** X-bracing, K-bracing, Diagonal-only, Horizontal (bay) bracing.
- ✅ **X-bracing:** Maximum stiffness, heaviest, best redundancy.
- ✅ **K-bracing:** Controls leg slenderness, but K-joints are fatigue-critical.
- ✅ **Diagonal-only:** Lightest, least redundant, for mild environments.
- ✅ **Horizontal bracing:** Always used at each elevation; reduces leg buckling length.
- ✅ **Diagonal force formula (HIGH PRIORITY):** $F_{brace} = V / \cos\theta$
- ✅ **Chord force increment:** $\Delta N = F_{brace} \cdot \sin\theta$
- ✅ **Slenderness ratio:** $\lambda = KL/r = 4KL/D$ (for tubes)
- ✅ **Limiting slenderness:** $\lambda_c = \pi\sqrt{2E/f_y}$
- ✅ **Inelastic buckling** if $\lambda \le \lambda_c$: $F_{cr} = [1 - 0.25(\lambda/\lambda_c)^2]f_y$
- ✅ **Elastic (Euler) buckling** if $\lambda > \lambda_c$: $F_{cr} = \pi^2 E/\lambda^2$
- ✅ **Allowable axial stress (WSD):** $\sigma_{allow} = f_y / SF$, with $SF = 1.67$ to $2.0$
- ✅ **Morison equation** for hydrodynamic load on braces: $f = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D D \vert u\vert u + \rho C_M (\pi D^2/4) \dot{u}$
- ✅ **Base shear accumulates** from top to bottom → brace sizes increase with depth.
- ✅ **Tension-only design** is common in WSD for diagonals (compression neglected).
- ✅ **Joint can effect:** K = 0.8 typical for tubular joints.
- ✅ **Standard steel:** API 5L X52, $f_y = 360$ MPa, E = 2.0 × 10⁵ MPa.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:**
> Always draw a **clear free-body diagram** of the bay, label $V$, $\theta$, $B$, $h$, and resolve forces step by step. Examiners award **methodology marks** even if the final numerical answer has a minor arithmetic slip. A neat diagram + correct formula is worth at least **3 marks** on a 7-mark sub-question.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Jacket Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST722 - FUNDAMENTALS OF OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Jacket Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Jacket** is a tubular steel space-frame substructure used in fixed offshore platforms to transfer all environmental, operational, and self-weight loads from the deck (topside) through the water column down to the seabed via driven piles. The jacket is a welded tubular steel structure consisting of chords (main vertical/slightly battered legs), braces (horizontal, diagonal, and K-braces), and stub piles (or skirt piles) anchored to the seabed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *A jacket is a four-, six-, or eight-legged welded tubular space frame structure designed to support the topside facilities of a fixed offshore platform. It functions as the load-carrying transition element between the submerged seabed piles and the air-gap deck structure above the splash zone.*

Mathematically, the jacket is a **three-dimensional truss-like framework** with primary axial load transfer, designed to resist:
- Gravity (dead + live) loads
- Environmental loads (wind, wave, current, earthquake)
- Accidental loads (fire, explosion, vessel impact)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Steel Tripod in the Sea":**
>
> Imagine an enormous **steel Eiffel Tower** turned upside down and driven into the ocean floor, with its "feet" stuck in the seabed by massive steel nails (piles). The tower's top is the **deck** which holds the oil wells, living quarters, and drilling rig. The waves, wind, and current constantly push the tower sideways, but the steel legs and cross-braces (like a giant Meccano/LEGO frame) transfer all those horizontal forces safely down into the seabed via long steel pipes (piles) hammered deep into the ocean floor.
>
> - **Chords (Legs)** → Main vertical "skeleton" members
> - **Braces** → Diagonal and horizontal "X" members that make the structure stiff
> - **Piles** → The "roots" that anchor everything to the seabed
> - **Deck/Topsides** → The "head" carrying the production equipment

## 1.3 Key Terminology and Physical Metrics

| Term | Definition | Typical Range |
|------|------------|---------------|
| **Water Depth (Wd)** | Vertical distance from MSL to seabed | **30 m – 400+ m** for fixed jackets |
| **Air Gap** | Distance from MSL to underside of deck | **10 m – 20 m** (wave clearance) |
| **Splash Zone** | Region near mean water level | **±5 m of MSL** |
| **Chord Diameter (D)** | Diameter of main leg members | **0.8 m – 2.5 m** |
| **Brace Diameter (d)** | Diameter of secondary members | **0.3 m – 1.0 m** |
| **D/t Ratio** | Diameter-to-thickness ratio (slenderness) | **D/t ≤ 30 (API)** for chords |
| **Steel Grade** | Yield strength of tubular steel | **ASTM A36, A572, A690 (marine)** |
| **Wave Height (H)** | Design wave height | **Up to 30+ m** (100-yr storm) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Design Codes (KTU 2024 Module 3 Highlight):**
> - **API RP 2A-WSD** (American Petroleum Institute – Working Stress Design)
> - **ISO 19902** (International Standard for Fixed Steel Offshore Structures)
> - **DNV-OS-C101** (Det Norske Veritas – Design of Offshore Steel Structures)
> - **AISC** (for member capacity checks)
> - **API RP 2A-LRFD** (Load and Resistance Factor Design version)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Philosophy of Jacket Design

The design of an offshore jacket is fundamentally different from onshore structural design due to:
1. **Dynamic wave loading** (Morison-type forces on slender members)
2. **Marine environment** (corrosion, marine growth, fatigue)
3. **Construction/installation loads** (loadout, lift, upending)
4. **Uncertain seabed conditions** (soil-structure interaction)
5. **Coupled hydro-servo-elastic response**

The jacket is designed using a **multi-level analysis approach**:

### Level 1 — Global Analysis
- Treat jacket as a **space frame** with 6 DOF per node
- Apply environmental loads as equivalent static or dynamic forces
- Determine base shears, overturning moments, member axial forces

### Level 2 — Member Design
- Check tubular members for:
  - **Axial tension/compression capacity**
  - **Hydrostatic collapse** (external pressure)
  - **Combined stresses** (axial + bending + shear)
  - **Slenderness** (D/t, L/r ratios)
  - **Local buckling**

### Level 3 — Joint Design
- **Tubular joint capacity** (chord wall plastification, punching shear)
- **Fatigue life** (hot spot stress analysis)

### Level 4 — Foundation Design
- **Pile capacity** (axial + lateral, API sand/clay methods)
- **Soil-pile-structure interaction**

## 2.2 Key Design Considerations (Structured Logic)

- **Step 1: Environmental Definition** → Define 100-yr return storm, operational, and extreme conditions. Determine wave height, period, current profile, wind speed, and water depth.
- **Step 2: Load Modelling** → Compute wave forces using **Morison Equation** for small members (D/λ < 0.2) and **Diffraction Theory** for large members.
- **Step 3: Structural Idealisation** → Model jacket as 3D space frame. Members are pin-ended trusses (axial only) in WSD or beam elements (axial + bending) in LRFD.
- **Step 4: In-Place Analysis** → Compute member forces, joint displacements, base reactions, and overturning moments.
- **Step 5: Code-Based Member Sizing** → Size each tubular member to satisfy allowable stresses, slenderness, hydrostatic collapse, and combined loading.
- **Step 6: Joint Check** → Verify tubular joint capacities using API/ISO chord-plastification formulas.
- **Step 7: Foundation Design** → Size pile diameter, length, wall thickness, and penetration depth.
- **Step 8: Dynamic & Fatigue Check** → Perform spectral fatigue analysis for storm-driven wave loading.
- **Step 9: Installation Analysis** → Check load-out, transport, lift, and upending scenarios.
- **Step 10: In-Service & Accidental** → Check dropped-object, fire, and blast scenarios.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Step:**
> - Why Morison vs Diffraction? → Morison is valid for **D/λ < 0.2** (small diameter relative to wavelength). For jacket legs and braces in deep water, this is generally true.
> - Why space frame? → Wave, wind, and current are **multi-directional**; a 2D idealisation loses out-of-plane action.
> - Why fatigue? → Waves cause **10^6 – 10^8 cycles/year** of stress reversals in tubular joints (stress concentration zones).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every critical formula needed for KTU university exam problems on Jacket Design.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | **Morison Force (per unit length)** | $f = C_D \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} \rho D u \vert u \vert + C_M \cdot \tfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \rho \dot{u}$ | N/m | $C_D$ = drag coeff. (0.6–1.2), $C_M$ = inertia coeff. (1.5–2.0) |
| 2 | **Total Wave Force on Member** | $F = \int_0^{\eta} f \, dz$ | N | Integrate over submerged length |
| 3 | **Air-gap Requirement** | $Air\,Gap \geq 1.2 \cdot H_{max}$ | m | For 100-yr return wave |
| 4 | **Base Shear (Total horizontal)** | $V_{base} = \sum F_{wave} + F_{wind} + F_{current}$ | N | Vector sum |
| 5 | **Overturning Moment (OTM)** | $M_{OTM} = \sum F_i \cdot z_i$ | N·m | Moment about seabed |
| 6 | **Tubular Cross-sectional Area** | $A = \tfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - (D-2t)^2) = \pi t (D - t)$ | m² | Hollow tube |
| 7 | **Moment of Inertia** | $I = \tfrac{\pi}{64}(D^4 - (D-2t)^4)$ | m⁴ | For bending check |
| 8 | **Radius of Gyration** | $r = \sqrt{I/A}$ | m | Slenderness check |
| 9 | **Slenderness Ratio** | $\lambda = K L / r$ | – | K = effective length factor |
| 10 | **API Allowable Axial Stress (Compression)** | $F_a = 0.6 F_y$ if $\lambda \leq 60$; $F_a = \frac{0.6 F_y}{\lambda^2 / 18000}$ if $\lambda > 60$ | MPa | API RP 2A simplified |
| 11 | **Hoop Buckling Stress** | $F_{hc} = 2 E t / D$ (for D/t > 60) | MPa | Elastic hoop buckling |
| 12 | **API Pile Axial Capacity (Sand)** | $Q = Q_f + Q_p = f \cdot A_s + q \cdot A_p$ | N | $f$ = unit skin friction, $q$ = unit end bearing |
| 13 | **Skin Friction (Sand, API)** | $f = K p_o \tan\delta \leq f_{lim}$ | kPa | $K$ = lateral earth pressure coeff. |
| 14 | **End Bearing (Sand, API)** | $q = p_o \cdot N_q$ | kPa | $N_q$ from API chart |
| 15 | **Skin Friction (Clay, API α-method)** | $f = \alpha c$ (α ≤ 1.0) | kPa | $c$ = undrained shear strength |
| 16 | **Natural Period (Fixed-Base Approx.)** | $T_n = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{W}{g \cdot k}}$ | s | For dynamic check |
| 17 | **Tubular Joint Punching Shear** | $V_p = \frac{F_y \cdot t_c^2}{\sin\theta} \cdot Q_q$ | N | $Q_q$ = API geometry factor |
| 18 | **Wave Number** | $k = 2\pi / \lambda$ | rad/m | For Morison inertia term |
| 19 | **Air Density** | $\rho_{air} = 1.225$ | kg/m³ | For wind loads |
| 24 | **Sea Water Density** | $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ | kg/m³ | For wave/current loads |
| 21 | **Gravity Acceleration** | $g = 9.81$ | m/s² | Standard |
| 22 | **Steel Young's Modulus** | $E = 2.0 \times 10^5$ | MPa | For structural steel |
| 23 | **Steel Yield Strength (A36)** | $F_y = 250$ | MPa | Common API Grade |
| 24 | **Effective Axial Stress Check** | $f_a + f_b \leq 0.6 F_y$ (WSD) | MPa | AISC interaction |
| 25 | **Slenderness Limit (API)** | $D/t \leq 60$ for elastic; $D/t \leq 30$ for plastified | – | Hydrostatic collapse check |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility of These Formulas:**
> These equations form the **backbone of every offshore jacket design software** (SACS, SESAM, USFOS, ABAQUS-AQWA). Engineers use them to size members, design piles, and certify platforms to Classification Societies (DNV, ABS, Lloyd's) for operation in hostile environments like the North Sea, Gulf of Mexico, or Indian Ocean.

## 2.4 Types of Jacket Configurations

| Type | Description | Use Case |
|------|-------------|----------|
| **Four-Legged** | Square plan, 4 main chords | Shallow water (<150 m), moderate topsides |
| **Six-Legged** | Hexagonal plan | Larger topsides, deep water |
| **Eight-Legged** | Square plan with 4 corner + 4 skirt legs | Heavy topsides, deep water |
| **Battered vs Vertical Legs** | Legs inclined outward at base (battered) | Resist overturning moment via wider base |
| **Pile-Through vs Skirt Piles** | Piles pass through legs (pile-through) or driven externally | Pile-through simplifies connection |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Morison Equation for Wave Force on a Jacket Member

The **Morison Equation** is the most important equation in jacket design. It gives the horizontal wave force per unit length on a slender vertical cylinder.

### Statement:
For a stationary vertical cylinder of diameter $D$ subjected to a non-breaking wave with horizontal water particle velocity $u(z,t)$ and acceleration $\dot{u}(z,t)$, the total horizontal force per unit length is:

$$
f(z,t) = f_D + f_I
$$

where $f_D$ is the **drag force** and $f_I$ is the **inertia force**.

### Derivation Step-by-Step:

**Step 1 — Drag Force (from dimensional analysis + empirical $C_D$):**
Drag arises from the pressure differential on the upstream and downstream faces of the cylinder. The drag force is proportional to:
- Fluid density $\rho$
- Square of velocity $u^2$
- Projected area per unit length $D$

$$
f_D = C_D \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho D u \vert u \vert
$$

> The **absolute value** ensures the force always opposes the direction of motion. $C_D$ is the **drag coefficient** (dimensionless), determined experimentally (typically 0.6–1.2 for smooth cylinders, higher with marine growth).

**Step 2 — Inertia Force (from potential flow theory):**
For an inviscid fluid accelerating past a cylinder, the net pressure force is given by the **added mass effect**. For a circular cylinder:

$$
F_{inertia,total} = C_M \cdot \rho \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \dot{u}
$$

Divided by length to get force per unit length:

$$
f_I = C_M \cdot \rho \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \dot{u}
$$

> $C_M$ is the **inertia coefficient** (typically 1.5–2.0 for a smooth circular cylinder; equals **2.0** from potential theory for a fixed cylinder in an inviscid fluid).

**Step 3 — Combine Drag and Inertia:**
The total force per unit length is the sum:

$$
f(z,t) = C_D \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho D u \vert u \vert + C_M \cdot \rho \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \dot{u}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Force by Integration:**
To find the total horizontal force on a member from the seabed ($z = -d$) to the free surface ($z = \eta$):

$$
F(t) = \int_{-d}^{\eta} f(z,t) \, dz
$$

> The integration is usually done numerically because both $u(z,t)$ and $\dot{u}(z,t)$ vary with depth according to linear (Airy) or higher-order wave theory.

**Step 5 — Application Limits:**
The Morison equation is valid when the **member is small relative to the wavelength**:

$$
\frac{D}{\lambda} < 0.2
$$

If $D/\lambda \geq 0.2$, **diffraction theory** must be used (MacCamy-Fuchs for fixed cylinders, or full 3D diffraction for the whole platform).

**Step 6 — Airy Wave Kinematics (Substitute into Morison):**
For a small-amplitude (Airy) wave of height $H$, period $T$, water depth $d$, the horizontal velocity and acceleration at depth $z$ are:

$$
u(z,t) = \frac{\pi H}{T} \cdot \frac{\cosh(k(z+d))}{\sinh(kd)} \cdot \cos(\omega t - kx)
$$

$$
\dot{u}(z,t) = -\frac{2\pi^2 H}{T^2} \cdot \frac{\cosh(k(z+d))}{\sinh(kd)} \cdot \sin(\omega t - kx)
$$

where:
- $k = 2\pi/\lambda$ is the wave number
- $\omega = 2\pi/T$ is the angular frequency
- $\lambda$ is the wavelength, found from the **dispersion relation** $\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kd)$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** For a KTU problem, you will typically be given $H$, $T$, $d$, and $D$. Compute $k$ from the dispersion relation (iteratively or from a table), then compute $u$ and $\dot{u}$ at a few depths, evaluate $f(z,t)$ at the wave crest instant (or at $t = T/4$ for max force), and integrate numerically using Simpson's rule or trapezoidal rule.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

### Problem Statement:
A vertical tubular brace of an offshore jacket has:
- Diameter $D = 0.8$ m
- Located in water depth $d = 100$ m
- Subjected to an Airy wave of $H = 12$ m, $T = 10$ s
- $C_D = 1.0$, $C_M = 2.0$
- $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

Compute the **maximum total horizontal force** on the member, assuming the wave crest is at the member.

### Solution:

**Step 1: Compute wavelength from dispersion relation**

$$
\omega^2 = gk \tanh(kd) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \left(\frac{2\pi}{10}\right)^2 = 9.81 \cdot k \cdot \tanh(100k)
$$

This gives $k \approx 0.0677$ rad/m, so $\lambda = 2\pi/k \approx 92.8$ m.

**Step 2: Compute max velocity at the surface (z = 0)**

$$
u_{max}(0) = \frac{\pi H}{T} \cdot \frac{\cosh(kd)}{\sinh(kd)} = \frac{\pi \cdot 12}{10} \cdot \frac{\cosh(6.77)}{\sinh(6.77)}
$$

Since $kd = 6.77$ is large, $\cosh/\sinh \approx 1$. So:

$$
u_{max}(0) \approx \frac{\pi \cdot 12}{10} \cdot 1.0 = 3.77 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3: Compute max force per unit length at the surface (drag dominates at surface)**

$$
f_{D,max} = C_D \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho D u^2 = 1.0 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 0.8 \cdot (3.77)^2
$$

$$
f_{D,max} = 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 0.8 \cdot 14.21 \approx 5826 \text{ N/m}
$$

$$
f_{I,max} = C_M \cdot \rho \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \dot{u}_{max}
$$

$$
\dot{u}_{max}(0) = \frac{2\pi^2 H}{T^2} \cdot \frac{\cosh(kd)}{\sinh(kd)} = \frac{2\pi^2 \cdot 12}{100} \cdot 1.0 \approx 2.37 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

$$
f_{I,max} = 2.0 \cdot 1025 \cdot \frac{\pi (0.8)^2}{4} \cdot 2.37 = 2.0 \cdot 1025 \cdot 0.503 \cdot 2.37 \approx 2442 \text{ N/m}
$$

**Step 4: Total maximum force per unit length at surface**

$$
f_{max} = 5826 + 2442 = 8268 \text{ N/m} \approx 8.27 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**Step 5: Total force by numerical integration**
For a KTU exam, this would normally be done with Simpson's rule over 5–10 depth intervals. The integrated total force on the brace is approximately:

$$
F_{total} \approx \int_{-100}^{0} f(z) \, dz \approx 0.4 \cdot 100 \cdot 8268 \approx 3.3 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 330 \text{ kN}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why drag is highest at the surface:** Water particle velocity is maximum at the surface and decays exponentially with depth (cosh/sinh factor). So the upper portion of the brace experiences the largest forces — which is why the **upper horizontal brace** is often the most heavily loaded.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Axial Capacity of a Tubular Member (API WSD)

For a circular hollow section (CHS) tubular in axial compression:

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area**

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4}\left[D^2 - (D-2t)^2\right] = \pi t(D - t)
$$

**Step 2 — Slenderness ratio**

$$
\lambda = \frac{KL}{r}, \quad r = \sqrt{\frac{I}{A}}, \quad I = \frac{\pi}{64}\left[D^4 - (D-2t)^4\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Allowable axial stress (API RP 2A, WSD)**

If $\lambda \leq 60$:

$$
F_a = 0.6 F_y
$$

If $60 < \lambda \leq 200$:

$$
F_a = \frac{0.6 F_y}{\lambda^2 / 18000}
$$

**Step 4 — Allowable axial load**

$$
P_{allow} = F_a \cdot A
$$

> This must be **≥ the maximum factored load** on the member from the in-place analysis.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Pile Axial Capacity in Cohesionless Soil (API Sand Method)

The total axial pile capacity is the sum of **skin friction** $Q_f$ and **end bearing** $Q_p$:

$$
Q = Q_f + Q_p = \int_0^L f(z) \cdot C(z) \, dz + q \cdot A_p
$$

where $C(z) = \pi D_p$ is the pile circumference at depth $z$.

**Skin friction in sand (API method):**

$$
f(z) = K(z) \cdot p_o'(z) \cdot \tan\delta(z) \leq f_{lim}
$$

- $K$ = lateral earth pressure coefficient (0.8 for non-displacement piles; up to 1.0 for driven)
- $p_o'(z) = \gamma' z$ = effective overburden pressure
- $\delta$ = soil-pile friction angle
- $f_{lim}$ = limiting unit skin friction (API table)

**End bearing in sand (API method):**

$$
q = p_o'(L) \cdot N_q
$$

where $N_q$ is the **bearing capacity factor** from API charts (function of friction angle $\phi$).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Jacket Platform. List its main components. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**
A jacket platform is a fixed offshore steel structure used for oil/gas production. Main components:
1. **Topside (Deck)** – Carries production equipment, drilling rig, quarters.
2. **Jacket (Substructure)** – Welded tubular steel space frame that transfers loads to the seabed.
3. **Piles (Foundation)** – Long steel pipes driven through the legs into the seabed.
4. **Conductors** – Pipes that guide the wellbore from the deck down through the jacket and into the seabed.
5. **Boat landing, helideck, flare boom** – Auxiliary facilities.

---

### Question 2: Write the Morison Equation and explain each term. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
$$
f(z,t) = C_D \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho D u \vert u \vert + C_M \cdot \rho \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \dot{u}
$$

- $f(z,t)$ = horizontal wave force per unit length (N/m) at depth $z$ and time $t$
- $C_D$ = drag coefficient (dimensionless, typically 0.6–1.2)
- $C_M$ = inertia coefficient (dimensionless, typically 1.5–2.0)
- $\rho$ = density of seawater (**1025 kg/m³**)
- $D$ = member diameter (m)
- $u, \dot{u}$ = horizontal water particle velocity (m/s) and acceleration (m/s²)
- First term = **drag force** (velocity-squared, dominant for large waves)
- Second term = **inertia force** (acceleration-dependent, dominant for small waves)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Forgetting the **absolute value** on $u$. The drag force is always in the direction of flow, so the sign must follow the sign of $u$. Marks lost if you write $u^2$ without the absolute value.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A: [14 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the various types of loads acting on a fixed offshore jacket platform. Classify them and give typical values. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The loads on a jacket platform are classified as:

**1. Permanent (Dead) Loads:**
- Self-weight of jacket: typically **5,000–20,000 kN** depending on size
- Self-weight of topside: typically **10,000–50,000 kN**
- Weight of equipment, modules, conductors

**2. Variable (Live/Functional) Loads:**
- Live load on deck: **2–5 kPa** for operating areas
- Drilling hook load: **5,000–10,000 kN** during drilling
- Storage and liquid loads: variable based on tank filling

**3. Environmental Loads:**
- Wind: 0.5–3 kPa on exposed surfaces (depends on location)
- Wave: dominant load, computed using Morison or diffraction (can exceed 100,000 kN)
- Current: 0.5–2.0 m/s surface velocity superimposed on wave
- Earthquake: pseudo-static or response spectrum analysis in seismically active zones
- Ice (Arctic platforms): 100–1000 kN/m²

**4. Accidental Loads:**
- Vessel impact: 5,000–50,000 kN depending on vessel size
- Fire and explosion: 100–300 kPa overpressure
- Dropped objects: object weight × dynamic factor

> **Load combination example (API operating):**
> $L = 1.3D + 1.3L_{live} + 1.3E_{operating}$
> **Load combination example (API extreme):**
> $L = 1.1D + 1.1E_{extreme} + 0.75L_{live}$

---

**Q. (b)** With a neat sketch, describe the procedure for in-place analysis of a fixed offshore jacket. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Metocean Definition:**
Define 100-year return storm, 1-year operational, and transit conditions. Get $H_{max}$, $T_p$, current, wind.

**Step 2 — Space Frame Model:**
Create a 3D model in software (SACS, SESAM, STAAD.Pro). Each member is a tubular beam element with 6 DOF per node.

**Step 3 — Apply Loads:**
- Gravity (self-weight) on every member and node
- Wave + current using Morison equation on all submerged members
- Wind on above-water members
- Equipment/live loads on deck

**Step 4 — Wave Load Computation:**
Use Stokes 2nd order or stream function wave theory (not Airy, for high seas). Compute Morison forces on all braces and legs. Distribute forces to joints.

**Step 5 — Solve for Member Forces:**
Static analysis gives axial force, shear, and bending moment in every member, plus base reactions.

**Step 6 — Check Member Capacities:**
Verify each member satisfies API/ISO axial, bending, shear, and combined stress limits.

**Step 7 — Check Joint Capacities:**
Use API tubular joint formulas for chord plastification and punching shear.

**Step 8 — Check Pile Capacities:**
Pile axial + lateral capacity must exceed base reactions.

**Step 9 — Dynamic Check (if required):**
For deep water or soft soil, perform dynamic analysis to get natural period, mode shapes, and spectral response.

**Step 10 — Documentation:**
Compile report with load summary, member utilization, joint utilization, and pile utilization for certification.

---

### Question B: [14 Marks] **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the API method of pile capacity computation in cohesionless (sand) and cohesive (clay) soils. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Pile Capacity in Sand (Cohesionless Soil — API RP 2A):**

Total axial capacity:
$$
Q = Q_f + Q_p = f_s \cdot A_s + q \cdot A_p
$$

**Skin friction (unit):**
$$
f_s = K \cdot p_o' \cdot \tan\delta \leq f_{lim}
$$
- $K$ = 0.8 (non-displacement) to 1.0 (displacement/driven)
- $p_o' = \gamma' z$ = effective overburden at depth $z$
- $\delta$ = soil-pile friction angle (typically $\phi - 5°$ for steel piles)
- $f_{lim}$ from API tables (e.g., 67 kPa for loose sand, 115 kPa for dense sand)

**End bearing (unit):**
$$
q = p_o'(L) \cdot N_q
$$
- $N_q$ from API chart as a function of $\phi$
- $N_q$ ranges from 12 ($\phi = 30°$) to 200+ ($\phi = 45°$)

**Pile Capacity in Clay (Cohesive Soil — API α-Method):**

$$
Q = Q_f + Q_p = (\alpha \cdot c) \cdot A_s + (9c) \cdot A_p
$$

**Skin friction (unit):**
$$
f_s = \alpha \cdot c
$$
- $c$ = undrained shear strength at depth $z$
- $\alpha$ = adhesion factor (≤ 1.0), decreases with $c$ from API chart

**End bearing (unit):**
$$
q = 9 c_u
$$
(For piles penetrating into clay; the 9 comes from $N_c = 9$ for deep foundations.)

---

**Q. (b)** A vertical tubular member of diameter 1.0 m, thickness 0.04 m, and length 30 m is fixed at both ends in a jacket frame. Compute its axial compression capacity using API WSD. Take $F_y = 250$ MPa and $E = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional properties**

$$
A = \pi t (D - t) = \pi \cdot 0.04 \cdot (1.0 - 0.04) = \pi \cdot 0.04 \cdot 0.96 = 0.1206 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
I = \frac{\pi}{64}\left[D^4 - (D-2t)^4\right] = \frac{\pi}{64}\left[1.0^4 - 0.92^4\right] = \frac{\pi}{64}\left[1.0 - 0.7164\right] = 0.01393 \text{ m}^4
$$

$$
r = \sqrt{I/A} = \sqrt{0.01393/0.1206} = \sqrt{0.1155} = 0.340 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Effective length**

For fixed-fixed, $K = 0.65$ (or conservatively use $K = 0.8$ per API). Using $K = 0.65$:

$$
KL = 0.65 \cdot 30 = 19.5 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Slenderness ratio**

$$
\lambda = \frac{KL}{r} = \frac{19.5}{0.340} = 57.4
$$

**Step 4 — Allowable stress (API RP 2A)**

Since $\lambda = 57.4 < 60$, the member is in the **compact (inelastic) range**:

$$
F_a = 0.6 F_y = 0.6 \cdot 250 = 150 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Axial capacity**

$$
P_{allow} = F_a \cdot A = 150 \times 10^6 \cdot 0.1206 = 1.809 \times 10^7 \text{ N} \approx 18.1 \text{ MN}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $P_{allow} \approx 18.1$ MN

**Step 6 — Hydrostatic collapse check**
Outer diameter ratio: $D/t = 1.0/0.04 = 25$. Since $D/t < 60$ (elastic) and $< 30$ (no reduction), no hydrostatic collapse reduction needed. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall on Slenderness:**
> 1. Using $K = 1.0$ when the member is fixed-fixed. The effective length factor depends on **end conditions**: pin-pin $K=1.0$, fixed-fixed $K=0.65$, fixed-pin $K=0.80$, cantilever $K=2.1$.
> 2. Forgetting to check the **slenderness transition at $\lambda = 60$** and using the wrong formula. Marks lost!
> 3. Not checking **hydrostatic collapse** for deep-water braces. Marks lost on this!

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks on Jacket Design (KTU 2024):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in numerical answers.** Always write kN, MN, MPa, m, s with the answer. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 2. **Wrong sign in Morison drag term.** Always include $\vert u \vert$ (absolute value). Writing $u^2$ is technically wrong for direction reversal.
> 3. **Mixing up WSD and LRFD factors.** API WSD uses **0.6 F_y** allowable; LRFD uses $\phi F_y$ with $\phi = 0.9$. The exam will specify which.
> 4. **Skipping the wave kinematics step.** You must show how you got $u$ and $\dot{u}$ — not just quote "wave force = 100 kN."
> 5. **Ignoring the slenderness check for braces.** Even if axial force is small, a long slender brace can fail in Euler buckling.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Jacket Design (Module 3):**

- [x] **Jacket = tubular space frame** with chords, braces, and piles; 4/6/8 legs; battered or vertical.
- [x] **Morison Equation** is the cornerstone: $f = C_D \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho D u\vert u\vert + C_M \cdot \rho \cdot \tfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \dot{u}$
- [x] **Morison valid only for $D/\lambda < 0.2$**; use diffraction otherwise.
- [x] **Air gap $\geq 1.2 H_{max}$** to prevent waves striking the deck.
- [x] **Slenderness** check: $D/t \leq 30$ (no reduction), $30 < D/t \leq 60$ (reduced allowable), $D/t > 60$ (no good for hydrostatic).
- [x] **API WSD compression:** $F_a = 0.6 F_y$ for $\lambda \leq 60$; reduce for $\lambda > 60$.
- [x] **Pile capacity = skin friction + end bearing** in both sand and clay.
- [x] **API sand:** $f = K p_o' \tan\delta$, $q = p_o' N_q$.
- [x] **API clay:** $f = \alpha c$, $q = 9 c$.
- [x] **Tubular joints** fail by chord plastification, punching shear, or fatigue.
- [x] **Fatigue** is critical for jacket joints — millions of wave cycles per year.
- [x] **Standard codes:** API RP 2A, ISO 19902, DNV-OS-C101.
- [x] **Dynamic check** required for soft soil or deep water.
- [x] **Installation analysis** (loadout, lift, upending) is mandatory before fabrication.
- [x] **Material:** Marine-grade steel (A36, A572, A690) with yield strength ~250–350 MPa.
- [x] **Corrosion allowance** of 3–6 mm typically added to wall thickness.
- [x] **Cathodic protection** with sacrificial anodes (Al or Zn) in the submerged zone.
- [x] **Wave kinematics:** Use Stokes 2nd/3rd order for moderate to deep water; stream function for high seas.
- [x] **Base shear and OTM** are critical for pile foundation sizing.
- [x] **Effective length factor K** depends on end conditions: pin-pin=1.0, fixed-fixed=0.65, fixed-pin=0.8, cantilever=2.1.
- [x] **Wave return period:** Operational = 1 yr, Extreme = 100 yr, Survival = 10,000 yr (for some regions).

> **Best of luck for your KTU exams! Master the Morison equation, the API slenderness formulas, and the pile capacity derivations — these are the three most-tested topics in Module 3.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pile Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST722 - FUNDAMENTALS OF OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pile Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pile Design** in offshore structural engineering refers to the systematic analytical and geotechnical process of dimensioning, proportioning, and detailing long, slender structural members (called **piles** or **foundation piles**) that transfer superstructure loads—axial, lateral, and moment—through weak overlying seabed soils to competent deeper bearing strata. For fixed offshore platforms (jackets, jack-ups, and gravity-base hybrids), pile design is governed primarily by the American Petroleum Institute Recommended Practice **API RP 2A-WSD / API RP 2GEO** along with ISO 19901-4 and DNV-OS-J101 codes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Pile design is the determination of pile length, diameter, wall thickness, penetration depth, and material grade required to safely resist combined axial (tensile and compressive), lateral, and moment loads imposed by the offshore superstructure, while satisfying serviceability, drivability, and fatigue limit states."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Underground Anchor" Intuition

Imagine trying to keep a tall flagpole standing on a beach during a strong wind. A short stick stuck into the sand will easily topple. But if you attach a long pole that goes **deep into the ground**, the surrounding sand grips it along its entire length (called **skin friction**), and the tip rests on a firmer layer (called **end bearing**). The wind pushes the flagpole sideways—now the buried portion resists bending because the soil all around pushes back.

An **offshore pile** does exactly this for a multi-million-tonne oil platform in the open ocean. The seabed soil is the "sand," the pile is the "long pole," the platform is the "flagpole," and waves, wind, and currents take the place of the wind. The deeper the pile goes and the larger its diameter, the more the soil can "hug" it and the more wind/wave load it can resist.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Offshore Value |
|---|---|
| **Pile Diameter (D)** | **0.6 m to 3.0 m** (24 in to 120 in) |
| **Penetration Depth (L)** | **30 m to 130 m** below seabed |
| **Wall Thickness (t)** | **25 mm to 75 mm** |
| **Yield Strength (f_y)** | **350 MPa to 500 MPa** (API 5L X52 to X80) |
| **Submerged Unit Weight of Soil (γ')** | **8 kN/m³ to 11 kN/m³** |
| **Sea Water Unit Weight (γ_w)** | **10.05 kN/m³** |
| **Design Wave Load Return Period** | **100-year storm** |

> [!NOTE]
> Offshore piles are predominantly **open-ended steel tubular pipes** that are driven into the seabed using hydraulic or steam hammers. Some large-diameter piles (D > 2 m) may be drilled and grouted, called **drilled-and-grouted piles**.

## 1.4 Classification of Offshore Piles

1. **By Function:**
   - **Working Piles**: Carry operational (in-service) platform loads.
   - **Skirt Piles**: Short piles used to anchor jack-up spudcans or suction caissons.
   - **Mooring Piles**: Resist tension from catenary or taut-leg mooring systems.

2. **By Installation Method:**
   - **Driven Piles** (most common, hammer-driven).
   - **Drilled and Grouted Piles**.
   - **Suction Piles / Caissons** (used for floating platforms).

3. **By Loading Direction:**
   - **Axially Loaded Piles** (compression/tension).
   - **Laterally Loaded Piles** (with bending moment).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pile-Soil Interaction: The Fundamental Mechanism

A pile interacts with the surrounding soil in three primary modes:

1. **Axial Compression**: Resistance = Skin Friction (along shaft) + End Bearing (at tip).
2. **Axial Tension (Uplift)**: Resistance = Skin Friction ONLY (no tip resistance in tension).
3. **Lateral Loading**: Resistance = Soil Reaction (governed by p-y curves from API RP 2A).

> [!NOTE]
> For **cyclic loading** (waves, which dominate offshore design), the soil's effective stress reduces progressively, lowering capacity. API accounts for this via **cyclic degradation factors**.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Design Philosophy (Load & Resistance Factor Design, LRFD)

Step 1 — **Establish Design Loads** from in-place analysis (metocean → structural model → pile head loads: Axial P, Horizontal H, Moment M).

Step 2 — **Determine Soil Profile** from geotechnical site investigation (CPT, borehole, lab tests). Stratify into layers and obtain $\phi$, $c$, $\gamma'$, $S_u$ (undrained shear strength).

Step 3 — **Compute Axial Capacity** using API RP 2A (effective stress method for sands, $\alpha$-method for clays).

Step 4 — **Compute Lateral Capacity** using p-y curve analysis (Reese, Matlock, or API sand/clay formulations).

Step 5 — **Conduct Driveability Analysis** to confirm pile can be installed with available hammer.

Step 6 — **Check Serviceability**: deflection, rotation, scour, fatigue.

Step 7 — **Iterate on pile length and diameter** until all limit states are satisfied with adequate margin.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-critical** for KTU Module 3 questions on Pile Design.

### 2.3.1 Axial Compressive Capacity — API RP 2A Method

For **cohesionless soils (sands)**, the total compressive capacity is:

$$
Q_c = Q_f + Q_p = f_s \cdot A_s + q_p \cdot A_p
$$

where the **unit skin friction** and **unit end bearing** are:

$$
f_s = K \cdot \sigma_{v0}' \cdot \tan(\delta) \le f_{s,\max}
$$

$$
q_p = N_q \cdot \sigma_{v0}'
$$

### 2.3.2 Skin Friction Limit for Sand (API)

$$
f_{s,\max} = \sigma_{v0}' \cdot \beta \quad \text{with} \quad \beta = K \tan(\delta)
$$

For displacement piles in silica sand (API):

$$
f_{s,\max} = \text{min}\!\left(0.5 \cdot \sigma_{v0}', \, 120 \text{ kPa}\right) \quad \text{(in SI, } 0.5\sigma_{v0}' \text{ for high OCR)}
$$

### 2.3.3 End Bearing in Sand (API)

$$
q_p = N_q \cdot \sigma_{v0}'
$$

with $N_q$ depending on friction angle $\phi'$:

| $\phi'$ (deg) | 20 | 25 | 28 | 30 | 32 | 35 | 40 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $N_q$ | 12.4 | 25 | 40 | 55 | 70 | 100 | 200 |

### 2.3.4 Axial Capacity in Clay — $\alpha$-Method (Total Stress)

$$
f_s = \alpha \cdot S_u
$$

$$
Q_p = 9 \cdot S_u \cdot A_p
$$

where $\alpha$ (adhesion factor) is the **API alpha curve**:

$$
\alpha = 
\begin{cases}
0.5 \cdot \psi^{-0.5} & \text{for } \psi \le 1.0 \\
0.5 \cdot \psi^{-0.25} & \text{for } \psi > 1.0
\end{cases}
$$

with $\psi = S_u / \sigma_{v0}'$.

### 2.3.5 Tension (Uplift) Capacity

$$
Q_t = f_s \cdot A_s \quad \text{(tip contribution neglected)}
$$

A **buoyancy correction** is required offshore:

$$
Q_{t,\text{net}} = Q_t - W_p
$$

where $W_p$ = submerged weight of the pile.

### 2.3.6 Lateral Capacity — p-y Curve Concept

Lateral soil reaction at depth $z$ is given by a non-linear spring:

$$
p = p(y, z)
$$

where $y$ is lateral deflection. For **static API sand**:

$$
p = A \cdot p_u \cdot \tanh\!\left(\frac{k \cdot H \cdot z}{A \cdot p_u} \cdot y\right)
$$

with $A = \max\!\left(0.9, \, 3 - 0.8 \frac{z}{D}, \, 0.9\right)$ (shape factor, varies with $z/D$).

The **ultimate lateral resistance** $p_u$ for sand at shallow depth:

$$
p_{u,shallow} = (\gamma' \cdot z) \cdot \left[\frac{K_0 \cdot z \tan\phi' \sin\beta}{\tan(\beta - \phi') \cos\alpha} + \frac{\tan\beta}{\tan(\beta - \phi')}(D + z\tan\beta\tan\alpha) + K_0 \cdot z \tan\beta(\tan\phi' \sin\beta - \tan\alpha)\right]
$$

For **clay** (Matlock soft clay):

$$
p_u = 
\begin{cases}
3 S_u + \gamma' z + J \frac{S_u \cdot z}{D} & \text{for } 0 \le z < z_R \\
9 S_u \cdot D & \text{for } z \ge z_R
\end{cases}
$$

with $z_R = 6 D / (J + \gamma' D / S_u)$ and $J$ = empirical constant (0.25 to 0.5).

### 2.3.7 Combined Axial + Lateral Interaction

The **unit skin friction** is reduced under combined loading using a reduction factor (Briaud model):

$$
f_{s,\text{combined}} = R_f \cdot f_{s,\text{axial}}
$$

where $R_f$ depends on the cyclic lateral displacement amplitude. For small deflections, $R_f \approx 1.0$; for large deflections, $R_f$ can drop to **0.5 to 0.7**.

### 2.3.8 Minimum Penetration Depth Rule of Thumb

> [!NOTE]
> For jacket piles in sands, API suggests **minimum penetration = 30 m** or **6 × D**, whichever is greater, below the seabed to develop adequate lateral fixity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fixed Offshore Platforms (Jacket, Tripod, Compliant Tower)**: Driven steel pipe piles, typically 1.5 m to 2.5 m diameter.
- **Jack-Up Rigs**: Spudcans (not piles) but use skirt piles for preloading.
- **FPSO / SPAR Mooring**: Suction piles or driven anchor piles.
- **Offshore Wind Turbines (Monopiles)**: 5 m to 10 m diameter piles driven 30 m to 50 m deep, designed primarily for **lateral loading** from wind/wave thrust.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Axial Compressive Capacity in Sand (Effective Stress Method)

The unit skin friction on a pile shaft in sand is derived from the Mohr-Coulomb failure criterion. The normal effective stress acting on the pile shaft at depth $z$ is:

$$
\sigma_h' = K \cdot \sigma_{v0}'
$$

The shear stress mobilized at the pile-soil interface is:

$$
f_s = \sigma_h' \cdot \tan(\delta)
$$

Substituting $\sigma_h'$:

$$
f_s = K \cdot \sigma_{v0}' \cdot \tan(\delta)
$$

The total **shaft resistance** over penetration depth $L$ and shaft surface area $A_s = \pi D L$:

$$
Q_f = \int_0^L f_s(z) \cdot \pi D \, dz
$$

Assuming $K$ and $\delta$ constant with depth and $\sigma_{v0}' = \gamma' z$ (linearly increasing):

$$
Q_f = \int_0^L K \cdot \gamma' z \cdot \tan(\delta) \cdot \pi D \, dz
$$

$$
Q_f = K \cdot \tan(\delta) \cdot \pi D \cdot \gamma' \cdot \frac{L^2}{2}
$$

$$
Q_f = \frac{1}{2} \pi D L^2 \gamma' K \tan(\delta)
$$

For the **end bearing**, the bearing capacity theory (analogous to a deep foundation) gives:

$$
q_p = N_q \cdot \sigma_{v0}'(L) = N_q \cdot \gamma' L
$$

$$
Q_p = q_p \cdot A_p = N_q \cdot \gamma' L \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

Therefore the **total compressive capacity**:

$$
\boxed{Q_c = \frac{1}{2} \pi D L^2 \gamma' K \tan(\delta) + \frac{\pi D^2}{4} N_q \gamma' L}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Reduction Factor for Combined Axial-Lateral Loading (Briaud Model)

Define a dimensionless lateral displacement parameter $\zeta$:

$$
\zeta = \frac{y_0}{D}
$$

where $y_0$ is the lateral deflection amplitude under cyclic loading.

The reduction factor $R_f$ for skin friction is given by an empirical exponential decay:

$$
R_f = 1 - 0.20 \log_{10}(N) \cdot \left(1 - e^{-3\zeta}\right)
$$

where $N$ = number of load cycles.

**Worked Example:** For $N = 100$ and $\zeta = 0.10$:

$$
R_f = 1 - 0.20 \cdot \log_{10}(100) \cdot (1 - e^{-0.30})
$$

$$
R_f = 1 - 0.20 \cdot 2 \cdot (1 - 0.741)
$$

$$
R_f = 1 - 0.40 \cdot 0.259 = 1 - 0.1036 = 0.896
$$

So skin friction is reduced by ~10% under cyclic lateral loading.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Exam Standard)

> **Problem:** A steel pipe pile of diameter $D = 1.5$ m is driven **25 m** into a sand deposit with $\gamma' = 9$ kN/m³, $\phi' = 32°$, $\delta = 24°$, $K = 1.0$. Compute (a) unit skin friction at mid-depth, (b) total shaft resistance, (c) total end bearing, (d) total compressive capacity. Use $N_q = 70$ for $\phi' = 32°$.

### (a) Unit Skin Friction at Mid-Depth (z = 12.5 m)

$$
\sigma_{v0}'(z) = \gamma' z = 9 \times 12.5 = 112.5 \text{ kPa}
$$

$$
f_s = K \cdot \sigma_{v0}' \cdot \tan(\delta) = 1.0 \times 112.5 \times \tan(24°)
$$

$$
f_s = 112.5 \times 0.4452 = 50.09 \text{ kPa}
$$

### (b) Total Shaft Resistance

$$
Q_f = \frac{1}{2} \pi D L^2 \gamma' K \tan(\delta)
$$

$$
Q_f = 0.5 \times \pi \times 1.5 \times 25^2 \times 9 \times 1.0 \times \tan(24°)
$$

$$
Q_f = 0.5 \times \pi \times 1.5 \times 625 \times 9 \times 0.4452
$$

$$
Q_f = 0.5 \times 3.1416 \times 1.5 \times 625 \times 9 \times 0.4452
$$

Step-by-step multiplication:

$$
0.5 \times 3.1416 = 1.5708
$$

$$
1.5708 \times 1.5 = 2.3562
$$

$$
2.3562 \times 625 = 1472.625
$$

$$
1472.625 \times 9 = 13253.625
$$

$$
13253.625 \times 0.4452 = 5901.31 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_f \approx 5901 \text{ kN}}
$$

### (c) Total End Bearing

$$
\sigma_{v0}'(L) = 9 \times 25 = 225 \text{ kPa}
$$

$$
q_p = N_q \cdot \sigma_{v0}' = 70 \times 225 = 15750 \text{ kPa}
$$

$$
A_p = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 1.5^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 2.25}{4} = 1.7671 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
Q_p = q_p \cdot A_p = 15750 \times 1.7671 = 27832.4 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_p \approx 27832 \text{ kN}}
$$

### (d) Total Compressive Capacity

$$
Q_c = Q_f + Q_p = 5901 + 27832 = 33733 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_c \approx 33.73 \text{ MN}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, **factor of safety** of 1.5 to 2.0 is applied (or LRFD resistance factors $\phi = 0.7$ to $0.8$ per API).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Pile Capacity in Sand vs Clay

| Parameter | Sand (Cohesionless) | Clay (Cohesive) |
|---|---|---|
| Governing Strength | Friction angle $\phi'$ | Undrained shear $S_u$ |
| Design Method | Effective stress (API $\beta$-method) | Total stress ($\alpha$-method) |
| Shaft Resistance $f_s$ | $K \sigma_{v0}' \tan\delta$ | $\alpha S_u$ |
| Tip Resistance $q_p$ | $N_q \sigma_{v0}'(L)$ | $9 S_u$ |
| Cyclic Sensitivity | Moderate (degrades by ~10-20%) | High (degrades by ~30-50%) |
| Drainage Condition | Drained (long term) | Undrained (short term) |
| Critical Issue | Liquefaction under cyclic | Pore pressure buildup |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Pile Design Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Pile Design Problem] --> B[Step 1: Collect Metocean and Soil Data]
    B --> C[Step 2: Compute Pile Head Loads - P, H, M]
    C --> D[Step 3: Select Trial Pile Geometry - D, t, L]
    D --> E[Step 4: Compute Axial Capacity Qc and Qt]
    E --> F[Step 5: Compute Lateral Capacity via p-y Curves]
    F --> G[Step 6: Check Combined Loading Interaction]
    G --> H{All Limit States Satisfied?}
    H -- No --> I[Revise D, t, or L]
    I --> D
    H -- Yes --> J[Step 7: Driveability and Hammer Check]
    J --> K[Step 8: Fatigue and Scour Check]
    K --> L[Step 9: Final Pile Specification]
    L --> M[End: Issue Construction Drawings]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Pile-Soil Interaction Modes

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Superstructure
        S1[Platform Jacket Deck]
    end
    subgraph PileHead
        H1[Pile Head - Fixed Connection]
    end
    subgraph PileBody
        P1[Steel Pipe Pile]
        P2[Soil Surrounding Pile]
    end
    subgraph PileTip
        T1[Pile Tip / Closed End]
    end
    subgraph Seabed
        SB1[Sand Layer]
        SB2[Clay Layer]
        SB3[Bearing Stratum]
    end
    S1 --> H1
    H1 --> P1
    P1 -- Skin Friction --> P2
    P2 --> SB1
    P2 --> SB2
    P1 -- End Bearing --> T1
    T1 --> SB3
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Lateral p-y Curve System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Applied Lateral Load H at Pile Head] --> L1[Pile Segment 1 - Top 0 to 5D]
    L1 --> L2[Pile Segment 2 - 5D to 10D]
    L2 --> L3[Pile Segment 3 - 10D to 20D]
    L3 --> L4[Pile Segment 4 - Below 20D]
    L1 -- p1 --> L1a[Soil Spring p1 with p-y Curve 1]
    L2 -- p2 --> L2a[Soil Spring p2 with p-y Curve 2]
    L3 -- p3 --> L3a[Soil Spring p3 with p-y Curve 3]
    L4 -- p4 --> L4a[Soil Spring p4 with p-y Curve 4]
    L1a --> L1
    L2a --> L2
    L3a --> L3
    L4a --> L4
    L4 --> L5[Pile Tip - Fixity Achieved]
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Typical Offshore Jacket Pile Assembly (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph AboveWater
        AW1[Topside Facilities]
        AW2[Helideck and Derrick]
    end
    subgraph SplashZone
        SZ1[Jacket Bracing]
        SZ2[Boat Landing]
    end
    subgraph BelowWater
        BW1[Conductor Pipes]
        BW2[Horizontal Braces]
        BW3[Diagonal Braces]
    end
    subgraph SeabedRegion
        SD1[Pile Sleeves Welded to Jacket Legs]
        SD2[Grouted Connection]
        SD3[Steel Pipe Pile]
        SD4[Soil Layers - Clay over Sand]
        SD5[Pile Tip - Bearing Stratum]
    end
    AW1 --> SZ1
    AW2 --> SZ1
    SZ1 --> BW1
    SZ1 --> BW2
    SZ1 --> BW3
    BW3 --> SD1
    SD1 --> SD2
    SD2 --> SD3
    SD3 --> SD4
    SD3 --> SD5
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the term "Skin Friction" in the context of offshore pile design. How is it computed for sands using the API method? [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Skin friction is the shear stress developed along the lateral surface of a pile due to relative movement between the pile and the surrounding soil. It contributes to the axial load-carrying capacity of the pile.

> **API Formula for Sand:**
> $$f_s = K \cdot \sigma_{v0}' \cdot \tan(\delta)$$
> where $K$ is the lateral earth pressure coefficient, $\sigma_{v0}'$ is the effective vertical overburden stress, and $\delta$ is the pile-soil interface friction angle.

> For driven piles in silica sand, API caps $f_s$ at:
> $$f_{s,\max} = \min(0.5 \sigma_{v0}', \, 120 \text{ kPa})$$

### Question 2: What is a "p-y curve" and why is it used in offshore pile design? [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** A p-y curve is a non-linear relationship between the soil reaction per unit length ($p$, kN/m) and the lateral deflection ($y$, m) of a pile at a given depth $z$.

> **Why Used:** Offshore piles are subjected to large lateral loads from waves, currents, and wind. p-y curves capture the **non-linear soil-pile interaction** that simple subgrade-reaction (Winkler) springs cannot. They account for soil type (sand/clay), cyclic loading degradation, and depth-dependent soil behavior. They form the basis of **API RP 2A lateral pile analysis** and are essential for jacket pile design.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

#### (a) Explain the API RP 2A method for computing the axial compressive capacity of a pile in (i) sand and (ii) clay. State all relevant formulas with a clear figure. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction:** The API RP 2A method is the industry-standard for offshore pile design. The axial compressive capacity is given by:
$$Q_c = Q_f + Q_p$$
where $Q_f$ = shaft (skin friction) resistance and $Q_p$ = end bearing.

**(i) Pile in Sand (Effective Stress / $\beta$-Method):**

The unit skin friction is:
$$f_s = K \cdot \sigma_{v0}' \cdot \tan(\delta) = \beta \cdot \sigma_{v0}'$$

API caps this at $f_{s,\max} = 120$ kPa (or $0.5\sigma_{v0}'$ for high OCR). Total shaft resistance:
$$Q_f = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_{s,i} \cdot A_{s,i} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_{s,i} \cdot \pi D \cdot \Delta L_i$$

End bearing:
$$q_p = N_q \cdot \sigma_{v0}'(L), \quad Q_p = q_p \cdot A_p = N_q \cdot \sigma_{v0}'(L) \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**(ii) Pile in Clay ($\alpha$-Method, Total Stress):**

Unit skin friction:
$$f_s = \alpha \cdot S_u$$

where $\alpha$ is the **API adhesion factor** from the alpha curve:
$$\alpha = 0.5 \psi^{-0.5} \text{ for } \psi \le 1.0, \quad \alpha = 0.5 \psi^{-0.25} \text{ for } \psi > 1.0$$
with $\psi = S_u / \sigma_{v0}'$.

End bearing:
$$Q_p = 9 S_u \cdot A_p$$

#### (b) A steel pipe pile of diameter 1.2 m is driven 30 m into a soft clay with constant $S_u = 50$ kPa, $\gamma' = 7$ kN/m³. Compute (i) average unit skin friction, (ii) total shaft resistance, and (iii) total end bearing. Use API $\alpha$-method. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Average Effective Stress at Mid-Depth (z = 15 m):**
$$\sigma_{v0}' = \gamma' z = 7 \times 15 = 105 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\psi$ at Mid-Depth:**
$$\psi = \frac{S_u}{\sigma_{v0}'} = \frac{50}{105} = 0.476$$

Since $\psi < 1.0$, use $\alpha = 0.5 \psi^{-0.5}$:
$$\alpha = 0.5 \times (0.476)^{-0.5} = 0.5 \times 1.4496 = 0.7248$$

**Step 3 — Unit Skin Friction:**
$$f_s = \alpha \cdot S_u = 0.7248 \times 50 = 36.24 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 4 — Total Shaft Resistance:**
$$A_s = \pi D L = \pi \times 1.2 \times 30 = 113.10 \text{ m}^2$$
$$Q_f = f_s \cdot A_s = 36.24 \times 113.10 = 4099.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — End Bearing:**
$$A_p = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 1.44}{4} = 1.131 \text{ m}^2$$
$$Q_p = 9 S_u \cdot A_p = 9 \times 50 \times 1.131 = 508.9 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Total Compressive Capacity:**
$$Q_c = Q_f + Q_p = 4099.5 + 508.9 = 4608.4 \text{ kN} \approx 4.61 \text{ MN}$$

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

#### (a) Describe the p-y curve concept for lateral pile design. How does the API RP 2A formulation differ for sand versus clay? State the formulas for ultimate lateral resistance $p_u$. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Concept:** A p-y curve describes the non-linear relationship between soil reaction $p$ and pile deflection $y$ at a given depth $z$. They are used in **Winkler-type** analysis where the soil is modeled as a series of independent non-linear springs along the pile.

**Construction Steps:**
1. Divide pile into segments of length $\Delta L = 0.5D$ to $1.0D$.
2. At each depth, compute $p_u$ (ultimate resistance).
3. Compute initial subgrade modulus $k$.
4. Build $p$ vs $y$ curve using the API non-linear equation.

**For Sand (API):**

The general form of the p-y curve:
$$p = A \cdot p_u \cdot \tanh\!\left(\frac{k \cdot H \cdot z}{A \cdot p_u} \cdot y\right)$$

where $A$ is a depth-dependent shape factor:
$$A = \max\!\left(0.9, \, 3 - 0.8 \frac{z}{D}, \, 0.9\right)$$

The ultimate resistance at shallow depth:
$$p_{u,shallow} = \gamma' z \left[ \frac{K_0 z \tan\phi' \sin\beta}{\tan(\beta - \phi') \cos\alpha} + \frac{\tan\beta}{\tan(\beta - \phi')} (D + z\tan\beta\tan\alpha) + K_0 z \tan\beta(\tan\phi' \sin\beta - \tan\alpha) \right]$$

At deep depth ($z \ge z_{cr}$): $p_{u,deep} = A \cdot N_q \cdot \sigma_{v0}'(z)$ (sand).

**For Clay (Matlock Soft Clay, Static):**

For $0 \le z < z_R$:
$$p_u = 3 S_u + \gamma' z + J \frac{S_u z}{D}$$

For $z \ge z_R$:
$$p_u = 9 S_u D$$

with $z_R = 6D / (J + \gamma' D / S_u)$ and $J = 0.25$ to $0.5$.

**Differences:**
- Sand p-y curves depend on $\phi'$ and $\sigma_{v0}'$ (effective stress).
- Clay p-y curves depend on $S_u$ (total stress).
- Sand exhibits greater depth dependency.
- Clay is more sensitive to cyclic degradation.

#### (b) For a soft clay with $S_u = 40$ kPa, $\gamma' = 6$ kN/m³, pile diameter $D = 1.0$ m, and $J = 0.5$, compute the ultimate lateral resistance $p_u$ at depths $z = 1$ m, $z = 3$ m, and $z = 10$ m using the Matlock soft clay model. Comment on the trend. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $z_R$:**
$$z_R = \frac{6D}{J + \gamma' D / S_u} = \frac{6 \times 1.0}{0.5 + (6 \times 1.0) / 40}$$

Denominator: $0.5 + 0.15 = 0.65$

$$z_R = \frac{6}{0.65} = 9.23 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — At $z = 1$ m (shallow, $z < z_R$):**

$$p_u = 3 S_u + \gamma' z + J \frac{S_u z}{D}$$

$$
p_u = 3(40) + 6(1) + 0.5 \cdot \frac{40 \times 1}{1.0}
$$

$$
p_u = 120 + 6 + 20 = 146 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**Step 3 — At $z = 3$ m (still shallow, $z < z_R$):**

$$
p_u = 120 + 6(3) + 0.5 \cdot \frac{40 \times 3}{1.0}
$$

$$
p_u = 120 + 18 + 60 = 198 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**Step 4 — At $z = 10$ m (deep, $z > z_R$):**

$$
p_u = 9 S_u D = 9 \times 40 \times 1.0 = 360 \text{ kN/m}
$$

**Comment:** The ultimate lateral resistance $p_u$ increases with depth. It grows linearly in the shallow zone ($z < z_R$) due to the $\gamma' z$ term and $S_u z / D$ term. Beyond the transition depth $z_R = 9.23$ m, $p_u$ becomes constant at $9 S_u D$ because the failure mechanism transitions from a **wedge-type shallow failure** to a **flow-around (deep) failure** where the soil flows around the pile like a cylindrical flow.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> - Do **NOT** forget the factor $9$ in $Q_p = 9 S_u A_p$ for clay — many students write $Q_p = S_u A_p$, losing 1-2 marks.
> - Do **NOT** use **total** stress in the sand formula — always use **effective** stress $\sigma_{v0}'$ (the prime symbol is critical).
> - For tension piles, always subtract the **buoyancy-adjusted weight** of the pile $W_p = (\gamma_s - \gamma_w) \cdot V_p$.
> - In cyclic loading, apply the **cyclic degradation factor** (typically 0.5 to 0.8) to skin friction, or expect 2-3 marks deduction.
> - When computing $\alpha$, check whether $\psi \le 1.0$ or $\psi > 1.0$ — using the wrong exponent loses 2 marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Pile Design:**

- **Pile = Structural member transferring loads from superstructure to deep bearing strata** through skin friction + end bearing.

- **Open-ended steel tubular piles** are the offshore standard (D = 0.6 m to 3.0 m, t = 25 to 75 mm, L = 30 to 130 m).

- **API RP 2A** is the governing code; ISO 19901-4 and DNV-OS-J101 are alternatives.

- **Axial Compression Capacity**: $Q_c = Q_f + Q_p$.

- **Shaft Friction in Sand**: $f_s = K \sigma_{v0}' \tan\delta \le 120$ kPa.

- **End Bearing in Sand**: $q_p = N_q \sigma_{v0}'$ (at tip).

- **Shaft Friction in Clay**: $f_s = \alpha S_u$ (alpha-method).

- **End Bearing in Clay**: $Q_p = 9 S_u A_p$ (note the factor 9).

- **Tension (Uplift) Capacity**: $Q_t = f_s A_s$ (no tip), minus pile buoyancy weight.

- **p-y Curves** are used for **lateral analysis**; Matlock (clay) and Reese (sand) are standard.

- **Combined Loading**: Cyclic lateral loading reduces skin friction by 10-30% via $R_f$ factor.

- **Design Steps**: Loads → Soil → Capacity → Driveability → Serviceability → Final Spec.

- **Typical FoS**: 1.5 to 2.0 (WSD) or LRFD resistance factors 0.7-0.9.

- **Key Empirical Constants to Memorize**: $N_q$ (sand) = 12.4, 25, 40, 55, 70, 100, 200 for $\phi' = 20°, 25°, 28°, 30°, 32°, 35°, 40°$ respectively.

- **Minimum penetration** rule: $L_{min} \ge \max(30 \text{ m}, 6D)$ for jacket piles.

- **Driveability** is a critical check — ensure available hammer can install the pile without overstress.

- **Scour** must be accounted for — typically 1.5D to 2D additional reduction in lateral capacity.

- **Cyclic degradation** of soil capacity is unique to offshore design — land-based codes (IS 2911) do not emphasize it.

- **Failure Modes to Check**: (1) Axial compression/tension, (2) Lateral + moment, (3) Combined interaction, (4) Buckling, (5) Fatigue (cyclic), (6) Driveability damage, (7) Corrosion, (8) Scour.

- **Modulus of Subgrade Reaction** $k$ is the initial slope of p-y curve — depends on soil and depth.

- **KTU 2024 Exam Tip**: 14-mark Part B questions almost always pair a **theory sub-part (7 marks)** with a **numerical sub-part (7 marks)** on pile capacity. Master both the formulas and the API alpha curve for maximum marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deck Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST722 - FUNDAMENTALS OF OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Deck Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deck (Topside):** A deck is a horizontal structural assembly, typically fabricated from stiffened steel plate panels, which is supported by the jacket (substructure) via deck legs or conductor pipes. The deck provides the working surface for all production, drilling, utilities, and accommodation facilities on a fixed offshore platform.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deck Design:** The engineered process of proportioning, detailing, and analysing the topside structural framing — including primary girders, secondary beams, plating, and stiffeners — such that it can safely resist all **in-service, fabrication, transportation, lift, and operational loads** while satisfying serviceability, fatigue, and code-compliance requirements (API RP 2A, AISC, DNV, ISO 19902).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Factory Floor" of the Platform

Imagine a high-rise apartment block built on stilts in the middle of the sea. The stilts are the **jacket**, and the building itself is the **deck**. Just like a multi-storey building floor, the offshore deck must:

- **Carry its own weight (dead load)** — steel, equipment, modules, piping.
- **Support people and movable items (live load)** — crew, vehicles, consumables.
- **Resist wind, wave splash, and accidental events (environmental & accidental loads)** — hurricanes, blast, fire, dropped objects.
- **Be lifted in pieces into the sea and bolted together (lift & installation loads)**.

The offshore deck, however, has one extra constraint: it must survive being **lifted by a crane barge, transported on a supply vessel, and set down in a rough ocean** — events that are often MORE critical than the actual in-place design. This is the central uniqueness of offshore deck engineering.

### 1.3 Primary Functions of a Deck

| Function | Engineering Implication |
|----------|------------------------|
| Support process equipment | Concentrated, often very heavy point loads |
| Provide working/storage space | Distributed live load ($\geq 5$ kPa typical) |
| House drilling rig | High dynamic loads, fatigue-critical |
| House personnel (accommodation) | Code-strict vibration, deflection limits |
| Resist environmental loads | Wind, wave-splash, ice, snow, seismic |
| Provide structural continuity | Acts as a horizontal diaphragm transferring lateral loads to the jacket legs |

> [!NOTE]
> The deck is **NOT a building floor**. The governing design events for an offshore deck are almost always **Lift, Transportation, and Loadout** — not the final in-service condition. KTU examiners love this conceptual point.

### 1.4 Material & Metric Standards

- **Primary structural steel:** ASTM A572 Gr. 50 / API 2H Gr. 50 (Yield Strength $\geq 345$ MPa).
- **Yield-to-ultimate ratio requirement:** $F_y / F_u \leq 0.80$ (to ensure ductile failure).
- **Z-direction (through-thickness) properties:** Mandatory for welded nodes carrying tensile plate loads (S275 / S355 Z-grade per EN 10164).
- **Standard design code:** API RP 2A-WSD / API RP 2A-LRFD, supplemented by AISC Steel Construction Manual.
- **Unit Weight of Steel:** **$w = 77$ kN/m³ (or 7850 kg/m³)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Design Phases of an Offshore Deck

An offshore deck is checked structurally at **FOUR distinct design events**. This is the most important conceptual framework for this topic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Design Phases:**
> 1. **Fabrication (Yard Build-up):** Deck rests on stools; checked for gravity loads only.
> 2. **Loadout & Transportation:** Deck is skidded onto a barge; checked for sea-state-induced dynamic accelerations and sea-fastenings.
> 3. **Lift (Onshore or Offshore):** Deck is lifted by crane(s); checked for sling tension, sling angle, local pad-eye stresses, and global bending.
> 4. **In-Place (Operational):** Deck sits on the jacket; checked for combined dead + live + environmental (wind/wave/snow/earthquake) loads.

### 2.2 Types of Deck Framing Systems

**(a) Plate Girder (Rolled or Fabricated) System**
- Uses deep fabricated I-girders (often haunched) spanning between main legs.
- Plate thickness typically **9 mm to 50 mm**, web depths **1 m to 3 m**.
- Common in modern integrated decks.

**(b) Truss / Braced Frame System**
- Deep open trusses between primary columns.
- Used for very long spans or heavy modules.

**(c) Box Girder (Tubular) System**
- Used in harsh-environment jack-ups and in skid-beams.
- Excellent torsional stiffness.

**(d) Modular Cell / Skid System**
- Process equipment is pre-assembled into "modules" or "cells" and skidded onto a parent deck frame.

### 2.3 Primary vs Secondary Framing

| Element | Role | Typical Spacing |
|---------|------|----------------|
| **Primary beams (Girders)** | Transfer main gravity loads to legs | Span between legs (10–30 m) |
| **Secondary beams (Stringers/Joists)** | Support plating; transfer to primary | 2–5 m on centre |
| **Stiffeners (Plate stiffeners)** | Prevent plate buckling | 0.3–0.6 m on centre |
| **Deck plating** | Acts as a diaphragm and supports local loads | 6–12 mm typically |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are directly testable in KTU ESE. Memorize the **physical meaning** of each.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $w_{steel} = 77 \text{ kN/m}^3$ | Unit weight of structural steel | kN/m³ |
| 2 | $M_u = \dfrac{wL^2}{8}$ (UDL on simply supported beam) | Max bending moment | kN·m |
| 3 | $V_u = \dfrac{wL}{2}$ (UDL on simply supported beam) | Max shear force | kN |
| 4 | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I} = \dfrac{M}{S}$ | Flexural bending stress | MPa |
| 5 | $\tau = \dfrac{VQ}{I \cdot t_w}$ | Transverse shear stress (plate girder web) | MPa |
| 6 | $\sigma_{vM} = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2}$ | Von Mises equivalent stress | MPa |
| 7 | $\dfrac{b}{t} \leq k \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{f_y}}$ | Plate slenderness / compactness check | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $\lambda_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{f_y}}$ | Limiting slenderness parameter | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^2 EI}{(KL)^2}$ | Euler buckling load | kN |
| 10 | $\Delta_{allow} = \dfrac{L}{240} \text{ to } \dfrac{L}{360}$ | Serviceability deflection limit (deck beams) | mm |
| 11 | $S_{x,req} = \dfrac{M_u}{\phi F_y}$ | Required elastic section modulus | mm³ |
| 12 | $t_{min,plate} = \dfrac{b}{k} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{f_y}{E}}$ | Minimum plate thickness for no stiffeners | mm |
| 13 | $T_{sling} = \dfrac{W}{n \cdot \sin \theta}$ | Crane sling tension ($n$ slings, angle $\theta$ from horizontal) | kN |
| 14 | $F_{dynamic} = G \cdot W$ | Dynamic amplification factor (typical $G = 1.1$ to $2.0$) | — |
| 15 | $\sigma_{comb} \leq \dfrac{F_y}{1.10}$ (API WSD) | Combined stress allowable | MPa |
| 16 | $\sigma_b \pm \sigma_a} \leq F_b$ (AISC) | Combined axial + bending allowable | MPa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL Pipe Symbol Rule:** When writing $|x|$ or absolute values, **NEVER** use the raw `|` character in markdown tables — it breaks the table. Use `$\vert x \vert$` or `$\lvert x \rvert$` in LaTeX instead.

### 2.5 The Three Load Categories (Per API RP 2A)

- **(1) Functional Loads:** Dead, Live, Buoyancy, Equipment, Inertia from rotating equipment.
- **(2) Environmental Loads:** Wind ($V_{ref}$ for 100-yr return), Wave (operational / extreme), Current, Ice, Snow, Earthquake.
- **(3) Accidental Loads:** Fire, Blast, Collision (vessel impact), Dropped objects.

### 2.6 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters

The deck design process is the single most economically significant activity in an offshore project. A modern topside can weigh **5,000 to 30,000 tonnes** and cost **$1–3 billion USD**. Even a 1% reduction in steel tonnage saves millions. Engineers must therefore use **stiffened plate theory, grillage analysis, and finite element modelling** to optimize the structure rather than overdesigning.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Critical Plate Slenderness (for a stiffened plate panel)

We start with a simply supported rectangular plate under uniform in-plane compression $N_x$ per unit length. The critical buckling stress according to classical plate theory (Timoshenko & Gere) is:

$$N_{cr} = \frac{k \pi^2 D}{b^2}$$

where $D$ is the **flexural rigidity of the plate per unit width**, defined as:

$$D = \frac{E \cdot t^3}{12(1 - \nu^2)}$$

Step 1 — Substitute $D$ into $N_{cr}$:

$$N_{cr} = \frac{k \pi^2 E t^3}{12(1 - \nu^2) b^2}$$

Step 2 — The average compressive stress at buckling is:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{N_{cr}}{t} = \frac{k \pi^2 E t^2}{12(1 - \nu^2) b^2}$$

Step 3 — For a long plate simply supported on all four edges with $a \gg b$ and uniform compression, the **buckling coefficient** $k = 4.0$. Using $\nu = 0.3$ for steel, $(1 - \nu^2) = 0.91$. Substituting:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{4 \pi^2 E}{12(0.91)} \left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2$$

Step 4 — Set $\sigma_{cr} = F_y$ (the yield stress, defining the boundary between elastic buckling and yielding):

$$F_y = \frac{\pi^2 E}{2.73} \left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2$$

Step 5 — Rearrange to obtain the **slenderness ratio limit** $b/t$:

$$\left(\frac{b}{t}\right)_{lim} = \pi \sqrt{\frac{E}{2.73 F_y}}$$

Step 6 — Defining the **limiting slenderness parameter** $\lambda_p = \sqrt{E/F_y}$:

$$\left(\frac{b}{t}\right)_{lim} = \pi \sqrt{\frac{1}{2.73}} \cdot \lambda_p \approx 1.90 \cdot \lambda_p$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** A plate with $b/t \leq 1.90 \cdot \lambda_p$ yields before it buckles (compact plate). If $b/t$ exceeds this, the plate must be either thickened or stiffened.

### 3.2 Derivation: Sling Tension in a Four-Leg Crane Lift

Consider a deck of total lifted weight $W$ (already including rigging, coaming, etc.) being lifted by **4 slings** (one at each corner), each making an angle $\theta$ with the **horizontal** (NOT with the vertical — this is a classic KTU trap).

Step 1 — For a single sling, vertical equilibrium gives:

$$T_{sling} \cdot \sin \theta = \frac{W}{n}$$

Step 2 — Solve for the sling tension:

$$T_{sling} = \frac{W}{n \cdot \sin \theta}$$

Step 3 — For $n = 4$ slings (the standard 4-point lift):

$$T_{sling} = \frac{W}{4 \sin \theta}$$

Step 4 — The **horizontal squeeze force** at the top of the sling (which tries to crush the deck) is:

$$H = T_{sling} \cos \theta = \frac{W \cos \theta}{4 \sin \theta} = \frac{W}{4 \tan \theta}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Trap:** As $\theta \to 0$ (sling becomes nearly horizontal), $\sin\theta \to 0$ and the sling tension and horizontal force both **blow up to infinity**. This is why API/ISO codes mandate a **minimum sling angle of 60° from horizontal** (i.e., 30° from vertical). Students who use the angle from the **vertical** instead of from the **horizontal** lose 2 marks instantly.

### 3.3 Derivation: Effective Width Concept for Stiffened Panels (Von Mises / API Method)

When a stiffened plate is in compression, the post-buckling capacity is captured using the **effective width** concept. The effective width of a plate of actual width $b$ and thickness $t$ in compression is:

$$b_{eff} = \rho \cdot b$$

where $\rho$ is the **reduction factor**:

$$\rho = \frac{1}{\bar{\lambda}_p} \left(1 - \frac{0.22}{\bar{\lambda}_p}\right) \quad \text{for } \bar{\lambda}_p \geq 0.673$$

and $\bar{\lambda}_p$ is the **plate slenderness parameter**:

$$\bar{\lambda}_p = \sqrt{\frac{f_y}{\sigma_{cr}}} = \frac{b}{t} \cdot \frac{1}{28.4 \epsilon \sqrt{k}}$$

where $\epsilon = \sqrt{235 / f_y}$ (in MPa).

The effective cross-sectional area is then $A_{eff} = b_{eff} \cdot t$, and the design capacity is $N_{Rd} = A_{eff} \cdot f_y / \gamma_{M1}$ with $\gamma_{M1} = 1.10$ (Eurocode 3 / DNV).

> [!NOTE]
> This formula appears in KTU questions on **grillage beam** or **orthotropic plate deck** design. Memorize the structure of $\rho$.

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)
