**Problem:** A deck plating panel is **3.0 m long × 2.0 m wide × 10 mm thick**, simply supported on all edges, in uniform compression along the long edge. Steel is **ASTM A36 ($F_y = 250$ MPa, $E = 2.0 \times 10^5$ MPa, $\nu = 0.3$)**. Find the **critical buckling load per metre width**.

**Step 1** — Plate slenderness ratio:

$$\frac{b}{t} = \frac{2000}{10} = 200$$

**Step 2** — Compute $\lambda_p$:

$$\lambda_p = \sqrt{\frac{E}{F_y}} = \sqrt{\frac{200000}{250}} = \sqrt{800} \approx 28.28$$

**Step 3** — Compactness check:

$$1.90 \cdot \lambda_p = 1.90 \times 28.28 \approx 53.74$$

Since $b/t = 200 \gg 53.74$, the plate will **buckle elastically** before yielding. We must use plate-buckling theory.

**Step 4** — For $a/b = 3.0/2.0 = 1.5$, the buckling coefficient $k \approx 4.0$ (long plate limit).

**Step 5** — Critical stress:

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{k \pi^2 E}{12(1 - \nu^2)} \left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2 = \frac{4 \pi^2 (200000)}{12(0.91)} \left(\frac{10}{2000}\right)^2$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{4 \times 9.8696 \times 200000}{10.92} \times (0.005)^2$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{7895680}{10.92} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = 722864 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5} \approx 18.07 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6** — Critical load per unit width:

$$N_{cr} = \sigma_{cr} \cdot t = 18.07 \text{ MPa} \times 10 \text{ mm} = 180.7 \text{ N/mm} = 180.7 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 7 — Conclusion:** Since $\sigma_{cr} = 18.07$ MPa $\ll F_y = 250$ MPa, the panel will buckle at less than 8% of yield stress. **Stiffeners are mandatory.** Adding a stiffener at mid-width reduces $b$ from 2000 mm to 1000 mm, which reduces $b/t$ from 200 to 100, raising $\sigma_{cr}$ by a factor of 4 to ~72 MPa — still elastic, but vastly improved.

> [!TIP]
> This is a **classic KTU 14-mark problem**. Practice the substitution and the bookkeeping of units.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Deck Design Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram captures the entire deck-design process from concept to fabrication release, including all the iterative design-event checks.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Functional Specification] --> B[Conceptual Deck Layout]
    B --> C[Define Primary Grid, Leg Spacing]
    C --> D[Estimate Dead and Live Loads]
    D --> E[Apply Equipment Point Loads]
    E --> F[Environmental Loads Wind Wave Snow EQ]
    F --> G[Accidental Loads Fire Blast Impact]
    G --> H[Define Four Design Phases]
    H --> H1[Fabrication]
    H --> H2[Loadout and Transport]
    H --> H3[Lift Onshore Offshore]
    H --> H4[In Place Operational]
    H1 --> I[Build Global FE Model]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    I --> J[Run Linear Static Analysis]
    J --> K[Code Check API AISC DNV]
    K --> L{Unity Check less than 1.0?}
    L -->|No| M[Resize and Revise Members]
    M --> I
    L -->|Yes| N[Fatigue Check Hot Spot S N]
    N --> O[Deflection and Vibration Check]
    O --> P[Connection Design Pad Eye Bracket]
    P --> Q[Corrosion and Coating Specs]
    Q --> R[Drawing and BoM Release]
    R --> S[Fabrication and Yard]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Node IDs are alphanumeric (`A`, `B`, `H1`, `H4`) and labels contain plain text only — this obeys the Mermaid safeguards required by KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE.

### 4.2 Schematic: Typical Two-Level Integrated Offshore Deck

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TopDeck
        TD1[Drilling Module]
        TD2[Derrick Substructure]
        TD3[Pipe Rack 01]
        TD4[Helideck Platform]
    end
    subgraph ProductionDeck
        PD1[Process Module 01]
        PD2[Separation Train]
        PD3[Manifold Area]
        PD4[Compressor Skid]
    end
    subgraph CellarDeck
        CD1[Wellhead Bay]
        CD2[Conductor Slots]
        CD3[Mud Pump Skid]
        CD4[Electrical Switchgear]
    end
    subgraph Jacket
        J1[Main Leg 01]
        J2[Main Leg 02]
        J3[Main Leg 03]
        J4[Main Leg 04]
    end
    TopDeck --> ProductionDeck
    ProductionDeck --> CellarDeck
    CellarDeck --> J1
    CellarDeck --> J2
    CellarDeck --> J3
    CellarDeck --> J4
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Primary vs Secondary Framing Layout (Plan View Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Main Leg 01] --- B[Primary Girder G1]
    B --- C[Main Leg 02]
    C --- D[Primary Girder G2]
    D --- E[Main Leg 03]
    E --- F[Primary Girder G3]
    F --- G[Main Leg 04]
    G --- H[Primary Girder G4]
    H --- A
    P1[Secondary Beam B1] -.-> B
    P2[Secondary Beam B2] -.-> B
    P3[Secondary Beam B3] -.-> B
    P4[Secondary Beam B4] -.-> B
    S1[Stiffener S1] -.-> P1
    S2[Stiffener S2] -.-> P1
    S3[Stiffener S3] -.-> P1
```

### 4.4 Schematic: Sling Geometry for a 4-Point Lift

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Deck Weight W] --> B[Crown Block]
    B --> C1[Sling 01 angle theta]
    B --> C2[Sling 02 angle theta]
    B --> C3[Sling 03 angle theta]
    B --> C4[Sling 04 angle theta]
    C1 --> D1[Pad Eye 01]
    C2 --> D2[Pad Eye 02]
    C3 --> D3[Pad Eye 03]
    C4 --> D4[Pad Eye 04]
    D1 --> E[Deck Corner 01]
    D2 --> F[Deck Corner 02]
    D3 --> G[Deck Corner 03]
    D4 --> H[Deck Corner 04]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Caption Hook:** The angle $\theta$ is measured from the **horizontal**. The smaller the angle, the larger the horizontal squeeze force at the deck corners — hence the code-mandated minimum of **60° from horizontal**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
**"Distinguish between the 'In-Place' design condition and the 'Lift' design condition for an offshore deck. Why are both required?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **In-Place** condition represents the deck in its final installed state on the jacket, subjected to combined **dead + live + environmental (wind, wave, snow, earthquake) + accidental** loads. The structural checks are based on **operational** load combinations (e.g., API 1.2D + 1.6L + 1.2W).

The **Lift** condition represents the deck suspended by crane slings during installation. The deck is checked for **sling tension, horizontal squeeze forces at pad eyes, local pad-eye bearing stresses, and global bending from self-weight distributed only at the lift points.** Sling tension is amplified by the **Dynamic Amplification Factor (DAF = 1.10 to 2.0)** depending on sea state.

Both checks are required because **different limit states govern each condition**: the lift condition often produces **higher local stresses** at the pad-eye connections than the in-place condition, even though the total dead load is identical. Failing to check the lift condition has historically caused catastrophic failures during installation (e.g., Alexander Kielland 1980, Ocean Ranger 1982 — root causes were lift and stability related).

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**"What is the function of secondary framing in an offshore deck? How is it different from primary framing?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Primary framing** consists of the main girders spanning between the jacket legs. It transfers the deck's global gravity and lateral loads down to the substructure.

**Secondary framing** (stringers, joists, plate stiffeners) supports the **deck plating** and **distributes localized loads** (foot traffic, equipment bases, pipe racks) to the primary framing. The plating itself acts as a **horizontal diaphragm** that transfers lateral wind/wave/earthquake loads to the vertical bracing.

The two are differentiated by **stiffness and load path**: primary members carry the bulk of the gravity load and are sized for global strength, while secondary members are sized for **local plate-bending and buckling** of the panels they support. The typical hierarchy is **plating → stiffeners → secondary beams → primary girders → legs**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the **four design phases** that an offshore deck must be structurally checked for. Briefly explain the load cases considered in each phase. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A square deck panel has a **plan dimension of 3.0 m × 3.0 m**, plate thickness **12 mm**, and is made of **ASTM A36 steel** ($F_y = 250$ MPa, $E = 2.0 \times 10^5$ MPa, $\nu = 0.3$). It is simply supported on all four edges and is in uniform in-plane compression along one direction. Calculate: **(i)** the slenderness ratio, **(ii)** whether the plate yields before buckling, **(iii)** the elastic critical buckling stress and the critical load per metre width. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:**

The four design phases are:

1. **Fabrication (Yard Build-up):** The deck is assembled in the fabrication yard on temporary stools/blocks. Loads: self-weight (dead), construction live load (1.0 kPa), and wind at the yard (lower than offshore). Code: AISC allowable stresses.

2. **Loadout and Sea Transportation:** The deck is skidded onto a transport barge. Loads: self-weight (acceleration-corrected), sea-state-induced **roll, pitch, heave** dynamic accelerations (typically 0.05g to 0.20g). Sea-fastenings must be designed for the maximum transport motion envelope.

3. **Lift (Onshore or Offshore):** The deck is lifted by one or two crane barges. Loads: self-weight × **Dynamic Amplification Factor (DAF = 1.1 onshore, 1.5 to 2.0 offshore)**, sling tension, pad-eye local stresses, sling horizontal squeeze. Sling angle is code-limited to **60° minimum from horizontal**.

4. **In-Place (Operational):** The deck is set on the jacket legs and is in its final service state. Loads: dead + live (5 to 15 kPa) + environmental (operational & extreme wind, wave, current, snow, earthquake) + accidental (fire, blast, dropped objects). Per API RP 2A, the 100-year return-period environmental loads govern.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently describe the phases but **forget to mention the load combination for each phase** — this is a 2-mark deduction. State explicitly: **Lift is DAF-amplified dead; In-place is 1.2D + 1.6L + 1.2W; Transportation includes sea-state accelerations.**

---

**Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Slenderness ratio:**

$$\frac{b}{t} = \frac{3000}{12} = 250$$

**(ii) Yield-before-buckling check:**

Limiting slenderness $= 1.90 \cdot \lambda_p = 1.90 \cdot \sqrt{200000/250} = 1.90 \cdot 28.28 = 53.74$

Since $b/t = 250 \gg 53.74$, the plate **buckles elastically before yielding**. Stiffeners are required.

**(iii) Critical buckling stress (assume $k = 4.0$ for simply supported square panel under uniform compression):**

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{k \pi^2 E}{12(1 - \nu^2)} \left(\frac{t}{b}\right)^2 = \frac{4 \pi^2 (2.0 \times 10^5)}{12(0.91)} \left(\frac{12}{3000}\right)^2$$

$$\sigma_{cr} = \frac{7895680}{10.92} \times (0.004)^2 = 722864 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-5} \approx 11.57 \text{ MPa}$$

Critical load per metre width:

$$N_{cr} = \sigma_{cr} \cdot t = 11.57 \text{ MPa} \times 12 \text{ mm} = 138.8 \text{ N/mm} \approx 138.8 \text{ kN/m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion for (b):** The plate buckles at only **4.6% of yield**. A practical design would add **3 stiffeners** per metre, reducing the sub-panel $b/t$ to ~62, which raises $\sigma_{cr}$ to ~232 MPa — close to yield, indicating an efficient stiffened-panel design.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Describe the **plate-girder system** used in modern offshore decks. With neat sketches, explain the role of **flanges, web, bearing stiffeners, and intermediate transverse stiffeners** in resisting bending and shear. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A topside deck weighing **$W = 8000$ kN** is being lifted by **4 equal slings**, each making an angle of **$\theta = 65°$ with the horizontal**. Calculate: **(i)** the tension in each sling, **(ii)** the horizontal squeeze force at the top of the sling, **(iii)** the **minimum allowable sling angle** if the maximum permissible sling tension is **3000 kN**, and **(iv)** the comment on the safety of using 60° angle. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:**

A **plate girder** is a fabricated I-section built up from three plates: two **flanges** (top and bottom) and one **web**. Plate girders are used in offshore decks when **rolled sections are inadequate** for the required moment of inertia or when the span is long (>15 m).

- **Flanges** resist the **bending moment**; the area is concentrated at the extreme fibres where bending stress $\sigma = Mc/I$ is highest. The flange area is sized to satisfy $S_x \geq M_u / (\phi F_y)$.
- **Web** resists the **transverse shear force** $V$. The shear stress is $\tau = VQ/(I \cdot t_w)$. For deep webs, the shear capacity is governed by **web buckling** rather than yield, requiring the slenderness check $h/t_w \leq 5.5 \cdot \sqrt{E/F_y}$ for unstiffened webs.
- **Bearing stiffeners** are placed at points of concentrated load (e.g., at the leg-to-girder interface) to prevent **web crippling** and **local web yielding**. They act as short columns transferring the load from flange to web to flange.
- **Intermediate transverse stiffeners** divide the web into panels to prevent **shear buckling** (diagonal tension field failure). Spacing is governed by $a/h$ ratios in AISC §G2.

Together, the plate girder provides the **flexural and shear stiffness** required to transfer the deck's gravity loads to the supporting legs while keeping the self-weight of the deck low.

---

**Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $W = 8000$ kN, $n = 4$ slings, $\theta = 65°$ (from horizontal).

**(i) Sling tension:**

$$T_{sling} = \frac{W}{n \cdot \sin \theta} = \frac{8000}{4 \cdot \sin 65°} = \frac{8000}{4 \cdot 0.9063} = \frac{8000}{3.625} \approx 2206.9 \text{ kN}$$

**(ii) Horizontal squeeze force:**

$$H = T_{sling} \cdot \cos \theta = 2206.9 \cdot \cos 65° = 2206.9 \cdot 0.4226 \approx 932.6 \text{ kN}$$

Equivalently, $H = W / (4 \tan \theta) = 8000 / (4 \times 2.1445) = 932.6$ kN. ✓

**(iii) Maximum allowable sling angle:**

Setting $T_{sling} = 3000$ kN:

$$3000 = \frac{8000}{4 \cdot \sin \theta_{min}} \Rightarrow \sin \theta_{min} = \frac{8000}{4 \times 3000} = 0.6667$$

$$\theta_{min} = \arcsin(0.6667) \approx 41.8°$$

So the **minimum allowable sling angle from horizontal is ~41.8°**. Practically, codes require a more conservative minimum of **60° from horizontal**, so 41.8° is **not code-compliant** — but the calculation shows the **lower bound** for the given $T_{max}$.

**(iv) Safety at 60° angle:**

$$T_{sling}(60°) = \frac{8000}{4 \cdot \sin 60°} = \frac{8000}{4 \times 0.8660} = \frac{8000}{3.464} \approx 2309.4 \text{ kN}$$

$$H(60°) = \frac{8000}{4 \cdot \tan 60°} = \frac{8000}{6.928} \approx 1154.7 \text{ kN}$$

At 60°, sling tension = 2309.4 kN, which is well below the 3000 kN capacity (utilization = 77%). **The 60° lift is safe but only by a 23% margin; with dynamic amplification (DAF = 1.25 offshore), the tension becomes 2887 kN — utilization 96%, leaving only 4% margin. Operators should consider a steeper sling angle (e.g., 70° from horizontal) for a safer lift.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Two common errors in lift problems: (a) using the angle from the **vertical** (which gives wrong values — for a 60° from vertical, the answer is 65° from horizontal); (b) **forgetting the DAF**. Always state the DAF used.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Rapid-Revision Checklist for Deck Design:**

- **Definition:** The deck is the topside structural assembly that supports all functional equipment and personnel; it is checked for fabrication, transportation, lift, and in-place design events.
- **Four Design Phases:** Fabrication → Loadout/Transport → Lift → In-Place. Each has its own load combination.
- **Lift Angle:** Always measured from the **horizontal**. Code minimum: **60° from horizontal**. Tensions blow up as $\theta \to 0$.
- **Sling Tension Formula:** $T = W / (n \sin\theta)$ — memorize, do not derive in the exam.
- **Dynamic Amplification Factor (DAF):** 1.1 onshore, 1.5–2.0 offshore lift; multiplies the static weight to get the effective lifting load.
- **Plate Slenderness Limit:** $b/t \leq 1.90 \cdot \sqrt{E/F_y}$ to ensure yielding before buckling. Above this, plate buckles elastically → must add stiffeners.
- **Plate Buckling Stress:** $\sigma_{cr} = k \pi^2 E / [12(1-\nu^2)] \cdot (t/b)^2$, with $k = 4.0$ for the standard simply supported case.
- **Effective Width (Post-Buckling):** $b_{eff} = \rho \cdot b$ with $\rho$ as a function of plate slenderness $\bar{\lambda}_p$.
- **Steel:** ASTM A36 ($F_y=250$ MPa) or A572 Gr. 50 ($F_y=345$ MPa) are the most common grades.
- **Unit Weight of Steel:** **$77$ kN/m³** — high-yield exam point.
- **Deflection Limit:** $L/240$ to $L/360$ for deck beams under live load.
- **Primary vs Secondary:** Primary = main girders to legs; Secondary = beams to primary; Stiffeners = plate to secondary; Plating = skin.
- **Codes:** API RP 2A-WSD/LRFD, AISC Steel Manual, DNV-OS-C101, ISO 19902.
- **Z-Direction Steel:** Required at welded connections with through-thickness tension to prevent lamellar tearing.
- **Fatigue Hot-Spots:** Tubular joints in the jacket and welded bracket connections in the deck are governed by the **S-N curve approach** of API RP 2A.
- **Pipe Symbol in Tables:** Always use `$\vert x \vert$` (LaTeX) — never raw `|x|` — in KTU handwritten answer tables too.
- **Mark Strategy for 14-mark question:** 2 marks for sketch + 3 marks for narrative + 2 marks for numerical setup + 5 marks for substitution and final answer — keep arithmetic separate from explanation.
- **Common Lost Marks:** Wrong sling-angle reference, no DAF, omitting transport phase, using $E = 2.1 \times 10^5$ vs $2.0 \times 10^5$ (both acceptable but state it).
- **Last-Mantra:** *In offshore, lift governs more often than in-place.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Appurtenances
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST722 - FUNDAMENTALS OF OFFSHORE STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Appurtenances # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Appurtenances (in Offshore Structures):** Appurtenances are the **secondary structural and mechanical components** that are attached to or supported by the primary jacket/fixed platform structure, but are **not part of the main load-bearing jacket framing**. They are essential auxiliary systems that enable the functional operation, safety, accessibility, and production capability of an offshore platform. According to API RP 2A (Recommended Practice for Planning, Designing, and Constructing Fixed Offshore Platforms) and ISO 19902, appurtenances are typically designed as **non-structural or secondary structural items** but must be engineered to withstand the same environmental loads (wind, wave, current, seismic) as the primary structure. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For the 2024 scheme, appurtenances fall under **Module 3 – Design of Offshore Structures**, and questions typically focus on **functional description, design loads, and design philosophy** of each appurtenance. --- ## 1.2 Classification of Appurtenances Offshore platform appurtenances are broadly classified into the following categories: | Category | Examples | Primary Function | |----------|----------|------------------| | **Access & Transfer** | Boat landings, gangways, stairways, ladders | Personnel/material transfer between vessel and platform | | **Impact Protection** | Bumpers, fenders, fender piles | Absorb berthing/collision energy from boats & supply vessels | | **Aviation Support** | Helidecks, helideck support frames, lighting | Helicopter landing for crew change & medevac | | **Lifting & Handling** | Crane pedestals, crane rails, winches, davits | Material handling, pipe laying, construction | | **Marine Operations** | Flare booms, drilling derricks, production modules | Hydrocarbon processing & disposal | | **Pipeline/Riser** | Risers, J-tubes, I-tubes, caissons, conductor pipes | Riser protection & guidance | | **Corrosion Protection** | Sacrificial anodes (Al, Zn), impressed current systems | Cathodic protection of submerged steel | | **Safety & Survival** | Life boat stations, life raft platforms, muster areas | Emergency evacuation | | **Navigation & Visibility** | Navigation lights, fog horns, day markers, aviation warning lights | Safe marine & air navigation | --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Analogy:** Think of an offshore platform like a **busy airport built on stilts in the middle of the ocean**. The main steel **jacket and deck** are like the runway and terminal building. But for the airport to *function*, you need: > - A **jet bridge (boat landing)** for passengers to walk from boat → terminal > - **Wheel chocks (bumpers)** so boats don't crash into the terminal > - A **helipad (helideck)** for VIP arrivals > - **Luggage conveyor belts (risers & J-tubes)** to bring cargo (oil/gas) up from the sea > - **Loudspeakers & warning lights (navigation aids)** for safety > > **All these "extras" are the appurtenances.** They look small compared to the main structure, but without them the platform cannot operate, and the workers cannot survive. --- ## 1.4 Why Appurtenances are Important in KTU Examinations > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Exam Trap:** Many students treat appurtenances as "small attachments" and write them off. However, in real offshore engineering, **appurtenance failures are the #1 cause of platform incidents and downtime**. Boards expect you to know: > - The **load path** from each appurtenance to the main jacket > - The **design codes** (API RP 2A, ISO 19902, NORSOK N-001) > - The **wave/current loading** on slender appurtenance members > - The **fatigue and corrosion** considerations # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Treatment of Major Appurtenances ### 2.1.1 Boat Landings A **boat landing** is a framed structure attached to the outer face of a jacket leg (or between two legs) that provides a **safe landing zone** for crew boats and supply vessels transferring personnel and materials. **Typical Configuration:** - Constructed at **mean lower low water (MLLW) + 1.5 m to 2.0 m** elevation - 4 to 6 horizontal **"browse" levels** spaced at **~1.5 m to 2.0 m vertical intervals** - A **"keeper" bar** (sloped cylindrical pipe, typically 5° to 15° from vertical) to hold the boat against the structure - **Internal finger barge** sometimes provided for heavy weather - **Safety gate** at the upper end connecting to the deck access **Design Loads (per API RP 2A):** - **Boat impact load:** Typically **100 kN to 200 kN** applied horizontally at the boat landing elevation (project-specific) - **Wave slam** and **buoyancy** on submerged members - **Current drag** on keeper bar - **Dead load + live load (2 kN/m²)** on browse levels - **Environmental loads** (wind, wave) combined as per API LRFD **Design Philosophy:** - Designed to allow **one boat to remain in contact** during design storm conditions - Keeper bar designed as a **cantilever beam** with concentrated horizontal load at the elevation of boat hull contact - Browse levels designed as **simply supported** between jacket legs --- ### 2.1.2 Bumpers (Fenders) **Bumpers** are tubular/structural members placed in front of the platform legs or boat landing to **absorb accidental berthing impact** of vessels, preventing damage to the primary jacket. **Key Design Parameters:** - **Berthing energy** (E) absorbed by elastic/plastic deformation: $$E = \frac{1}{2} \cdot F \cdot \delta$$ where: - $F$ = Berthing force (kN) - $\delta$ = Deflection of fender system (m) - **Equivalent static berthing force** (per PIANC guidelines): $$F = \sqrt{2 \cdot E \cdot k_e}$$ where $k_e$ is the **effective stiffness** of the fender-vessel system (kN/m). - The required **energy absorption capacity** of the bumper is: $$E = \frac{1}{2} \cdot C_m \cdot C_e \cdot C_s \cdot C_c \cdot W \cdot V^2$$ where: - $C_m$ = Mass coefficient (~1.3 to 2.0 for typical supply vessels) - $C_e$ = Eccentricity coefficient (~0.5 to 1.0) - $C_s$ = Softness coefficient (~0.9 to 1.0) - $C_c$ = Berth configuration coefficient - $W$ = Displacement weight of vessel (tonnes) - $V$ = Berthing velocity (m/s), typically **0.15 to 0.5 m/s** > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Bumper design is essentially an **energy balance problem** — the kinetic energy of the approaching vessel must be absorbed by the elastic/plastic deformation of the bumper without exceeding allowable stress limits in the supporting structure. --- ### 2.1.3 Helideck A **helideck** is a circular or octagonal landing platform mounted on top of the offshore platform (or on a cantilevered bracket) for **helicopter operations**. **Standard Sizing:** - For helicopters up to **Sikorsky S-92 / EH101** class: **D = 22.2 m (73 ft)** circular net area - For smaller helicopters (Bell 212, AS365): **D = 16.5 m (54 ft)** to **18.5 m (61 ft)** - Marked with a **circle of diameter 0.5 × D** - Perimeter safety net of width **1.5 m** around the deck **Design Loads (per CAP 437 / ICAO / API):** - **Uniformly distributed load (UDL):** typically **2.5 kN/m²** (sustained) over the entire deck - **Concentrated helicopter landing load:** the **Maximum Take-Off Weight (MTOW)** of the design helicopter applied through a **1.5 m × 1.5 m patch** (or per skid/track footprint) - **Dynamic load factor (DLF):** **1.5** to **1.75** for landing impact - **Wind loading on the helicopter** (parked condition) - **Wave loading on helideck support** (for cantilevered designs) **Critical Design Equation — Effective Load:** $$P_{eff} = MTOW \times (1 + DLF) \times g$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget to apply the **dynamic load factor** for helicopter landing. The 1.5× multiplier is a **mandatory** inclusion in KTU board answers for helideck design. --- ### 2.1.4 Risers and J-Tubes **Risers** are pipes that transport hydrocarbons from the seabed (wellhead) up to the production deck. **J-tubes** are curved tubular conduits attached to the jacket that **protect and guide** the risers from the seabed up to the deck. **J-Tube Geometry:** - The J-tube follows the **J-shape** because it starts at the **mudline**, goes down/along the seabed, curves upward, and runs up the jacket leg to the deck - Typically constructed of **pipe sizes 16" to 36"** diameter - Multiple J-tubes may be bundled together **Design Considerations:** - **Wave and current loading** on the J-tube - **VIV (Vortex-Induced Vibration)** of the long slender J-tube - **Bending stress** at the curved section (high stress concentration) - **Scour** at the J-tube seabed exit - **Fatigue** at the bend **Hydrodynamic Loading (Morison's Equation):** $$F = C_d \cdot \frac{\rho \cdot D \cdot u \cdot \vert u \vert}{2} + C_m \cdot \frac{\pi \cdot D^2}{4} \cdot \rho \cdot \dot{u}$$ where: - $C_d$ = Drag coefficient (~0.7 for smooth pipe) - $C_m$ = Inertia coefficient (~2.0) - $D$ = Pipe diameter - $u$ = Fluid velocity normal to pipe - $\dot{u}$ = Fluid acceleration --- ### 2.1.5 Cathodic Protection (Anodes) Submerged steel in seawater corrodes electrochemically. **Cathodic protection** is provided by attaching **sacrificial anodes** (typically aluminum or zinc alloys) to the jacket. **Anode Mass Calculation:** $$M = \frac{I \cdot T \cdot 8760}{C \cdot u_e}$$ where: - $M$ = Required anode mass (kg) - $I$ = Mean design current density (A/m²) of coated steel - $T$ = Design life (years), typically **20 to 30 years** - $C$ = Anode utilization factor (~0.85 for Al-alloy) - $u_e$ = Electrochemical capacity (A·h/kg), typically **2500 A·h/kg for Al-Zn-In alloy** **Number of Anodes:** $$N = \frac{A_{submerged} \cdot I_{design}}{I_{anode}}$$ where $A_{submerged}$ is the total submerged surface area of the jacket, $I_{design}$ is the design current density, and $I_{anode}$ is the current output of a single anode. --- ### 2.1.6 Crane Pedestal and Crane Pedestal Bracket A **crane pedestal** is a tubular column extending above the deck that supports the slewing bearing of an offshore crane. It must be designed for: - **Crane weight** (dead load) - **Hoisted load** (live load, e.g., **10 tonnes to 100+ tonnes**) - **Dynamic amplification factor (DAF):** **1.1 to 1.3** - **Wind loading on crane** (operating and storm conditions) - **Inertia loads** from slewing motion - **Combination of vertical, horizontal, and moment loads** at the pedestal base **Resultant Base Reaction:** $$M_{base} = M_{overturning} + V \cdot h_{pedestal}$$ where the overturning moment is generated by the hoisted load and wind, and $h_{pedestal}$ is the height of the pedestal. --- ### 2.1.7 Flare Booms A **flare boom** is a cantilevered horizontal structure extending from the platform that supports the **flare tip** for safe disposal of hydrocarbon gases. It must be designed to: - Maintain **flame tilt ≤ 5°** under wind - Withstand **heat radiation loads** (though minimal at structural supports) - Handle **gas exit velocity** and **jet impingement** effects - Provide **safe length** to keep the flame away from the main platform (typical **30 m to 60 m** horizontal reach) --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Typical Units / Values | KTU Reference | |---|-------------------|-----------|------------------------|---------------| | 1 | Bumper berthing energy | $E = \frac{1}{2} C_m C_e C_s C_c W V^2$ | kJ | API RP 2A | | 2 | Equivalent static force | $F = \sqrt{2 E k_e}$ | kN | PIANC | | 3 | Helideck effective load | $P_{eff} = MTOW(1+DLF) \cdot g$ | kN | CAP 437 | | 4 | Helideck UDL | $w = 2.5$ | kN/m² | API / ICAO | | 5 | Morison hydrodynamic force | $F = C_d \frac{\rho D u \vert u \vert}{2} + C_m \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \rho \dot u$ | kN/m | API RP 2A | | 6 | Anode mass | $M = \frac{I \cdot T \cdot 8760}{C \cdot u_e}$ | kg | NORSOK N-001 | | 7 | Number of anodes | $N = A_{sub} \cdot I_{design} / I_{anode}$ | — | NORSOK N-001 | | 8 | Wave slam on horizontal member | $F = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_s D^2 \dot u$ | kN | API RP 2A | | 9 | Buoyancy of flooded member | $F_b = \rho_{sw} g V_{displaced}$ | kN | — | | 10 | Combined stress check | $\sigma_{combined} \leq \phi \cdot \sigma_y$ | MPa | AISC / API | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on absolute values in tables:** The Morison equation uses $\vert u \vert$ to denote absolute value. In KTU board exams, write this as $u \cdot \vert u \vert$ (or $u \cdot \mid u \mid$) and **avoid** using the pipe character `|` inside markdown table cells. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Why KTU stresses this topic:** Appurtenance design accounts for **~15% to 25% of the total structural weight** of a typical fixed offshore platform. Failures of boat landings, bumpers, and helideck support frames are leading causes of: > - Personnel injury (boat landing failures during monsoons) > - Helicopter accidents (helideck structural collapse) > - Hydrocarbon release (riser/J-tube fatigue failures) > > Mastery of appurtenance design directly translates to **safer, more economical platforms** in the Indian offshore sector (Mumbai High, KG Basin, etc.). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Bumper / Fender Equivalent Static Force The kinetic energy of a berthing vessel must be fully absorbed by the elastic deformation of the fender system and the elastic response of the vessel hull. **Step 1 — Write the kinetic energy of the berthing vessel.** $$E_{kinetic} = \frac{1}{2} m_{eff} V^2$$ where $m_{eff} = C_m \cdot W / g$ is the **effective (hydrodynamic) mass** of the vessel accounting for added mass of surrounding water. **Step 2 — Multiply by correction factors for berthing direction, berth configuration, softness, and eccentricity.** $$E_{design} = C_m \cdot C_e \cdot C_s \cdot C_c \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{W}{g} \cdot V^2$$ Combining constants and writing $W$ in tonnes, $V$ in m/s, and absorbing $g = 9.81$ into the constant: $$E_{design} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot C_m C_e C_s C_c \cdot W \cdot V^2 \quad [kJ \text{ if W in tonnes, V in m/s}]$$ **Step 3 — Apply the energy balance. The strain energy stored in the fender equals the design energy.** $$E_{design} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot F \cdot \delta$$ where $F$ is the maximum berthing force and $\delta$ is the fender deflection. **Step 4 — Express the deflection in terms of force and effective stiffness.** $$\delta = \frac{F}{k_e}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute into the energy equation and solve for F.** $$E_{design} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot F \cdot \frac{F}{k_e} = \frac{F^2}{2 k_e}$$ Solving: $$F^2 = 2 \cdot E_{design} \cdot k_e$$ $$F = \sqrt{2 \cdot E_{design} \cdot k_e} \quad \text{(Equivalent Static Berthing Force)}$$ **Step 6 — Practical substitution with all factors:** $$\boxed{F = \sqrt{ C_m C_e C_s C_c \cdot W \cdot V^2 \cdot k_e }}$$ This force $F$ is applied horizontally at the bumper elevation, and the supporting structure is designed for the resulting moment and shear. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Required Anode Mass for Cathodic Protection **Step 1 — Total current demand.** The submerged steel surface area to be protected is $A_{sub}$ (m²). At a mean design current density of $I_d$ (A/m²), the total current required is: $$I_{total} = A_{sub} \cdot I_d \quad \text{[A]}$$ **Step 2 — Total charge required over the design life.** For a service life of $T$ years, the total charge consumed is: $$Q_{total} = I_{total} \cdot T \cdot 8760 \quad \text{[A·h]}$$ (The factor 8760 converts years to hours.) **Step 3 — Mass of anode required to deliver this charge.** A given mass of anode material $M$ can deliver $M \cdot u_e$ ampere-hours, but only a fraction $C$ of the anode is utilized before it becomes ineffective: $$Q_{delivered} = M \cdot C \cdot u_e \quad \text{[A·h]}$$ **Step 4 — Equate supply and demand:** $$M \cdot C \cdot u_e = A_{sub} \cdot I_d \cdot T \cdot 8760$$ **Step 5 — Solve for M:** $$\boxed{M = \frac{A_{sub} \cdot I_d \cdot T \cdot 8760}{C \cdot u_e} \quad \text{[kg]}}$$ **Step 6 — Number of anodes.** Each anode delivers an output current $I_{anode}$ (typically 4 to 8 A for a typical bracelet anode). The number required is: $$N = \frac{I_{total}}{I_{anode}} = \frac{A_{sub} \cdot I_d}{I_{anode}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Example (KTU Numerical Style) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem:** A supply vessel of displacement **W = 1500 tonnes** berths at **V = 0.30 m/s** against an offshore platform bumper. Given $C_m = 1.5$, $C_e = 0.8$, $C_s = 0.95$, $C_c = 1.0$, and effective fender stiffness $k_e = 500$ kN/m, determine the **equivalent static berthing force** to be applied to the bumper. **Step 1 — Design energy:** $$E = \frac{1}{2} \cdot C_m \cdot C_e \cdot C_s \cdot C_c \cdot W \cdot V^2$$ $$E = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1.5 \cdot 0.8 \cdot 0.95 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 1500 \cdot (0.30)^2$$ $$E = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1.14 \cdot 1500 \cdot 0.09$$ $$E = 0.5 \cdot 1.14 \cdot 135 = 76.95 \text{ kJ}$$ **Step 2 — Equivalent static force:** $$F = \sqrt{2 \cdot E \cdot k_e} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 76.95 \cdot 500}$$ $$F = \sqrt{76950} = 277.4 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 3 — Fender deflection check:** $$\delta = \frac{F}{k_e} = \frac{277.4}{500} = 0.555 \text{ m}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Result:** The bumper and its supporting structure must be designed for a **horizontal force of 277.4 kN** applied at the elevation of the boat hull contact, producing a corresponding overturning moment at the jacket leg. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Offshore Platform Appurtenances ```mermaid flowchart TB AirDomain["Atmospheric Zone
Wind, Helicopter Operations"] SplashDomain["Splash Zone
Wave Slam, Corrosion, Boat Impact"] SubseaDomain["Submerged Zone
Hydrodynamic Loads, Scour, Marine Growth"] SoilDomain["Seabed Domain
Pile-Soil Interaction"] subgraph AirLevel[Air Level Appurtenances] HD["Helideck"] CR["Crane Pedestal"] FB["Flare Boom"] NAVAID["Navigation Lights and Fog Horns"] end subgraph DeckLevel[Deck Level Appurtenances] WL["Walkways and Gangways"] LIFEBOAT["Life Boat Stations"] DAVIT["Davits and Hoists"] end subgraph SplashLevel[Splash Zone Appurtenances] BL["Boat Landing"] BUMP["Bumpers and Fenders"] KEEPER["Keeper Bars"] end subgraph JacketLevel[Jacket Mounted Appurtenances] JT["J-Tubes"] RIS["Risers and Caissons"] AN["Sacrificial Anodes"] end subgraph SubseaLevel[Subsea Appurtenances] PILE["Skirt Piles and Conductor Slots"] SCR["Steel Catenary Risers Protection"] end AirDomain --> AirLevel AirDomain --> DeckLevel SplashDomain --> SplashLevel SplashDomain --> JacketLevel SubseaDomain --> SubseaLevel SoilDomain --> SubseaLevel ``` ## 4.2 Boat Landing Configuration Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR J1["Jacket Leg 1"]:::leg J2["Jacket Leg 2"]:::leg BL1["Browse Level 1
Elevation +1.5 m"]:::browse BL2["Browse Level 2
Elevation +3.0 m"]:::browse BL3["Browse Level 3
Elevation +4.5 m"]:::browse KEEP["Sloped Keeper Bar
5 to 15 deg from vertical"]:::keeper SG["Safety Gate to Deck"]:::gate BOAT["Berthing Supply Vessel"]:::vessel J1 --- BL1 J1 --- BL2 J1 --- BL3 BL1 --- BL2 BL2 --- BL3 BL3 --- SG KEEP --- BL1 KEEP --- BL2 KEEP --- BL3 BOAT -.impacts.-> KEEP BOAT -.rests on.-> BL1 classDef leg fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#37474f,stroke-width:2px classDef browse fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:2px classDef keeper fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#b71c1c,stroke-width:3px classDef gate fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px classDef vessel fill:#90caf9,stroke:#0d47a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 Load Path from Appurtenance to Jacket ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Environmental Load
Wind, Wave, Berthing, Helicopter"] --> B["Appurtenance Member
Boat Landing Bumper Helideck Beam"] B --> C["Appurtenance Bracket
and Shear Plates"] C --> D["Jacket Horizontal Brace
or Leg"] D --> E["Tubular Joint Can
Critical Stress Zone"] E --> F["Pile and Foundation
Soil Resistance"] F --> G["Equilibrium Achieved
Loads Resisted by Seabed"] ``` ## 4.4 J-Tube Geometry Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph DECK[Platform Deck] TIE["Riser Tie-Down Point"] end subgraph VERT[Vertical Section along Jacket Leg] VERT_RUN["Vertical J-Tube Run
Held by Clamps"] end subgraph BEND[Bend Section] BEND_TUBE["Curved Bend Section
High Bending Stress Zone"] end subgraph SEABED[Seabed] EXIT["J-Tube Exit Point
at Mudline"] SEEP["Seabed Approach
Susceptible to Scour"] end TIE --> VERT_RUN VERT_RUN --> BEND_TUBE BEND_TUBE --> EXIT EXIT --> SEEP ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]** **Q1. Define the term "appurtenances" in the context of fixed offshore steel platforms. List any four common appurtenances with their primary function.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Appurtenances** are auxiliary structural and mechanical components attached to the primary offshore platform structure that are not part of the main load-bearing jacket framing, but are essential for the platform's functional operation, safety, and accessibility. | # | Appurtenance | Primary Function | |---|--------------|------------------| | 1 | Boat landing | Safe transfer of personnel/materials from boats | | 2 | Bumpers / fenders | Absorb accidental berthing energy of vessels | | 3 | Helideck | Helicopter landing for crew change / medevac | | 4 | J-tubes | Guide and protect risers from seabed to deck | | 5 | Sacrificial anodes | Cathodic protection against corrosion | *(Any 4 to be listed; 1 mark for definition + 0.5 each for the four = 3 marks)* --- **Q2. State the standard equation for the equivalent static berthing force on a platform bumper and explain the physical significance of the **softness coefficient** $C_s$.** **Model Answer:** $$F = \sqrt{ 2 \cdot C_m \cdot C_e \cdot C_s \cdot C_c \cdot W \cdot V^2 \cdot k_e }$$ where $C_s$ is the **softness coefficient** (typically 0.9 to 1.0). It accounts for the fact that when a **soft fender** is used, the **vessel's own hull deformation** absorbs part of the berthing energy, **reducing the load on the fender**. A rigid fender (low compliance) implies $C_s$ approaches 1.0, while a soft fender yields a lower $C_s$ and hence a smaller berthing force on the platform structure. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / May 2024 Pattern]** --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **Q. (a)** Describe in detail the **functional requirements, typical configuration, and design loads** of a **boat landing** on a fixed offshore jacket platform. **\[7 marks\]** **Model Answer:** A **boat landing** is a framed structure attached to the outer face of a jacket leg that allows safe transfer of personnel and supplies from crew boats to the platform. **Functional Requirements:** - Provide a safe platform for crew to step off the boat at any tidal state - Hold the boat securely against the structure during loading/unloading - Withstand the environmental loads of the offshore environment **Typical Configuration:** - Multiple horizontal **browse levels** (4 to 6) spaced at **1.5 m to 2.0 m** vertical intervals - A **sloped keeper bar** (5° to 15° from vertical) of typically 300 mm to 600 mm diameter pipe - A **safety gate** at the upper end connecting to the main deck walkway - Located on the leeward side of the platform, sheltered from predominant wave direction **Design Loads:** - **Boat impact / contact force** (100 to 200 kN horizontally) at the keeper bar elevation - **Wave slamming** on the keeper bar and horizontal members in the splash zone - **Buoyancy** of flooded tubular members - **Current drag** on the keeper bar - **Dead load + live load** of 2 kN/m² on browse levels - **Wind load** on the entire structure The design is carried out as per **API RP 2A** using allowable stress design, with the keeper bar designed as a cantilever carrying the maximum berthing load. --- **Q. (b)** A **helideck** of diameter **22.2 m** is to be designed for a helicopter with **MTOW = 12 tonnes**. Determine the **effective concentrated load** to be used for structural design, given the dynamic load factor DLF = 1.75. Also, compute the total **uniformly distributed load** on the deck. **\[7 marks\]** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Effective concentrated load (landing impact):** $$P_{eff} = MTOW \times (1 + DLF) \times g$$ $$P_{eff} = 12{,}000 \text{ kg} \times 2.75 \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$$ $$P_{eff} = 323{,}730 \text{ N} = 323.73 \text{ kN}$$ This load is applied through a **1.5 m × 1.5 m patch** representing the helicopter skid/track footprint. **Step 2 — Total uniformly distributed load:** The standard UDL for helidecks is **2.5 kN/m²** over the entire deck area. $$A_{deck} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (22.2)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 492.84 = 387.07 \text{ m}^2$$ $$W_{UDL} = 2.5 \times 387.07 = 967.68 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 3 — Summary:** | Load Type | Magnitude | Application | |-----------|-----------|-------------| | Concentrated (MTOW × DLF) | **323.73 kN** | 1.5 m × 1.5 m patch | | UDL | **967.68 kN** | Over 387.07 m² | | Safety net UDL | typically 0.5 kN/m² | 1.5 m wide ring | The helideck supporting structure (cantilever beams + bracing) is then designed for the **most adverse combination** of these loads with wind, wave, and buoyancy per API load combinations. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **Q. (a)** Explain the principle of **cathodic protection** of an offshore jacket platform. Derive the expression for the **required mass of sacrificial anodes** for a 20-year design life. **\[7 marks\]** **Model Answer:** **Principle of Cathodic Protection:** When two dissimilar metals are electrically connected in seawater, the more active (anodic) metal corrodes preferentially, **protecting** the more noble (cathodic) metal. In offshore platforms, **aluminum alloy (Al-Zn-In) or zinc alloy anodes** are bolted/welded to the submerged jacket. The anodes corrode (sacrifice themselves), while the jacket steel is protected. The protective current density required is typically: - **Initial:** 150 to 200 mA/m² - **Mean (steady-state):** **80 to 120 mA/m²** for bare steel, much lower for coated steel **Derivation of Anode Mass:** Total current required for the submerged area $A_{sub}$ over design life $T$: $$Q = A_{sub} \cdot I_d \cdot T \cdot 8760 \quad \text{[A·h]}$$ Mass of anode to deliver this charge, accounting for utilization factor $C$: $$M = \frac{Q}{C \cdot u_e} = \frac{A_{sub} \cdot I_d \cdot T \cdot 8760}{C \cdot u_e} \quad \text{[kg]}$$ **Numerical example (illustrative):** For $A_{sub} = 8000$ m², $I_d = 0.1$ A/m², $T = 20$ yr, $C = 0.85$, $u_e = 2500$ A·h/kg: $$M = \frac{8000 \times 0.1 \times 20 \times 8760}{0.85 \times 2500} = \frac{14{,}016{,}000}{2125} = 6595 \text{ kg}$$ This mass is distributed among **N anodes**, each of output current $I_{anode}$: $$N = \frac{A_{sub} \cdot I_d}{I_{anode}} = \frac{800}{I_{anode}}$$ For $I_{anode} = 5$ A → $N = 160$ anodes. --- **Q. (b)** Describe with neat sketches the **J-tube system** used on offshore platforms. What are the key design considerations for J-tubes? **\[7 marks\]** **Model Answer:** A **J-tube** is a large-diameter pipe (typically 16" to 36") welded to the jacket leg, shaped like the letter "J". It starts at the **mudline (seabed)**, runs along the seabed for some distance, curves upward through a **smooth bend**, and continues vertically up the jacket leg to the deck, where the riser pipe is tied down and connected to the production manifold. **Key Design Considerations:** 1. **Wave and current loading** on the long vertical and horizontal runs — analyzed using **Morison's equation** for slender members 2. **Vortex-Induced Vibration (VIV)** — slender J-tubes in currents can develop lock-in vibrations; suppressed using **strakes or fairings** 3. **Bending stress at the J-bend** — high stress concentration; the bend is reinforced with thicker walls and carefully designed radius 4. **Fatigue** — J-tubes experience millions of wave cycles; fatigue analysis per API RP 2A is mandatory 5. **Scour** at the seabed exit — the J-tube exit must be protected against scour by **mattresses or rock dumping** 6. **Cathodic protection** of the J-tube (often integrated with jacket anodes) 7. **Pipe-in-pipe** thermal expansion effects for hot production risers 8. **Pull-in and hang-off** operations during installation > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write only the *name* "J-tube" without explaining its **functional role** (riser protection), **shape rationale** (J-shape for stress distribution and installation), or **design loads** (Morison + VIV). All three are required to score full marks in the 7-mark question. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Reasons for Mark Deductions in Appurtenance Questions (KTU Board):** > 1. **Confusing appurtenances with primary structure** — Appurtenances are NOT the jacket itself; writing about jacket design in an appurtenance question is a **complete off-topic answer**. > 2. **Missing dynamic load factors** — For helideck and crane pedestal questions, the **DLF is mandatory**. Forgetting it costs ~1 to 2 marks. > 3. **No units in numerical answers** — KTU strictly requires SI units (kN, m, kN/m²) in the final answer. > 4. **Skipping the load path** — Always show how the appurtenance load is transferred to the jacket leg and finally to the foundation. > 5. **Forgetting wave/current loading** — Appurtenance members in the splash zone must include **Morison's equation** analysis. > 6. **Confusing bumpers with boat landings** — Bumpers *protect against* impact; boat landings *facilitate* personnel transfer. They are different structures. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Appurtenances** - [ ] **Definition:** Appurtenances = secondary/auxiliary structures on the platform, NOT part of the primary jacket framing. - [ ] **Boat Landing:** Multiple browse levels (1.5–2.0 m spacing) + sloped keeper bar (5°–15°); designed for 100–200 kN berthing load. - [ ] **Bumper/Fender:** Designed using energy balance: $E = \frac{1}{2} C_m C_e C_s C_c W V^2$; equivalent static force $F = \sqrt{2 E k_e}$. - [ ] **Helideck:** Standard size **D = 22.2 m** for S-92 class; UDL = **2.5 kN/m²**; concentrated load = MTOW × (1+DLF) × g, with **DLF = 1.5 to 1.75**. - [ ] **J-Tube:** J-shaped conduit for riser protection; design includes wave/current (Morison), VIV, fatigue at bend, and scour. - [ ] **Cathodic Protection:** Al-Zn-In anodes; mass formula $M = \frac{A_{sub} I_d T \cdot 8760}{C u_e}$; design life typically 20–30 years. - [ ] **Crane Pedestal:** Vertical column above deck; designed for hoisted load + wind + DAF (1.1–1.3) + slewing inertia. - [ ] **Flare Boom:** Cantilevered horizontal structure; flame tilt ≤ 5°; length 30–60 m. - [ ] **Codes:** API RP 2A, API RP 2L, ISO 19902, NORSOK N-001, CAP 437 (helideck), PIANC (fendering). - [ ] **Morison's Equation:** $F = C_d \frac{\rho D u \vert u \vert}{2} + C_m \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \rho \dot{u}$ — used for all slender appurtenance members. - [ ] **Always show the load path:** Appurtenance → Bracket → Jacket leg → Joint can → Pile → Soil. - [ ] **Environmental Loads:** Wind, wave (Morison + slamming), current, seismic, marine growth, buoyancy (flooded members). - [ ] **Common Mistake:** Treating appurtenances as "minor" — they account for **15–25% of platform structural weight** and are the leading cause of safety incidents.

topics.title: Floating Structures: Basic Configurations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST722 - FUNDAMENTALS OF OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Floating Structures: Basic Configurations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Floating Offshore Structure** is a buoyant, water-borne engineering system that derives its entire load-bearing capacity from **hydrostatic buoyancy** (Archimedes' principle) rather than from a fixed seabed foundation. According to the **KTU 2024 OENST722 syllabus**, floating structures represent a critical class of marine installations deployed in water depths where fixed jacket platforms become economically and structurally unfeasible (typically beyond **300 m**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Floating structures are compliant or partially compliant marine systems that maintain station through a combination of buoyancy, mooring tethers, and dynamic positioning (DP), while supporting topside hydrocarbon processing, drilling, or renewable energy generation facilities.

The **Basic Configurations** of floating structures refer to the standard geometric hull forms that have evolved through decades of offshore engineering practice. These include:

1. **Ship-shaped (Monohull) Structures** — Drillships, FPSOs, FSOs, Shuttle Tankers
2. **Semi-Submersibles (Column-Stabilized Units)** — Conventional, MOSAIC, Aasta Hansteen-type
3. **Spar Platforms** — Classic Spar, Truss Spar, Cell Spar
4. **Barge / Pontoon Structures** — Shallow-water drilling and production units
5. **Tension Leg Platforms (TLP)** — Vertically moored buoyant structures

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a floating offshore structure like a **giant steel ship that refuses to leave the harbor**. Just as a cruise ship floats using its hollow steel hull filled with air (displacing a weight of water equal to the ship's weight), a floating oil rig does the same — but instead of moving across oceans, it stays anchored at one spot using giant chains or tendons to the seabed, like a hot-air balloon tied to the ground.

Imagine placing a **solid steel block** in water — it sinks. Now imagine the same block **hollowed out** so that it traps a large volume of air inside. It floats! This is precisely the principle behind every floating offshore structure: engineers design a hull with enormous internal **watertight compartments** (pontoons, columns) that displace a massive volume of seawater, generating enough upward buoyant force to support the weight of the steel structure plus the topside facilities (drilling rig, processing plant, crew quarters weighing thousands of tonnes).

### 1.3 Governing Physical Principles

The fundamental physical constant relevant to this module is the **density of seawater**:

$$\rho_{sw} \approx \mathbf{1025 \,\, kg/m^3}$$

This value is **higher** than freshwater (1000 kg/m³) due to dissolved salts, which is why marine engineers must use **ρ_sw = 1025 kg/m³** in all displacement and buoyancy calculations.

The **gravitational acceleration** used in offshore engineering is standardized at:

$$g = \mathbf{9.81 \,\, m/s^2}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Examiners frequently test whether students remember to use **seawater density (1025 kg/m³)** rather than freshwater density in all offshore buoyancy problems. A common 1-mark error is using 1000 kg/m³, leading to a 2.5% underestimation of buoyancy.

---

### 1.4 Intuitive Classification of Floating Configurations

| Configuration | Visual Intuition | Primary Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Ship-shaped (Hull)** | Like a giant oil tanker with a flat helipad on top | Deep-water production, drilling |
| **Semi-submersible** | Like a table standing on two giant empty cans | Harsh-environment drilling, production |
| **Spar** | Like a giant vertical swimming pool hanging from chains | Ultra-deep-water production |
| **Barge** | Like a flat-bottomed river boat — very stable but heavy | Shallow-water (< 100 m) operations |
| **TLP** | Like a jellyfish anchored to the seabed with elastic strings | Deep-water production with minimal heave |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Fundamental Buoyancy Equation (Archimedes' Principle)

Every floating structure, regardless of its configuration, must satisfy the **fundamental floating equilibrium condition**:

$$\boxed{F_{buoyancy} = W_{total}}$$

In expanded form:

$$\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{displaced} = m_{total} \cdot g$$

Which simplifies to:

$$\boxed{\rho_{sw} \cdot V_{displaced} = m_{total}}$$

This is the **single most important equation** in floating offshore engineering. It tells us:

- To support a heavier topside → we need a **larger displaced volume** (bigger hull or deeper pontoons)
- For a given hull size → the **draft increases** until equilibrium is reached

### 2.2 Stability Concepts: The Metacentric Height

A floating structure is not just about floating — it must also **resist overturning** when subjected to waves, wind, and operational loads. The key parameter is the **metacentric height (GM)**:

$$\boxed{GM = KB + BM - KG}$$

Where:
- **KB** = Vertical distance from keel (K) to center of buoyancy (B)
- **BM** = Metacentric radius = $I / V_{displaced}$ (I = second moment of waterplane area)
- **KG** = Vertical distance from keel (K) to center of gravity (G)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability Criterion:** For **positive stability** (structure returns upright after tilting), we require:
>
> $$GM > 0 \quad \text{(typically } GM \geq 0.15 \text{ m for offshore units)}$$

### 2.3 Draft and Freeboard Relationship

The **draft (T)** is the depth of the hull below the waterline, while **freeboard (F)** is the height of the deck above the waterline. For a structure of total depth D:

$$\boxed{T + F = D}$$

The draft can be calculated from the floating equilibrium:

$$T = \frac{m_{total}}{\rho_{sw} \cdot A_{waterplane}}$$

Where $A_{waterplane}$ is the horizontal cross-sectional area of the hull at the waterline.

### 2.4 Mooring and Station-Keeping Forces

For floating structures to remain on station, they require **restoring forces** from one or more of:

1. **Catenary Mooring Systems** — Steel chains or wire ropes forming a catenary curve
2. **Taut Mooring Systems** — Synthetic fiber ropes (polyester) in straight-line tension
3. **Tension Leg Systems** — Vertical steel tendons (TLPs only)
4. **Dynamic Positioning (DP)** — Computer-controlled thrusters

The **horizontal restoring force** from a catenary mooring line is:

$$F_H = w \cdot (s - L_H)$$

Where:
- $w$ = submerged weight per unit length of chain
- $s$ = total length of chain on seabed
- $L_H$ = horizontal distance from anchor to fairlead

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $F_B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{disp}$ | Archimedes' buoyant force | N (Newtons) |
| 2 | $\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot V_{disp}$ | Displacement (mass) | kg or tonnes |
| 3 | $T = \Delta / (\rho_{sw} \cdot A_{WP})$ | Equilibrium draft | m |
| 4 | $GM = KB + BM - KG$ | Metacentric height | m |
| 5 | $BM = I_{WP} / V_{disp}$ | Metacentric radius | m |
| 6 | $I_{WP} = L \cdot B^3 / 12$ | Waterplane 2nd moment (rectangle) | m⁴ |
| 7 | $F_H = w \cdot (s - L_H)$ | Catenary horizontal force | N |
| 8 | $T_{tendon} = (W - F_B) / n$ | TLP tendon tension | N |
| 9 | $f_n = (1/2\pi)\sqrt{g \cdot k / m}$ | Heave natural frequency | Hz |
| 10 | $\tau = 2\pi \sqrt{k/m}$ | Natural period (SDOF) | s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are not academic abstractions — they are used daily by naval architects at companies like **TechnipFMC, SBM Offshore, and Aker Solutions** to design the next generation of FPSOs and semi-submersibles for fields in the Gulf of Mexico, North Sea, and offshore India (KG Basin, Mumbai High).

### 2.6 Why Each Configuration Exists — The Design Philosophy

Each floating configuration emerged as an **engineering answer to a specific operational challenge**:

- **Ship-shaped FPSO:** Storage + production in one unit; ideal for remote deep-water fields
- **Semi-submersible:** Excellent heave stability due to small waterplane area; ideal for drilling in harsh seas (North Sea, Norwegian Sea)
- **Spar:** Minimal heave response (heave natural period > 25 s, well above wave periods); ideal for ultra-deep water (> 2000 m)
- **Barge:** Low cost, simple construction; ideal for shallow, calm waters (e.g., Persian Gulf, Bay of Bengal)
- **TLP:** Vertical compliance only, minimal horizontal motion; ideal for dry tree wells in deep water

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU:** Remember the acronym **"SS-SB"** for the four main deep-water floating types: **S**hip, **S**emi-sub, **S**par, plus **B**arge (shallow water).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Floating Equilibrium Draft Equation

**Starting from first principles — Archimedes' Principle:**

**Step 1:** Consider a floating body with total mass $m_{total}$ in static equilibrium. The buoyant force must exactly balance the gravitational (weight) force.

$$F_{buoyancy} = F_{weight}$$

**Step 2:** Express the buoyant force as the weight of the displaced fluid volume $V_{disp}$:

$$F_{buoyancy} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{disp}$$

**Step 3:** Express the weight force as the product of total mass and gravity:

$$F_{weight} = m_{total} \cdot g$$

**Step 4:** Equate and cancel $g$ from both sides:

$$\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{disp} = m_{total} \cdot g$$

$$\rho_{sw} \cdot V_{disp} = m_{total}$$

**Step 5:** Assume a prismatic hull with constant waterplane area $A_{WP}$. The displaced volume equals waterplane area times draft:

$$V_{disp} = A_{WP} \cdot T$$

**Step 6:** Substitute back into the equilibrium equation:

$$\rho_{sw} \cdot A_{WP} \cdot T = m_{total}$$

**Step 7:** Solve explicitly for the equilibrium draft $T$:

$$\boxed{T = \frac{m_{total}}{\rho_{sw} \cdot A_{WP}}}$$

This is the **operating draft** of the floating structure in calm water.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Metacentric Radius BM for a Rectangular Waterplane

**Step 1:** The metacentric radius is defined as:

$$BM = \frac{I_{WP}}{V_{disp}}$$

**Step 2:** For a rectangular waterplane area of length $L$ and breadth $B$, the second moment of area about the longitudinal centerline (for roll stability) is:

$$I_{WP} = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting:

$$BM = \frac{L \cdot B^3 / 12}{V_{disp}} = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12 \cdot V_{disp}}$$

**Step 4:** If the displaced volume is approximated as $V_{disp} = L \cdot B \cdot T$:

$$BM = \frac{L \cdot B^3}{12 \cdot L \cdot B \cdot T} = \frac{B^2}{12 \cdot T}$$

$$\boxed{BM = \frac{B^2}{12T}}$$

This is a **high-yield result** for KTU problems involving rectangular hulls (barges, semi-submersible pontoons).

---

### 3.3 Derivation: TLP Tendon Tension

**Step 1:** A Tension Leg Platform is **excessively buoyant** in its installed condition — its buoyancy exceeds its weight. The tendons hold it down.

**Step 2:** Define the **pretension** as the net upward buoyant force after subtracting weight:

$$F_{excess} = F_B - W = (\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{disp}) - (m_{total} \cdot g)$$

**Step 3:** This excess force is shared equally among $n$ vertical tendons:

$$T_{tendon} = \frac{F_B - W}{n} = \frac{(\rho_{sw} \cdot V_{disp} - m_{total}) \cdot g}{n}$$

**Step 4:** In the typical TLP design, the pretension is sized so that the tendons remain **taut at all times**, even at the trough of the largest design wave:

$$\boxed{T_{tendon} = \frac{F_B - W - F_{wave,down}}{n} \geq 0}$$

Where $F_{wave,down}$ is the maximum downward hydrodynamic force at the wave trough.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Basic Configurations

| Parameter | Ship-shaped (FPSO) | Semi-submersible | Spar | Barge | TLP |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Typical Water Depth** | 50 – 3000 m | 30 – 3000 m | 500 – 3000 m | < 100 m | 300 – 2500 m |
| **Heave Natural Period** | 8 – 12 s | 15 – 25 s | 25 – 35 s | 5 – 8 s | 2 – 4 s |
| **Waterplane Area** | Very large | Small | Tiny | Very large | Small |
| **Hull Material** | Steel | Steel | Steel | Steel/concrete | Steel |
| **Motion Behavior** | Compliant | Semi-compliant | Compliant | Stiff | Vertically stiff |
| **Storage Capacity** | Up to 2×10⁶ bbl | None (production only) | None | Limited | None |
| **Station-keeping** | Spread mooring + DP | Mooring + DP | Catenary mooring | Anchors | Vertical tendons |
| **Dry Tree Wells** | No | Possible (MOSAIC) | Yes | No | Yes (native) |
| **Examples** | Marlim, FPSO Espoir | Scarabeo 9, Asgard B | Perdido, Hoover | Trident | Hibernia, Jolliet |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A rectangular steel barge has length $L = 80$ m, breadth $B = 20$ m, and depth $D = 8$ m. Its total mass (hull + topside) is $m = 12,000$ tonnes. Calculate:
- (a) The operating draft in seawater
- (b) The metacentric radius $BM$ about the longitudinal axis
- (c) The freeboard

**Solution:**

**(a) Draft calculation:**

Convert mass: $m = 12,000 \times 1000 = 12,000,000$ kg

Waterplane area: $A_{WP} = L \times B = 80 \times 20 = 1600$ m²

Apply the draft formula:

$$T = \frac{m}{\rho_{sw} \cdot A_{WP}} = \frac{12,000,000}{1025 \times 1600}$$

$$T = \frac{12,000,000}{1,640,000} = 7.317 \text{ m}$$

**(b) Metacentric radius:**

$$BM = \frac{B^2}{12T} = \frac{20^2}{12 \times 7.317} = \frac{400}{87.80} = 4.556 \text{ m}$$

**(c) Freeboard:**

$$F = D - T = 8 - 7.317 = 0.683 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** The freeboard is small (0.68 m), indicating the barge is heavily loaded. In real offshore practice, such low freeboard is dangerous and additional buoyancy (pontoons) would be added.

---

### 3.6 Derivation: Catenary Mooring Line Geometry

**Step 1:** Consider a chain of total length $L$ suspended between the fairlead (at the hull) and the anchor (at the seabed), forming a catenary curve.

**Step 2:** Define the suspended length $s$ (the part hanging in water) and the part lying on the seabed.

**Step 3:** The classic catenary equation in the horizontal-vertical plane:

$$y = a \cdot \cosh\left(\frac{x}{a}\right) - a$$

Where $a = T_H / w$ is the catenary parameter (ratio of horizontal tension to submerged weight per unit length).

**Step 4:** The horizontal restoring force is:

$$\boxed{F_H = w \cdot \sqrt{s^2 - h^2} - T_{anchor}}$$

For typical catenary systems, $T_{anchor} \approx 0$ (just lifts off the seabed), giving:

$$F_H = w \cdot \sqrt{s^2 - h^2}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Hint:** For catenary problems, you will typically be given $w$, $s$, $h$ and asked to find $F_H$. Memorize the formula $F_H = w \sqrt{s^2 - h^2}$ and you will score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Classification of Floating Offshore Structures

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["FLOATING OFFSHORE STRUCTURES"] --> B0["DEEP WATER DAMP GT 100M"]
    A0 --> C0["SHALLOW WATER DAMP LT 100M"]
    
    B0 --> D0["MOORED COMPLIANT"]
    B0 --> E0["MOORED STIFF"]
    B0 --> F0["DYNAMIC POSITIONED"]
    
    D0 --> G0["SHIP SHAPED FPSO"]
    D0 --> H0["SEMI SUBMERSIBLE"]
    D0 --> I0["SPAR PLATFORM"]
    
    E0 --> J0["TENSION LEG PLATFORM"]
    E0 --> K0["MINI TLP"]
    
    F0 --> L0["DRILLSHIP"]
    F0 --> M0["DP SEMI SUB"]
    
    C0 --> N0["BARGE"]
    C0 --> O0["JACKUP BARGE"]
    C0 --> P0["PONTOON UNIT"]
    
    G0 --> G1["HULL FORM LONGITUDINAL"]
    H0 --> H1["COLUMNS AND PONTOONS"]
    I0 --> I1["VERTICAL CYLINDER"]
    J0 --> J1["PONTOONS WITH TENDONS"]
    L0 --> L1["SHIP SHAPED WITH THRUSTERS"]
    N0 --> N1["FLAT BOTTOM RECTANGULAR"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Semi-Submersible

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["TOPSIDE DECK DRY TREES PROCESSING ACCOMMODATION"] --> B1["UPPER DECK STRUCTURE"]
    B1 --> C1["BRACING FRAMEWORK"]
    C1 --> D1["TRANSVERSE PONTOONS 3 OR 4 UNITS"]
    C1 --> E1["VERTICAL COLUMNS 4 TO 8 UNITS"]
    D1 --> F1["LOWER HULL PONTOONS BUOYANCY TANKS"]
    E1 --> G1["BALLLAST TANKS FOR DRAFT ADJUSTMENT"]
    F1 --> H1["MOORING CHAIN CONNECTORS FAIRLEADS"]
    G1 --> I1["SUBSEA ANCHOR FOUNDATION"]
    H1 --> I1
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Spar Platform

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2["TOPSIDE DRILLING AND PRODUCTION DECK"] --> B2["HARD TANK BUOYANCY SECTION"]
    A2 --> C2["PRODUCTION RISERS FLEXIBLE"]
    B2 --> D2["TRUSS SECTION CROSS BRACED TUBES"]
    D2 --> E2["SOFT TANK BUOYANCY SECTION"]
    E2 --> F2["MOORING CHAIN ATTACHMENT POINTS"]
    F2 --> G2["CATENARY MOORING LINES TO SEABED"]
    A2 --> H2["HEAVE PLATE AT KEEL DAMPING"]
    E2 --> H2
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Architecture of a TLP

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A3["TOPSIDE PRODUCTION DECK"] --> B3["PONTOON BUOYANCY 4 UNITS"]
    A3 --> C3["DRY TREE WELLS VERTICAL"]
    B3 --> D3["COLUMNS 4 CORNER POSTS"]
    D3 --> E3["TLP FOUNDATION TEMPLATE PILE"]
    E3 --> F3["VERTICAL TENDONS 3 OR 4"]
    F3 --> G3["TLP BASE ON SEABED"]
    A3 --> H3["TOP TENSIONED RISERS"]
    F3 --> H3
```

### 4.5 Schematic Table — Visual Comparison of Hull Forms

| Configuration | Side View Schematic | Plan View Schematic |
|---|---|---|
| **Ship-shaped (FPSO)** | Long horizontal hull, deckhouse on top, turret at bow/stern | Long rectangular with mooring chain spread |
| **Semi-submersible** | Two parallel pontoons (underwater) connected by columns to elevated deck | Symmetric rectangular with corner columns |
| **Spar** | Tall vertical cylinder, partially submerged, deep draft | Circular in plan view |
| **Barge** | Flat shallow rectangular box hull with low profile | Simple rectangle |
| **TLP** | Four corner columns with pontoons at base, vertical tendons | Square in plan view |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note to Students:** Since this is a text-based Mermaid block, only the functional block architecture is rendered. For visual hull form diagrams, refer to standard textbooks such as *Chakrabarti (2005)* — *Handbook of Offshore Engineering*, which contains the definitive line drawings of each configuration.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**Define a floating offshore structure. List the four main basic configurations used in the oil and gas industry.**

**Model Answer:**

A floating offshore structure is a buoyant marine installation that derives its load-bearing capacity entirely from **hydrostatic buoyancy** (Archimedes' principle) rather than from a fixed seabed foundation. It maintains its position through mooring systems, tendons, or dynamic positioning.

The four main basic configurations are:

1. **Ship-shaped structures** (FPSOs, FSOs, drillships)
2. **Column-stabilized units / Semi-submersibles**
3. **Spar platforms**
4. **Tension Leg Platforms (TLPs)**

*(Barges are also sometimes listed as a fifth configuration for shallow-water applications.)*

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**State Archimedes' principle as applied to floating offshore structures. What is the standard density of seawater used in offshore engineering?**

**Model Answer:**

**Archimedes' Principle:** A body fully or partially immersed in a fluid experiences an upward buoyant force equal to the **weight of the fluid displaced** by the body.

Mathematically:

$$F_B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{disp}$$

The **standard density of seawater** used in offshore engineering calculations is:

$$\rho_{sw} = \mathbf{1025 \,\, kg/m^3}$$

---

### **Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model 2024]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **basic configuration of a semi-submersible floating structure**. Discuss its advantages over a ship-shaped FPSO for operations in harsh sea environments. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rectangular barge of length **90 m**, breadth **25 m**, and depth **9 m** carries a topside mass of **15,000 tonnes**. The hull mass is **5,000 tonnes**. Calculate:
- (i) The operating draft in seawater
- (ii) The metacentric radius $BM$ (assume rectangular waterplane)
- (iii) The freeboard at this loading condition **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Semi-submersible configuration (7 marks):**

A **semi-submersible** consists of the following main components:

- **Pontoons (Lower Hulls):** 2 to 4 large horizontal cylindrical or rectangular buoyancy tanks placed underwater, providing the bulk of the displaced volume.
- **Vertical Columns:** 4 to 8 vertical columns rising from the pontoons, supporting the deck above the waterline.
- **Upper Deck (Topside):** The working platform carrying drilling/processing equipment, accommodation, helideck.
- **Bracing:** Diagonal and horizontal members connecting the columns to provide structural integrity.
- **Ballast Tanks:** Compartments that can be flooded or deballasted to adjust draft and stability.

**Sketch (in words):** The structure resembles a "table standing on two cans" — the table is the deck, the cans are the columns, and the lower part is the submerged pontoon.

**Advantages over ship-shaped FPSO in harsh environments:**

1. **Smaller waterplane area** → Heave natural period (15–25 s) is well separated from wave periods (5–15 s) → reduced vertical motion.
2. **Better motion characteristics** in roll and pitch due to the wide column spacing and small waterplane area.
3. **Higher deck clearance** (air gap) → reduces wave slamming and green water loading.
4. **Excellent stability** in survival conditions (e.g., 100-year storm).
5. **Used extensively in the North Sea, Norwegian Sea, and offshore Brazil** where wave heights exceed 10 m.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Length $L = 90$ m
- Breadth $B = 25$ m
- Depth $D = 9$ m
- Topside mass $m_{top} = 15,000$ tonnes = $15,000,000$ kg
- Hull mass $m_{hull} = 5,000$ tonnes = $5,000,000$ kg

**Step 1: Total mass**

$$m_{total} = 15,000,000 + 5,000,000 = 20,000,000 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2: Waterplane area**

$$A_{WP} = L \times B = 90 \times 25 = 2250 \text{ m}^2$$

**(i) Operating draft:**

$$T = \frac{m_{total}}{\rho_{sw} \cdot A_{WP}} = \frac{20,000,000}{1025 \times 2250}$$

$$T = \frac{20,000,000}{2,306,250} = 8.672 \text{ m}$$

**(ii) Metacentric radius $BM$:**

$$BM = \frac{B^2}{12T} = \frac{25^2}{12 \times 8.672} = \frac{625}{104.06} = 6.006 \text{ m}$$

**(iii) Freeboard:**

$$F = D - T = 9 - 8.672 = 0.328 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The freeboard is critically low (33 cm), suggesting the barge is near its maximum capacity. In practice, additional safety margin would require a deeper hull or larger breadth.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Describe the **basic configuration of a Spar platform**. With the help of a diagram, explain its three main structural sections and discuss why it is preferred for ultra-deep-water applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A semi-submersible hull in operating condition has a displacement of **48,000 tonnes** and a waterplane second moment of area $I_{WP} = 1.2 \times 10^5$ m⁴. The center of buoyancy is at KB = **8.5 m** above the keel, and the center of gravity is at KG = **14.2 m** above the keel. Calculate:
- (i) The metacentric radius $BM$
- (ii) The metacentric height $GM$
- (iii) Comment on the stability of the structure. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Spar platform configuration (7 marks):**

A **Spar platform** is a deep-draft, cylindrical floating structure used for ultra-deep-water oil and gas production. It has three main sections:

1. **Hard Tank (Upper Buoyancy Section):** The uppermost sealed compartment providing positive buoyancy. Can be flooded during tow-out to lower the structure.
2. **Truss Section (Middle):** A space-frame of tubular members that reduces weight and material cost while allowing the Spar to "swing" through waves with minimum hydrodynamic loading.
3. **Soft Tank (Lower Buoyancy Section):** The bottom sealed compartment, often containing the **ballast** (typically heavy iron ore or mineral drilling cuttings) needed to lower the center of gravity and increase stability.

**Why preferred for ultra-deep water (> 2000 m):**

- **Very small waterplane area** (spar diameter is small, ~30–40 m) → Heave natural period is **25–35 seconds**, which is far longer than any wave period (4–20 s). This means the Spar does **not respond to wave heave**, behaving like a fixed structure vertically.
- **Deep draft** (typically 150–200 m) → wave particle motion is negligible at this depth → minimal wave excitation.
- **Catenary mooring** provides station-keeping without the need for a complex tendon system.
- Examples: **Hoover Spar** (Gulf of Mexico, 1463 m), **Perdido Spar** (2438 m — world record at installation).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Displacement $\Delta = 48,000$ tonnes = $48,000,000$ kg
- $I_{WP} = 1.2 \times 10^5$ m⁴
- $KB = 8.5$ m
- $KG = 14.2$ m
- $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³

**Step 1: Displaced volume**

$$V_{disp} = \frac{\Delta}{\rho_{sw}} = \frac{48,000,000}{1025} = 46,829.27 \text{ m}^3$$

**(i) Metacentric radius $BM$:**

$$BM = \frac{I_{WP}}{V_{disp}} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^5}{46,829.27} = 2.5625 \text{ m}$$

**(ii) Metacentric height $GM$:**

$$GM = KB + BM - KG$$

$$GM = 8.5 + 2.5625 - 14.2 = -3.1375 \text{ m}$$

**(iii) Stability comment:**

Since $GM = -3.14$ m is **negative**, the structure is **unstable in its current condition** — it will capsize if tilted. This is a serious design flaw that must be corrected by:
- Lowering the center of gravity $G$ (e.g., adding ballast in the keel)
- Increasing the waterplane second moment $I_{WP}$ (wider columns)
- Reducing $KB$ by lowering the center of buoyancy

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical Required GM:** For offshore semi-submersibles, $GM$ is required to be **between 0.5 m and 3.0 m** for adequate stability without excessive rigidity.

---

### 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
>
> 1. **Using freshwater density (1000 kg/m³) instead of seawater (1025 kg/m³)** — This is the **#1 most common error** in KTU floating structure problems. Examiner will deduct 1 mark minimum.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to include the topside mass** — Students often compute draft using only the hull mass. The total mass must include topside, ballast, and payload.
>
> 3. **Confusing $I_{WP}$ and $I$** — $I_{WP}$ is the second moment of the **waterplane area** (at the waterline), not of the underwater hull. For roll, use $B^3/12$; for pitch, use $L^3/12$.
>
> 4. **Sign convention in $GM = KB + BM - KG$** — Many students incorrectly write $GM = KG - KB - BM$ and end up with a wrong sign, flipping the stability conclusion.
>
> 5. **Mixing up FPSO vs FSO vs FSU** — FPSO = Floating Production Storage Offloading (processes oil); FSO = Floating Storage Offloading (no processing); FSU = Floating Storage Unit (simplest). Examiners love to test this distinction.
>
> 6. **In sketches:** Always label major components (pontoons, columns, deck, mooring lines). A sketch without labels gets only 50% marks even if correct.

---

### 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION CHECKLIST — Floating Structures: Basic Configurations**

✅ **Archimedes' Principle** is the foundation: $F_B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{disp}$

✅ **Standard seawater density** is **1025 kg/m³** — not 1000!

✅ **Five basic configurations:** Ship-shaped FPSO, Semi-submersible, Spar, Barge, TLP

✅ **Floating equilibrium:** Buoyancy = Weight → $\rho_{sw} \cdot V_{disp} = m_{total}$

✅ **Draft formula:** $T = m_{total} / (\rho_{sw} \cdot A_{WP})$

✅ **Metacentric height:** $GM = KB + BM - KG > 0$ for stability

✅ **Metacentric radius (rectangular):** $BM = B^2 / (12T)$ for roll

✅ **FPSO** = production + storage in one unit; **Drillship** = drilling only

✅ **Semi-submersible** has small waterplane → heave period 15–25 s

✅ **Spar** has very small waterplane → heave period 25–35 s (wave periods are 4–20 s)

✅ **TLP** has **vertical tendons** that hold it down (excess buoyancy)

✅ **Mooring types:** Catenary, Taut, Tendons, DP thrusters

✅ **TLP tendon tension:** $T_{tendon} = (F_B - W)/n$

✅ **Catenary horizontal force:** $F_H = w \cdot \sqrt{s^2 - h^2}$

✅ **Hull materials:** Steel (most common), concrete (some Spar designs, e.g., Heidrun)

✅ **Water depth ranges:** Barge (< 100 m) → TLP/Semi/FPSO (up to 3000 m) → Spar (up to 3000 m)

✅ **Stability criterion:** $GM \geq 0.15$ m minimum (offshore), $GM$ in range **0.5–3.0 m** typical

✅ **Sketch essentials:** Always show pontoons, columns, deck, mooring/anchors, and label draft and freeboard

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Strategy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST722 - FUNDAMENTALS OF OFFSHORE STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Design Strategy # MODULE 3 — DESIGN STRATEGY FOR OFFSHORE STRUCTURES ## 1.
