Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Design Strategy** in offshore engineering is the systematic, code-compliant, and risk-informed framework that governs how an offshore structure (fixed platform, floating system, subsea installation, or FPSO) is conceived, proportioned, analyzed, and verified against environmental, operational, accidental, and fatigue loads throughout its service life. It establishes the design philosophy (working stress vs. limit state), the target reliability level, the governing limit states, the load and resistance factor formats, and the verification procedures prescribed by classification societies and certifying authorities such as **API (American Petroleum Institute)**, **DNV (Det Norske Veritas)**, **ISO 19900-series**, and **ABS (American Bureau of Shipping)**. In the context of the KTU 2024 scheme, *Design Strategy* is treated as a **holistic, lifecycle-based decision-making process** that integrates hydrodynamics, structural mechanics, geotechnics, material science, fabrication logistics, and inspection/maintenance planning into a single coherent engineering workflow. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Cruise Ship Captain's Rulebook.** > Imagine you are the captain of a massive cruise ship that must sail through a hurricane season, polar ice, calm bays, and crowded ports — for 25 years. You do not design the ship for *one* worst storm; you design it for a *spectrum* of expected conditions, with a clear hierarchy: > 1. **Everyday comfort** (SLS) → no excessive vibration for passengers. > 2. **Surviving a once-in-50-year storm** (ULS) → ship must not break apart. > 3. **Surviving 25 years of wave slap, slam, and vibration** (FLS) → no fatigue cracks. > 4. **Surviving a collision or fire** (ALS) → ship must not sink immediately, allowing evacuation. > > The *Design Strategy* is exactly this rulebook — but for a steel jacket in 200 m of water. The captain (designer) writes down, **before drawing a single line**, what *failure means* and *how unlikely* it must be. This is the heart of design strategy. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Numerical Anchors Used in Offshore Design Strategy** > - **Design service life of a fixed offshore platform:** typically **25 years** (some Norwegian Sector rules require 30+ years). > - **Return period for ULS environmental load (100-year storm):** $T_R = 100$ years. > - **Return period for ALS (accidental):** typically $10^4$ to $10^5$ wave conditions, but with reduced safety factors. > - **Annual probability of failure (target reliability) for ULS:** $\beta \approx 3.7$ (equivalent to $P_f \approx 10^{-4}$ per year). > - **Annual probability of failure for ALS:** $\beta \approx 2.3$ to $2.6$ (i.e., $P_f \approx 10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$). > - **Partial safety factor on environmental loads (ULS, ULS-SEA):** $\gamma_E \approx 1.35$ in ISO 19902 / NORSOK. > - **Partial safety factor on material resistance:** $\gamma_M \approx 1.05$ to $1.25$ depending on code. --- ## 2. Why a "Strategy" and Not Just a "Design"? The word *strategy* is deliberate. Offshore structures are unique among civil engineering artefacts because: | Aspect | Onshore Civil Structure | Offshore Structure | |---|---|---| | **Load predictability** | Quasi-static, well-known | Highly stochastic, wave-current-wind coupled | | **Inspectability** | Easy, continuous | Extremely difficult, underwater | | **Repairability** | Routine | Cost-prohibitive, weather-dependent | | **Consequence of failure** | Localised | Catastrophic (life, environment, asset, hydrocarbon release) | | **Design horizon** | 50–100 years | 25–30 years (then decommission) | > [!NOTE] > Because **inspection and repair are 10×–100× more expensive offshore than onshore**, the design must *front-load* safety. This is why offshore codes use **partial factor (LRFD) limit-state design** rather than the older *working stress design (WSD)*. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Offshore Design Strategy A complete design strategy rests on **four pillars**. Mastering these is essential for KTU Module-3 questions. ### Pillar 1 — Design Philosophy Two competing philosophies exist: **(a) Working Stress Design (WSD) — Allowable Stress Design (ASD)** $$\sigma_{applied} \le \frac{\sigma_{yield}}{FS}$$ where $FS$ is a *single* global factor of safety, typically $1.5$ to $2.0$ for steel offshore structures under combined loading. **(b) Limit State Design (LSD) — Load and Resistance Factor Design (LRFD)** $$\sum_i \gamma_i \cdot S_{n,i} \le \phi \cdot R_n = \frac{R_n}{\gamma_M}$$ where each load and resistance has its *own* partial safety factor calibrated to a target reliability index $\beta$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why LRFD has won the offshore world:** WSD applies a *blanket* safety factor. LRFD applies *targeted* factors — large where uncertainty is high (e.g., environmental loads, soil capacity) and small where uncertainty is low (e.g., steel yield stress). This produces a structure that is **more uniformly reliable across all failure modes**, not over-safe in some places and under-safe in others. ### Pillar 2 — Limit States (the "Failure Menu") A limit state is a condition beyond which the structure no longer satisfies the design requirements. Offshore codes recognise **four families**: | Code Abbrev. | Limit State | What it Checks | Return Period / Basis | |---|---|---|---| | **SLS** | Serviceability Limit State | Deflection, vibration, rotation exceed service thresholds (e.g., conductor inclination, deck drift) | Frequent / operational conditions | | **ULS** | Ultimate Limit State | Collapse, buckling, yielding, foundation failure under extreme load | 100-year return period (ULS-SEA) | | **FLS** | Fatigue Limit State | Cumulative damage from cyclic wave loading $D = \sum \frac{n_i}{N_i} < 1$ | Spectral fatigue analysis over 25 yrs | | **ALS** | Accidental Limit State | Survival after a discrete event (vessel impact, fire, dropped object, blowout) | Single-event, $P_f$ target lower | > [!NOTE] > In ISO 19902 and API RP 2A-WSD, ULS is sometimes further split into: > - **ULS-SEA** (Severe Environmental Assessment) — intact structure, 100-yr storm. > - **ULS-AEA** (Abnormal Environmental Assessment) — abnormal metocean conditions, sometimes 1000-yr return. ### Pillar 3 — Load Modelling Hierarchy Loads are categorised into **six families** (ISO 19900 / API): 1. **Permanent (G):** Self-weight, ballast, permanent equipment. 2. **Variable functional (Q):** Live load from personnel, drilling supplies, product in storage tanks. 3. **Environmental (E):** Wind, wave, current, ice, snow, earthquake. 4. **Deformation (D):** Settlement, creep, thermal expansion. 5. **Accidental (A):** Collision, fire, explosion, dropped object. 6. **Fatigue-induced** (treated implicitly via FLS check, not as a separate load case). ### Pillar 4 — Reliability Calibration The target reliability index $\beta$ is the linchpin. It is defined via: $$P_f = \Phi(-\beta) = \int_{-\infty}^{-\beta} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{t^2}{2}\right) dt$$ where $\Phi$ is the standard normal CDF. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reliability–Probability of Failure Conversion** > - $\beta = 2.0 \Rightarrow P_f \approx 2.3 \times 10^{-2}$ (ALS) > - $\beta = 3.0 \Rightarrow P_f \approx 1.3 \times 10^{-3}$ > - $\beta = 3.7 \Rightarrow P_f \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-4}$ (ULS, ISO 19902) > - $\beta = 4.0 \Rightarrow P_f \approx 3.2 \times 10^{-5}$ > - $\beta = 4.7 \Rightarrow P_f \approx 1.3 \times 10^{-6}$ (FLS for redundant members) --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table is the single most important high-yield reference for Module 3. Memorise the structure, not just the formulas.** | # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Working Stress (WSD) design | $\sigma_a \le \dfrac{\sigma_y}{FS}$ | $FS = 1.5$ to $2.0$ (offshore steel) | | 2 | Limit State (LRFD) design | $\sum \gamma_i S_{n,i} \le \phi R_n = \dfrac{R_n}{\gamma_M}$ | $\gamma_i, \gamma_M$ from code | | 3 | Reliability index → $P_f$ | $P_f = \Phi(-\beta)$ | $\Phi$ = Std normal CDF | | 4 | Log-normal $P_f$ for resistance $R$ and load $S$ | $\beta = \dfrac{\ln(R_m/S_m)}{\sqrt{V_R^2 + V_S^2}}$ | $V_R, V_S$ are COVs | | 5 | Miner's Rule (Fatigue damage) | $D = \sum_i \dfrac{n_i}{N_i} \le 1$ | $n_i$ = cycles at stress range, $N_i$ = capacity | | 6 | 100-yr return period (Gumbel) | $x_T = \mu + \sigma \cdot \left[-\ln\!\left(-\ln(1-1/T)\right)\right]$ | $T$ in years | | 7 | Pile capacity (Axial) | $Q_d = \dfrac{Q_s}{\gamma_{Rs}} + \dfrac{Q_p}{\gamma_{Rp}}$ | API RP 2A formulation | | 8 | Load combination (ULS, ISO) | $1.1 G + 1.3 Q + 1.3 E$ (typical) | Per ISO 19902 | | 9 | Load combination (ALS) | $1.0 G + 0.7 Q + 1.0 A$ (or with $0.9 E$) | Reduced $\gamma$ for accidental | | 10 | Partial factor on env. load | $\gamma_E = 1.35$ (ULS-SEA) | May be 0.7–1.0 for ALS combos | | 11 | Material factor (steel) | $\gamma_M = 1.05$ (yield), $1.25$ (ultimate) | Per code | | 12 | Probability of failure target | $P_f \le 10^{-4}$ / yr (ULS), $\beta \ge 3.7$ | ISO 19902 | | 13 | Fatigue design factor (DF) | $DF = \dfrac{\text{allowable stress range}}{\text{hot-spot stress range}}$ | $DF \ge 2.0$ for joints | | 14 | Conductor guide tolerance | $\theta_{tol} \le 0.5^\circ$ from vertical | SLS check | | 15 | Reserve Strength Ratio (RSR) | $RSR = \dfrac{R_{ult}}{L_{100-yr}}$ | Should be $\ge 1.3$ for fixed platforms | > **Notation:** $G$ = permanent load, $Q$ = variable functional, $E$ = environmental, $A$ = accidental, $S_n$ = nominal load effect, $R_n$ = nominal resistance, $\phi$ = resistance factor, $V_R$ = COV of resistance, $V_S$ = COV of load, $\mu, \sigma$ = mean, std dev. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Utility of the Design Strategy Framework > [!NOTE] > The design strategy is not academic decoration — it is the legal and contractual basis for the entire offshore project. Insurance underwriters (e.g., Lloyd's, GIC, Munich Re) require a **certified design strategy** before providing **Energy Insurance** for an offshore platform worth USD 500 million to USD 5 billion. The certifying authority (DNV, ABS, BV) audits the strategy document before any drawing is approved. A poorly framed design strategy is a direct cause of *project delay*, *budget overrun*, and in tragic cases, *catastrophic failure* (e.g., Ocean Ranger, 1982; Alexander Kielland, 1980 — both involved inadequate limit-state thinking and over-reliance on WSD without FLS). --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Working Stress to Reliability-Based LRFD This derivation shows *why* and *how* partial safety factors emerge from reliability theory — a frequent KTU Part-B question. ### Step 0 — Define the Failure Function Let $R$ be the structural resistance (e.g., ultimate moment capacity) and $S$ the load effect (e.g., applied bending moment). Both are random variables. Failure occurs when: $$g(R,S) = R - S < 0$$ > The *limit state* is the boundary where $g(R,S) = 0$. ### Step 1 — Assume Lognormal Distributions Offshore resistance $R$ and load $S$ are typically modelled as **lognormal** random variables (cannot be negative, skewed right). Let $R_m$ and $S_m$ be their medians, and $V_R$, $V_S$ their coefficients of variation (COV = std dev / mean). For a lognormal variable $X$: $$\text{Mean: } \mu_X = X_m \sqrt{1 + V_X^2}, \quad \text{Variance: } \sigma_X^2 = \mu_X^2 V_X^2$$ ### Step 2 — Linearise Using the First-Order Second-Moment (FOSM) Method The reliability index in FOSM is defined at the *design point* where the limit state $g = 0$ is tangent to the failure surface: $$\beta = \frac{\mu_{R-S}}{\sigma_{R-S}}$$ For lognormal $R$ and $S$ that are *independent*, expanding the linearised form yields: **Step 2a — Mean and variance of the difference:** $$\mu_{R-S} = \mu_R - \mu_S, \quad \sigma_{R-S}^2 = \sigma_R^2 + \sigma_S^2$$ **Step 2b — Substituting the lognormal moments:** $$\mu_R = R_m \sqrt{1 + V_R^2}, \quad \sigma_R = R_m \sqrt{V_R^2 + V_R^4}$$ **Step 2c — For typical offshore COVs ($V_R, V_S \ll 1$, so $V^4 \ll V^2$), drop the higher-order term:** $$\sigma_R \approx R_m V_R, \quad \sigma_S \approx S_m V_S$$ **Step 2d — Substituting back into $\beta$:** $$ \begin{aligned} \beta &= \frac{\mu_R - \mu_S}{\sqrt{\sigma_R^2 + \sigma_S^2}} \\ &= \frac{R_m \sqrt{1 + V_R^2} - S_m \sqrt{1 + V_S^2}}{\sqrt{R_m^2 V_R^2 (1+V_R^2) + S_m^2 V_S^2 (1+V_S^2)}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2e — Again, for $V_R, V_S \ll 1$, $\sqrt{1+V^2} \approx 1$:** $$ \boxed{\;\beta \;\approx\; \frac{\ln(R_m / S_m)}{\sqrt{V_R^2 + V_S^2}}\;} $$ This is the **Hasofer–Lind / Cornell reliability index** for lognormal variables — a *very common* formula in KTU questions and offshore reliability textbooks. ### Step 3 — Inverse Problem: Find the Central Safety Factor From Step 2e, solving for the median safety factor $FS_{med} = R_m / S_m$: $$ \begin{aligned} \beta \sqrt{V_R^2 + V_S^2} &= \ln(R_m / S_m) \\ R_m / S_m &= \exp\!\left(\beta \sqrt{V_R^2 + V_S^2}\right) \\ \end{aligned} $$ Therefore, the **central (median) factor of safety** required to achieve a target $\beta$ is: $$ \boxed{\;FS_{med} \;=\; \exp\!\left(\beta \sqrt{V_R^2 + V_S^2}\right)\;} $$ **Numerical Illustration (a KTU-favourite problem type):** Given: $V_R = 0.15$, $V_S = 0.25$, target $\beta = 3.7$ (ULS). Find $FS_{med}$. $$ \begin{aligned} \beta \sqrt{V_R^2 + V_S^2} &= 3.7 \times \sqrt{0.15^2 + 0.25^2} \\ &= 3.7 \times \sqrt{0.0225 + 0.0625} \\ &= 3.7 \times \sqrt{0.0850} \\ &= 3.7 \times 0.2915 \\ &= 1.0786 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{\;FS_{med} = e^{1.0786} \approx 2.94\;} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** When the load uncertainty is large (storm waves), the *central* safety factor must be ~3.0 — not 1.5 — to deliver the 100-yr reliability level. This is the mathematical reason WSD's blanket factor of 1.5–2.0 is *inadequate* for offshore platforms under environmental loading. ### Step 4 — Convert Central FS to Partial Factors (LRFD Format) LRFD expresses safety through *split* factors: $$\gamma_S \cdot S_n \le \frac{R_n}{\gamma_R} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad R_n / S_n \ge \gamma_S \gamma_R$$ By calibrating so that $R_n = R_m / k_R$ and $S_n = k_S S_m$ (where $k_R, k_S$ are fractile factors), and matching the target $\beta$, one obtains code-prescribed values such as $\gamma_S = 1.35$, $\gamma_R = 1.05$ to $1.25$. **Closed-form calibration (Cornell-style):** $$ \begin{aligned} \gamma_S &= FS_{med} \cdot \frac{k_S \sqrt{1 + V_S^2}}{1} \\ \gamma_R &= \frac{1}{k_R \sqrt{1 + V_R^2}} \end{aligned} $$ with $k_S$ being the load fractile (e.g., 98th percentile for $S_n$) and $k_R$ the resistance fractile (e.g., 5th percentile for $R_n$). --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Miner's Rule and Fatigue Damage (FLS) ### Step 1 — S-N Curve (Basquin-type) For a tubular joint under wave loading, the number of cycles to failure at stress range $\Delta\sigma$ is: $$N = K \cdot (\Delta\sigma)^{-m}$$ where $K$ is a fatigue strength coefficient and $m$ is the S-N slope (typically $m = 3$ for tubular joints in air, $m = 5$ for in seawater with cathodic protection — per DNV-RP-C203 / API RP 2A). ### Step 2 — Miner's Linear Damage Rule For a stress-range spectrum $\{(\Delta\sigma_i, n_i)\}$ over the design life: $$D = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i (\Delta\sigma_i)^{m}}{K}$$ The FLS check requires $D \le 1$ (or $D \le \eta$ where $\eta$ is a design fatigue factor, often 0.5–3.0 depending on inspectability). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Miner's rule is *linear* and *non-interactional*.** It assumes each cycle's damage accumulates independently. In reality, sequence effects (e.g., overload underload) and mean-stress shifts can alter damage, but the rule remains the industry baseline because of its simplicity and conservative calibration. ### Step 3 — Spectral Fatigue (Continuous Form) For a narrow-band Gaussian process with stress-range spectrum $S_\sigma(\omega)$, the Rayleigh distribution of peaks gives: $$D = \frac{T_L}{K} \cdot \int_0^\infty f^0 \cdot S_\sigma(f)^m \cdot (2\sqrt{2} \sigma_{rms} \sqrt{2})^m \, df$$ where $T_L$ is the design life in seconds and $f^0$ is the zero-crossing frequency. This is the **Dirlik / Wirsching** spectral fatigue formulation used in spectral FLS analysis. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Return Period and Design Wave Height For a long-term distribution of individual wave heights, the Gumbel distribution is standard: $$F(H) = \exp\!\left(-\exp\!\left(-\frac{H - a}{b}\right)\right)$$ The $T$-year return value is found from $F(H_T) = 1 - 1/T$: $$ \begin{aligned} 1 - \frac{1}{T} &= \exp\!\left(-\exp\!\left(-\frac{H_T - a}{b}\right)\right) \\ -\frac{1}{T} &\approx -\exp\!\left(-\frac{H_T - a}{b}\right) \quad \text{(rare-event approximation)} \\ \exp\!\left(-\frac{H_T - a}{b}\right) &= \frac{1}{T} \\ \frac{H_T - a}{b} &= -\ln(1/T) = \ln T \end{aligned} $$ $$ \boxed{\;H_T = a + b \cdot \ln T\;} $$ For $T = 100$ years: $$H_{100} = a + b \ln(100) = a + 4.605\, b$$ > **Where $a$ and $b$ are the Gumbel location and scale parameters, estimated from the sample mean $\bar{H}$ and standard deviation $s_H$ of annual maxima:** > $$a = \bar{H} - 0.5772\, b, \quad b = \frac{s_H}{\pi / \sqrt{6}} \approx \frac{s_H}{1.2825}$$ --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Design Strategy Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A0([Project Initiation]) --> A1[Define Functional

Calibrated to Beta] A6a --> A7[Identify Governing
Limit States] A6b --> A7 A7 --> A7a[SLS] A7 --> A7b[ULS] A7 --> A7c[FLS] A7 --> A7d[ALS] A7a --> A8[Build Load
Model Set] A7b --> A8 A7c --> A8 A7d --> A8 A8 --> A9[Global Structural
Analysis Inplace and

API 2U or AISC] A10 --> A11{Compliance

Iterate Design] A12 --> A9 A11 -->|Yes| A13[Fatigue Hot Spot
Analysis FLS] A13 --> A14[Accidental Event
Screening ALS] A14 --> A15[Reserve Strength
and Robustness] A15 --> A16[Independent Review
by Certifying Body] A16 --> A17([Design Approved
for Construction]) ``` ## 4.2 Limit State Hierarchy and Decision Logic ```mermaid flowchart LR L0([All Limit States
must be checked]) --> L1[Serviceability
SLS] L0 --> L2[Ultimate
ULS] L0 --> L3[Fatigue
FLS] L0 --> L4[Accidental
ALS] L1 --> M1[Check deflection
drift and vibration
under operational loads] L2 --> M2[Check collapse and
buckling under
100 year storm] L3 --> M3[Apply Miners rule

beta target 2.3] M1 --> O1{Satisfied} M2 --> O2{Satisfied} M3 --> O3{Satisfied} M4 --> O4{Satisfied} O1 -->|No| R1[Resize for stiffness] O2 -->|No| R2[Increase member size] O3 -->|No| R3[Improve joint detail] O4 -->|No| R4[Add bracing or
fire protection] O1 -->|Yes| OK1((OK)) O2 -->|Yes| OK2((OK)) O3 -->|Yes| OK3((OK)) O4 -->|Yes| OK4((OK)) ``` ## 4.3 WSD versus LRFD Comparison (Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph C1[Working Stress Design WSD] W1[Applied Load S] --> W2[Single Factor FS] W3[Material Capacity R] --> W2 W2 --> W4[Check S times FS less than R] end subgraph C2[Limit State LRFD] L1a[Permanent Load G] --> L1b[Partial Factor 1.1] L1c[Variable Load Q] --> L1d[Partial Factor 1.3] L1e[Environmental Load E] --> L1f[Partial Factor 1.35] L1b --> L1g[Sum of Factored Loads] L1d --> L1g L1f --> L1g L1h[Material Capacity Rn] --> L1i[Resistance Factor phi] L1i --> L1j[Design Resistance] L1g --> L1k[Check L1g less than L1j] L1j --> L1k end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** The LRFD architecture shows that *each* load family is factored *separately* before combination. This is the structural embodiment of the "tiered safety" philosophy. The WSD architecture shows a single lumped factor, which cannot distinguish the vastly different uncertainties in dead load (low) and storm wave load (high). --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **Q: Define "Limit State" in the context of offshore structure design. Differentiate between ULS, SLS, and FLS with one example each.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** A *limit state* is a condition beyond which an offshore structure ceases to satisfy the design performance requirements. The three principal limit states are: - **ULS (Ultimate Limit State):** corresponds to *collapse, severe deformation, or loss of equilibrium*. Example: tubular member buckling under a 100-year storm wave load. - **SLS (Serviceability Limit State):** corresponds to *conditions that impair normal use* without collapse. Example: excessive deck drift causing crane misalignment, or conductor angle exceeding 0.5°. - **FLS (Fatigue Limit State):** corresponds to *progressive damage under cyclic loading*. Example: fatigue cracking at a tubular joint after 25 years of wave-induced stress cycles. > (3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 each for differentiation with example) --- ### Question 2 **Q: What is the significance of the reliability index $\beta$ in offshore design strategy? State the typical target value for ULS and what $P_f$ it corresponds to.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** The reliability index $\beta$ quantifies the *margin of safety* between structural resistance $R$ and load effect $S$ in a probabilistic sense. It is related to the annual probability of failure $P_f$ by: $$P_f = \Phi(-\beta)$$ where $\Phi$ is the standard normal CDF. For offshore platforms under ULS, the target $\beta$ is typically **3.7**, corresponding to: $$P_f = \Phi(-3.7) \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per year}$$ This is the reliability level calibrated into ISO 19902 and DNV-OS-C101. It reflects an acceptable balance between safety, cost, and the consequence class of the structure. > (3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for target, 1 for numerical $P_f$) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (Option 1) — Total 14 Marks **Q: Discuss in detail the design philosophy of offshore structures with reference to (a) Working Stress Design and (b) Limit State Design. Compare their merits and justify why LRFD has become the global standard.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` (7 + 7 marks) #### Part (a) — Working Stress Design (WSD) — 7 marks **Model Answer:** **1. Definition and Formulation (2 marks)** In Working Stress Design (also called Allowable Stress Design), the structure is proportioned so that the *maximum expected* (working) stress in any member, under the most adverse unfactored service load combination, does not exceed an *allowable* stress derived by dividing the material's yield or ultimate strength by a *single, global* factor of safety (FS): $$\sigma_{max, working} \le \sigma_{allow} = \frac{\sigma_{yield}}{FS}$$ Typical FS values for offshore steel platforms: **1.5 to 2.0** for primary structural members, **2.0 to 2.5** for joints. **2. Advantages (2 marks)** - Conceptually simple; engineers can quickly check by hand. - Decades of empirical calibration in API RP 2A-WSD. - Works well for structures with low load uncertainty (e.g., building frames, bridges). **3. Limitations (3 marks)** - *Single FS cannot differentiate* between the low uncertainty in dead load and the high uncertainty in storm wave load. This leads to **over-conservatism in some components and under-conservatism in others** — i.e., *non-uniform reliability*. - Cannot explicitly handle fatigue or accidental events. - Incompatible with modern risk-based and consequence-class thinking. - Empirical, not transparent: FS values are *implicitly* calibrated to past good designs, not to quantified target reliability. #### Part (b) — Limit State Design (LSD/LRFD) — 7 marks **Model Answer:** **1. Definition and Format (2 marks)** Limit State Design verifies the structure against a *defined set of limit states* (SLS, ULS, FLS, ALS) using *partial safety factors* on both loads and resistance: $$\sum_i \gamma_i S_{n,i} \le \phi R_n = \frac{R_n}{\gamma_M}$$ where $\gamma_i$ are load factors, $\phi$ is the resistance factor, and subscript $n$ denotes *nominal* (code-characteristic) values. **2. The Four Limit States (2 marks)** - **SLS** — serviceability (drift, vibration). - **ULS** — collapse/buckling under 100-yr storm. - **FLS** — Miner's rule cumulative damage. - **ALS** — survival after collision, fire, dropped object. **3. Reliability-Based Calibration (2 marks)** The partial factors are *calibrated* so that the design delivers a target reliability $\beta$ (e.g., 3.7 for ULS). The probabilistic link is: $$\beta = \frac{\ln(R_m/S_m)}{\sqrt{V_R^2 + V_S^2}}$$ This allows the same target $\beta$ to be achieved with *load-specific* and *resistance-specific* factors. **4. Why LRFD is the Global Standard (1 mark)** ISO 19902, DNV-OS-C101, API RP 2A-LRFD, NORSOK N-001, and Eurocode 3 all use LRFD for offshore steel structures because it provides: - **Uniform reliability** across all failure modes. - **Transparent, code-auditable** safety. - **Compatibility with risk-based inspection** planning. - **Direct integration with FLS and ALS** checks. > (Marks distributed as: 2 + 2 + 3 for (a); 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 for (b) — totals to 14) --- ### Question B (Option 2) — Total 14 Marks **Q: An offshore tubular joint has a hot-spot stress range history given below. The S-N curve for the joint in seawater with cathodic protection is $N = 10^{12} \cdot (\Delta\sigma)^{-3}$ where $\Delta\sigma$ is in MPa. The total design life is 25 years.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` | Stress Range Block $\Delta\sigma_i$ (MPa) | Cycles per year $n_{i,yr}$ | |---|---| | 30 | $2.0 \times 10^5$ | | 50 | $5.0 \times 10^4$ | | 80 | $1.0 \times 10^4$ | | 120 | $1.0 \times 10^3$ | **(a) Calculate the annual fatigue damage $D_{yr}$ and the lifetime damage $D_{LT}$. Comment on whether the design satisfies the FLS criterion. (7 marks)** **(b) If the design fatigue factor required is $DF = 3.0$, determine the maximum allowable 25-year cumulative damage and explain the design implications. (7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Fatigue Damage Calculation (7 marks)** **Step 1:** Total cycles over 25-year life: $n_i = 25 \times n_{i,yr}$. **Step 2:** Apply the S-N curve $N_i = 10^{12} / (\Delta\sigma_i)^3$ to find cycles to failure for each stress range. For $\Delta\sigma = 30$ MPa: $$N_1 = \frac{10^{12}}{30^3} = \frac{10^{12}}{27{,}000} = 3.70 \times 10^7$$ Total cycles over 25 years: $n_1 = 25 \times 2.0 \times 10^5 = 5.0 \times 10^6$ For $\Delta\sigma = 50$ MPa: $$N_2 = \frac{10^{12}}{50^3} = \frac{10^{12}}{125{,}000} = 8.00 \times 10^6$$ Total cycles: $n_2 = 25 \times 5.0 \times 10^4 = 1.25 \times 10^6$ For $\Delta\sigma = 80$ MPa: $$N_3 = \frac{10^{12}}{80^3} = \frac{10^{12}}{512{,}000} = 1.95 \times 10^6$$ Total cycles: $n_3 = 25 \times 1.0 \times 10^4 = 2.5 \times 10^5$ For $\Delta\sigma = 120$ MPa: $$N_4 = \frac{10^{12}}{120^3} = \frac{10^{12}}{1.728 \times 10^6} = 5.79 \times 10^5$$ Total cycles: $n_4 = 25 \times 1.0 \times 10^3 = 2.5 \times 10^4$ **Step 3:** Apply Miner's rule: $$D_{LT} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} \frac{n_i}{N_i}$$ $$ \begin{aligned} D_{LT} &= \frac{5.0 \times 10^6}{3.70 \times 10^7} + \frac{1.25 \times 10^6}{8.00 \times 10^6} + \frac{2.5 \times 10^5}{1.95 \times 10^6} + \frac{2.5 \times 10^4}{5.79 \times 10^5} \\ &= 0.1351 + 0.1563 + 0.1282 + 0.0432 \\ &= 0.4628 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Annual damage: $$D_{yr} = \frac{0.4628}{25} = 0.01851 \; \text{per year}$$ > [!NOTE] > **FLS criterion (basic):** $D \le 1$. Here $D_{LT} = 0.46 < 1$, so the joint *passes* the basic FLS check. However, because the joint is in a *subsea* location with limited inspectability, a **design fatigue factor** $DF = 3.0$ should be applied. (7 marks: 1.5 for setting up $N_i$, 1.5 for cycles, 3 for summation, 1 for conclusion) **Part (b) — Design Fatigue Factor Application (7 marks)** **Step 1:** The design fatigue factor (DF) is applied such that the *allowable* damage is reduced: $$D_{allow} = \frac{1}{DF} = \frac{1}{3.0} = 0.333$$ **Step 2:** Compare $D_{LT}$ with $D_{allow}$: $$D_{LT} = 0.463 \quad \text{vs} \quad D_{allow} = 0.333$$ Since $0.463 > 0.333$, the joint **fails the FLS criterion** with $DF = 3.0$. **Step 3:** Required reduction in damage: $$\frac{D_{LT}}{D_{allow}} = \frac{0.463}{0.333} = 1.39$$ The damage is ~39% over the allowable. **Step 4:** Design implications and remediation (3 marks): - **Joint redesign:** Improve the joint geometry (e.g., increase the chord wall thickness, use a doubler plate, increase the brace-to-chord diameter ratio to fall in the favourable range $\beta = d_2/d_1 \approx 0.5$–$0.7$). - **Weld profile improvement:** Grind flush, use butt welds instead of fillet welds to reduce SCF. - **Material upgrade:** Use higher-toughness steel or a fatigue-resistant cast-steel node. - **Operational reduction:** Limit vessel draft, or move equipment that excites the high-stress ranges. - **In-service inspection:** Add to RBI (Risk-Based Inspection) plan with annual underwater NDT. - **Redesign of the brace connection:** Move the joint away from the splash zone if possible, or use a grouted connection. (7 marks: 1 for allowable, 1 for comparison, 1 for ratio, 1 for DF interpretation, 3 for design actions) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in Module 3 "Design Strategy" Questions:** > 1. **Confusing WSD's FS with LRFD's $\gamma$.** They are *not* numerically comparable. WSD's $FS = 2.0$ does *not* equal $\gamma_R = 2.0$. LRFD splits safety across *load* and *resistance* factors. > 2. **Mixing units in Miner's rule.** $N_i$ must be cycles to failure at the *same* stress range $\Delta\sigma_i$ used in $n_i$. A 1% unit slip is worth 1 mark. > 3. **Forgetting the return-period storm designation.** ULS checks use the *100-year* return period. Using the *annual maximum* gives unconservative results. > 4. **Applying WSD to fatigue.** Fatigue is fundamentally a *cumulative damage* problem (Miner's rule) — WSD's single FS cannot capture it. You *must* invoke FLS separately. > 5. **Omitting the design fatigue factor (DF) in FLS.** The basic $D \le 1$ is the *laboratory* criterion. Offshore, $D \le 1/DF$ with $DF = 2$–$3$ is the *design* criterion. Forgetting this is a 2-mark loss. > 6. **Stating "$P_f = \beta$" — wrong direction.** $P_f$ decreases as $\beta$ increases. Memorise the relationship $P_f = \Phi(-\beta)$. > 7. **Ignoring the load combination *direction* of partial factors.** ALS uses *reduced* load factors (because the structure is already in a damaged state, low-frequency events dominate), and *reduced* material factors (because we accept temporary overloading). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3: Design Strategy** > - **Definition:** Design strategy = the systematic, code-compliant, reliability-targeted framework governing how an offshore structure is conceived, proportioned, and verified. > - **Two philosophies:** WSD (single FS) vs. **LRFD** (partial factors) — LRFD is the global standard. > - **Four limit states to remember in order:** **SLS → ULS → FLS → ALS.** > - **SLS** = serviceability (drift, vibration, deck tilt). > - **ULS** = collapse under 100-year storm; $\beta \approx 3.7$ target. > - **FLS** = Miner's rule $D = \sum n_i/N_i \le 1/DF$ with $DF \ge 2$ for uninspectable joints. > - **ALS** = accidental events (collision, fire, dropped object); $\beta \approx 2.3$–$2.6$. > - **Six load families:** $G$ (permanent), $Q$ (variable), $E$ (environmental), $D$ (deformation), $A$ (accidental), plus implicit fatigue load. > - **Key partial factors to memorise (ISO 19902 / NORSOK):** $\gamma_G = 1.1$, $\gamma_Q = 1.3$, $\gamma_E = 1.35$ (ULS-SEA), $\gamma_M = 1.05$–$1.25$. > - **Reliability–probability mapping:** $\beta = 3.7 \Rightarrow P_f \approx 10^{-4}$ (ULS); $\beta = 2.3 \Rightarrow P_f \approx 10^{-2}$ (ALS). > - **Central safety factor formula:** $FS_{med} = \exp(\beta \sqrt{V_R^2 + V_S^2})$ — appears in nearly every KTU Part-B question. > - **Gumbel return period:** $H_T = a + b \ln T$; for 100 years, $\ln 100 = 4.605$. > - **Design service life:** 25 years for fixed platforms (NORSOK); 30+ years for Norwegian Sector. > - **Reserve Strength Ratio (RSR):** $RSR = R_{ult}/L_{100-yr} \ge 1.3$ for fixed platforms. > - **Why LRFD wins:** uniform reliability across failure modes, transparent calibration, integrates risk and consequence class. > - **Consequence class matters:** Class I (low) $\to$ Class III (high) drives both $\beta$ and the partial factors. > - **Inspectability matters:** if a joint is in 200 m of water, $DF$ goes up. > - **Codes to cite in answers:** API RP 2A-LRFD, ISO 19902, ISO 19901-1 to -7, DNV-OS-C101, DNV-RP-C203 (fatigue), NORSOK N-001, NORSOK N-004. > - **Avoid these five common errors:** (1) using WSD for fatigue, (2) confusing $\gamma$ with FS, (3) wrong units in Miner's rule, (4) omitting DF, (5) using annual max instead of 100-yr storm. ---

topics.title: Design Consideration for Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST722 - FUNDAMENTALS OF OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design Consideration for Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Design Consideration for Components" in Offshore Engineering?

In offshore structural engineering, **"components"** refer to the individual load-carrying members that make up a complete offshore installation. These include the **tubular chords**, **tubular braces**, **joints**, **deck beams**, **hull girders**, **mooring lines**, **risers**, and **piles**.

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> *Design considerations for components* in an offshore structure refer to the set of engineering rules, limit-state criteria, code provisions (chiefly API RP 2A / ISO 19902), material selection guidelines, and analytical checks applied to individual structural elements to ensure they safely withstand environmental loads (waves, wind, current, seismic), functional loads (self-weight, payload), and accidental loads (collision, fire, blast) throughout the design service life of typically **20 to 30 years**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**
> Design of components in offshore platforms revolves around **limit-state design philosophy**, not the older allowable-stress design. Both **Ultimate Limit State (ULS)** and **Fatigue Limit State (FLS)** must be verified.

## 1.2 The Hierarchy of Offshore Components

```
OFFSHORE PLATFORM
   ├── Superstructure (Deck)
   │     ├── Deck plating
   │     ├── Deck beams (primary/secondary)
   │     └── Module support frames
   ├── Substructure (Jacket)
   │     ├── Tubular Braces (diagonals, horizontals, K-braces)
   │     ├── Tubular Chords (vertical legs)
   │     └── Tubular Joints (T, Y, K, X, DT, TT)
   ├── Foundation
   │     ├── Piles (driven/drilled)
   │     └── Mudmats (for GBS)
   ├── Mooring System (for floating units)
   │     ├── Chain/Wire/Rope lines
   │     └── Anchors
   └── Risers & Conduits
```

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Underwater Skeleton"

Imagine you are designing the **skeleton of a deep-sea giant** that must stand in a stormy ocean for 30 years. Each bone in this skeleton is a *component*. Before placing a bone, the engineer must answer:

| Engineering Question | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|
| How thick must the bone be? | Bone diameter & wall thickness |
| Will it crush from outside pressure? | Deep-sea crush / buckling |
| Will it snap from repeated wave hits? | Fatigue (constant wave slapping) |
| Will it rust in salt water? | Corrosion allowance |
| Where two bones meet, who is stronger? | Joint / Stress Concentration Factor |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Golden Rule of Offshore Component Design:**
> Every component must satisfy: **Applied Stress ≤ Allowable Stress (or Code Capacity)**, with adequate reserve for **fatigue**, **buckling**, **corrosion**, and **accidental damage**.

## 1.4 The Governing Design Codes

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects familiarity with the following primary codes:

- **API RP 2A-WSD / LRFD** — American Petroleum Institute Recommended Practice (most widely used)
- **ISO 19902** — International Standard for fixed steel offshore structures
- **DNV-OS-C101 / DNV-RP-C203** — Det Norske Veritas (for fatigue and buckling)
- **AISC** — American Institute of Steel Construction (deck design)
- **AWS D1.1** — American Welding Society (welding)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "Which code is used?", the safe answer is *API RP 2A* unless specifically asked about fatigue or buckling, in which case reference *DNV-RP-C203*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design Philosophy — Limit States

Modern offshore design (API LRFD & ISO 19902) uses **Limit State Design**. A *limit state* is a condition beyond which the structure no longer satisfies the design requirements.

### A. Ultimate Limit State (ULS)
The structure (or component) reaches its maximum load-carrying capacity. Examples:
- Yielding of gross section
- Buckling of tubular member
- Plastic collapse / rupture
- Loss of static equilibrium

**Generic ULS Equation:**

$$R_d \geq \sum \gamma_i \cdot S_i$$

where $R_d$ = design resistance (factored), $\gamma_i$ = partial safety factor, $S_i$ = nominal load effect.

### B. Fatigue Limit State (FLS)
The component accumulates fatigue damage under repeated wave loading until a crack initiates / propagates to a critical size.

**Miner's Rule (Palmgren-Miner):**

$$D = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{n_i}{N_i} \leq 1.0$$

where $n_i$ = applied cycles at stress range $S_i$, $N_i$ = cycles to failure at $S_i$ from S-N curve.

### C. Serviceability Limit State (SLS)
The structure remains functional — deflections, vibrations, and rotations stay within tolerable limits.

### D. Accidental Limit State (ALS)
The structure withstands one defined accidental event (vessel collision, dropped object, fire, blast).

## 2.2 Loads Acting on Offshore Components

| Load Category | Examples | Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Dead Load (DL)** | Self-weight of jacket, deck, equipment | Gravity |
| **Live Load (LL)** | Personnel, supplies, drill payload | Functional |
| **Environmental (E)** | Wave, wind, current, seismic | Nature |
| **Hydrostatic (H)** | External water pressure on submerged members | Buoyancy |
| **Dynamic (D)** | Inertia forces, wave slam, vortex shedding | Time-varying |
| **Accidental (A)** | Collision, fire, dropped objects | Operational |
| **Construction (C)** | Lifting, transportation, launching | Temporary |

**Typical LRFD Load Combination (API / ISO 19902):**

$$U = 1.1\,DL + 1.1\,LL + 1.35\,(E + H)$$

For extreme environmental conditions, the ULS governs; for fatigue, an annually-recurring sea state governs.

## 2.3 Design of Tubular Members (Chords & Braces)

### A. Geometric Parameters

For a hollow circular steel tube:
- Outer diameter: $D$ (or $d_o$)
- Wall thickness: $t$
- Length between restraints: $L$
- Radius of gyration: $r = \dfrac{\sqrt{D^2 + (D-2t)^2}}{4}$
- Slenderness ratio: $\lambda = \dfrac{KL}{r}$

### B. Hydrostatic Collapse (External Pressure)

For a long, perfect cylindrical tube under external pressure $p_e$, the **elastic critical buckling pressure** (Bresse-Bryan formula) is:

$$p_{cr} = \dfrac{2 \cdot E}{1-\nu^2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{D}\right)^3$$

For **real (imperfect) tubes**, the API RP 2A / DNV recommends using the **DNV-RP-C202 / API-2U** characteristic collapse pressure which accounts for initial ovality $e_0$ and eccentricity $e_1$.

The interaction formula (combined axial + bending + external pressure) per API RP 2A WSD:

$$\frac{f_a}{F_a} + \frac{f_b}{F_b} + \frac{f_e}{F_{ehe}} \leq 1.0$$

where $f_a, f_b, f_e$ are axial, bending, hoop stresses, and $F_a, F_b, F_{ehe}$ are allowable values.

### C. Stress Resultants in a Tubular Member

| Stress | Formula | Cause |
|---|---|---|
| **Axial** | $f_a = \dfrac{N}{A}$ | Tension/compression |
| **Bending** | $f_b = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I}$ | Wave-induced bending |
| **Shear** | $f_v = \dfrac{V}{0.5 \cdot A}$ | Transverse force |
| **Hoop** | $f_h = \dfrac{p_e \cdot D}{2 t}$ | External pressure |
| **Combined (Von Mises)** | $f_{vm}=\sqrt{f_a^2+f_b^2-f_a f_b+3 f_v^2}$ | Multi-axial |

### D. Local Buckling Check (API RP 2A)

For braces, $D/t$ ratio is limited:

$$\frac{D}{t} \leq 60 \quad \text{(for ULS, no grout)}$$

$$\frac{D}{t} \leq 300 \quad \text{(for hydrostatic, submerged)}$$

## 2.4 Design of Tubular Joints

Tubular joints are the **most fatigue-critical** components in an offshore jacket.

### A. Joint Types (KTU Must-Know)

```
   T-joint        Y-joint         K-joint         X-joint
     |              \              / \             / \
     |               \            /   \           /   \
 ────●───         ────●────    ────●────     ────●●●────
```

### B. Stress Concentration Factors (SCF)

SCFs amplify nominal brace stresses at the weld toe. The most widely used set is **Efthymiou (1988)** equations for SCF in tubular joints.

For a **simple T-joint** under axial brace load:
$$SCF_{AC} = 1.31 \, \gamma \, \beta^{0.65} \tau^{1.1} \alpha^{0.5}$$

with parameter $\gamma = \dfrac{D}{2T}$, $\beta = \dfrac{d}{D}$, $\tau = \dfrac{t}{T}$, $\alpha = \dfrac{2L}{D}$.

### C. Joint Capacity

For a **multi-planar joint**, the **API RP 2A punching shear check** governs:

$$V_p = \tau_c \cdot \sin\theta \cdot \left[ \alpha + \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1-\frac{2\beta}{\sin\theta}}}\right] \cdot \frac{\pi D t}{4}$$

where $\tau_c$ = punching shear strength, $\theta$ = brace angle to chord, $\alpha, \beta$ = geometric ratios.

## 2.5 Fatigue Design — The Marine Nemesis

Tubular joints experience **billions** of small wave-induced cycles — fatigue dominates.

### A. Hot-Spot Stress Approach

$$\Delta\sigma_{HS} = SCF_{HS} \cdot \Delta\sigma_{nom}$$

The fatigue life uses the **T-Bilinear S-N curve** (per DNV-RP-C203):

$$
\Delta\sigma^{m} \cdot N = K
$$

with two slopes: $m_1 = 3$ for $\Delta\sigma \geq \Delta\sigma_{sw}$ and $m_2 = 5$ for $\Delta\sigma < \Delta\sigma_{sw}$. The "knee point" is $\Delta\sigma_{sw} \approx 1.0 \cdot 10^7$ cycles for joints in seawater with cathodic protection.

### B. Damage Calculation (Miner's Rule)

$$D = \sum_{i} \frac{n_i}{N_i} = \frac{n_1}{K \Delta\sigma_1^{-m_1}} + \frac{n_2}{K \Delta\sigma_2^{-m_2}} + \ldots$$

A fatigue design life factor of **3 to 5** is applied, requiring $D \leq 0.2$ for a 25-year service life.

## 2.6 Corrosion Allowance

Because of salt-water corrosion, an extra wall thickness is added to submerged members:

$$t_{design} = t_{structural} + t_{corrosion}$$

Typical values per ISO 19902:
- **Atmospheric zone:** 1 mm (splash: 3 mm)
- **Submerged zone:** 1 mm (with cathodic protection)
- **Splash zone:** 3–6 mm
- **Internal (flooded):** 1.5 mm

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — High-Yield Formula Table (for revision)**

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Code Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Slenderness | $\lambda = KL/r$ | API / ISO |
| 2 | Elastic hoop stress | $f_h = pD/(2t)$ | Thin-walled vessel |
| 3 | Elastic buckling pressure | $p_{cr} = \dfrac{2E}{1-\nu^2}(t/D)^3$ | Bresse-Bryan |
| 4 | Combined stress (API) | $f_a/F_a + f_b/F_b + f_e/F_{ehe} \leq 1$ | API RP 2A WSD |
| 5 | Local buckling limit | $D/t \leq 60$ (ULS) | API RP 2A |
| 6 | Von Mises stress | $\sqrt{f_a^2+f_b^2-f_a f_b+3f_v^2}$ | Yield criterion |
| 7 | Miner's damage | $D = \sum n_i / N_i \leq 1$ | Palmgren-Miner |
| 8 | S-N curve (joints) | $\Delta\sigma^m \cdot N = K$ | DNV-RP-C203 |
| 9 | Effective stress | $\Delta\sigma_{HS} = SCF \cdot \Delta\sigma_n$ | Hot-spot method |
| 10 | Efthymiou SCF (T, axial) | $1.31\,\gamma\,\beta^{0.65}\tau^{1.1}\alpha^{0.5}$ | Efthymiou 1988 |
| 11 | API punching shear | $V_p = \tau_c \sin\theta[\,\alpha+1/\sqrt{1-2\beta/\sin\theta}\,]\pi D t/4$ | API RP 2A |
| 12 | LRFD ULS combination | $1.1 DL + 1.1 LL + 1.35 (E + H)$ | ISO 19902 |
| 13 | Hydrostatic end-cap | $p_{end} = \rho g h$ | Buoyancy |
| 14 | Pile axial capacity | $Q = Q_s + Q_p = f_s A_s + q_p A_p$ | API RP 2A Geotech |

> [!WARNING]
> Use `\vert` not `|` in any displayed math — markdown tables will break otherwise.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hoop Stress in a Thin-Walled Cylinder

Consider a long cylindrical shell of radius $R$, wall thickness $t$, subjected to internal pressure $p_i$. Take a free body of unit length, half-cut along a diameter.

**Step 1 — Free Body Diagram:**
Cut the cylinder along its longitudinal axis. The pressure acts on the projected area $2R \cdot 1$, and is resisted by hoop tension in the two walls (each of area $t \cdot 1$).

**Step 2 — Equilibrium:**

$$
\sum F_x = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; p_i \cdot 2R = 2 \, f_h \cdot t
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for hoop stress:**

$$
f_h = \frac{p_i \, R}{t} = \frac{p_i \, D}{2t}
$$

> This is the famous **"thin-wall pressure-vessel formula"** — valid when $t/R \leq 0.05$.

**Step 4 — Specialization for offshore external pressure:**

For an underwater tubular brace at depth $h$:

$$
p_e = p_{atm} + \rho_w g h
$$

with $\rho_w = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (seawater), $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

Thus,

$$
f_h = \frac{(\rho_w g h) \, D}{2t}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Elastic Buckling of a Long Cylindrical Shell

The most important structural failure mode for a deep-water tubular member is **buckling under hydrostatic pressure**.

**Step 1 — Assumptions:**
- Long, perfect, isotropic cylindrical shell.
- Radius $R$, thickness $t$, Young's modulus $E$, Poisson's ratio $\nu$.
- Loaded by uniform external pressure $p$.

**Step 2 — Classical Bryan-Bresse equation** (small deflection theory):

$$
p_{cr} = \frac{2 E}{1 - \nu^2} \cdot \frac{t}{R}^{\,3} = \frac{2 E}{1 - \nu^2} \left(\frac{t}{R}\right)^3
$$

**Step 3 — Substituting $R = D/2$:**

$$
p_{cr} = \frac{16 \, E}{1 - \nu^2} \cdot \left(\frac{t}{D}\right)^{3}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical illustration** with steel ($E = 200{,}000$ MPa, $\nu = 0.3$), $D = 1.5$ m, $t = 0.025$ m:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{cr} &= \frac{16 \times 200{,}000}{1 - 0.3^2} \cdot \left(\frac{0.025}{1.5}\right)^3 \;\text{MPa} \\
&= \frac{3{,}200{,}000}{0.91} \cdot \left(0.01667\right)^3 \\
&= 3{,}516{,}484 \times 4.63 \times 10^{-6} \\
&\approx 16.3 \;\text{MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Real (imperfect) tubes:**

In reality, geometric imperfections (ovality $\Delta_0$, eccentricity) and material non-linearity reduce this. DNV-RP-C202 uses knockdown factors $K_{p}$ typically 0.6–0.8:

$$
p_{actual} = K_p \cdot p_{cr} \approx 0.7 \cdot p_{cr}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does the cube of $t/D$ matter so much?** Because for a 5% reduction in $t$, the buckling capacity drops by **14.3%**. Wall thickness must be engineered carefully in deep water.

## 3.3 Derivation: Stress Interaction (API RP 2A)

**Step 1 — Three orthogonal stresses in a brace:**
- $f_a$ = axial (from wave-inertia)
- $f_b$ = bending (from V-I-M base shear)
- $f_e$ = hoop (from hydrostatic pressure)

**Step 2 — API interaction formula:**

$$
\frac{f_a}{F_a} + \frac{f_b}{F_b} + \frac{f_e}{F_{ehe}} \leq 1.0
$$

**Step 3 — Compute each term** with sample data:
- $f_a = 80$ MPa, $F_a = 200$ MPa → $80/200 = 0.400$
- $f_b = 120$ MPa, $F_b = 240$ MPa → $120/240 = 0.500$
- $f_e = 50$ MPa, $F_{ehe} = 250$ MPa → $50/250 = 0.200$

**Step 4 — Sum:**

$$
0.400 + 0.500 + 0.200 = 1.100 > 1.0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{NOT ACCEPTABLE}
$$

The brace must be **re-sized** (larger diameter, thicker wall, or higher-grade steel) so the sum $\leq 1.0$.

## 3.4 Derivation: Miner's Fatigue Damage (Worked Numerical Example)

**Given:** A tubular T-joint experiences the following stress ranges during a 1-year storm season:

| Sea State $i$ | $\Delta\sigma_i$ (MPa) | Cycles $n_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 60 | 200,000 |
| 2 | 40 | 1,000,000 |
| 3 | 25 | 5,000,000 |

The S-N curve for tubular joints (DNV T-Bilinear, seawater with CP) gives:

- For $\Delta\sigma \geq 52$ MPa: $N = 10^{12.48} \Delta\sigma^{-3}$
- For $\Delta\sigma < 52$ MPa: $N = 10^{15.10} \Delta\sigma^{-5}$

**Step 1 — Sea State 1:** $\Delta\sigma_1 = 60$ MPa

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_1 &= 10^{12.48} \cdot 60^{-3} \\
\log N_1 &= 12.48 - 3 \log 60 \\
\log N_1 &= 12.48 - 3(1.778) = 12.48 - 5.335 = 7.145 \\
N_1 &= 10^{7.145} = 1.40 \times 10^{7} \;\text{cycles}
\end{aligned}
$$

Damage:

$$
D_1 = \frac{n_1}{N_1} = \frac{200{,}000}{1.40 \times 10^7} = 0.0143
$$

**Step 2 — Sea State 2:** $\Delta\sigma_2 = 40$ MPa (use slope 5 branch)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log N_2 &= 15.10 - 5 \log 40 \\
&= 15.10 - 5(1.602) = 15.10 - 8.010 = 7.090 \\
N_2 &= 10^{7.090} = 1.23 \times 10^{7}
\end{aligned}
$$

Damage:

$$
D_2 = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{1.23 \times 10^{7}} = 0.0813
$$

**Step 3 — Sea State 3:** $\Delta\sigma_3 = 25$ MPa (slope 5)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log N_3 &= 15.10 - 5 \log 25 \\
&= 15.10 - 5(1.398) = 15.10 - 6.989 = 8.111 \\
N_3 &= 10^{8.111} = 1.29 \times 10^{8}
\end{aligned}
$$

Damage:

$$
D_3 = \frac{5{,}000{,}000}{1.29 \times 10^{8}} = 0.0388
$$

**Step 4 — Total Annual Damage:**

$$
D_{annual} = D_1 + D_2 + D_3 = 0.0143 + 0.0813 + 0.0388 = 0.1344
$$

**Step 5 — 25-Year Damage:**

$$
D_{25\,yr} = 25 \times 0.1344 = 3.36 > 1.0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{FAILURE}
$$

**Step 6 — With safety factor of 3 on design life** (FATF = 3):

$$
D_{design} = 3 \times D_{annual} \times 25 = 10.08 \quad \text{(CATASTROPHIC)}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The joint must be redesigned — either by reducing SCF (weld profiling), increasing wall thickness, or improving the weld post-treatment (grinding, peening).

## 3.5 Derivation: Corrosion Allowance Addition

**Step 1 — Structural (effective) thickness needed:**

$$
t_{struct} = \frac{p_e \, D}{2 \, f_h \, \eta}
$$

where $\eta$ = design efficiency ($\approx 0.85$ for seamless pipe).

**Step 2 — Total ordered thickness:**

$$
t_{order} = t_{struct} + t_{corr} + t_{mill\,tol}
$$

with $t_{corr} = 3 \text{ mm}$ (splash zone) and $t_{mill\,tol} = 0.5 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 3 — Numerical check:**
For $D = 1.5$ m, $p_e = 0.6$ MPa, $f_h = 165$ MPa, $\eta = 0.85$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{struct} &= \frac{0.6 \times 1500}{2 \times 165 \times 0.85} = \frac{900}{280.5} = 3.21 \text{ mm} \\
t_{order} &= 3.21 + 3.0 + 0.5 = 6.71 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** Order next standard pipe wall — say **8 mm** — and verify $D/t = 1500/8 = 187.5 \leq 300$ for hydrostatic and $60$ for ULS bracing.

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix — Design Check Hierarchy

| Step | Check | Governing Code | Critical at |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Material selection (yield, charpy, weldability) | API Spec 2H / EN 10225 | All zones |
| 2 | Local buckling ($D/t$) | API RP 2A, DNV-C202 | ULS, deep water |
| 3 | Combined stress (axial+bending+hoop) | API RP 2A WSD §3.3 | Operating storm |
| 4 | Joint punching shear | API RP 2A §4.4 | Extreme load |
| 5 | Joint SCF evaluation | Efthymiou / Marshall | Fatigue |
| 6 | Hot-spot fatigue damage | DNV-RP-C203 | Service life |
| 7 | Corrosion allowance | ISO 19902 | Submerged/splash |
| 8 | Accidental damage (e.g., boat impact) | API RP 2A §6 | Dropped objects |
| 9 | Pile capacity | API RP 2A Geotech | Foundation |
| 10 | Hydrostatic collapse | DNV-RP-C202 | Deep water tubulars |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
