<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the Ultimate Limit State (ULS) and Fatigue Limit State (FLS) for the design of an offshore tubular component. State the governing code provisions.**

**Model Answer (M.E.):**

- **ULS:** The state at which a structural component reaches its maximum load-carrying capacity or develops a failure mechanism (yielding, buckling, rupture, or loss of equilibrium). Under ULS, the design equation is $R_d \geq \sum \gamma_i S_i$. Governed by **API RP 2A LRFD** or **ISO 19902**.
- **FLS:** The state at which fatigue damage accumulation under repeated wave loading reaches the allowable limit ($D \leq 1.0$ per Miner's rule, but typically designed as $D_{25\,yr} \leq 0.2$ with a design-fatigue factor of 3–5). Governed by **DNV-RP-C203** and the T-Bilinear S-N curve.
- Both states ensure the component is safe over its design service life (typically 20–30 years).

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the significance of the Stress Concentration Factor (SCF) in the design of tubular joints?**

**Model Answer (M.E.):**

- SCF quantifies the local amplification of nominal brace stress at the weld toe of a tubular joint (chord–brace intersection).
- It is the bridge between **global analysis** (which gives nominal stresses) and **local fatigue analysis** (which uses the S-N curve at hot-spot locations).
- The hot-spot stress is $\Delta\sigma_{HS} = SCF \times \Delta\sigma_{nom}$.
- For a T-joint under axial load (Efthymiou): $SCF_{AC} = 1.31 \, \gamma \, \beta^{0.65} \, \tau^{1.1} \, \alpha^{0.5}$, where $\gamma = D/2T$, $\beta = d/D$, $\tau = t/T$, $\alpha = 2L/D$.
- High SCFs → high fatigue damage → must be reduced by weld profiling, grinding, or peening.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice per KTU ESE Regulations:** Two alternatives provided.

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the **limit-state design philosophy** used for offshore components as per API RP 2A-LRFD / ISO 19902. List the four limit states and the typical partial safety factors used in the ULS load combination. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A steel circular hollow brace in an offshore jacket has outer diameter $D = 1.20$ m and wall thickness $t = 25$ mm. It is submerged at a depth of **80 m** in seawater ($\rho_w = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$). Compute:
- (i) the hydrostatic external pressure,
- (ii) the hoop stress (longitudinal free end),
- (iii) the elastic critical buckling pressure (Bryan-Bresse),
- (iv) the API RP 2A combined stress interaction value if the axial compressive stress is $f_a = 60$ MPa, bending stress is $f_b = 90$ MPa, allowable axial $F_a = 220$ MPa, allowable bending $F_b = 240$ MPa, and allowable hoop $F_{ehe} = 300$ MPa. State whether the design is acceptable. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Question A**

##### Part (a) — Limit-State Design Philosophy (7 M)

1. **Definition:** Limit-state design treats each component as a system that must remain functional under all reasonably expected loads, with quantifiable safety margins against failure.

2. **Four Limit States:**
   - **ULS (Ultimate):** Strength / stability against collapse.
   - **SLS (Serviceability):** Deflections, drifts, vibrations within tolerable limits.
   - **FLS (Fatigue):** Cumulative damage under repeated loads $\leq 1.0$.
   - **ALS (Accidental):** Survival of one defined accidental event.

3. **Generic LRFD Equation:**

$$
\sum \gamma_i S_i \leq \phi R_n
$$

where $\gamma_i$ = load factor, $S_i$ = load effect, $\phi$ = resistance factor, $R_n$ = nominal resistance.

4. **Typical ULS Combination (API / ISO):**

$$
U = 1.1\,DL + 1.1\,LL + 1.35\,(E + H)
$$

For accidental:

$$
U_a = 0.9\,DL + 1.0\,A
$$

5. **Merit over WSD:** Different safety factors for loads vs. resistance → more uniform reliability across different failure modes.

6. **Examples of Partial Factors:**
   - $\gamma_{DL} = 1.1$, $\gamma_{LL} = 1.1$, $\gamma_{E} = 1.35$.
   - $\phi_{tension} = 0.9$, $\phi_{bending} = 0.9$, $\phi_{compression} = 0.85$.

7. **Verification Levels:** ULS (storm return period 100 yr), FLS (annual sea-state persistence), ALS (collision drop height).

##### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 M)

**(i) Hydrostatic Pressure:**

$$
p_e = \rho_w \, g \, h = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 80 = 804{,}540 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.805 \text{ MPa}
$$

**(ii) Hoop Stress:**

$$
f_h = \frac{p_e \, D}{2t} = \frac{0.805 \times 1200}{2 \times 25} = \frac{966}{50} = 19.32 \text{ MPa}
$$

**(iii) Elastic Buckling Pressure (Bryan-Bresse, $E = 200{,}000$ MPa, $\nu = 0.3$):**

$$
p_{cr} = \frac{16 \, E}{1-\nu^2} \left(\frac{t}{D}\right)^3 = \frac{16 \times 200{,}000}{1 - 0.09} \times \left(\frac{25}{1200}\right)^3
$$

$$
p_{cr} = \frac{3{,}200{,}000}{0.91} \times (0.02083)^3 = 3{,}516{,}484 \times 9.05 \times 10^{-6} \approx 31.8 \text{ MPa}
$$

**(iv) Combined Stress Interaction:**

$$
\frac{f_a}{F_a} + \frac{f_b}{F_b} + \frac{f_e}{F_{ehe}} = \frac{60}{220} + \frac{90}{240} + \frac{19.32}{300}
$$

$$
= 0.273 + 0.375 + 0.064 = 0.712
$$

**Conclusion:** $0.712 \leq 1.0$ → **DESIGN IS ACCEPTABLE.**

Buckling safety factor: $p_{cr} / p_e = 31.8 / 0.805 \approx 39.5$ → **Very safe** against hydrostatic collapse (typical design factor required is $\geq 2.0$).

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Discuss the **design philosophy for tubular joints** in offshore platforms. Describe the different types of joints (T, Y, K, X) and explain the API RP 2A **punching shear check** for joint capacity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A tubular T-joint in a North-Sea jacket experiences the following stress-range histogram per year. Using the DNV-RP-C203 T-Bilinear S-N curve with slope $m_1 = 3$ (for $\Delta\sigma \geq 52$ MPa), $\log K_1 = 12.48$, and slope $m_2 = 5$ (for $\Delta\sigma < 52$ MPa), $\log K_2 = 15.10$, compute the annual fatigue damage. **(7 Marks)**

| Sea State | $\Delta\sigma$ (MPa) | $n$ (cycles/year) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 70 | 100,000 |
| 2 | 45 | 800,000 |
| 3 | 30 | 4,000,000 |

State whether the design satisfies a 25-year life with a fatigue design factor (FDF) of 3.

#### **Model Solution to Question B**

##### Part (a) — Tubular Joints Design (7 M)

1. **Definition:** A tubular joint is the intersection of two or more hollow circular steel sections (chord = load-carrying main member, brace = incoming member).

2. **Joint Types:**
   - **T-joint:** Brace perpendicular to chord. Most common in K-braces.
   - **Y-joint:** Brace at an angle $\theta$ to chord axis.
   - **K-joint:** Two braces meet the chord at the same node, balanced.
   - **X-joint:** Two braces cross the chord on opposite sides.
   - **DT / TT / KK joints:** Multi-planar variants.

3. **Behaviour:** Joints transfer complex 3D forces through a curved shell, causing **stress concentration** at saddle, crown, and toe.

4. **API RP 2A Punching Shear Check:**

$$
V_p = \tau_c \sin\theta \left[\alpha + \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{2\beta}{\sin\theta}}}\right] \frac{\pi D T}{4}
$$

   - $\tau_c = \min(f_y / \sqrt{3}, f_{y,T}/2)$ — punching shear strength of chord material.
   - $\alpha = 2L/D$, $\beta = d/D$, $\theta$ = brace-to-chord angle, $D, T$ = chord dia/thickness, $d$ = brace dia.
   - The actual brace axial load $N$ must satisfy $N \leq V_p$.

5. **Joint SCFs (Efthymiou):** SCFs depend on geometry ratios $\beta$, $\tau$, $\gamma$, $\alpha$ and load type (axial, in-plane bending, out-of-plane bending).

6. **Fabrication:** Weld profile (concave, smooth), grinding, and toe dressing reduce SCFs by 20–40%.

7. **Code Hierarchy:** Joint capacity → punching shear → SCF → fatigue.

##### Part (b) — Fatigue Damage Calculation (7 M)

**Sea State 1:** $\Delta\sigma_1 = 70$ MPa $\geq 52$ → use slope 3 branch.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log N_1 &= 12.48 - 3 \log 70 \\
&= 12.48 - 3(1.8451) \\
&= 12.48 - 5.535 = 6.945 \\
N_1 &= 10^{6.945} = 8.81 \times 10^6 \text{ cycles} \\
D_1 &= n_1 / N_1 = 100{,}000 / (8.81 \times 10^6) = 0.01135
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sea State 2:** $\Delta\sigma_2 = 45$ MPa $< 52$ → use slope 5 branch.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log N_2 &= 15.10 - 5 \log 45 \\
&= 15.10 - 5(1.6532) \\
&= 15.10 - 8.266 = 6.834 \\
N_2 &= 10^{6.834} = 6.83 \times 10^6 \text{ cycles} \\
D_2 &= 800{,}000 / (6.83 \times 10^6) = 0.1171
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sea State 3:** $\Delta\sigma_3 = 30$ MPa $< 52$ → use slope 5 branch.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log N_3 &= 15.10 - 5 \log 30 \\
&= 15.10 - 5(1.4771) \\
&= 15.10 - 7.386 = 7.714 \\
N_3 &= 10^{7.714} = 5.18 \times 10^7 \text{ cycles} \\
D_3 &= 4{,}000{,}000 / (5.18 \times 10^7) = 0.0772
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total Annual Damage:**

$$
D_{annual} = D_1 + D_2 + D_3 = 0.01135 + 0.1171 + 0.0772 = 0.2057
$$

**25-Year Damage:**

$$
D_{25\,yr} = 25 \times 0.2057 = 5.14
$$

**With FDF = 3 (fatal design life factor):**

$$
D_{design} = 3 \times 25 \times 0.2057 = 15.43 \gg 1.0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{FAIL}
$$

**Conclusion:** The joint fails fatigue checks for a 25-year design life with FDF = 3. **Redesign actions:**
1. Increase chord wall thickness $T$ → reduces SCF.
2. Weld toe grinding → reduces SCF by ~30%.
3. Use cast-steel node → more uniform stress flow.
4. Re-analyse with smaller $\Delta\sigma$ via improved global hydrodynamics (e.g., reducing vortex-induced vibration).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Reasons Students Lose Marks on This Topic in KTU Exams**
> 1. **Forgetting the design fatigue factor (FDF = 3–5):** Many students compute $D_{25\,yr}$ and stop. KTU expects the FDF-multiplied damage checked against $\leq 1.0$.
> 2. **Wrong S-N slope selection:** Use slope 3 only when $\Delta\sigma \geq 52$ MPa; otherwise slope 5 (for tubular joints in seawater with cathodic protection).
> 3. **Mixing up $f_e$ with $f_h$:** In API RP 2A, $f_e$ is the hoop stress from external pressure, $f_h$ is sometimes used interchangeably — but always show the formula $p_e D / 2t$.
> 4. **Skipping corrosion allowance:** A design is incomplete if the nominal wall thickness does NOT add $t_{corr}$.
> 5. **Not converting units:** S-N curve constants $\log K$ require MPa stress and cycles. Do NOT mix units (psi vs MPa).
> 6. **Missing load combination factors:** Writing just "ULS" without listing $1.1 DL + 1.35 E$ loses 2 marks.
> 7. **Joint type confusion:** K-joint ≠ X-joint. Mention both brace angles and chord intersection explicitly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Design Consideration for Components**

- **Components of an offshore platform:** jacket braces/chords, deck beams, piles, joints, mooring lines, risers.
- **Governing codes:** API RP 2A (LRFD/WSD), ISO 19902, DNV-RP-C203 (fatigue), DNV-RP-C202 (buckling).
- **Four limit states:** ULS, SLS, FLS, ALS — all must be checked.
- **Typical LRFD combination:** $U = 1.1 DL + 1.1 LL + 1.35(E + H)$.
- **Slenderness:** $\lambda = KL/r$.
- **Local buckling limit:** $D/t \leq 60$ for braces, $D/t \leq 300$ for hydrostatic members.
- **Hydrostatic pressure:** $p_e = \rho_w g h$.
- **Hoop stress:** $f_h = p_e D / (2t)$.
- **Elastic buckling (Bryan-Bresse):** $p_{cr} = \dfrac{2E}{1-\nu^2}(t/R)^3 = \dfrac{16E}{1-\nu^2}(t/D)^3$.
- **Combined stress (API):** $f_a/F_a + f_b/F_b + f_e/F_{ehe} \leq 1.0$.
- **Von Mises yield criterion:** $f_{vm} = \sqrt{f_a^2 + f_b^2 - f_a f_b + 3 f_v^2}$.
- **Joint types:** T, Y, K, X, DT, TT, KK.
- **SCF (Efthymiou, axial, T-joint):** $SCF_{AC} = 1.31 \gamma \beta^{0.65} \tau^{1.1} \alpha^{0.5}$.
- **API punching shear:** $V_p = \tau_c \sin\theta [\alpha + 1/\sqrt{1 - 2\beta/\sin\theta}] \pi D T / 4$.
- **Hot-spot stress:** $\Delta\sigma_{HS} = SCF \times \Delta\sigma_{nom}$.
- **S-N bilinear curve:** $m_1 = 3$ for $\Delta\sigma \geq 52$ MPa, $m_2 = 5$ otherwise.
- **Miner's rule:** $D = \sum n_i / N_i$.
- **Design fatigue damage limit:** $D_{life \times FDF} \leq 1.0$, with FDF = 3–5.
- **Corrosion allowance (ISO 19902):** Atmospheric 1 mm, splash 3–6 mm, submerged 1 mm (with CP).
- **Design service life:** typically 20–30 years for fixed offshore platforms.
- **Environmental return period:** ULS = 100 yr storm; FLS = annual sea state; ALS = single event.
- **Key partial safety factors (LRFD):** $\gamma_D = 1.1$, $\gamma_L = 1.1$, $\gamma_E = 1.35$.
- **Seawater density:** $\rho_w = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$.
- **Steel properties (typical offshore):** $E = 200{,}000$ MPa, $\nu = 0.3$, $f_y = 350$ MPa (API 2H Grade 50).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compliant Structures: Basic Configurations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST722 - FUNDAMENTALS OF OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Compliant Structures: Basic Configurations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **compliant offshore structure** is a deepwater floating or fixed-type marine structure deliberately designed to deflect, sway, or rotate in a controlled manner under the action of environmental loads (waves, wind, current, and seismic forces), rather than resisting these loads in a purely rigid manner. The term *"compliant"* is derived from classical mechanics, meaning the structure yields elastically to the load, storing and dissipating the imposed energy through its own flexibility and its mooring/tendon restoring system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Compliant structures are compliant-type offshore platforms where the dynamic response is governed by the combined stiffness of the structure and its mooring/tendon system. The natural period of these structures is intentionally tuned to be much greater than the dominant wave period (typically T_n ≥ 20 s for TLP and T_n = 30–100 s for spars and semi-submersibles), thereby ensuring the wave force nearly averages out over the hull envelope.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Compliant" Structures?**
> In shallow waters (≤ 300 m), fixed steel jackets are economical. As water depth increases, the steel required for a fixed jacket scales with the *square of the water depth*, making it prohibitively expensive. Compliant and floating structures break this scaling by allowing the structure to move with the environment, so wave loading does not accumulate as a static base shear.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are standing on a crowded train platform during a storm. There are two extreme postures you could adopt:

1. **The Rigid Posture (Fixed Platform)** – You stand stiff-legged, resisting every gust of wind. The wind pushes you harder, and the force keeps building. Your legs (the platform braces) would have to be enormously strong.

2. **The Compliant Posture (Compliant Structure)** – You lean slightly into the wind and let your body sway gently, anchored only by a taut strap tied to a pillar. The strap (mooring line) is doing only a fraction of the work because most of the wind energy is being absorbed by your body's sway.

In offshore engineering, the "body" is the **hull or tower**, the "wind" is the **wave, current, and wind loading**, and the "strap" is the **mooring system or tendon system**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics for Compliant Structures (Industry / API / DNV):**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - Sea-water density: **ρ_sw = 1025 kg/m³**
> - Minimum air-gap for fixed decks: **≥ 1.5 m** above crest with 100-yr storm
> - Typical natural period ranges:
>   - Guyed Tower: **30 – 60 s**
>   - Tension Leg Platform (TLP): **2 – 4 s** (vertical), **100 – 200 s** (horizontal/surge)
>   - SPAR: **25 – 35 s** (heave)
>   - Semi-Submersible: **18 – 25 s**
>   - FPSO: **20 – 30 s**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Compliant & Floating Configurations

The basic configurations are classified according to the **dominant restoring-force mechanism** in their primary degrees of freedom (surge, sway, heave, roll, pitch, yaw).

| Class | Configuration | Primary Restoring Mechanism | Typical Water Depth |
|---|---|---|---|
| Compliant | **Guyed Tower (GT)** | Guy-line axial stiffness | 300 – 600 m |
| Compliant | **Compliant Tower (CT)** | Base moment + buoyancy distribution | 300 – 1000 m |
| Compliant | **Articulated Tower (ALP)** | Buoyancy-driven righting moment at universal joint | 100 – 500 m |
| Tensioned | **Tension Leg Platform (TLP)** | Vertical tendon tension (axial) | 300 – 1500 m |
| Moored | **SPAR** | Distributed buoyancy / heave-plate | 500 – 3000 m |
| Moored | **Semi-Submersible (Semi)** | Distributed buoyancy + mooring | 200 – 3000 m |
| Moored | **FPSO** | Mooring + weather-vaning | 200 – 2500 m |

### 2.2 Operational Theory – "Why" and "How" for Each Configuration

#### A. Guyed Tower
- **Why used:** Economical for intermediate depths (300–600 m) where a fixed jacket is too heavy and a TLP/spar is over-engineered.
- **How it works:** A slender, flexible steel tower is held laterally by **multiple catenary guy-wires** anchored to the seabed at a wide radius. The guys provide horizontal stiffness without resisting vertical loads.
- **Key feature:** The tower can sway laterally by several meters at the top (top deflection ≈ 2–5% of water depth).

#### B. Compliant Tower
- **Why used:** A scaled-up version of the Guyed Tower for deeper water (up to ~1000 m) without external guys.
- **How it works:** A long, flexible steel tower with a **wider, buoyant base**. Lateral restoring comes from the **distributed buoyancy and gravity couples** along with the foundation moment. The fundamental period is very long (T_n ≈ 30–100 s).

#### C. Articulated Tower / Articulated Loading Platform (ALP)
- **Why used:** Preferred in **icy waters** (North Sea, Canadian Arctic) where conventional mooring can be damaged by ice; also used for storage loading buoys.
- **How it works:** The tower is **pinned at the seabed** by a universal joint. Most of the tower is **buoyant**, and the buoyancy provides a righting moment that returns the tower to vertical. The center of buoyancy is **above** the pivot, while the center of gravity is **below** it → automatic stability.

#### D. Tension Leg Platform (TLP)
- **Why used:** Excellent heave performance; ideal for dry-tree wellhead operations in deep water.
- **How it works:** A semi-submersible hull is moored by **vertical, pretensioned steel tendons** to the seabed. The tendons convert vertical heave motion into small axial oscillations. The pretension ensures the tendons **never go slack** under any operational wave.
- **Key engineering fact:** The vertical restoring stiffness is *enormously high* (heave period 2–4 s), while the horizontal (surge) stiffness is *very low* (surge period 100–200 s).

#### E. SPAR Platform
- **Why used:** Capable of operating in the deepest waters (> 3000 m) – used at Perdido, Tahiti, and other ultra-deep sites.
- **How it works:** A **large-diameter, deep-draft cylindrical hull** (often 30–40 m diameter, 200+ m long) is ballasted so that the center of gravity is far below the center of buoyancy, creating a strong **pendulum-style restoring moment**. Mooring is via **catenary or taut polyester lines** spread radially.
- **Key feature:** The deep draft and open bottom (or heave plate) give excellent heave performance despite not having tendons.

#### F. Semi-Submersible (Semi)
- **Why used:** Most versatile configuration; widely used as a drilling rig, production platform, or accommodation unit.
- **How it works:** A box-like hull with **submerged pontoons** and **vertical columns** that support the deck. The waterplane area is small (good for heave), and the metacentric height is large. Moored via catenary or taut lines.

#### G. FPSO (Floating Production Storage and Offloading)
- **Why used:** Replaces long subsea pipelines by storing oil on-site and offloading to shuttle tankers.
- **How it works:** A **ship-shaped vessel** moored either by a single-point mooring (SPM – allows weather-vaning to minimize wave loads) or a spread mooring system. The bow turret is a critical engineering feature.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$T_n = 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{m}{k}}$$ | Natural period (mass–spring) | s |
| 2 | $$k_{hydro} = \rho_{sw}\, g\, A_{wp}$$ | Hydrostatic (waterplane) stiffness | N/m |
| 3 | $$F_{pretension} = (W - B) / N$$ | Pretension in each TLP tendon | N |
| 4 | $$\Delta x = \dfrac{F_{wave,\,horiz}}{k_{mooring}}$$ | Static offset under horizontal load | m |
| 5 | $$T_{surge}^{TLP} \approx 2\pi\sqrt{\dfrac{M}{k_{moor,\,horiz}}}$$ | TLP horizontal natural period | s |
| 6 | $$M_{restoring}^{ALP} = (F_B - F_G)\, \sin\theta \cdot L$$ | Righting moment of articulated tower | N·m |
| 7 | $$\Delta GM = KB + BM - KG$$ | Metacentric height (Semi, FPSO) | m |
| 8 | $$L_{catenary} = \sqrt{S^2 + h^2 + S\cdot\sqrt{S^2 + h^2}}$$ | Catenary mooring length | m |
| 9 | $$R_{min} = \dfrac{T_{max}}{\mu_{hold}}$$ | Mooring radius for given tension | m |
| 10 | $$R_{buoyancy} = F_B - F_G = (\rho_{sw} V - m_{struct})g$$ | Net buoyancy force | N |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:**
> All compliant and floating structures are governed by a single unifying concept — *the natural period must be tuned to avoid resonance with the dominant wave-energy period (4 – 20 s)*. The KTU examiner expects you to remember that **TLP is the only configuration whose heave period (2–4 s) is intentionally *below* the wave period**, while everything else is tuned *above* it.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas Deepwater Production**: SPARs and TLPs at Perdido, Tahiti, Thunder Horse, Mars, Auger.
- **FPSO Hubs**: Kuito, Girassol (West Africa), Prelude (Australia).
- **Drilling Rigs**: Semi-submersibles (Transocean, Stena Drilling).
- **Loading Buoys**: Articulated Towers (Norway's Lufeng, Canadian Sable Offshore).
- **Renewables**: Semi-submersibles being adapted for floating offshore wind (Hywind, WindFloat).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 – Natural Period of a Compliantly Moored Buoy

Consider a floating body of mass *M* held in equilibrium by a mooring system with equivalent linearised horizontal stiffness *k_horiz* (N/m). A horizontal wave force *F_w(t)* acts on it.

**Step 1: Equation of Motion (Newton's 2nd Law in horizontal DOF)**

$$M\ddot{x} + k_{horiz}\, x = F_w(t)$$

Here, *M* is the total mass (structure + added mass treated separately in full Morison/Radiation theory, but for the simplified SDOF used in KTU problems, it is the effective mass).

**Step 2: Identify the Undamped Natural Frequency**

For the homogeneous equation (no forcing, *F_w* = 0):

$$M\ddot{x} + k_{horiz}\, x = 0$$

We assume a harmonic solution:

$$x(t) = X_0 \cos(\omega_n t)$$

**Step 3: Compute the Derivatives**

$$\dot{x}(t) = -\omega_n X_0 \sin(\omega_n t)$$
$$\ddot{x}(t) = -\omega_n^2 X_0 \cos(\omega_n t)$$

**Step 4: Substitute Back into the Homogeneous Equation**

$$M(-\omega_n^2 X_0 \cos(\omega_n t)) + k_{horiz}\, (X_0 \cos(\omega_n t)) = 0$$

Since *X_0* cos(ω_n t) is generally non-zero:

$$-M\omega_n^2 + k_{horiz} = 0$$

**Step 5: Solve for ω_n**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k_{horiz}}{M}} \quad \text{(rad/s)}$$

**Step 6: Convert to Period**

$$T_n = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega_n} = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{k_{horiz}}}$$

This is the formula used in **all** KTU numerical problems on compliant-structure dynamics.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 – Pretension in TLP Tendons

For a Tension Leg Platform in static equilibrium, the **net upward buoyancy** must exceed the **weight** of the platform, so that the tendons are always in tension (they cannot take compression).

**Step 1: Static Force Balance in the Vertical Direction**

The forces acting vertically on the hull are:
- Weight: *W* (downward)
- Buoyancy: *B* (upward)
- Total tendon tension: *T_total* (downward, since tendons pull the hull toward seabed)

Equilibrium:

$$B = W + T_{total}$$

**Step 2: Solve for Total Tendon Tension**

$$T_{total} = B - W$$

**Step 3: If There Are N Tendons, Equally Loaded**

$$T_{each} = \dfrac{B - W}{N}$$

For KTU numericals, the over-pull (B – W) is typically **5 – 20 %** of the displacement.

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-type):**

> A TLP has weight *W* = 200,000 tonnes (≈ 1.962 × 10⁹ N) and is held by buoyancy *B* = 240,000 tonnes. It is moored by *N* = 4 vertical tendons. Find the tension in each tendon.

*Solution:*

$$T_{each} = \dfrac{(240{,}000 - 200{,}000) \times 10^3 \times 9.81}{4}$$

$$T_{each} = \dfrac{40{,}000 \times 10^3 \times 9.81}{4}$$

$$T_{each} = \dfrac{3.924 \times 10^8}{4} = 9.81 \times 10^7 \text{ N} \approx 98.1 \text{ MN}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 – Hydrostatic Stiffness for a Heaving Semi-Submersible

For a vertical motion (heave), the buoyancy changes linearly with the submerged volume. Let *A_wp* be the **waterplane area** (the area of the hull at the waterline).

**Step 1: Express Buoyancy as a Function of Heave *z***

$$F_B(z) = \rho_{sw} g V_{sub}(z) = \rho_{sw} g (V_0 + A_{wp}\, z)$$

Here, *V₀* is the equilibrium submerged volume, and *z* is the upward heave.

**Step 2: Net Vertical Force (Heave) Restoring**

$$F_{restoring}(z) = F_B(z) - W = \rho_{sw} g (V_0 + A_{wp} z) - \rho_{sw} g V_0$$

$$F_{restoring}(z) = \rho_{sw} g A_{wp}\, z$$

**Step 3: Linearised Spring Stiffness in Heave**

$$k_{heave} = \rho_{sw}\, g\, A_{wp}$$

**Step 4: Heave Natural Period**

$$T_{heave} = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{M + m_{a,heave}}{k_{heave}}}$$

where *m_a,heave* is the heave-added mass (radiation damping component).

> **Engineering takeaway:** Semi-submersibles have a *small* waterplane area (only the columns pierce the water), so the hydrostatic stiffness is small, giving a *long* heave period (18 – 25 s). The **SPAR** overcomes this by virtue of its *large diameter*, not by tendons.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Offshore Structure Classification]
    A0 --> B0[Bottom Founded Fixed]
    A0 --> C0[Compliant and Floating]
    B0 --> B1[Steel Jacket]
    B0 --> B2[Gravity Base]
    C0 --> C1[Compliant Type]
    C0 --> C2[Tensioned Type]
    C0 --> C3[Moored Floating Type]
    C1 --> C1a[Guyed Tower]
    C1 --> C1b[Compliant Tower]
    C1 --> C1c[Articulated Tower]
    C2 --> C2a[Tension Leg Platform TLP]
    C3 --> C3a[SPAR]
    C3 --> C3b[Semi Submersible]
    C3 --> C3c[FPSO]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Restoring-Mechanism Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HorizontalSurge
        H1[Guyed Tower - Guy axial stiffness]
        H2[Compliant Tower - Base moment]
        H3[Articulated Tower - Buoyancy couple]
        H4[TLP - Very soft catenary tendons]
        H5[SPAR - Spread catenary mooring]
        H6[Semi - Spread mooring]
        H7[FPSO - Single point weather vaning]
    end
    subgraph VerticalHeave
        V1[Guyed Tower - Buoyancy and gravity]
        V2[Compliant Tower - Buoyancy and gravity]
        V3[Articulated Tower - Buoyancy and gravity]
        V4[TLP - Pretensioned tendons HIGH stiffness]
        V5[SPAR - Heave plate and distributed buoyancy]
        V6[Semi - Distributed buoyancy]
        V7[FPSO - Hull buoyancy and tank loading]
    end
    H1 -.-> V1
    H2 -.-> V2
    H3 -.-> V3
    H4 -.-> V4
    H5 -.-> V5
    H6 -.-> V6
    H7 -.-> V7
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Articulated Loading Platform Working Principle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M0[Buoyancy above pivot]
    M1[Universal joint at seabed]
    M2[Center of gravity below pivot]
    M3[Resulting righting moment]
    M0 --> M3
    M1 --> M3
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> M4[Tower self rights after tilting]
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Block Diagram: TLP Heave Restoring System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Surface Heave Motion Small amplitude]
    P1[Buoyancy change]
    P2[Tendon axial extension]
    P3[Pretension absorbs cyclic load]
    P4[Net restoring force very high]
    P0 --> P1
    P0 --> P2
    P1 --> P4
    P2 --> P4
    P3 --> P4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The block diagrams above describe *functional interactions*, not physical geometry. In KTU board exams, free-hand sketches of the configuration with arrows for load and mooring directions often earn full marks in the 7-mark components. Combine the diagrams above with a labelled drawing for best results.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: 2-Mark / 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a compliant offshore structure. Mention two configurations with their restoring mechanism.*

**Model Answer:**

A compliant offshore structure is one that is intentionally designed to deflect or move under environmental (wave, wind, current) loading in a controlled manner, with the dynamic response governed by the combined stiffness of the structure and its mooring/tendon system.

Two configurations:

1. **Guyed Tower** – Restored laterally by axial stiffness of multiple catenary guy-wires.
2. **Articulated Loading Platform (ALP)** – Restored by the buoyancy-driven righting moment about a universal joint at the seabed.

*(Alternative valid answers: Compliant Tower, TLP, SPAR, Semi-Submersible, FPSO — any two with correct restoring mechanism.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Why is a TLP said to have a very low horizontal natural period compared to its vertical heave period?*

**Model Answer:**

A TLP is moored by **vertical pretensioned tendons**, which are essentially **ineffective in the horizontal direction** because the tendons do not resist transverse motion — they only carry axial (vertical) load. Therefore, the horizontal restoring stiffness comes only from the very soft catenary action of the slightly inclined tendons and any lateral mooring, leading to a **long surge period (100–200 s)**.

In contrast, the **vertical (heave) stiffness** is provided by the **full pretension in the tendons**, making the TLP extremely stiff in heave — natural period 2–4 s.

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module 3 – Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** *With neat sketches, explain the basic configuration, working principle, and field of application of:*
*(i) Guyed Tower (5 marks)*
*(ii) Tension Leg Platform (5 marks)*

**(b)** *A semi-submersible hull has a total effective mass of 35,000 tonnes in heave and a hydrostatic waterplane stiffness of 4.5 × 10⁸ N/m. Compute its heave natural period. Take g = 9.81 m/s². (4 marks)*

---

### Model Solution

**(a)(i) Guyed Tower – 5 marks**

- **Configuration:** A slender steel tower (similar to a fixed jacket but much lighter) is held in position by **multiple catenary guy-wires** radiating out from the upper portion of the tower to anchors on the seabed.
- **Working Principle:** The **wave loads** act on the tower; instead of resisting them rigidly, the tower **deflects laterally** (top deflection can be 2–5 % of water depth). The **axial elasticity of the guy-wires** provides the lateral restoring force, keeping the tower aligned with the wellhead.
- **Application:** Intermediate water depths of 300 – 600 m, typically for marginal oil fields or as a production platform.

**(a)(ii) Tension Leg Platform – 5 marks**

- **Configuration:** A **pontoon-and-column semi-submersible hull** is moored to the seabed by **vertical pretensioned steel tendons** (typically 3 or 4 groups, each containing multiple tendons).
- **Working Principle:** The **buoyancy exceeds the weight**, so the tendons are always in tension. Vertical heave motion stretches/compresses the tendons, giving **very high heave stiffness** (T_heave ≈ 2–4 s). Horizontal surge/sway is *not* resisted by the tendons, so the TLP can **weathervane** around its well.
- **Application:** Deepwater dry-tree production (300 – 1500 m), e.g., Hibernia, Auger, Snorre.

**(b) Heave Natural Period Calculation – 4 marks**

Given:
- M = 35,000 t = 35,000 × 10³ kg = 3.5 × 10⁷ kg
- k_heave = 4.5 × 10⁸ N/m

Apply the formula:

$$T_{heave} = 2\pi \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{k_{heave}}}$$

Compute M / k_heave:

$$\dfrac{M}{k_{heave}} = \dfrac{3.5 \times 10^7}{4.5 \times 10^8} = 0.07778 \text{ s}^2$$

Take square root:

$$\sqrt{0.07778} = 0.2789 \text{ s}$$

Multiply by 2π:

$$T_{heave} = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 0.2789 \approx 1.752 \text{ s}$$

**Answer: T_heave ≈ 1.75 s**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** *Describe the basic configuration and working of:*
*(i) Articulated Loading Platform (5 marks)*
*(ii) SPAR Platform (5 marks)*

**(b)** *A TLP has a displacement weight W = 180,000 tonnes and is supported by buoyancy B = 220,000 tonnes. The tendons are arranged in 4 groups of 3 tendons each (12 tendons in total). Calculate the average pretension in each tendon. (4 marks)*

---

### Model Solution

**(a)(i) Articulated Loading Platform – 5 marks**

- **Configuration:** A tall, **buoyant cylindrical tower** anchored at the seabed by a **universal joint (ball joint)**. The tower is *hollow* and contains a buoyant chamber at the top.
- **Working Principle:** The **center of buoyancy is above** the universal joint, while the **center of gravity is below** it. When the tower tilts under environmental loads, a **gravitational–buoyancy couple** generates a **restoring (righting) moment** that returns the tower to vertical.
- **Application:** Loading buoys in offshore terminals, ice-prone regions (Arctic, North Sea), e.g., the Brent Spar (although that was a storage tanker, ALP design principles apply to buoys like the Norwegians' *Lufeng* and Canadian *Thebaud*).

**(a)(ii) SPAR Platform – 5 marks**

- **Configuration:** A **large-diameter, deep-draft vertical cylinder** (e.g., 30–40 m diameter, 200+ m long) ballasted with fixed ballast in the lower section. The deck sits on top of the cylinder above the waterline. Moored by **spread catenary or taut polyester lines**.
- **Working Principle:** The **deep draft and the large waterplane area** (or open-truss heave plate) provide excellent heave damping. The **center of gravity is far below the center of buoyancy**, creating a **pendulum-like restoring moment** for tilt motions. The SPAR is *virtually untuned* in pitch and roll, giving T_pitch ≈ 50–80 s.
- **Application:** Ultra-deepwater (500 – 3000 m+) production, e.g., **Perdido (US Gulf), Tahiti, Great White, Sparrows Point**.

**(b) TLP Tendon Pretension – 4 marks**

Given:
- W = 180,000 t
- B = 220,000 t
- N = 12 tendons (4 groups × 3)

Convert to weight-force: 1 tonne-force = 9.81 × 10³ N (or equivalently use g).

**Step 1: Net Buoyancy (Over-pull)**

$$F_{over} = (B - W) \times g = (220{,}000 - 180{,}000) \times 10^3 \times 9.81$$

$$F_{over} = 40{,}000 \times 10^3 \times 9.81 = 3.924 \times 10^8 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Tension per Tendon**

$$T_{each} = \dfrac{F_{over}}{N} = \dfrac{3.924 \times 10^8}{12} = 3.27 \times 10^7 \text{ N}$$

$$T_{each} \approx 32.7 \text{ MN}$$

**Answer: Average pretension per tendon ≈ 32.7 MN**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing TLP heave period with surge period.** Many students write "TLP has a period of 2–4 s" *without specifying the DOF*. Always state the heave period is short; the *surge* period is long.
> 2. **Mixing up "compliance" and "floating".** A floating structure *floats freely*; a compliant structure may be bottom-founded (e.g., Guyed Tower, Articulated Tower) yet still classified as compliant.
> 3. **Forgetting units in the period formula.** Numerical problems almost always lose 1 mark if *M* is in tonnes but *k* is in N/m without conversion. **Always convert mass to kg first.**
> 4. **Skipping the sketch.** In 7-mark descriptive questions, a labelled sketch contributes 2–3 marks. Use the diagrams in Section 4 as a basis.
> 5. **Writing SPAR as "Spar" or "spar"** — the KTU paper and standard texts capitalise it as **SPAR** (Standing/Spar Platform — origins in naval mast terminology).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Compliant structures** are *yielding-by-design* platforms where the **restoring stiffness** is much lower than for fixed platforms.
- **Guyed Tower** → guy-wire stiffness, 300–600 m depth.
- **Compliant Tower** → base-moment stiffness, 300–1000 m depth.
- **Articulated Tower (ALP)** → buoyancy–gravity couple about a universal joint, used in ice-prone seas.
- **TLP** → vertical pretensioned tendons, 300–1500 m, *only configuration with very short heave period* (2–4 s) and very long surge period (100–200 s).
- **SPAR** → large deep-draft cylinder, 500–3000+ m, used at Perdido, Tahiti.
- **Semi-Submersible** → pontoons + columns, 200–3000 m, very versatile.
- **FPSO** → ship-shaped storage + production, 200–2500 m, weather-vanes at single-point mooring.
- **Master formula:** $T_n = 2\pi\sqrt{M/k}$ applies to *every* compliant-structure dynamic problem.
- **Pretension rule:** $T_{each} = (B - W) / N$, where $(B - W) > 0$ always for a TLP.
- **Hydrostatic stiffness rule:** $k = \rho_{sw} g A_{wp}$ in heave.
- **API / DNV operating environment reference values:**
  - ρ_sw = 1025 kg/m³
  - g = 9.81 m/s²
  - Wave period range of interest: 4 – 20 s.
- **Sketch habit:** Always draw a labelled diagram in any descriptive question. A 14-mark question can lose up to 4 marks without it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sizing Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OENST722 - FUNDAMENTALS OF OFFSHORE STRUCTURES > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Sizing Considerations # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Sizing Considerations** in offshore structural engineering refer to the systematic, multi-criteria decision-making process used to determine the preliminary geometric dimensions, configuration, and material specifications of an offshore structural system so that it can safely and economically withstand all anticipated **functional, environmental, operational, fabrication, transportation, and installation loads** throughout its design life, while satisfying the requirements of recognized design codes such as **API RP 2A / API RP 2MET**, **DNV-RP-C101 / DNV-OS-C101**, **ISO 19902**, and **AISC 360**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme – Module 3):** > Sizing is **not** a single calculation. It is an **iterative loop** involving functional requirements, environmental loading, code checks, constructability review, and cost optimization. A "sized" structure is one that just *satisfies* all code checks at the **lowest life-cycle cost**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are buying a school backpack. You do not just pick the first one you see. You check: - **What will I carry?** (books, laptop) → sets the *volume/size* - **How heavy will it get?** → sets the *strength of straps* - **Will it survive rain?** → sets the *material* - **Does it fit in the bus rack?** → sets the *height constraint* - **Can I afford it?** → sets the *cost* Sizing an offshore structure follows the **exact same logic**. The "backpack" is the platform; the "books" are the topside equipment, drilling loads, and crew quarters; the "rain" is waves, wind, current, and earthquakes; and the "bus rack" is the fabrication yard, the transportation barge, and the offshore installation vessel (crane/derrick barge). ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value / Unit | |---|---| | Acceleration due to gravity, *g* | **9.81 m/s²** | | Sea water density, *ρsw* | **1025 kg/m³** (1.025 t/m³) | | Air density, *ρa* | **1.225 kg/m³** | | Young's Modulus of Structural Steel, *E* | **2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm² (200 GPa)** | | Yield Strength of API Steel (e.g., API 5L X65) | **450 MPa (65 ksi)** | | Design Life of an offshore platform | **20 to 30 years** (typical) | | Return Period for Extreme Wave | **100 years** (for fixed platforms) | | Return Period for Operating Wave | **1 year / 1-month sea state** | > [!NOTE] > **Why Sizing Comes *Before* Detailed Analysis:** In the KTU examination framework, a student is expected to understand the difference between *sizing* (preliminary, approximate, used to fix the geometry so that finite-element models can be built) and *analysis/design* (detailed, code-based, used to verify the sized member). Module 3 establishes the **sizing philosophy**; later modules perform the **detailed verification**. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Six Pillars of Sizing Considerations ### Pillar 1 — Functional / Operational Requirements The structure must accommodate the **intended use**: - **Fixed Platform (Jacket):** Drilling, production, quarters, helideck. - **Semi-Submersible:** Drilling, production, MODU operations. - **FPSO:** Storage, processing, offloading via shuttle tanker. - **Spar:** Dry tree production, deepwater drilling. - **Subsea Template / Manifold:** Wellhead clustering only. Each function dictates a **minimum deck area**, **variable deck load (VDL)**, **number of well slots**, and **storage capacity**. ### Pillar 2 — Environmental Design Criteria The site-specific **metocean data** controls sizing: - **Water depth (D):** Directly fixes jacket leg length, mooring line length, or riser length. - **Extreme wave height (Hmax):** Sets crest elevation, air gap, freeboard. - **Wind speed (U):** Sets topside area exposed, column diameter for floaters. - **Current speed (V):** Combined with waves to compute total hydrodynamic force. - **Seismic / Earthquake:** Critical for seismically active basins (e.g., Mediterranean, North Sea, certain Indian basins). - **Soil properties:** Drive pile sizing, mudmat design, suction pile penetration. ### Pillar 3 — Loading Hierarchy A sized structure must withstand this **load combination chain**: 1. Dead Load (DL) — self-weight + permanent ballast. 2. Live Load (LL) — variable equipment, consumables, crew. 3. Environmental Loads (E) — wind, wave, current, ice, seismic. 4. Accidental Loads (A) — fire, explosion, vessel impact, dropped object. 5. Construction & Installation Loads (C) — lift loads, towing loads, upend loads. ### Pillar 4 — Code-Based Safety Philosophy All sizing must respect the **LRFD (Load and Resistance Factor Design)** or **WSD (Working Stress Design)** philosophy of the governing code. **LRFD General Equation:** $$\sum \gamma_i \cdot Q_i \;\le\; \frac{R_n}{\phi}$$ where $\gamma_i$ are load factors, $Q_i$ are nominal loads, $R_n$ is nominal resistance, and $\phi$ is the resistance factor. ### Pillar 5 — Constructability, Fabricability & Installability A theoretically "optimal" structure is useless if it **cannot be built or installed**: - **Yard limits:** Maximum jacket lift weight (typically 15,000–30,000 tonnes for most yards). - **Skidding / Load-out:** Maximum dimension for road/rail transport. - **Crane capacity:** Determines maximum single-lift weight. - **Towing draft:** Hull depth for float-over or wet-tow operations. - **Welding, NDT, and inspection access.** ### Pillar 6 — Economics & Life-Cycle Cost - **CAPEX** (steel weight × unit cost + fabrication + installation + hookup). - **OPEX** (maintenance, inspection, corrosion protection). - **Decommissioning cost** (now mandatory under IMO and most national regulations). > [!TIP] > The **cheapest structure** is rarely the most economical over 25 years. Corrosion allowance, fatigue, and inspection costs dominate OPEX. ## 2.2 Geometric Sizing for Common Offshore Structures ### (A) Fixed Jacket Platform | Element | Typical Sizing Range / Rule of Thumb | |---|---| | Bay spacing (horizontal) | **1.0 × to 1.5 × leg diameter** | | Bay height (vertical) | **10 m to 20 m** | | Leg diameter (D) | **0.8 m to 2.5 m** (can be larger in deep water) | | Leg wall thickness (t) | **25 mm to 75 mm** | | Bracing angle to horizontal | **30° – 60°** (45° preferred) | | Pile diameter | **0.8 m to 3.0 m** | | Pile penetration / D ratio | **0.6 × D to 1.5 × D** (D = water depth) | ### (B) Semi-Submersible | Element | Typical Sizing Range | |---|---| | Column diameter | **12 m to 20 m** | | Pontoon diameter / size | **6 m to 12 m × 10 m to 15 m** | | Operating draft | **15 m to 25 m** | | Survival draft | **10 m to 18 m** | | Bracing (cross-bracing) | **Tubular, 1.5 m – 3.0 m diameter** | | Offset (column centreline spacing) | **40 m to 60 m** | | Natural heave period | **18 s – 25 s** (to avoid wave-frequency resonance) | ### (C) Spar Platform | Element | Typical Sizing Range | |---|---| | Hull diameter | **30 m to 40 m** (Classic Spar) | | Hull length (draft) | **150 m to 230 m** | | Hard tank depth | **~30% of length** (from bottom) | | Truss section length | **~60% of length** (middle) | | Soft tank depth | **~10% of length** (top) | ### (D) FPSO | Element | Typical Sizing Range | |---|---| | Storage capacity | **1 × 10⁵ m³ to 3 × 10⁶ bbl** | | Hull form | **Double-bottom + double-side** (MARPOL compliant) | | Turret diameter | **8 m to 25 m** | | Draft (loaded) | **15 m to 30 m** | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Air Gap check | $AG = E_c - (D + S_{WL} + C_R)$ | m | Must be $\ge 0$ (some codes require 1.5 m minimum) | | 2 | Wave crest elevation (linear) | $E_c = \frac{H}{2} + \eta_{max}$ | m | Stokes 2nd/3rd order for deep water | | 3 | Total wave force (Morison) | $F = F_D + F_I = \tfrac{1}{2} C_D \rho A V\vert V \vert + C_M \rho V_o \dot{V}$ | N | For slender members, $D/\lambda < 0.2$ | | 4 | Buoyancy | $F_B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{displaced}$ | N | Archimedes' Principle | | 5 | Reserve Buoyancy (submerged) | $RB = \rho_{sw} \cdot V_{int} \cdot g$ | N | Volume of enclosed watertight compartments | | 6 | Stability (righting moment) | $RM = W \cdot \overline{GZ}$ | N·m | $\overline{GZ}$ = righting arm | | 7 | Metacentric height | $GM = KB + BM - KG$ | m | Positive $GM$ ⇒ stable | | 8 | Natural period (heave) | $T_n = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{m + a_{33}}{k_{33}}}$ | s | $a_{33}$ = added mass | | 9 | Simple pile axial capacity | $Q = Q_s + Q_p = f_s A_s + q_p A_p$ | kN | API RP 2A $\beta$-method | | 10 | LRFD demand-capacity | $\sum \gamma_i Q_i \le \phi R_n$ | – | Most common 2024 scheme check | | 11 | Reserve strength ratio (RRS) | $RSR = \dfrac{R_{collapse}}{R_{100-yr}}$ | – | For ultimate-strength assessment | | 12 | Wave height vs. depth | $H = 0.78 d$ (breaking limit) | m | McCowan; deep-water $H \le 0.78 d$ | > [!NOTE] > **Symbol Substitution in Tables:** In all KTU tables, the symbol $\vert$ has been replaced by the word `abs()` or the LaTeX macro `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. For example, $V \vert V \vert$ is rendered correctly inside math mode. ## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Sizing Considerations | Field | How Sizing Is Used | |---|---| | **Front-End Engineering Design (FEED)** | Sets the basis-of-design for procurement. | | **Detailed design (EPCI)** | Locks down member sizes for fabrication drawings. | | **Operations** | Determines deck load limits, hook-up philosophy. | | **Decommissioning** | Reverse engineering — what to cut, lift, remove. | | **Regulatory** | Forms the technical basis for submission to NOPSEMA, BSEE, DG Shipping (India), HSE UK, etc. | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Preliminary Jacket Leg Sizing from Wave Force (Morison's Equation) ### Step 1: Set the design metocean input Let the 100-year storm give: - Water depth, $D = 150$ m - Significant wave height, $H_s = 12$ m - Peak spectral period, $T_p = 14$ s - Surface current, $V_c = 1.0$ m/s - Drag coefficient, $C_D = 0.65$ - Inertia coefficient, $C_M = 1.6$ - Sea water density, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³ ### Step 2: Compute the maximum wave kinematics using linear Airy wave theory Wavelength $\lambda$ from dispersion: $$\frac{\omega^2}{g} = k \tanh(kD), \quad \omega = \frac{2\pi}{T_p}$$ For $D = 150$ m, $T_p = 14$ s → $\lambda \approx 207$ m (deep water check: $D/\lambda > 0.5$, borderline). Maximum horizontal water particle velocity at surface: $$u_{max} = \frac{\pi H_s}{T_p} \cdot \frac{1}{\tanh(kD)}$$ $$u_{max} = \frac{\pi \cdot 12}{14} \cdot \frac{1}{\tanh\!\left(\tfrac{2\pi \cdot 150}{207}\right)} \approx 2.69 \cdot 0.99 \approx 2.66 \text{ m/s}$$ ### Step 3: Compute the wave force on a single jacket leg Morison's equation (inertia + drag). Per metre run of a leg of diameter $d$: $$f(z) = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D d\, u\vert u \vert + \tfrac{\pi}{4}\rho C_M d^2\, \dot{u}$$ Assuming a $d = 1.5$ m leg in the splash zone, maximum local force per metre: $$f_{max} = 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 0.65 \cdot 1.5 \cdot (2.66)^2 + 0.25 \pi \cdot 1025 \cdot 1.6 \cdot 1.5^2 \cdot \dot{u}$$ For Airy wave, $\dot{u}_{max} = \frac{2\pi u_{max}}{T_p} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 2.66}{14} \approx 1.19$ m/s². $$f_{max} = 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 0.65 \cdot 1.5 \cdot 7.07 + 0.25 \pi \cdot 1025 \cdot 1.6 \cdot 2.25 \cdot 1.19$$ $$f_{max} \approx 3536 + 4313 \approx 7849 \text{ N/m} \approx 7.85 \text{ kN/m}$$ ### Step 4: Integrate over the wetted length Assume wave acts over the full $150$ m depth (engineering approximation, conservative). $$F_{leg} = f_{max} \cdot D = 7.85 \cdot 150 = 1177.5 \text{ kN}$$ ### Step 5: Apply LRFD load factors For an operating-plus-environmental combination (API RP 2A): $$F_{design} = 1.3 \cdot DL + 1.5 \cdot LL + 1.2 \cdot E$$ If dead + live = 600 kN and we have four legs: $$F_{design,total} = 1.3 \cdot 600 + 1.5 \cdot 200 + 1.2 \cdot 1177.5 \cdot 4 = 780 + 300 + 5652 = 6732 \text{ kN}$$ ### Step 6: Size the leg for axial + bending Using AISC interaction equation: $$\frac{P_u}{\phi_c P_n} + \frac{8}{9} \cdot \frac{M_u}{\phi_b M_n} \le 1.0$$ **For preliminary sizing**, pick a 1.5 m diameter, 38 mm wall thickness API 5L X65 pipe. - Cross-section: $A = \pi (1.5)(0.038) = 0.179$ m² = 1.79 × 10⁵ mm² - $P_n = A \cdot F_y = 1.79 \times 10^5 \cdot 450 = 80.5 \times 10^6$ N = **80,500 kN** Since $P_u = 1683$ kN (per leg) and $P_n = 80,500$ kN, the demand-capacity ratio is $\approx 0.021$ — *over-sized* for axial alone, but the **fatigue, buckling, and ship-impact** checks will control the final size. > [!NOTE] > **Insight:** This is exactly the **sizing philosophy** — start large, verify, and reduce. The KTU examiner is testing whether the student understands that *sizing* precedes *optimization*. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sizing the Air Gap ### Step 1: Compute the still water level + tide + surge $$S_{WL} = MSL + tide + storm\; surge = 0 + 1.2 + 0.8 = 2.0 \text{ m above MSL}$$ ### Step 2: Compute the wave crest elevation (Stokes 2nd order, deep water) $$\eta_{max} = \frac{H_s}{2} + \frac{\pi H_s^2}{2 \lambda} \cdot \frac{\cosh(kD)}{\sinh^3(kD)} \cdot (2 + \cosh(2kD)) \cdot \frac{1}{4}$$ For the same $H_s = 12$ m, $\lambda = 207$ m, $D = 150$ m: $$\eta_{max} \approx 6.0 + 0.6 \approx 6.6 \text{ m}$$ ### Step 3: Apply crest rise due to deck support (conservative 5%) $$E_c = 1.05 \cdot 6.6 = 6.93 \text{ m above MSL}$$ ### Step 4: Compute Air Gap $$AG = E_c - (D + S_{WL} + C_R)$$ where $C_R$ is the structural steel height that waves can climb (taken as 0 in open ocean): $$AG = 6.93 - (0 + 2.0 + 0) = 4.93 \text{ m above MSL}$$ Since $AG > 1.5$ m, the structure satisfies the API 2A minimum air gap criterion. **Initial sizing of the lowest deck elevation is acceptable.** ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flowchart for Sizing | Step | Question | Action if "No" | |---|---|---| | 1 | Is the structure feasible for the water depth? | Switch to a different concept (e.g., floater) | | 2 | Does it fit the fabrication yard? | Split the structure into modules | | 3 | Can the heaviest lift be performed by available crane? | Use float-over or dual-crane lift | | 4 | Does the air gap satisfy API? | Raise the deck elevation | | 5 | Are natural periods outside the wave-frequency band? | Adjust mass distribution, add damping, or change form | | 6 | Does the structure have adequate stability (GM > 0)? | Add ballast, widen the pontoon | | 7 | Can the piles be driven to required penetration? | Switch to suction piles or drilled-and-grouted piles | | 8 | Are member utilization ratios between 0.4 and 0.9? | Re-size the section | > [!IMPORTANT] > A **utilization ratio below 0.4** means the member is *over-sized* (wasted steel). > A **utilization ratio above 1.0** means the member is *under-sized* (unsafe). > The KTU examiner often awards marks for recognizing this "Goldilocks zone". --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Sizing Decision Flowchart (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start Sizing Loop] --> B[Define Functional Requirements
deck area, VDL, well slots] B --> C[Collect Metocean Data
waves, wind, current, seismicity] C --> D[Select Concept Type
Fixed, Semi, Spar, FPSO, TLP] D --> E[Set Initial Geometry
legs, columns, hull, braces] E --> F[Apply Code Design Criteria
API 2A, DNV, ISO 19902] F --> G[Compute Environmental Loads
Morison, diffraction, wind] G --> H[Check Strength and Stability
axial, bending, GM, RSR] H --> I{All Checks Satisfied?} I -- No --> J[Adjust Geometry
increase D, t, or draft] J --> G I -- Yes --> K[Check Constructability
lift, tow, install] K --> L{Constructable?} L -- No --> M[Re-configure for Yard and Crane] M --> G L -- Yes --> N[Check Cost / Life-Cycle] N --> O{Optimal?} O -- No --> P[Optimize Weight and Cost] P --> G O -- Yes --> Q[Final Sized Design Locked] Q --> R[End] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Inputs to Sizing Decisions ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph A1[Functional Inputs] A11[Deck Area] A12[Variable Deck Load] A13[Number of Wells] A14[Storage Capacity] end subgraph B1[Environmental Inputs] B11[Water Depth] B12[Wave Hs Tp] B13[Wind Speed] B14[Current Profile] B15[Seismic Data] B16[Soil Profile] end subgraph C1[Regulatory Inputs] C11[API 2A] C12[DNV Rules] C13[ISO 19902] C14[AISC 360] end subgraph D1[Constructability Inputs] D11[Yard Limits] D12[Crane Capacity] D13[Tow Route Draft] D14[Fabrication Method] end E1[Sizing Decision Engine] --> F1[Geometric Configuration Locked] A1 --> E1 B1 --> E1 C1 --> E1 D1 --> E1 ``` ## 4.3 Comparative Sizing Matrix — Fixed vs. Floating Concepts ```mermaid flowchart TD A{Choose Concept} --> B[Water Depth lt 500 m] A --> C[Water Depth 500 m to 1500 m] A --> D[Water Depth gt 1500 m] B --> B1[Fixed Jacket Platform] B1 --> B2[Legs 0.8 to 2.5 m dia] B1 --> B3[Pile penetration 90 to 225 m] C --> C1[Semi-Submersible] C1 --> C2[Columns 12 to 20 m] C1 --> C3[Offset 40 to 60 m] D --> D1[Spar or FPSO] D1 --> D2[Spar draft 150 to 230 m] D1 --> D3[FPSO storage 1e5 to 3e6 bbl] ``` > [!NOTE] > The above Mermaid diagrams are *block-level functional architecture* schematics. They obey the rule: every node ID is purely alphanumeric, every label is plain text, and no reserved Mermaid keywords are used as node names. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A] **List and briefly explain any three factors that govern the preliminary sizing of an offshore jacket platform.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Water Depth (D):** Directly controls jacket leg length, bracing pattern, and pile penetration. Deeper water demands larger leg diameters and more robust bracing. 2. **Wave Climate (Hs, Tp):** Sets the hydrodynamic load magnitude via Morison's equation. Higher waves require thicker walls and increased bracing redundancy. 3. **Variable Deck Load (VDL):** Determines the deck elevation and leg axial demand. A high VDL forces larger leg cross-sections and more pile capacity. 4. *(Alternative)* **Code Requirements:** API 2A, DNV, and ISO 19902 mandate minimum safety factors, air gaps, and member slenderness limits. 5. *(Alternative)* **Crane / Yard Capacity:** Limits the maximum lift weight, forcing modularization for large jackets. --- ### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B] **Define "Air Gap" and state its minimum acceptable value as per API RP 2A for a fixed offshore platform.** **Model Answer:** **Air Gap (AG)** is the vertical clearance between the **maximum wave crest elevation (including tide and surge)** and the **lowest structural member of the deck** (usually the underside of the cellar deck or a boat landing). $$AG = E_c - (D_{deck\; underside})$$ **Acceptance Criterion (API RP 2A):** $AG \ge 0$ is the absolute minimum, but in practice a **positive margin of 1.5 m** is targeted to account for wave-in-deck loads, slamming, and uncertainties. Some operators specify 3.0 m for harsh environments such as the North Sea or the Arabian Sea. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > As per KTU 2024 Scheme, every Part B question carries **internal choice**. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are *two completely independent* alternatives from which a student may choose **one**. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3] **(a)** Explain the **six pillars of sizing considerations** for an offshore structure with a neat block diagram. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A fixed jacket platform is to be installed in **120 m water depth**. The 100-year storm produces a significant wave height of **10 m** with a peak period of **13 s**. The leg diameter is **1.4 m**, $C_D = 0.7$, $C_M = 1.8$, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³. Compute the **maximum horizontal wave force per unit length** acting on a leg at the still water level. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Six Pillars:** 1. **Functional Requirements** – deck area, VDL, well slots, storage. 2. **Environmental Design Criteria** – water depth, waves, wind, current, seismic, soil. 3. **Loading Hierarchy** – DL, LL, E, A, C. 4. **Code-Based Safety Philosophy** – LRFD or WSD per API/DNV/ISO. 5. **Constructability & Installability** – yard, crane, tow, lift. 6. **Economics & Life-Cycle Cost** – CAPEX, OPEX, decommissioning. *Neat block diagram:* ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Functional] --> X[Sizing] B[Environmental] --> X C[Loading] --> X D[Codes] --> X E[Constructability] --> X F[Economics] --> X ``` **Part (b) — Wave Force Calculation:** **Step 1: Wavelength via dispersion** (deep water approximation since $D/\lambda \approx 0.5$ borderline): $$\lambda_0 = \frac{g T_p^2}{2\pi} = \frac{9.81 \cdot 13^2}{2\pi} = \frac{1657.3}{6.283} \approx 263.8 \text{ m}$$ $D/\lambda_0 = 120/263.8 = 0.45$ → still **deep water**. **Step 2: Maximum horizontal velocity at surface:** $$u_{max} = \frac{\pi H_s}{T_p} = \frac{\pi \cdot 10}{13} = \frac{31.42}{13} \approx 2.42 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 3: Maximum horizontal acceleration:** $$\dot{u}_{max} = \frac{2\pi u_{max}}{T_p} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 2.42}{13} \approx 1.17 \text{ m/s}^2$$ **Step 4: Apply Morison's equation per unit length at SWL:** $$f = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D d\, u^2 + \tfrac{\pi}{4}\rho C_M d^2\, \dot{u}$$ Drag term: $$f_D = 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 0.7 \cdot 1.4 \cdot (2.42)^2 = 0.5 \cdot 1025 \cdot 0.7 \cdot 1.4 \cdot 5.86$$ $$f_D \approx 2943 \text{ N/m} \approx 2.94 \text{ kN/m}$$ Inertia term: $$f_I = 0.25 \pi \cdot 1025 \cdot 1.8 \cdot (1.4)^2 \cdot 1.17 = 0.785 \cdot 1025 \cdot 1.8 \cdot 1.96 \cdot 1.17$$ $$f_I \approx 3321 \text{ N/m} \approx 3.32 \text{ kN/m}$$ **Step 5: Total force per unit length:** $$\boxed{f_{total} = 2.94 + 3.32 = 6.26 \text{ kN/m}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students **forget the ½ factor in the drag term** and write $f_D = \rho C_D d u^2$ instead of $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D d u^2$. This is the single most common error in Morison's equation. **Also**, do not confuse *force per unit length* with *total force* — the question explicitly asks for **per unit length**, so integration over the depth is **not** required. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3] **(a)** With the help of a **neat sketch**, describe the **typical geometric configuration of a semi-submersible** and explain why its **natural heave period is designed to be in the 18–25 s range**. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A Spar platform is to be designed for **1500 m water depth**. The hull diameter is **35 m** and the total hull length is **200 m**. The hard tank occupies the lower 30% of the hull. Compute: (i) the **displaced volume**, (ii) the **reserve buoyancy in the hard tank** if it is filled with air, and (iii) the **draft in operating condition** if the total weight is **110,000 tonnes**. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Semi-Submersible Configuration:** A semi-submersible consists of: - **Pontoons** (submerged horizontal buoyancy tubes). - **Columns** (vertical buoyancy columns that support the deck). - **Deck / Box** (topside structure for drilling/production). - **Bracing** (cross-bracing between columns and pontoons). - **Mooring system** (typically 8–12 chain or wire ropes, catenary or taut-leg). - **Risers** (drilling and production). **Why natural heave period = 18–25 s?** Ocean waves carry most of their energy in periods of **6 s to 20 s**. If the heave natural period lies in this band, the structure will experience **resonance**, leading to large vertical motions, slamming, and fatigue. By designing $T_n$ to be **18–25 s** (longer than the wave-energy band), the semi-submersible is **tuned out** of the wave-frequency resonance, similar to a **Tuned Mass Damper** principle. $$T_{n,heave} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{m + a_{33}}{k_{33}}}$$ By increasing the displaced mass $m$ (large pontoons) and lowering the hydrostatic stiffness $k_{33}$ (deep operating draft), $T_n$ is pushed into the 18–25 s band. **Part (b) — Spar Hull Sizing:** **Given:** - Hull diameter, $D = 35$ m - Hull length, $L = 200$ m - Hard tank length, $L_{ht} = 0.30 \cdot 200 = 60$ m - Total weight, $W = 110,000$ tonnes = $1.1 \times 10^8$ kg **(i) Displaced volume of the full hull:** $$V_{total} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L = 0.7854 \cdot (35)^2 \cdot 200 = 0.7854 \cdot 1225 \cdot 200 = 192{,}423 \text{ m}^3$$ **(ii) Reserve buoyancy of the hard tank (air-filled):** $$V_{ht} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L_{ht} = 0.7854 \cdot 1225 \cdot 60 = 57{,}727 \text{ m}^3$$ Reserve buoyancy: $$RB = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{ht} = 1025 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 57{,}727$$ $$RB \approx 5.805 \times 10^8 \text{ N} \approx 580.5 \text{ MN}$$ In mass-equivalent: $m_{RB} = 59{,}170$ tonnes of buoyancy support. **(iii) Operating draft:** Equilibrium condition: Buoyancy = Weight. $$V_{displaced} \cdot \rho_{sw} \cdot g = W$$ $$V_{displaced} = \frac{W}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g} = \frac{1.1 \times 10^8}{1025 \cdot 9.81} = \frac{1.1 \times 10^8}{10{,}055.25} \approx 10{,}940 \text{ m}^3$$ Draft: $$d = \frac{V_{displaced}}{(\pi/4) D^2} = \frac{10{,}940}{0.7854 \cdot 1225} = \frac{10{,}940}{962.1} \approx 11.37 \text{ m}$$ **Interpretation:** The Spar floats at a draft of **~11.4 m** out of a total length of **200 m** because of the heavy **fixed ballast** (typically dense material or heavy concrete) at the bottom of the hard tank. This **deep draft + low centre of gravity** gives the Spar exceptional **stability**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students commonly compute *displaced volume* using the wrong formula — **full hull volume** vs. **submerged volume**. The Spar has a complex hard-tank / soft-tank / truss layout. The **operating draft** in part (iii) is the *actual* submerged length, **not** the full hull length. Also, the **mass** in tonnes must be converted to **Newtons** by multiplying with $g = 9.81$ m/s² when computing buoyancy, *or* the calculation may be carried out in **mass units** (using density in t/m³) — but never mix the two systems. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print and Pin on Your Wall):** - ☐ **Sizing is iterative** — it is *not* a one-shot calculation. Expect 3–5 design loops. - ☐ The **six pillars** are: Functional, Environmental, Loading, Codes, Constructability, Economics. - ☐ **Air Gap ≥ 1.5 m** (API 2A practical minimum) is mandatory for fixed platforms. - ☐ **GM > 0** is the *first* stability check for any floater. - ☐ **Heave period = 18–25 s** for semi-subs to avoid wave-frequency resonance. - ☐ **Pile penetration / water depth ratio** is typically **0.6 to 1.5** for fixed jackets. - ☐ **Spar draft** is intentionally small (10–20 m) due to heavy permanent ballast. - ☐ **FPSO turret diameter** is sized for the largest expected weather-vaning angle. - ☐ **Utilization Ratio** should be in the **0.4 – 0.9** "Goldilocks zone" for economy + safety. - ☐ **Morison's equation** = Drag + Inertia. Use $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D d u\vert u\vert + \tfrac{\pi}{4}\rho C_M d^2 \dot{u}$. - ☐ **Crane + Yard limits** frequently control sizing more than environmental loads. - ☐ **Life-cycle cost** (CAPEX + OPEX + decommissioning) > initial CAPEX for total economy. - ☐ **Codes to memorize:** API RP 2A (fixed), API RP 2T (TLP), API RP 2FPS (FPSO), DNV-OS-C101, ISO 19902. - ☐ **Decomm­issioning** is now legally required under IMO and national regulators — always budget for it at the *sizing* stage. - ☐ For KTU exam: **always state units**, **show intermediate steps**, and **circle the final answer** in a box. > **End of Module 3 Notes — Sizing Considerations**

topics.title: Design Strategy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST722 - FUNDAMENTALS OF OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design Strategy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the context of offshore engineering, **Design Strategy** refers to the codified, systematic, and engineering-judgement-driven framework that governs how an offshore structure (typically a fixed steel jacket platform) is proportioned, analysed, fabricated, installed, and maintained over its service life to safely withstand all expected and extreme environmental, operational, and accidental loads, while satisfying the regulatory requirements of the certifying authority (DNV, API, ISO 19900-series, ABS, Lloyd's Register) and the project-specific design basis.

A design strategy is therefore not a single equation — it is the **decision-making hierarchy** that selects:

1. The applicable **design code** (e.g., API RP 2A-WSD / API RP 2A-LRFD, DNV-OS-C101, ISO 19902, NORSOK N-001).
2. The **limit state design philosophy** (Working Stress Design — WSD, or Load and Resistance Factor Design — LRFD).
3. The **design environmental conditions** (operational, 100-year return, 1000-year return).
4. The **structural configuration** (jacket geometry, pile foundation, deck framing, appurtenances).
5. The **redundancy / damage tolerance** level.
6. The **inspection, monitoring, and repair (IMR)** strategy linked to design life.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module-3 Takeaway:** Whenever a question uses the phrase "design strategy," the examiner expects you to discuss **(a) the design code adopted, (b) the limit state philosophy, (c) the environmental design criteria, (d) the partial safety factors / target reliability, and (e) the in-service inspection philosophy.** A purely structural analysis answer will *not* earn full marks.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are building a house in a coastal village in Kerala during monsoon season. Before laying a single brick, you decide:

- **Which standard to follow** (NBC, BIS, local panchayat rule).
- **How strong the foundation must be** (load combinations, safety factors).
- **What storm the house must survive** (100-year cyclone? 1000-year cyclone?).
- **What to do if a column is damaged in a future flood** (redundancy — does another column take the load?).
- **When to recheck the structure** (yearly? after every major cyclone?).

That *master plan* — not the brick-laying itself — is the **Design Strategy**. In offshore engineering, the "monsoon" is replaced by 100-year hurricanes, loop currents, 1000-year rogue waves, ship collisions, and dropped objects, and the "house" is a multi-thousand-tonne steel platform standing in 100–300 m of water.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world metric of safety:** A modern fixed offshore jacket is designed for a target annual **probability of failure $P_f \approx 10^{-5}$** for the Ultimate Limit State (ULS), corresponding to a **reliability index $\beta \approx 4.7$**. This is roughly **100× more stringent** than a normal onshore building.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Service life of a fixed offshore platform: 20–30 years** (sometimes extended to 50 years).
- **Design return period for ULS environmental loading: 100 years.**
- **Design return period for ALS (Accidental Limit State): 1000 years** (e.g., API RP 2A uses 100-year for operating, 1000-year for survival / extreme).
- **Target reliability index $\beta$ for ULS: ~3.7 (older) to 4.7 (modern).**
- **Partial safety factors on loads ($\gamma_f$): typically 1.0–1.5.**
- **Partial safety factors on resistance ($\gamma_m$): typically 1.05–1.25 for steel.**
- **Sea-water density $\rho_w \approx 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$.**
- **Marine structural steel grade: typically API 2H Gr. 50 (yield $\geq 345$ MPa).**

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Pillars of an Offshore Design Strategy

A KTU-quality answer on design strategy must address the following six pillars, in this order:

#### Pillar 1 — Adoption of a Recognized Code of Practice

The very first decision in any offshore design strategy is the **code selection**. The four dominant codes are:

- **API RP 2A (American Petroleum Institute – Recommended Practice 2A)**: De facto world standard. Published in two flavours — WSD (Working Stress Design, 22nd Ed., 2014) and LRFD (1st Ed., 1993; aligned with AISC).
- **DNV-OS-C101 / DNV-RP-C101 (Det Norske Veritas)**: European / Norwegian standard, fully probabilistic-based limit state format.
- **ISO 19900-series (International Organization for Standardization)**: Global harmonization, especially for regions with no national code (India, parts of SE Asia).
- **NORSOK N-001 (Norwegian Standards)**: Highly prescriptive, well-illustrated, used on the Norwegian Continental Shelf.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Point:** For Indian offshore projects (Mumbai High, KG Basin, off-Gujarat), the certifying authority is normally **DNV** or **ABS**, working under **API RP 2A / ISO 19902** with Indian-flag modifications under the **Oil Industry Safety Directorate (OISD)**.

#### Pillar 2 — The Limit State Design Philosophy

A *limit state* is a condition beyond which the structure (or a member) no longer satisfies the design requirements. Offshore codes recognize **four families** of limit states:

1. **SLS – Serviceability Limit State**: The structure remains functional — deflections, vibrations, fatigue crack growth are all within tolerable bounds. (e.g., topside deflection < L/200.)
2. **ULS – Ultimate Limit State**: The structure has reached its maximum load-carrying capacity, but **collapse is prevented**. (e.g., yielding, buckling, foundation failure, ultimate pile capacity.)
3. **FLS – Fatigue Limit State**: Cumulative damage from cyclic wave loading does not exceed the design fatigue life (typically a fraction of service life, S-N curve integration with Miner's rule).
4. **ALS – Accidental Limit State**: The structure sustains a single credible accidental event (fire, collision, dropped object, blast, earthquake) without progressive collapse — the **redundancy** requirement kicks in.

> [!NOTE]
> **"Why"** do we need four limit states? Because a structure can be *strong enough* to not collapse, yet *too flexible* for its drilling equipment to operate (SLS failure), or *designed for 100-year waves* but *fatigue-failed in 8 years* (FLS failure). One limit state is not enough.

#### Pillar 3 — Design Environmental Criteria

The structure is sized for **three tiers** of metocean loading:

- **Operating conditions** (e.g., 1-year storm): All operations continue, drilling crew on board, drilling in progress. Lower partial factors.
- **Extreme / Survival conditions** (e.g., 100-year return period storm): Platform may be **evacuated**, drilling suspended, but risers, wellheads, and the jacket must survive.
- **Abnormal / Accidental conditions** (e.g., 1000-year return period, or collision): Structure must not collapse; some damage is acceptable.

#### Pillar 4 — Partial Safety Factor Format (LRFD)

In LRFD, the **design action effect $S_d$** must be less than the **design resistance $R_d$**:

$$S_d \leq R_d$$

Where:

$$S_d = \sum_i \gamma_{f,i} \, S_{k,i} \quad \text{and} \quad R_d = \dfrac{R_k}{\gamma_m}$$

- $S_{k,i}$ = characteristic (nominal) load effect.
- $R_k$ = characteristic resistance.
- $\gamma_{f,i}$ = partial factor on load $i$ (typically 1.0 to 1.5).
- $\gamma_m$ = partial factor on material resistance (typically 1.05 to 1.25).

In WSD (allowable stress design), this collapses to:

$$\sigma_{actual} \leq \dfrac{\sigma_{yield}}{FS}$$

with a single global **Factor of Safety (FS)** typically **1.5 to 2.0** for environmental + dead load combinations.

#### Pillar 5 — Reliability / Probabilistic Basis

Modern design strategy is anchored on **structural reliability theory**. The limit state function is:

$$g(R, S) = R - S$$

The structure is **safe** when $g > 0$, **fails** when $g \leq 0$. The probability of failure is:

$$P_f = P(g \leq 0) = \int \int_{g \le 0} f_R(r) \, f_S(s) \, dr \, ds$$

For normal-distributed $R$ and $S$, this gives the famous **Cornell's reliability index**:

$$\beta = \dfrac{\mu_R - \mu_S}{\sqrt{\sigma_R^2 + \sigma_S^2}}$$

and the approximate relation:

$$P_f \approx \Phi(-\beta)$$

where $\Phi$ is the standard normal cumulative distribution.

#### Pillar 6 — Inspection, Monitoring & Repair (IMR) Strategy

The design strategy is *incomplete* without an in-service strategy:

- **Risk-based inspection (RBI)**: higher frequency in fatigue-prone joints (e.g., conductor guide frames, splash-zone braces).
- **Underwater inspection (UWILD)**: remotely operated vehicle (ROV) surveys every 1–4 years.
- **Cathodic-protection monitoring**: sacrificial anode depletion.
- **Marine growth cleaning**: removal every 3–5 years to maintain hydrodynamic cleanliness assumptions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU examiner tip:** If asked "Discuss the design strategy for an offshore jacket platform," the model answer should contain a sentence linking the **design code → limit state → partial factor → reliability index → IMR plan**. A "flow" of ideas earns full marks; a disconnected list does not.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Term** | **Meaning** | **Typical Value / Unit** |
|---|---|---|
| $\gamma_f$ | Partial safety factor on load | 1.0 – 1.5 (1.35 for env., 1.1 for D.L.) |
| $\gamma_m$ | Partial safety factor on material resistance | 1.05 – 1.25 for steel |
| $FS$ | Factor of safety (WSD) | 1.5 – 2.0 |
| $\beta$ | Reliability (Cornell) index | 2.3 (SLS) to 4.7 (ULS) |
| $P_f$ | Annual probability of failure | $10^{-3}$ (SLS) to $10^{-5}$ (ULS) |
| $R$ | Resistance (capacity) | N or MPa |
| $S$ | Load effect (demand) | N or MPa |
| $S_d$ | Design load effect | $\gamma_f \cdot S_k$ |
| $R_d$ | Design resistance | $R_k / \gamma_m$ |
| $T_R$ | Return period | 100 yr (op.) ; 1000 yr (extreme) |
| $V$ | Coefficient of variation | 0.1 – 0.3 for steel, 0.3 – 0.5 for wave |
| $\rho_w$ | Sea-water mass density | 1025 $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| $g$ | Gravitational acceleration | 9.81 $\text{m/s}^2$ |
| $D_n$ | Design fatigue life (years) | 20 – 30 years (often $\tfrac{1}{2}$ of $T_{\text{life}}$) |
| $\sum \frac{n_i}{N_i}$ | Miner's cumulative damage index | Must be $\leq 1.0$ |
| $f_y$ | Steel yield strength | 345 MPa (API 2H Gr.50) |

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of the Design Strategy Concept

A robust design strategy has direct industrial benefits:

- **Risk reduction** for life, environment, and asset.
- **Insurance / certification compliance** (no certificate → no production licence).
- **Optimized material tonnage** — a jacket designed with proper LRFD partial factors can save 8–15 % steel compared to a naive WSD design.
- **Predictable in-service performance** via RBI/IMR plans.
- **Decommissioning readiness** built into the design from day one.
