topics.title: Material Considerations for Offshore Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST722 - FUNDAMENTALS OF OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Material Considerations for Offshore Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Does "Material Consideration" Mean in Offshore Engineering?

In the context of offshore structural engineering, **Material Considerations** refer to the systematic engineering process of selecting, specifying, qualifying, and protecting the construction materials used to fabricate structures that must survive the aggressive **marine environment** (salt water, splash zone, subsea, high humidity, wave impact, low temperatures, biological growth) for a design life typically of **20 to 30 years** (and up to 50 years for fixed platforms).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Material considerations* involve the selection of steels with adequate **strength, toughness, weldability, and corrosion resistance**, along with the design of *corrosion protection systems* (coatings, cathodic protection) and the management of **fatigue damage** due to wave and wind loading.

The offshore environment is fundamentally different from onshore because:
- The structure is **continuously wetted** (submerged zone).
- The **splash zone** undergoes wet-dry cycling — the most corrosive zone.
- The **atmospheric zone** faces salt spray, UV, and wind.
- Cyclic wave loading causes **fatigue**, often dominating the structural design.

---

## 1.2 The Three Primary Zones of an Offshore Structure

A jacket or monopile offshore structure is exposed to **four distinct corrosion zones**. Understanding these is essential before material selection.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Four Marine Exposure Zones (ISO 12944 / NORSOK M-501):**
>
> | Zone | Description | Corrosion Severity |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Atmospheric Zone** | Above wave action, exposed to air & salt spray | Moderate |
> | **Splash Zone** | Wetted by waves, sun, tide; wet-dry cycling | **Severe (highest)** |
> | **Submerged Zone** | Permanently below low water level | Moderate-High |
> | **Subsoil / Embedded Zone** | Below seabed (for piled structures) | Variable |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Skin of a Battleship"

Imagine a soldier standing waist-deep in seawater, with waves crashing against his chest every few seconds. His **boots (submerged zone)** get corroded by salt water. His **chest (splash zone)** takes the most punishment — wet, then dry, then wet, with sandblasting from waves. His **head and shoulders (atmospheric zone)** face salt-laden wind and sun.

A **coating system** is his raincoat. A **sacrificial anode (cathodic protection)** is a friend who volunteers to corrode so the soldier doesn't. **Material selection** is choosing a strong, tough alloy for the soldier's armor. Just like a military uniform, every layer (zone) needs a different protective strategy — *one solution does not fit all zones.*

---

## 1.4 Key Performance Requirements for Offshore Structural Materials

A material used in an offshore structure must simultaneously satisfy several demanding criteria:

1. **High Yield Strength** ($\sigma_y$ typically **355 MPa to 450 MPa**) — to reduce self-weight and wave forces.
2. **Excellent Toughness** (Charpy V-notch impact energy $\geq 27\text{ J}$ at $-40\,^\circ\text{C}$ or lower).
3. **Good Weldability** (low Carbon Equivalent Value, CEV $\leq 0.45$).
4. **Corrosion Resistance** (intrinsic + protection system).
5. **Fatigue Resistance** (S-N curve / fracture mechanics).
6. **Availability in Heavy Sections** (plates up to 100+ mm thick for tubular joints).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is toughness critical?** In the North Sea, water temperatures can drop to **$4\,^\circ\text{C}$**, and steel becomes brittle. A brittle fracture in a tubular joint can be catastrophic. Hence **fracture-mechanics-based material selection** is mandatory under standards like **NORSOK M-101** and **EN 1993-1-10**.

---

## 1.5 Standardization in Offshore Materials

Offshore materials are governed by an integrated set of codes:

- **API (American Petroleum Institute)** — API 2W, 2Y, 2Z (now largely superseded).
- **EN 10025** series — European structural steels.
- **NORSOK M-001 / M-101 / M-501 / M-601** — Norwegian standards widely used worldwide.
- **ISO 19902** — International standard for fixed offshore steel structures.
- **DNV-RP-C203** — Fatigue design.
- **DNV-RP-F101** — Corroded pipelines.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Material Classification for Offshore Use

Offshore structural steels are broadly classified by their **yield strength** and **toughness grade**. The most common designation is:

$$ \text{Steel Grade: } S \text{(or E)} \, 355 \, \text{G} / \text{N} / \text{QL} / \text{QL1} $$

Where:
- **S / E** = Structural / Engineering steel.
- **355** = Minimum yield strength in **MPa** ($\sigma_y = 355\,\text{MPa}$ for thickness $\leq 16\,\text{mm}$).
- **G / N / M** = Deoxidation practice (killed, normalized, thermomechanical).
- **QL / QL1** = Toughness at **$-40\,^\circ\text{C}$** with **30 J / 40 J** Charpy energy.

> [!NOTE]
> The most common offshore steel grade is **S355G10+N** or **S355ML** for primary tubular members, with **API 5L X65 / X70** for pipelines and **API 5CT** for risers.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Material Property

### 2.2.1 Strength ($\sigma_y$, $\sigma_u$)

- **Why needed:** Hydrostatic pressure + wave + wind + self-weight demand a high load-bearing capacity per unit weight.
- **Trade-off:** Higher strength → lower tonnage → less welding → but **reduced toughness and weldability** if achieved purely through carbon.

### 2.2.2 Toughness ($K_{IC}$, Charpy $KV$)

- **Why needed:** Resist brittle fracture in cold seas and at stress concentrators (tubular joints).
- **Metric:** Charpy V-notch energy, e.g., **27 J at $-40\,^\circ\text{C}$** for primary members.

### 2.2.3 Weldability (CEV)

- **Why needed:** Offshore structures are 100% welded. Hard, brittle heat-affected zones (HAZ) must be avoided.
- **Metric:** **Carbon Equivalent Value (CEV)** from **IIW formula**:

$$ \text{CEV} = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \frac{Ni + Cu}{15} $$

- **Rule of thumb:** CEV $\leq 0.45$ for thick sections, $\leq 0.40$ preferred for critical welds.

### 2.2.4 Fatigue Resistance

- **Why needed:** Offshore structures are subjected to **$10^7$ to $10^9$ cycles** of wave loading in their lifetime.
- **Metric:** S-N curve (Basquin's law):

$$ N \cdot S^m = A \quad \Rightarrow \quad \log N = \log A - m \log S $$

where $S$ = stress range, $N$ = cycles to failure, $m \approx 3$ for structural steel in air, $m \approx 3$ to $5$ in seawater with cathodic protection.

---

## 2.3 Corrosion: The Silent Killer

Corrosion in offshore structures is **electrochemical** — a galvanic cell forms on the steel surface in the presence of seawater (electrolyte) and oxygen.

The **general (uniform) corrosion rate** in seawater is typically:

- **Atmospheric zone:** $0.1 - 0.3\,\text{mm/year}$
- **Splash zone:** $0.3 - 0.5\,\text{mm/year}$ (peak corrosion)
- **Submerged zone:** $0.1 - 0.2\,\text{mm/year}$ (with marine growth)
- **Subsoil:** $0.05 - 0.1\,\text{mm/year}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For design, a **corrosion allowance** of **1 mm to 6 mm** is added to the wall thickness of tubular members depending on zone and design life, per **ISO 19902** and **NORSOK N-001**.

---

## 2.4 Corrosion Protection Systems (CPS)

A complete corrosion protection system has **three independent lines of defense**:

### Line 1: Protective Coatings

| Zone | Typical Coating | DFT (Dry Film Thickness) |
|---|---|---|
| Atmospheric | Epoxy / Polyurethane | $250 - 500\,\mu m$ |
| Splash | Glass-flake epoxy, elastomeric polyurethane, Monel sheathing | $1000 - 3000\,\mu m$ |
| Submerged | Antifouling epoxy + cathodic protection | $250 - 500\,\mu m$ |

### Line 2: Cathodic Protection (CP)

Two methods:
- **Sacrificial Anode (Galvanic) CP** — uses Al or Zn anodes.
- **Impressed Current CP (ICCP)** — uses inert anodes (Ti/MMO) and external DC power.

The protection criterion: shift the steel potential to **$-800\,\text{mV}$ to $-1100\,\text{mV}$ vs Ag/AgCl** reference.

The required protective current density:
- **Tropical seawater:** $i \approx 100 - 150\,\text{mA/m}^2$ (initial), dropping to $30 - 50\,\text{mA/m}^2$ at steady state.
- **Cold North Sea:** $i \approx 150 - 200\,\text{mA/m}^2$ initial.

The **total anode mass** required (sacrificial Al):

$$ M_{anode} = \frac{I_{total} \cdot T_{design} \cdot 8760}{C_a \cdot u_a} $$

where:
- $I_{total}$ = total protective current (A)
- $T_{design}$ = design life (years)
- $C_a$ = anode capacity (A·h/kg) — Al $\approx 2500$, Zn $\approx 780$
- $u_a$ = utilization factor ($\approx 0.85$)

### Line 3: Corrosion Allowance

Extra steel thickness added at the design stage to absorb corrosion loss over the design life:

$$ t_{design} = t_{structural} + t_{corrosion} $$

where $t_{corrosion} = (\text{corrosion rate}) \times (\text{design life})$, typically **$3\text{ mm}$ submerged, $6\text{ mm}$ splash, $1\text{ mm}$ atmospheric**.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Quick Revision)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this table — it covers 90% of numerical questions.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Carbon Equivalent (IIW) | $\text{CEV} = C + \dfrac{Mn}{6} + \dfrac{Cr+Mo+V}{5} + \dfrac{Ni+Cu}{15}$ | Weldability check |
| 2 | S-N Curve (Basquin) | $N \cdot S^m = A$ | Fatigue life prediction |
| 3 | Goodman / Gerber mean stress correction | $\dfrac{S_a}{S_e} + \dfrac{S_m}{S_{ut}} = 1$ | Fatigue with mean stress |
| 4 | Corrosion rate → thickness | $t_c = r_c \cdot T$ | Corrosion allowance |
| 5 | Anode mass | $M_a = \dfrac{I \cdot T \cdot 8760}{C_a \cdot u_a}$ | Cathodic protection design |
| 6 | Total protective current | $I = i \cdot A_{wet}$ | CP system sizing |
| 7 | Crack growth (Paris Law) | $\dfrac{da}{dN} = C (\Delta K)^m$ | Fracture-mechanics fatigue |
| 8 | Brittle fracture avoidance (API 2Z) | $T_{27J} \leq T_{design} - 3\,^\circ\text{C}$ | Toughness criterion |
| 9 | Stress intensity factor | $K = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a}$ | Fracture analysis |
| 10 | Min yield strength (NORSOK) | $\sigma_y \geq 355\,\text{MPa}$ for primary | Material selection |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles in this module directly drive:
- **Topside module fabrication** (e.g., S355J2+N for modules, S690QL for cranes).
- **Jacket tubular members** (S355G10+N, S420G2+M).
- **Risers and J-tubes** (API 5L X65 with 3LPE coating + CP).
- **Mooring chains** (R4, R4S, R5 grades per API 2SF).
- **Subsea manifolds and trees** (Inconel 625 cladding, super-duplex stainless steel).
- **FPSO hulls** (EH36, DH36 shipbuilding steel).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Corrosion Allowance for a Tubular Member

**Problem Statement:** A tubular jacket leg in the splash zone has a required structural wall thickness of **$28\,\text{mm}$**. The design life is **30 years**, and the corrosion rate in the splash zone is **$0.4\,\text{mm/year}$**. Determine the *as-built* thickness to be ordered from the steel mill.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify zones and corrosion rates.**

The splash zone corrosion rate is given as:

$$ r_c = 0.4\,\text{mm/year} $$

**Step 2 — Compute the total corrosion loss over design life.**

$$ t_{corrosion} = r_c \times T_{design} $$

$$ t_{corrosion} = 0.4 \times 30 = 12\,\text{mm} $$

**Step 3 — Apply the design thickness equation.**

$$ t_{design} = t_{structural} + t_{corrosion} $$

$$ t_{design} = 28 + 12 = 40\,\text{mm} $$

**Step 4 — Round up to nearest available plate.**

Standard plate thicknesses: …, 36, 40, 45, 50 mm. Hence, **order $t = 40\,\text{mm}$ plate**.

**Result:** The fabricator must procure a **40 mm thick plate** so that after 30 years of splash-zone corrosion, the residual wall is still ≥ 28 mm.

> [!NOTE]
> A KTU exam variant may ask for *uniform* corrosion in the submerged zone at $r_c = 0.15\,\text{mm/year}$ — the same procedure applies. The numerical answer is $28 + 0.15 \times 30 = 32.5 \approx 35\,\text{mm}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cathodic Protection Anode Mass Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A jacket platform has a wetted (submerged) surface area of **$8500\,\text{m}^2$**. The required protective current density is **$120\,\text{mA/m}^2$** initially, and the design life is **25 years**. Using **aluminum anodes** (capacity $C_a = 2700\,\text{A·h/kg}$, utilization $u_a = 0.85$), compute the total anode mass.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute total protective current.**

$$ I = i \times A_{wet} $$

$$ I = 120 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{A/m}^2 \times 8500\,\text{m}^2 $$

$$ I = 1020\,\text{A} $$

**Step 2 — Convert design life to hours.**

$$ T_{hours} = 25 \times 8760 = 219{,}000\,\text{hours} $$

(Note: $1\,\text{year} = 365.25 \times 24 = 8766\,\text{h}$, but 8760 is the conventional engineering value.)

**Step 3 — Compute total charge that must be delivered by the anodes.**

$$ Q_{total} = I \times T_{hours} $$

$$ Q_{total} = 1020 \times 219{,}000 = 223{,}380{,}000\,\text{A·h} = 2.234 \times 10^8\,\text{A·h} $$

**Step 4 — Account for anode utilization.**

Only 85% of the anode mass is consumed before it must be replaced. Effective charge per kg of anode installed:

$$ C_{effective} = C_a \times u_a = 2700 \times 0.85 = 2295\,\text{A·h/kg} $$

**Step 5 — Compute required anode mass.**

$$ M_{anode} = \frac{Q_{total}}{C_{effective}} $$

$$ M_{anode} = \frac{2.234 \times 10^8}{2295} $$

$$ M_{anode} \approx 97{,}320\,\text{kg} \approx 97.3\,\text{tonnes} $$

**Result:** Approximately **97 tonnes of aluminum anodes** must be distributed on the jacket structure (typically as bracelet anodes welded to legs and cross-braces).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Variant:** If **zinc anodes** are used ($C_a = 780\,\text{A·h/kg}$, $u_a = 0.90$), the same procedure yields:
> $$ M = \frac{2.234 \times 10^8}{780 \times 0.90} = \frac{2.234 \times 10^8}{702} \approx 318{,}200\,\text{kg} $$
> This illustrates why **aluminum is preferred** (about **3.3 times lighter** in this case).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Fatigue Life Using S-N Curve

**Problem Statement:** A tubular joint at a splash-zone brace-chord intersection experiences a stress range of **$\Delta\sigma = 90\,\text{MPa}$** due to wave loading. The S-N curve in air follows **$N \cdot S^3 = 2.0 \times 10^{12}$** (with $S$ in MPa, $N$ in cycles). If the structure experiences **$1.5 \times 10^6$ cycles/year** of this stress range, compute the fatigue life in years. (Take a safety factor on life of 3, and use a seawater-with-CP correction factor of **0.5** on stress.)

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the seawater correction.**

The fatigue strength in seawater with cathodic protection is reduced. A common DNV-RP-C203 approach uses a *knockdown factor* on stress or a steeper S-N slope. Here we are told to apply a **stress correction factor of 0.5** (i.e., $S$ is effectively halved to simulate the harsher environment), or more precisely we treat the "design" stress range as:

$$ S_{design} = \Delta\sigma = 90\,\text{MPa} $$

and use the **in-seawater-with-CP curve** with a steeper exponent (m = 5):

$$ N \cdot S^5 = K' $$

For the same material, when $m$ changes from 3 to 5, the constant must be re-derived. Assume the air curve at $S = 100\,\text{MPa}$ gives $N = 2.0 \times 10^{12} / 100^3 = 2 \times 10^6$ cycles, while the in-seawater curve at $S = 100\,\text{MPa}$ gives 1/10 of that, i.e., $N = 2 \times 10^5$ cycles. Then:

$$ K' = 2 \times 10^5 \times 100^5 = 2 \times 10^{15} $$

**Step 2 — Solve for N at $\Delta\sigma = 90\,\text{MPa}$.**

$$ N = \frac{K'}{S^5} = \frac{2 \times 10^{15}}{90^5} $$

$$ 90^5 = 5.9049 \times 10^9 $$

$$ N = \frac{2 \times 10^{15}}{5.9049 \times 10^9} \approx 3.387 \times 10^5\,\text{cycles} $$

**Step 3 — Apply safety factor on life (SF = 3).**

$$ N_{allow} = \frac{N}{SF} = \frac{3.387 \times 10^5}{3} \approx 1.129 \times 10^5\,\text{cycles} $$

**Step 4 — Convert to years.**

$$ T = \frac{N_{allow}}{\text{cycles/year}} = \frac{1.129 \times 10^5}{1.5 \times 10^6} \approx 0.0753\,\text{years} $$

This is far too short, indicating the joint **will fail by fatigue in about 27 days** if the stress range is sustained — clearly the joint is undersized.

**Step 5 — Reinterpret using a stress knockdown factor of 0.5.**

If the in-air $S$ is reduced by 0.5 (i.e., design S = 45 MPa) to reflect seawater:

$$ N = \frac{2 \times 10^{12}}{45^3} = \frac{2 \times 10^{12}}{91{,}125} \approx 2.19 \times 10^7\,\text{cycles} $$

With SF = 3: $N_{allow} = 7.31 \times 10^6$ cycles.

$$ T = \frac{7.31 \times 10^6}{1.5 \times 10^6} \approx 4.87\,\text{years} $$

**Result:** The fatigue life is approximately **4.87 years** — which is well below the 25-year design life, confirming that the joint must be redesigned or stress-relieved (e.g., via grinding the weld toe to improve the S-N class from F to F2).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two different interpretations above show how **environmental correction factors** can flip a design from "safe" to "fail". KTU examiners love to test whether you understand the *direction* of the correction.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Material vs. Application

| Application | Material Selected | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Jacket leg (submerged) | S355G10+N, ASTM A572 Gr.50 | High strength + good weldability + CP-compatible |
| Conductor / riser | API 5L X65 / X70 with 3LPE coating | High pressure + corrosion resistance |
| Topside deck plating | EH36 / DH36 (shipbuilding) | Good strength + weldability for module integration |
| Crane pedestal | S690QL (high-strength, quenched & tempered) | Yield > 690 MPa → weight reduction |
| Mooring chain | API 2SF R4 / R4S / R5 | Grade R5: 1000 MPa UTS, ductile for cyclic loading |
| Subsea manifold | Super-duplex SS (UNS S32750) | Chloride SCC resistance |
| Fire-water piping | API 5L Gr.B with FBE coating | Cost-effective + 30-year life |
| Splash-zone leg | S355G10+N + 6 mm Monel 400 sheathing | Resists wet-dry corrosion + mechanical impact |
| Ballast tank (FPSO) | ASTM A131 Grade A + epoxy + sacrificial Zn | Internal corrosion protection |
| Hot oil piping (topside) | ASTM A335 P11/P22 (Cr-Mo steel) | High-temperature creep resistance |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Carbon Equivalent Value (CEV) Check

**Problem Statement:** A steel plate has composition: **C = 0.16%, Mn = 1.40%, Cr = 0.05%, Mo = 0.02%, V = 0.01%, Ni = 0.03%, Cu = 0.04%**. Check if it is weldable using the IIW formula, with the standard offshore limit of CEV $\leq 0.45$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Substitute into the IIW formula.**

$$ \text{CEV} = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \frac{Ni + Cu}{15} $$

**Step 2 — Evaluate each term separately.**

$$ \frac{Mn}{6} = \frac{1.40}{6} = 0.2333 $$

$$ \frac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} = \frac{0.05 + 0.02 + 0.01}{5} = \frac{0.08}{5} = 0.0160 $$

$$ \frac{Ni + Cu}{15} = \frac{0.03 + 0.04}{15} = \frac{0.07}{15} = 0.00467 $$

**Step 3 — Sum all terms.**

$$ \text{CEV} = 0.16 + 0.2333 + 0.0160 + 0.00467 $$

$$ \text{CEV} = 0.4140 \approx 0.41 $$

**Step 4 — Compare with limit.**

$$ \text{CEV} = 0.41 < 0.45 \;\; \checkmark $$

**Result:** The steel **is weldable** using standard offshore welding procedures. No pre-heating beyond $100\,^\circ\text{C}$ is required for thin sections, but for plates thicker than 40 mm, a preheat of $100 - 150\,^\circ\text{C}$ is recommended per AWS D1.1.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If CEV > 0.45, the answer is: *"The steel is not readily weldable. Pre-heating to $150 - 200\,^\circ\text{C}$ and post-weld heat treatment (PWHT) are required, OR select a lower-carbon grade (e.g., S355ML with CEV $\leq 0.39$)"*.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 regulations, **Part A** has 5 questions of 3 marks each, and at least one is typically drawn from each module. The following are the most likely questions from this sub-topic.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List the four marine exposure zones of an offshore structure and identify the most severe corrosion zone.**

**Model Answer:**

The four zones, from top to bottom on a jacket platform, are:

1. **Atmospheric zone** — above wave crest, exposed to salt-laden air.
2. **Splash zone** — wetted by waves and tides, with wet-dry cycling.
3. **Submerged zone** — permanently below the lowest astronomical tide.
4. **Subsoil (embedded) zone** — below the seabed in soil or rock.

The **splash zone is the most severe** because the continuous wet-dry cycling accelerates the electrochemical corrosion process and prevents the formation of any stable protective film. The corrosion rate here can be **3 to 5 times higher** than in the submerged zone.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Define Carbon Equivalent Value (CEV) and state its significance for offshore fabrication.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Carbon Equivalent Value (CEV)** is a numerical index that quantifies the combined effect of alloying elements on the weldability of steel, expressed by the **IIW formula**:

$$ \text{CEV} = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \frac{Ni + Cu}{15} $$

**Significance for offshore fabrication:**
- Offshore structures are **100% welded**. High CEV leads to hard, crack-prone heat-affected zones (HAZ).
- NORSOK M-101 and EN 1993-1-10 require **CEV $\leq 0.45$** for primary load-bearing members.
- If CEV is high, **pre-heating** ($100 - 200\,^\circ\text{C}$) and **post-weld heat treatment (PWHT)** become mandatory, increasing fabrication cost and time.
- A lower CEV allows the use of **higher heat-input welding**, which is faster and more economical.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Part B questions carry **14 marks** and the student is given an **internal choice** between two questions. The two alternatives provided below are mutually independent — only one must be attempted.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Cathodic Protection & Corrosion Allowance

**[a] (7 Marks)** With the aid of a sketch, describe the **three lines of defense** used to protect an offshore jacket structure against corrosion. Highlight the role of cathodic protection, including the protection criterion and the difference between sacrificial and impressed-current systems.

**[b] (7 Marks)** A jacket platform has a wetted surface area of **$6000\,\text{m}^2$**. The required protective current density is **$130\,\text{mA/m}^2$**. The design life is **20 years**. Using **aluminum anodes** (capacity = **$2700\,\text{A·h/kg}$**, utilization = **$0.85$**), compute the total mass of anodes required. Also state the **protection potential** for steel in seawater.

---

### Model Solution to Q-A(a)

**Three lines of defense (7 marks):**

The protection of an offshore steel structure from marine corrosion is achieved through three independent, redundant lines of defense:

1. **Coatings (Paints):** Provide a physical and chemical barrier between the steel and the electrolyte. Common systems are:
   - **Atmospheric zone:** Epoxy primer + polyurethane topcoat, total DFT **$250 - 500\,\mu m$**.
   - **Splash zone:** Glass-flake epoxy or elastomeric polyurethane, DFT up to **$3000\,\mu m$**, often combined with Monel 400 sheathing for mechanical protection.
   - **Submerged zone:** Anti-corrosive epoxy, complemented by antifouling paint to prevent marine growth.

2. **Cathodic Protection (CP):** An electrochemical technique that suppresses the anodic (corroding) reaction by shifting the steel's electrochemical potential to a more negative value. The **protection criterion** is that the steel potential must lie in the range:
   $$ E_{steel} = -800\,\text{mV to } -1100\,\text{mV vs. Ag/AgCl\ reference\ electrode} $$
   - **Sacrificial Anode (Galvanic) CP:** Aluminum or zinc anodes are bolted or welded to the structure. The anodes corrode preferentially (more negative potential), protecting the steel. Simple, no power source, but large mass needed for long life.
   - **Impressed Current CP (ICCP):** An external DC power source drives current from inert anodes (Ti/MMO) to the steel. Lighter and adjustable, but requires power and monitoring. Common on FPSOs and pipelines.

3. **Corrosion Allowance:** An extra wall thickness (typically **$1 - 6\,\text{mm}$**) added to the structural thickness at the design stage. This acts as a *time buffer* — even if the first two lines partially fail, the structural integrity is maintained for the design life.

The redundancy ensures that if the coating is mechanically damaged (e.g., by a boat impact), CP takes over; and if CP is degraded, the corrosion allowance buys time for inspection and repair.

---

### Model Solution to Q-A(b) — Numerical

**Step 1 — Total current required:**

$$ I = i \times A = 0.130\,\text{A/m}^2 \times 6000\,\text{m}^2 = 780\,\text{A} $$

**Step 2 — Total charge over 20 years:**

$$ T_h = 20 \times 8760 = 175{,}200\,\text{hours} $$

$$ Q_{total} = I \times T_h = 780 \times 175{,}200 = 1.3666 \times 10^8\,\text{A·h} $$

**Step 3 — Effective anode capacity:**

$$ C_{eff} = 2700 \times 0.85 = 2295\,\text{A·h/kg} $$

**Step 4 — Anode mass:**

$$ M = \frac{Q_{total}}{C_{eff}} = \frac{1.3666 \times 10^8}{2295} \approx 59{,}545\,\text{kg} \approx 59.5\,\text{tonnes} $$

**Step 5 — Protection potential:**

The protection potential for steel in seawater is **$-800\,\text{mV to } -1100\,\text{mV}$ vs. Ag/AgCl reference** (or equivalently $-770$ to $-1050$ mV vs. saturated calomel electrode).

**Final Answer:** Total anode mass $\approx 59.5$ tonnes; protection potential $-800$ to $-1100$ mV vs Ag/AgCl.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Fatigue, Weldability & Material Selection

**[a] (7 Marks)** Explain the **S-N curve approach** to fatigue analysis of offshore tubular joints. With a neat sketch, describe how the design S-N curve is derived from experimental data, and state the role of cathodic protection in modifying the curve.

**[b] (7 Marks)** A steel plate has the following composition: **C = 0.14%, Mn = 1.30%, Cr = 0.04%, Mo = 0.03%, V = 0.01%, Ni = 0.05%, Cu = 0.03%**. Compute its Carbon Equivalent Value (CEV) using the IIW formula and comment on its weldability for offshore application.

---

### Model Solution to Q-B(a)

**S-N curve approach (7 marks):**

The **S-N (Stress–Number of cycles) curve**, also called the **Wöhler curve**, is the fundamental tool for fatigue assessment of offshore structures. It plots the **stress range** $S$ (or $\Delta\sigma$) on the y-axis (log scale) against the **number of cycles to failure** $N$ (log scale) on the x-axis, on a log-log basis.

**Derivation from experiments:**

1. Multiple identical small-scale specimens (e.g., tubular joint models) are tested under constant-amplitude cyclic loading.
2. Each specimen is loaded at a different stress range; the number of cycles to a defined failure criterion (e.g., through-thickness crack) is recorded.
3. The data points are plotted on a log-log scale, and a **straight line (or two-segment line)** is fitted.
4. The design curve is obtained by:
   - **Shifting the mean curve downward** by $2\sigma$ (two standard deviations of log N) to account for scatter.
   - **Slope m** is typically **3 for in-air** and **3 to 5 for in-seawater-with-CP** conditions.
5. The resulting design S-N curve is used in **Miner's rule** for variable-amplitude loading:

$$ D = \sum \frac{n_i}{N_i} \leq 1.0 $$

where $D$ = cumulative damage, $n_i$ = actual cycles at stress range $i$, and $N_i$ = cycles to failure at that stress range.

**Role of cathodic protection:**

In the submerged zone, **seawater with CP reduces fatigue life** significantly because hydrogen generated at the protected surface diffuses into the steel, causing **hydrogen embrittlement** at the crack tip. To account for this, the S-N curve is:
- Made **steeper** (m = 5 instead of 3) in design codes like **DNV-RP-C203**.
- Or a **knockdown factor on stress** of 0.5 is applied.

In air (atmospheric zone), m = 3 is used. This is why **joints in the splash and submerged zones** are critical for fatigue life.

---

### Model Solution to Q-B(b) — Numerical

**Step 1 — Substitute into IIW formula:**

$$ \text{CEV} = C + \frac{Mn}{6} + \frac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} + \frac{Ni + Cu}{15} $$

**Step 2 — Term-by-term calculation:**

$$ C = 0.14 $$

$$ \frac{Mn}{6} = \frac{1.30}{6} = 0.2167 $$

$$ \frac{Cr + Mo + V}{5} = \frac{0.04 + 0.03 + 0.01}{5} = \frac{0.08}{5} = 0.0160 $$

$$ \frac{Ni + Cu}{15} = \frac{0.05 + 0.03}{15} = \frac{0.08}{15} = 0.00533 $$

**Step 3 — Sum:**

$$ \text{CEV} = 0.14 + 0.2167 + 0.0160 + 0.00533 = 0.378 \approx 0.38 $$

**Step 4 — Weldability comment:**

$$ \text{CEV} = 0.38 < 0.45 \;\; (\text{NORSOK limit}) $$

The steel has **excellent weldability**. It can be welded using standard offshore procedures without pre-heating up to about **$30\,\text{mm}$** thickness, and with mild pre-heating of **$75 - 100\,^\circ\text{C}$** for thicker sections. No post-weld heat treatment (PWHT) is required for non-pressure applications. The grade qualifies as a **standard offshore structural steel**, suitable for jackets, decks, and topside modules.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the units on corrosion rate** — Always state it as "mm/year" or "$\mu m$/year" explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing "submerged" with "splash"** — The splash zone has 2-3× higher corrosion, and exam questions often hinge on this.
> 3. **Wrong protective potential sign** — Cathodic protection is **$-800$ mV to $-1100$ mV vs Ag/AgCl**. Writing $-700$ mV or $-1200$ mV will be marked wrong.
> 4. **Forgetting the utilization factor** in the anode mass equation. Many students write $M = I \cdot T / C_a$ and lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Using m = 3 in seawater-with-CP** — This is a common trap. The slope is **steeper (m = 3 to 5)** in corrosive environments.
> 6. **CEV calculation mistakes** — Watch out for the divisor: $Mn$ is divided by 6, $Cr+Mo+V$ by 5, $Ni+Cu$ by 15. Mixing up these denominators is the #1 error.
> 7. **Confusing the fatigue equation format** — Write it as $N \cdot S^m = A$ or $S^m \cdot N = A$ consistently. Don't use "log-log" without clearly stating the slope $m$.
> 8. **Skipping the diagram** in 7-mark questions — The 3-line corrosion defense diagram is worth at least 2 marks even if your written text is incomplete.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Module 3):**

- ☐ **Four marine zones:** atmospheric, splash, submerged, subsoil. **Splash is the worst.**
- ☐ **Three corrosion defenses:** coatings (barrier) + CP (electrochemical) + corrosion allowance (time).
- ☐ **CP criterion:** $-800$ to $-1100$ mV vs Ag/AgCl; two types — sacrificial (Al/Zn) and ICCP (Ti/MMO).
- ☐ **Anode mass formula:** $M = I \cdot T \cdot 8760 / (C_a \cdot u_a)$.
- ☐ **S-N curve:** $N \cdot S^m = A$, with $m = 3$ in air, $m = 3 - 5$ in seawater with CP.
- ☐ **CEV (IIW):** $C + Mn/6 + (Cr+Mo+V)/5 + (Ni+Cu)/15$, limit $\leq 0.45$ for offshore.
- ☐ **Common offshore grades:** S355G10+N, S420G2+M, S690QL, API 5L X65, API 5CT.
- ☐ **Toughness requirement:** Charpy $\geq 27$ J at $-40\,^\circ\text{C}$ for primary members.
- ☐ **Corrosion rate (typical):** splash 0.3-0.5 mm/yr; submerged 0.1-0.2 mm/yr; atmospheric 0.1-0.3 mm/yr.
- ☐ **Corrosion allowance:** 3-6 mm for splash, 1-3 mm for submerged, 0.5-1 mm for atmospheric.
- ☐ **Codes to remember:** API 2W/2Y/2Z, EN 10025, NORSOK M-101 / M-501, ISO 19902, DNV-RP-C203.
- ☐ **CRA materials:** Inconel 625, Super-duplex SS (UNS S32750), Monel 400 — for sour service and critical subsea.
- ☐ **Fatigue is dominant** in offshore design — ULS rarely governs; FLS (fatigue limit state) usually does.
- ☐ **Cathodic protection reduces fatigue life** (hydrogen embrittlement), use a steeper S-N curve.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Construction of Various Types of Offshore Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST722 - FUNDAMENTALS OF OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Construction of Various Types of Offshore Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Offshore Structure Construction** is the integrated engineering discipline encompassing the design, fabrication, assembly, transportation, and offshore installation of structural systems deployed in marine environments for the exploration, drilling, production, storage, and offloading of hydrocarbons or for harnessing renewable marine energy. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OENST722 – Module 4)**, this topic is defined as the systematic study of how fixed and floating offshore structures are physically realized—from the steel-cutting stage at a fabrication yard to the final hook-up and commissioning at the offshore site.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):**
> *"Construction of various types of offshore structures involves the sequential and integrated operations of material procurement, fabrication in yards (onshore or nearshore), load-out onto transportation barges, marine transportation, offshore lifting/launching, pile driving/anchoring, and final hook-up to produce a fully operational marine facility."*

The major classifications covered in **Module 4** are:

1. **Fixed (Bottom-Supported) Structures** – Steel Jacket Platforms, Concrete Gravity-Based Structures (GBS), Jackets on Piles, Compliant Towers.
2. **Floating Structures** – Semi-Submersibles, Tension Leg Platforms (TLP), SPAR Platforms, FPSOs (Floating Production Storage and Offloading), Drillships.
3. **Subsea Structures** – Manifolds, PLETs (Pipeline End Terminations), Subsea Trees, Templates.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine building a **house**, but instead of constructing it brick-by-brick on the plot of land, you have to:

- Build the entire skeleton in a **factory** far away from the plot.
- Roll it onto a **giant truck** (here, a transportation barge).
- Drive it across a **river** to the actual plot.
- Use a **giant crane** to place it perfectly in the middle of the river.
- Then **bolt it down** to the riverbed so waves cannot push it away.

This is exactly what offshore construction is—but the "river" is the ocean, the "factory" is the fabrication yard, the "truck" is a heavy-lift barge, the "crane" is a derrick/crane vessel, and the "bolts" are driven piles, suction caissons, or mooring lines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Intuition Box:**
> - **Yard Fabrication = Lego Assembly on a Table**
> - **Load-Out = Sliding the Lego Model onto a Conveyor**
> - **Tow-Out = Pushing the Conveyor across a Pool**
> - **Installation = Dropping the Lego into the Pool and Anchoring it**
> - **Hook-Up & Commissioning = Wiring it Up and Turning On the Lights**

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Engineering Constants

The following bolded constants and metrics recur throughout KTU board examinations and offshore engineering practice:

| **Parameter** | **Symbol / Unit** | **Typical Range** |
|---|---|---|
| Water Depth | $d$ (m) | 10 m (shallow) to 3000+ m (deep) |
| Wave Height (Operational) | $H_s$ (m) | 1.5 to 12 m |
| Design Wave Return Period | $T_r$ (years) | **100 years** (permanent structures) |
| Deck (Topside) Weight | $W_{deck}$ (tonnes) | 500 to 50,000 tonnes |
| Jacket Launch Weight | $W_{j}$ (tonnes) | 2,000 to 70,000 tonnes |
| Fabrication Yard Crane Capacity | $C_{crane}$ (tonnes) | 200 to 20,000 tonnes |
| Pile Penetration Depth | $L_p$ (m) | 30 m to 150+ m |
| Marine Operations Weather Window | $t_w$ (hours) | **48 to 72 hours** typical |

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

This topic is a **high-weightage conceptual module** because it directly tests the student's ability to integrate:

- Structural Mechanics (Module 1 & 2 foundations)
- Hydrodynamics (Module 3 wave/current loads)
- Marine Construction Logistics (current Module 4)
- Economic & Safety Constraints (Module 5 outlook)

A student who masters Module 4 can answer **KTU Part A (3 marks)** definitional questions fluently and lay the groundwork for **Part B (14 marks)** construction-sequence problems.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** Examiners love asking "Compare the construction of a Steel Jacket vs. a Concrete GBS" or "Draw a flowchart of jacket launch sequence." Memorize the **phases**, not just definitions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phased Breakdown of Offshore Construction

Offshore construction, regardless of structure type, follows **five universal phases**. Each phase is governed by specific engineering, logistical, and metocean (meteorological + oceanographic) constraints.

### Phase 1 — Engineering, Procurement & Fabrication Yard Preparation

1. **Detailed Engineering Drawings** are generated from the approved design basis.
2. **Material Procurement**: High-grade steel (API 5L X65, X70 for pipelines; **ASTM A572 Gr. 50** or **A36** for jackets) is ordered. Tubular joints, forged nodes, and sacrificial anodes are pre-cast.
3. **Yard Selection Criteria**: Proximity to a sheltered harbor, soil-bearing capacity (must withstand **>50 kPa** for jacket assembly), quay length, and heavy-lift crane availability.

### Phase 2 — Component Fabrication & Sub-Assembly

1. **Cutting & Forming**: Steel plates are CNC-cut, rolled into cans (cylindrical sections).
2. **Welding**: Tubular joints are welded using **SMAW (Shielded Metal Arc Welding)** or **FCAW (Flux-Cored Arc Welding)**. Each weld undergoes **NDT** (Non-Destructive Testing): UT (Ultrasonic), RT (Radiographic), MPI (Magnetic Particle Inspection).
3. **Can-Stack Assembly**: Individual cans are stacked vertically to form jacket legs.
4. **Horizontal Assembly**: Braces, horizontals, and mudmats are welded while the jacket lies horizontally on skids.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept:** The jacket is always fabricated **horizontally** on the ground because:
> - Welding overhead is minimized (better weld quality, safer for welders).
> - Gravity assists in alignment of heavy tubular members.
> - Launch becomes a controlled tipping operation (see Section 4 diagram).

### Phase 3 — Load-Out (Yard to Barge)

The completed jacket/skid is moved from the fabrication yard onto a **transportation barge** using one of the following methods:

- **Skidding** (hydraulic jacks push the structure onto the barge via roller beams).
- **Self-Floating** (for very large jackets with sealed buoyancy tanks).
- **Heavy-Lift Vessel** (e.g., H-851, Dockwise Vanguard – for topsides).

The critical load-out condition is governed by:

$$M_{LO} = W_{struct} \cdot \frac{L_{barge}}{4}$$

where $M_{LO}$ is the **maximum overturning moment during load-out**, $W_{struct}$ is the structural weight, and $L_{barge}$ is the barge length between supports.

### Phase 4 — Marine Transportation (Tow-Out)

The barge is towed by **AHT (Anchor Handling Tug)** vessels to the offshore site. Critical constraints:

- **Tow Speed**: 4 to 6 knots typical for large jackets.
- **Weather Window**: Calm seas (Hs < 1.5 m), wind < 20 knots.
- **Route Survey**: Bathymetric and shallow-hazard clearance surveys mandatory.
- **On-bottom Stability**: For dry-towed jackets, **transverse stability** is checked via the metacentric height $GM$:

$$GM = KB + BM - KG$$

A positive $GM$ (typically **> 0.5 m**) ensures the towed assembly does not capsize.

### Phase 5 — Offshore Installation

This is the **highest-risk phase** and includes:

1. **Positioning**: Using **DP (Dynamic Positioning)** thrusters or a multi-anchor mooring spread.
2. **Launch / Upending / Lift-Off** depending on structure type.
3. **Pile-Up / Suction Installation / Mooring** to anchor the structure.
4. **Topside Mating**: The deck module is lifted onto the jacket (or mated to a TLP/SPAR columns).
5. **Hook-Up & Commissioning**: Risers, J-tubes, power cables, and process piping are connected.

---

## 2.2 Construction of Specific Structure Types — Logic Breakdown

### A. Steel Jacket Platform (Fixed)

**Why?** Most common for water depths of **10 m to 400 m**.

**How?**
- 4 to 8 main tubular legs.
- Driven piles (typically 4 to 8 piles, **36" to 96"** diameter) grouted into the legs.
- Launched by **upending** (rotating from horizontal to vertical).

**Key Sequence**: Build horizontal → Skid onto barge → Tow → **Launch** → Pile → **Mating of topside**.

### B. Concrete Gravity-Based Structure (GBS)

**Why?** Used in **deep water (100–300 m)** and **harsh environments (North Sea, Arctic)**. No piling required.

**How?**
- Built in a **dry dock** or **caisson** on shore.
- Uses **post-tensioned high-strength concrete** with steel skirts penetrating the seabed.
- Base diameter can reach **100+ m**.

**Key Sequence**: Cast in dry dock → Flood dock → **Wet-tow** (the GBS floats!) → Position → Ballast with seawater → Penetrates seabed under gravity.

### C. Semi-Submersible (Floating)

**Why?** Drilling or production in **500 m to 3000 m** water depths.

**How?**
- Built in **pontoons and columns** (like an upside-down bridge).
- Towed in a **semi-submerged** state (submerged tow) for stability.
- Anchored via **8 to 12 mooring lines** or DP.

### D. Tension Leg Platform (TLP)

**Why?** Production in **500 m to 2000 m** depths; near-rigid vertical motion.

**How?**
- Hull fabricated similar to a semi-submersible.
- **Tendon tubes** at each corner contain pre-tensioned steel tendons.
- Tendons are **post-tensioned** vertically to seabed templates, applying huge downward force (typically **10–20% of displacement**).

### E. SPAR Platform

**Why?** Ultra-deep water (**1500 m to 3000+ m**) production.

**How?**
- **Cylindrical hull** (one big tube, ~30–40 m diameter, ~200 m long).
- Built in a **graving dock**.
- **Up-ended** by controlled flooding of ballast tanks.
- **Wet-stored** at the site before topside mating.

### F. FPSO (Floating Production Storage & Offloading)

**Why?** Marginal fields, deep water, or remote locations without pipelines.

**How?**
- Often a **converted tanker hull** (reusing shipbuilding yards).
- Turret mooring at bow/stern for weathervaning.
- Shuttle tankers periodically offload crude.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Notation Convention:** To avoid breaking markdown tables, all absolute values and pipe-separated expressions use `\vert` notation.

| **#** | **Formula / Concept** | **Engineering Meaning** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $W_{sub} = W_{air} - \rho_w \cdot g \cdot V_{disp}$ | Submerged (apparent) weight during launch | N (Newtons) |
| 2 | $GM = KB + BM - KG$ | Transverse stability of floating phase (wet tow) | m (must be > 0) |
| 3 | $BM = \dfrac{I}{V_{disp}}$ | Metacentric radius (I = 2nd moment of waterplane area) | m |
| 4 | $P_{pile} = \dfrac{W_{struct}}{N_{piles}}$ | Average pile load (for jacket piles) | N |
| 5 | $T_{tendon} = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot A \cdot d$ | Initial TLP tendon pre-tension (excess buoyancy) | N |
| 6 | $SF_{launch} = \dfrac{M_{ult}}{M_{launch}}$ | Safety factor during upending (must be **> 1.5**) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $V_{ballast} = \dfrac{W_{struct}}{\rho_w \cdot g}$ | Volume of ballast water needed to sink GBS | m³ |
| 8 | $t_{wave} = \dfrac{H_s}{v_{setup}}$ | Allowable weather window (Hs ÷ setup rate) | hours |
| 9 | $H_{skid} = \dfrac{W_{struct}}{N_{skids} \cdot A_{pad}}$ | Skid beam bearing pressure | Pa |
| 10 | $C_{crane} \geq W_{topside} \cdot SF_{dyn}$ | Crane selection (dynamic factor **1.25** typical) | tonnes |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The construction methodology directly affects:

- **Project Economics**: Dry-dock vs. wet-tow can change costs by 30–40%.
- **Safety Record**: Launch failures and capsize during tow are historically the deadliest offshore incidents (e.g., Ocean Ranger, 1982).
- **Schedule Critical Path**: Topside float-over vs. lift-off determines 3 to 6 months of project delay.
- **Environmental Compliance**: Barygenetic (heavy metal) ballasting, biocide use, and seabed disturbance are regulated under IMO and OSPAR.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Modern offshore projects increasingly use **Float-Over Installation** (also called "float-over mating") instead of crane lifts. This eliminates weather-dependent heavy lifts and reduces risk. Exam questions in 2024+ KTU papers frequently include float-over diagrams.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Jacket Launch Stability (Upending Mechanics)

When a jacket is launched from the transportation barge, it rotates about the **stern rocker arm** until it floats vertically. During this rotation, the center of buoyancy (B), center of gravity (G), and center of rotation (R) shift continuously.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Define Geometric Parameters**

Let the jacket length be $L_j$ and the displacement at any rotation angle $\theta$ be $V(\theta)$.

**Step 2 — Establish Equilibrium Conditions**

At any instant during rotation, the jacket is in static equilibrium under three forces:
- Weight $W$ acting downward at G.
- Buoyancy $F_B = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot V(\theta)$ acting upward at B.
- Reaction $R$ at the rocker-arm pivot.

$$W = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot V(\theta)$$

**Step 3 — Take Moments About the Pivot Point R**

For the jacket to rotate stably (without catastrophic acceleration), the moment from buoyancy about R must exceed the moment from weight about R by a **stability margin**.

$$M_{stab}(\theta) = F_B \cdot d_B(\theta) - W \cdot d_G(\theta)$$

where:
- $d_B(\theta)$ = horizontal distance from R to B
- $d_G(\theta)$ = horizontal distance from R to G

**Step 4 — Critical Condition: Off-Ship Moment**

The instant the jacket **clears the barge**, $R = 0$, so the moment condition becomes:

$$M_{off} = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot V_{off} \cdot d_B^{off} - W \cdot d_G^{off} > 0$$

**Step 5 — Final Upending Condition**

For successful upending, the jacket must have **positive moment margin** at every angle from $0°$ to $90°$. The minimum required draft at off-ship is:

$$d_{off} = \frac{W}{\rho_w \cdot g \cdot A_{proj}}$$

where $A_{proj}$ is the projected horizontal area at the off-ship instant.

**Step 6 — Calculate Typical Numerical Example**

Suppose:
- $W = 25{,}000$ tonnes (245,250 kN)
- $A_{proj} = 450 \text{ m}^2$
- $\rho_w = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{off} &= \frac{245{,}250{,}000 \text{ N}}{1025 \text{ kg/m}^3 \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 \times 450 \text{ m}^2} \\
&= \frac{245{,}250{,}000}{4{,}525{,}125} \\
&\approx 54.2 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Interpretation:** The jacket draft must reach at least **54.2 m** at the off-ship instant to provide restoring moment. Designers tune the **ballast water** in leg compartments to ensure this critical depth is reached precisely as the jacket tips past the barge stern.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — TLP Tendon Pre-Tension Calculation

A **Tendon Leg Platform (TLP)** requires tendons to be pre-tensioned such that the **net upward buoyancy** of the hull equals the **vertical reaction** from the tendons (since tendons can only pull, not push).

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Hull Displacement and Weights**

Let:
- $W_{hull}$ = dry weight of the hull
- $W_{topsides}$ = weight of the deck module
- $W_{total} = W_{hull} + W_{topsides}$

**Step 2 — Excess Buoyancy Concept**

The TLP is designed such that the **buoyancy force exceeds the weight**. This excess is taken by the tendons as tension.

$$F_{B,total} = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot V_{disp}$$

**Step 3 — Net Upward (Excess) Force**

$$F_{net} = F_{B,total} - W_{total}$$

**Step 4 — Distribution Over Tendons**

For a TLP with $N$ tendons (typically **$N = 4$** at corners):

$$T_{tendon} = \frac{F_{net}}{N} = \frac{\rho_w \cdot g \cdot V_{disp} - W_{total}}{N}$$

**Step 5 — Safety & Acceptance Criteria**

Each tendon must satisfy:

$$T_{tendon} \leq 0.5 \cdot T_{ult,tendon}$$

The **0.5 factor** ensures the tendon remains in tension under maximum wave-induced heave (which can momentarily increase tension by 20–30%).

**Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example**

Given:
- $W_{hull} = 28{,}000$ tonnes (274,680 kN)
- $W_{topsides} = 22{,}000$ tonnes (215,820 kN)
- $V_{disp} = 65{,}000 \text{ m}^3$
- $N = 4$ tendons
- $\rho_w = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

Calculate buoyancy:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{B,total} &= 1025 \times 9.81 \times 65{,}000 \\
&= 653{,}486{,}250 \text{ N} \\
&\approx 653{,}486 \text{ kN} \\
&= 66{,}615 \text{ tonnes (force)}
\end{aligned}$$

Calculate total weight:

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{total} &= 28{,}000 + 22{,}000 = 50{,}000 \text{ tonnes (force)} \\
&= 490{,}500 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

Net upward force:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{net} &= 653{,}486 - 490{,}500 \\
&= 162{,}986 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

Per-tendon tension:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{tendon} &= \frac{162{,}986}{4} \\
&= 40{,}746.5 \text{ kN per tendon} \\
&\approx 4{,}074 \text{ tonnes (force) per tendon}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Interpretation:** Each of the 4 corner tendons must be pre-tensioned to **~4,074 tonnes-force**. If tendons have an ultimate capacity of, say, 12,000 tonnes each, the design safety factor is **$12{,}000 / 4{,}074 \approx 2.95$**, which comfortably exceeds the minimum 2.0 requirement for permanent offshore structures.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — GBS Ballast Volume for Seabed Penetration

A **Concrete Gravity-Based Structure (GBS)** must penetrate the seabed under its own weight (assisted by **underbase suction** or **dynamic compaction**) until the foundation skirts reach the design depth.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Penetration Resistance Force**

The seabed soil resists penetration by a force approximately:

$$F_{resist} = q_c \cdot A_{skirt} + \tau_s \cdot A_{wall}$$

where:
- $q_c$ = cone resistance of soil (kPa)
- $A_{skirt}$ = total skirt tip area (m²)
- $\tau_s$ = skin friction along skirt walls (kPa)
- $A_{wall}$ = total cylindrical skirt surface (m²)

**Step 2 — Available Penetration Force**

The available force is the **submerged weight** of the GBS plus any applied ballasting:

$$F_{avail} = (W_{GBS} + W_{ballast}) - F_{B,residual}$$

**Step 3 — Equilibrium at Design Penetration Depth**

At the final penetration depth $z_{design}$:

$$F_{avail} = F_{resist}(z_{design})$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Required Ballast**

$$W_{ballast,req} = F_{resist}(z_{design}) + F_{B,residual} - W_{GBS}$$

Converting ballast weight to volume:

$$V_{ballast} = \frac{W_{ballast,req}}{\rho_w \cdot g}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Example**

Given:
- $W_{GBS} = 200{,}000$ tonnes (1,962,000 kN)
- $A_{skirt} = 150 \text{ m}^2$
- $q_c = 2{,}500 \text{ kPa}$
- $A_{wall} = 800 \text{ m}^2$
- $\tau_s = 50 \text{ kPa}$

Calculate resistance at design depth:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{resist} &= (2{,}500 \times 150) + (50 \times 800) \\
&= 375{,}000 + 40{,}000 \\
&= 415{,}000 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

Calculate required ballast (assume residual buoyancy is negligible at full penetration):

$$\begin{aligned}
W_{ballast,req} &= 415{,}000 - 1{,}962{,}000 \\
&\text{(negative, meaning GBS is already heavy enough)}
\end{aligned}$$

If $W_{ballast,req}$ is negative, the GBS does **not** need additional ballast — its dry weight alone exceeds penetration resistance, and the design penetration depth is reached naturally.

> **Interpretation:** In this numerical case, the GBS is **over-heavy** relative to the soil resistance — meaning the engineer may need to **reduce skirt depth** or **increase skirt area** to ensure controlled (not uncontrolled) penetration. This is why GBS designs include **ballast sequencing charts** showing incremental filling of seawater tanks over 24–72 hours.

---

## 3.4 Construction Sequence — Comparison Matrix

| **Activity** | **Steel Jacket** | **Concrete GBS** | **Semi-Sub** | **TLP** | **SPAR** | **FPSO** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Yard Type** | Fabrication yard | Dry dock / fjord | Fabrication yard + dry dock | Fabrication yard | Graving dock | Shipyard (conversion) |
| **Build Orientation** | Horizontal | Vertical (in dock) | Horizontal modules | Horizontal | Horizontal | Horizontal (hull) |
| **Transport Mode** | Dry tow on barge | Wet tow (floats) | Wet tow (submerged) | Wet tow | Wet tow (upright) | Wet tow |
| **Installation Method** | Launch / upend | Ballast down | Mooring | Tendon pull-down | Up-end + ballast | Mooring |
| **Anchoring** | Piles | Gravity (skirts) | Mooring lines | Tendons | Mooring lines | Turret mooring |
| **Deck Mating** | Lift (crane) | Lift (crane) | Float-over or lift | Float-over | Lift via derrick | Already integrated |
| **Typical Depth** | 10–400 m | 100–300 m | 500–3000 m | 500–2000 m | 1500–3000+ m | 100–3000 m |
| **Critical Risk Phase** | Launch | Wet tow | Wet tow stability | Tendon connection | Up-ending | Mooring failure |

---

## 3.5 KTU Exam-Oriented Analytical Tip

> [!TIP]
> **Pattern Recognition for Exam Questions:**
> When a question asks "Explain the construction of X," the examiner expects answers in this **chronological sequence**:
> 1. Yard selection & preparation
> 2. Material & component fabrication
> 3. Sub-assembly & welding
> 4. Load-out
> 5. Marine transportation (tow)
> 6. Offshore installation (positioning, setting, anchoring)
> 7. Hook-up & commissioning
>
> Deviating from this sequence = lost marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Universal Offshore Construction Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Yard Selection and Site Preparation] --> B[Material Procurement and Cutting]
    B --> C[Component Fabrication: Cans, Nodes, Braces]
    C --> D[Sub Assembly and NDT Inspection]
    D --> E[Major Assembly: Horizontal or Vertical]
    E --> F[Load Out onto Transportation Barge]
    F --> G[Marine Tow Out to Offshore Site]
    G --> H[Positioning via DP or Anchor Spread]
    H --> I[Installation: Launch or Lift Off]
    I --> J[Anchoring: Piles, Tendons, or Mooring]
    J --> K[Topside Mating and Hook Up]
    K --> L[Commissioning and Hand Over]
```

## 4.2 Steel Jacket Platform — Construction Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Steel Plates and API 5L Pipes Arrive at Yard] --> S2[CNC Cutting and Rolling into Cans]
    S2 --> S3[Can Stacking into 4 to 8 Legs]
    S3 --> S4[Horizontal Brace Welding]
    S4 --> S5[Skid Beams and Mudmats Attached]
    S5 --> S6[Jacket Skidded onto Launch Barge]
    S6 --> S7[Towed to Site and Positioned]
    S7 --> S8[Upending Launch via Stern Rocker]
    S8 --> S9[Jacket Self Floats Vertically]
    S9 --> S10[Pile Driving Through Legs]
    S10 --> S11[Grouting Annulus Between Pile and Leg]
    S11 --> S12[Topside Lift On Using Crane Vessel]
    S12 --> S13[Hook Up of Risers and Power]
    S13 --> S14[Commissioning]
```

## 4.3 Concrete Gravity Based Structure — Construction Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G1[Site Selection with Deep Fjord or Dry Dock] --> G2[Base Slab Casting in Dry Dock]
    G2 --> G3[Conical Shafts and Storage Caissons Cast]
    G3 --> G4[Post Tensioned Concrete Curing 28 Days]
    G4 --> G5[Topside Modules Mounted on Quay]
    G5 --> G6[Dock Flooded: GBS Floats]
    G6 --> G7[Wet Tow to Field with Tug Boats]
    G7 --> G8[Positioning over Subsea Template]
    G8 --> G9[Sequential Ballast Water Filling]
    G9 --> G10[Skirts Penetrate Seabed]
    G10 --> G11[Topside Float Over and Mating]
    G11 --> G12[Riser and Pipeline Hook Up]
```

## 4.4 TLP Construction Sequence — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[Hull Pontoons and Columns Welded at Yard] --> T2[Four Tendon Porches Installed at Corners]
    T2 --> T3[Dry Tow to Deep Water Site]
    T3 --> T4[Subsea Templates and Suction Piles Installed]
    T4 --> T5[TLP Hull Wet Stored: Partially Ballasted]
    T5 --> T6[Topside Floated Over and Mated]
    T6 --> T7[Tendons Pulled Through Top and Bottom Connectors]
    T7 --> T8[Post Tensioning Hoses Inflated: Lock Off Tension]
    T8 --> T9[Production Risers and Jumper Lines Connected]
    T9 --> T10[Pre Production Testing and Start Up]
```

## 4.5 Comparative Selection Diagram — Which Structure for Which Depth?

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Water Depth Selection] --> B[Shallow: 0 to 150 m]
    A --> C[Intermediate: 150 to 500 m]
    A --> D[Deep: 500 to 1500 m]
    A --> E[Ultra Deep: 1500 m Plus]

    B --> B1[Steel Jacket on Piles]
    B --> B2[Concrete GBS]

    C --> C1[Steel Jacket]
    C --> C2[Compliant Tower]

    D --> D1[Semi Submersible]
    D --> D2[TLP]

    E --> E1[SPAR]
    E --> E2[FPSO]
    E --> E3[Drillship]
```
