# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**Q: List the major phases involved in the construction of a steel jacket platform.**

**Model Answer:**

The major phases are:

1. **Engineering and Procurement** – Detailed design, material procurement (ASTM A572 steel, API pipes, sacrificial anodes).
2. **Yard Fabrication** – Plate cutting, rolling into cans, can-stacking into legs, horizontal assembly of braces and mudmats, NDT inspection of welds.
3. **Load-Out** – The completed jacket is skidded or rolled onto a transportation barge using hydraulic jacks and skid beams.
4. **Marine Transportation** – The barge is towed by AHT (Anchor Handling Tug) vessels to the offshore site at ~5 knots, observing weather windows.
5. **Offshore Installation** – The jacket is upended (launched from the barge) using stern rocker arms, then piles are driven through the legs and grouted.
6. **Topside Mating** – The deck module is lifted by a heavy-lift crane vessel and set on the jacket.
7. **Hook-Up and Commissioning** – Risers, J-tubes, power cables, and process modules are connected; the platform is tested and started up.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**Q: Differentiate between dry tow and wet tow methods of marine transportation.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **Dry Tow** | **Wet Tow** |
|---|---|---|
| **Barge/Support** | Cargo barge (structure rests on deck) | The structure itself floats |
| **Buoyancy** | Provided entirely by the barge | Provided by the structure's own sealed compartments |
| **Stability** | Controlled by barge ballast; check $GM > 0.5$ m | Controlled by metacentric height of the floating structure |
| **Examples** | Steel jackets, topside modules | Concrete GBS, SPAR hulls, semi-submersibles |
| **Risk Profile** | Dependent on barge intact stability | Dependent on the structure's own intact stability and watertight integrity |
| **Draft Limitation** | Limited by water depth on tow route | Limited by harbor/bridge clearances on tow route |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note on KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is internally divided as **Part (a) = 7 marks + Part (b) = 7 marks**, with internal choice between Question A and Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

**Q: Explain in detail the construction sequence of a Steel Jacket Platform. Also describe the launch/upending procedure with suitable diagrams.**

#### **Part (a) — Construction Sequence (7 Marks)**

The construction of a steel jacket platform proceeds in the following sequence:

**Step 1: Yard Preparation**
A suitable fabrication yard is selected with sufficient soil bearing capacity (>50 kPa), heavy-lift cranes (≥2,000 tonnes), and proximity to a sheltered harbor for load-out.

**Step 2: Material Procurement and Cutting**
Steel plates conforming to **ASTM A572 Gr. 50** and tubular sections (API 5L X65) are procured. Plates are CNC-cut and cold-rolled into cylindrical cans of prescribed diameters (typically 36" to 96").

**Step 3: Leg Fabrication**
The rolled cans are stacked vertically and welded using SMAW/FCAW processes to form the main legs (4 to 8 legs depending on the platform size). Each weld undergoes **100% NDT inspection** (UT + RT) per API 1104.

**Step 4: Horizontal Assembly**
The legs are laid horizontally on skids. Horizontal braces, K-braces, diagonal braces, and mudmats are welded in place. The jacket may be **100+ m tall** but is built horizontally to avoid overhead welding.

**Step 5: Appurtenances**
Conductor guides, J-tubes for risers, boat landings, helideck support frames, and sacrificial anodes (aluminum or zinc) are welded onto the jacket.

**Step 6: Load-Out**
The completed jacket is moved onto a launch barge using **hydraulic skidding systems**. Rollers are placed between the jacket and the barge deck; hydraulic jacks push the jacket over the rollers.

**Step 7: Marine Tow**
The barge is towed by 2 to 4 AHT vessels to the offshore site. The tow speed is 4–6 knots, with continuous monitoring of metocean conditions.

**Step 8: Site Positioning and Launch**
At the site, the barge is positioned using DP or mooring. The jacket is **upended** by controlled ballasting of leg compartments and released from the stern rocker arm.

**Step 9: Pile Installation and Grouting**
Steel piles (driven to refusal or to a design penetration depth using hydraulic hammers) are inserted through the jacket legs. The annulus between pile and leg is filled with **high-strength grout** (typically 50 MPa compressive strength).

**Step 10: Topside Mating**
The pre-assembled topside deck (500 to 50,000 tonnes) is lifted by a heavy-lift crane vessel and placed on top of the jacket legs.

**Step 11: Hook-Up and Commissioning**
Production risers, power cables, and process piping are connected; safety and control systems are tested; the platform is commissioned.

#### **Part (b) — Launch/Upending Procedure (7 Marks)**

The launch of a steel jacket is a critical engineering operation:

**Phase 1 — Pre-Launch Ballasting**
The launch barge is partially ballasted to give a stern trim of approximately **2 to 3 degrees**. The jacket is positioned such that its center of gravity lies slightly aft of the rocker-arm pivot.

**Phase 2 — Initial Rotation**
As ballasting continues, the jacket begins to rotate about the rocker arm. The leg compartments are flooded sequentially — the **upstream legs first, then the downstream legs** — to control the rate of rotation.

**Phase 3 — Off-Ship Condition**
At a critical rotation angle (typically 25° to 35° from horizontal), the jacket's center of buoyancy passes the pivot and the jacket **clears the barge stern**. The structure must have positive metacentric stability at this instant to prevent backward rotation.

**Phase 4 — Free Floating**
After clearing the barge, the jacket continues to rotate due to gravity and buoyancy, eventually reaching a vertical, freely floating position. At this point, the draft is approximately **50–60 m** for a deep-water jacket.

**Phase 5 — Upright Stability Check**
Engineers verify that the jacket has positive righting moment in roll and pitch; otherwise, additional ballast is added or pumping operations are reversed to recover stability.

**Phase 6 — Pile Driving**
Hydraulic hammers (e.g., Menck MHU 800, IHC S-2800) drive 4 to 8 steel piles through the legs. Piles may be 60–100 m long with diameters of 36–96 inches.

> **Stability Condition (analytical):** The upending is considered successful if:
>
> $$M_{stab}(\theta) > 0 \quad \forall \theta \in [0°, 90°]$$
>
> with a minimum safety factor of **1.5** against any instability.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Internal Choice Alternative]

**Q: Describe the construction of a Concrete Gravity-Based Structure (GBS). Compare its construction with that of a Steel Jacket Platform.**

#### **Part (a) — GBS Construction (7 Marks)**

A Concrete Gravity-Based Structure is constructed entirely of **reinforced and post-tensioned concrete**, typically in a sheltered fjord or dry dock:

**Step 1: Site Selection**
A deep fjord (e.g., Norwegian fjords such as Stavanger Fjord) or a large graving dock is selected. The site must have:
- Sufficient water depth for wet tow.
- Calm, sheltered waters.
- Aggregate and cement supply nearby.

**Step 2: Base Slab Casting**
The base slab (often **100+ m in diameter** and 5–15 m thick) is cast on a prepared foundation using high-strength concrete (C60–C80 grade).

**Step 3: Shaft Construction**
One to four cylindrical shafts (for oil storage or as structural columns) are slip-formed or jump-formed vertically. Steel tendons (post-tensioning cables) are installed and stressed after concrete curing (28 days).

**Step 4: Topside Integration**
The topside modules are often mounted **at the quayside** before wet tow, reducing offshore work.

**Step 5: Dock Flooding**
Sea valves are opened; the dry dock floods; the GBS floats due to its massive buoyancy (often >100,000 tonnes displacement).

**Step 6: Wet Tow**
The GBS is towed to the field at 2–4 knots by 3 to 5 tugs. Buoyancy compartments are carefully monitored.

**Step 7: Site Positioning**
The GBS is positioned within meters of the target using GPS and acoustic positioning.

**Step 8: Ballast-Down**
Sea water is pumped sequentially into base compartments, lowering the GBS at a controlled rate of **0.5–1 m/hour**.

**Step 9: Skirt Penetration**
Steel skirts at the base penetrate the seabed under the structure's weight. Penetration is monitored by **inclinometers and penetrometers**.

**Step 10: Grouting and Hook-Up**
The base cells may be filled with gravel or oil. Risers and pipelines are connected via J-tubes pre-installed in the shafts.

#### **Part (b) — Comparison Table (7 Marks)**

| **Aspect** | **Steel Jacket** | **Concrete GBS** |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Material** | Steel (ASTM A572, API 5L) | Reinforced & post-tensioned concrete |
| **Yard Type** | Fabrication yard | Dry dock / fjord |
| **Construction Orientation** | Horizontal | Vertical |
| **Number of Pieces** | Thousands of welded cans | Few large concrete pours |
| **Welding Intensity** | Very high | Minimal (post-tensioning only) |
| **Tow Method** | Dry tow (on barge) | Wet tow (self-floating) |
| **Installation Method** | Launch / upending | Controlled ballast-down |
| **Anchoring System** | Driven steel piles | Gravity & skirt penetration |
| **Water Depth Range** | 10–400 m | 100–300 m |
| **Construction Time** | 18–30 months | 24–40 months |
| **Removal / Decommissioning** | Difficult (cut piles) | Easier (no piles) |
| **Cost Driver** | Steel price volatility | Concrete and aggregate logistics |
| **Example Field** | Gulf of Mexico, North Sea (shallow) | North Sea (e.g., Troll, Statfjord) |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks — Examiner Pitfall List:**
>
> 1. **Skipping the chronological order** — Students list construction phases out of sequence (e.g., mention "hook-up" before "installation"). This loses **2 to 3 marks** in Part B.
> 2. **Confusing dry tow vs. wet tow** — Wet tow applies to structures that **float themselves** (GBS, SPAR, semi-sub). Students often write "wet tow" for jacket platforms, which is **wrong**.
> 3. **Forgetting numerical examples** — When asked to "explain with sketches," always include **at least one numerical value** (e.g., "draft = 54.2 m"). This demonstrates depth and earns full marks.
> 4. **Not mentioning NDT** — Welding quality assurance (UT, RT, MPI) is mandatory in any construction answer. Skipping it loses **1 mark**.
> 5. **Confusing tendons (TLP) with tethers** — TLPs use **tendons** (vertical, pre-tensioned). Students sometimes write "tethers" which is the older terminology and penalized.
> 6. **No mention of weather window** — Offshore installation is governed by **Hs < 1.5 m and wind < 20 knots**. Forgetting this loses **1 mark** in installation questions.
> 7. **Skipping the metocean criterion** — Always state the **design wave return period (100 years)** for permanent structures. This is a hallmark of a top-scoring answer.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4:**

- [ ] **Five Universal Phases**: Engineering → Fabrication → Load-Out → Tow → Installation → Hook-Up.
- [ ] **Jacket is built HORIZONTALLY** and launched by **upending** (rotation about stern rocker arm).
- [ ] **GBS is built VERTICALLY** in a **dry dock** and **floats itself** (wet tow).
- [ ] **Semi-submersibles** are towed in a **submerged condition** for stability.
- [ ] **TLPs** use **vertical pre-tensioned tendons** (not piles, not chains).
- [ ] **SPARs** are **single big cylindrical hulls** that are **up-ended** by controlled flooding.
- [ ] **FPSOs** are typically **converted tankers** with a **turret mooring**.
- [ ] **Piles** are driven by **hydraulic hammers** (Menck, IHC series).
- [ ] **Skids** transfer load from jacket to barge during load-out; check **bearing pressure** $<$\ allowable.
- [ ] **GM** must be **> 0.5 m** for towing stability.
- [ ] **Weather window** is **Hs < 1.5 m, wind < 20 knots** for installation.
- [ ] **Design wave return period** is **100 years** for permanent platforms.
- [ ] **Crane capacity** must exceed topside weight with a **dynamic factor of 1.25**.
- [ ] **Float-over installation** is increasingly replacing heavy-lift crane operations.
- [ ] **Tendon pre-tension** must satisfy $T_{tendon} \leq 0.5 \cdot T_{ult,tendon}$ (i.e., **SF ≥ 2.0**).
- [ ] **NDT (UT, RT, MPI)** is mandatory per **API 1104** for all structural welds.
- [ ] **Decommissioning** consideration: jackets require pile cutting; GBS are easier to remove.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Wisdom:** A single line drawing with proper labels in Part B can fetch 2 extra marks over a text-only answer. Practice sketching a **jacket in horizontal assembly** and a **TLP in tendon pull-down** before the exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Load Out Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST722 - FUNDAMENTALS OF OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Load Out Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LOAD OUT (Official KTU / Industry Definition)**
> *Load out* is the engineered operation of transferring a fully fabricated offshore structure (jacket, topside module, FPSO hull, or subsea template) from its onshore construction / fabrication site onto a **transportation barge or vessel** for subsequent marine towage to the offshore installation location. It is the critical interface operation between the fabrication yard and the offshore field.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for **OENST722 – Fundamentals of Offshore Structures**, load out is classified as a **Module 4 installation-engineering activity** belonging to the family of *marine operations*. The operation is governed by combined structural, naval-architectural (stability, draft, trim), geotechnical, and hydraulic-load principles.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why load out is a high-stakes activity:**
> 1. Structures weigh from **2,000 tonnes** (small jackets) to over **60,000 tonnes** (FPSO hulls / integrated topsides).
> 2. The center of gravity is **high and offset** for topsides — making stability the dominant concern.
> 3. Any failure during load out causes **total loss of asset, project delay of 6–18 months, and economic loss of 100–500 million USD**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Giant on a Flatbed Truck"

Imagine you have built a **fully furnished, two-storey house** in a workshop and you must now slide it onto a **giant flatbed truck** without damaging the furniture, the walls, or the truck.

- The **house** = offshore structure (jacket / topside).
- The **flatbed truck** = transportation barge.
- The **slide from workshop floor to truck** = the load out operation.

Three engineering problems instantly appear:
1. **The house is very heavy** → the floor (skidway / quay) must not collapse.
2. **The truck may tilt** when the house slides on → the truck (barge) needs **ballast water** to stay level.
3. **The wind / waves** may push the house while sliding → it must be **restrained by winches and sea-fastening**.

This is exactly the engineering challenge of load out. The structure is moved by one of several engineered methods, while the barge is precisely ballasted to maintain acceptable **draft, trim, and stability**.

---

## 1.3 Categorisation of Load Out Methods (Syllabus Map)

The KTU 2024 Module 4 syllabus lists the following canonical methods:

| S.No. | Method | Best Suited For |
|:-----:|:-------|:----------------|
| 1 | **Skidding (Sliding) Method** | Jackets, small-to-medium topsides, deck modules |
| 2 | **Heavy-Lift / Sling Method** | Medium topsides, modules, integrated decks |
| 3 | **Float-Over Method** | Very large / heavy integrated topsides (> 20,000 t) |
| 4 | **Roll-On / Roll-Off (Ro-Ro) Method** | Modules on tracks, jack-up rig legs |
| 5 | **Direct Lift (Sheerlegs / Crane Vessel)** | Modular pieces, bridge sections |
| 6 | **Self-Float Method** | Subsea hulls, large pontoons, gravity bases |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection of a method depends on:** weight of the structure, yard layout, tidal range at the quay, available equipment, water depth at the quayside, and the **weather window** (wave, wind, current limits) at the time of operation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Skidding (Sliding) Load Out Method

### 2.1.1 Operational Principle
The structure is dragged (skidded) along prepared **skidways** (rails, greased timbers, or PTFE / Teflon pads) from the fabrication yard onto the barge using:
- **Hydraulic ram pull** (modern preferred method), **or**
- **Winch + wire-rope pull** (legacy method).

> [!NOTE]
> Skidways are typically inclined at **1 in 20 to 1 in 12** (≈ 2.86° to 4.76°) to assist gravity-assisted sliding once the static friction is overcome.

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Skidding Operation
1. **Pre-load-out survey** — confirm skidway integrity, lubrication, and barge mooring lines.
2. **Position barge** at the quayside with its deck aligned with the skidway.
3. **Pre-ballast barge** so that the deck of the barge is **level with the quay** (matched elevation).
4. **Rig pulling system** — hydraulic push-pull rams anchored behind the structure.
5. **Incremental skidding** — structure moves in **stroke increments of 1–3 m**.
6. **Continuous ballast adjustment** — as weight shifts onto barge, deballast to keep draft and trim within limits.
7. **Final positioning and sea-fastening** — structure is welded / clamped to the barge.

### 2.1.3 Why the Skidding Method Works — Engineering "How"
- Friction between **PTFE pads (μ ≈ 0.05–0.10)** and **stainless steel skidway** is very low.
- **Hydraulic rams** can apply 100–500 tonnes of pulling force per unit, allowing smooth, controlled motion.
- The **barge deck level is matched with the quay level** so the structure does not climb a step.

---

## 2.2 Heavy-Lift / Sling Load Out Method

The structure is **suspended on slings** from the hook of a **sheerlegs vessel, crawler crane, or ring crane**. The slings are attached to pre-engineered **lift points / pad-eyes** on the structure.

- Typical lift capacity: **5,000 – 30,000 tonnes**.
- Used for **module integration, deck mating, jacket uprighting**.
- Sling tensions must be calculated for the **angle of sling**, **center of gravity location**, and **dynamic amplification factor (DAF ≈ 1.1–1.3)**.

---

## 2.3 Float-Over Load Out Method

The most sophisticated method, used for the heaviest integrated topsides.

### 2.3.1 Process
1. Barge is **ballasted down** so its deck is **2–3 m below the water**.
2. The structure is **pre-positioned above the submerged barge** on temporary supports (e.g., on the quay span or on support stools).
3. Barge is **deballasted** slowly — the structure descends onto the barge under controlled buoyancy.
4. Once the structure is fully seated, it is **welded and sea-fastened** to the barge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Float-over eliminates the need for heavy-lift vessels, but requires **very calm seas (Hs < 1.0 m), precise ballast control, and synchronized winch operations**.

---

## 2.4 Roll-On / Roll-Off (Ro-Ro) Method
The structure is mounted on **wheeled transporters (Self-Propelled Modular Trailers — SPMTs)** and rolled directly onto the barge. Used for modular construction and for **jack-up rig leg sections**.

---

## 2.5 Self-Float Method
For **buoyant structures** (e.g., subsea storage tanks, concrete gravity bases, large pontoon hulls) — the structure is **floated off the quay** by controlled flooding of internal compartments, then towed directly.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use the `\vert` or `\mid` rendering style for any absolute-value symbol so the markdown table does not break.

| # | Engineering Quantity | Governing Formula | Symbols & Units | Application in Load Out |
|:-:|:---------------------|:------------------|:----------------|:------------------------|
| 1 | **Skidding Friction Force** | $F_f = \mu \cdot N$ | $\mu$ = coefficient of friction (–), $N$ = normal force (N) | Resisting force on skid shoes |
| 2 | **Skidding Force on Inclined Skidway** | $F_s = W \sin\theta + \mu W \cos\theta$ | $W$ = weight (N), $\theta$ = skidway slope (rad) | Required pulling force |
| 3 | **Pulling Power (Hydraulic Ram)** | $P = \dfrac{F_s \cdot v}{\eta}$ | $v$ = skid velocity (m/s), $\eta$ = efficiency (0.85–0.95) | Sizing hydraulic power pack |
| 4 | **Barge Draft (Uniform Loading)** | $T = \dfrac{W_{total}}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot A_w}$ | $A_w$ = water-plane area (m²), $\rho_{sw}$ = 1025 kg/m³ | Final draft at quayside |
| 5 | **Trim Correction (Couple)** | $\Delta T_{trim} = \dfrac{M_{trim}}{M_{TPC} \cdot L_{BP}}$ | $M_{trim}$ = trimming moment (t·m), $M_{TPC}$ = MTPC (t/cm), $L_{BP}$ = length between perpendiculars (m) | Fore-aft barge inclination |
| 6 | **Metacentric Height (Stability)** | $GM = KB + BM - KG$ | $KB$ = height of buoyancy centre (m), $BM = \dfrac{I_T}{V_{disp}}$, $KG$ = height of CoG (m) | Barge stability check |
| 7 | **Winch Line Tension (Two-line)** | $T_1 = \dfrac{W \cdot \sin\alpha}{2 \sin\beta \cos\phi}$ | $\alpha$ = ramp angle, $\beta$ = line angle, $\phi$ = friction angle | Holding-back force on barge |
| 8 | **Sling Tension (4-Point Lift)** | $F_{sling} = \dfrac{W}{n \cos\gamma}$ | $n$ = number of slings, $\gamma$ = sling angle from vertical | Pad-eye / sling design |
| 9 | **Dynamic Amplification (Lift)** | $F_{dyn} = DAF \times F_{static}$ | $DAF = 1.1 - 1.3$ | Lift-point design |
| 10 | **Bearing Pressure (Skid Shoe)** | $p_b = \dfrac{W_{shoe}}{A_{shoe}}$ | $A_{shoe}$ = contact area (m²) | Skidway structural check |
| 11 | **Required Ballast Volume** | $V_{ballast} = \dfrac{\Delta W}{\rho_{sw}}$ | $\Delta W$ = weight to displace (t) | Pre-load-out ballasting |
| 12 | **Float-Over Submergence** | $T_{sub} = T_{unloaded} + h_{clear} + h_{structure}$ | $h_{clear}$ = clearance (m), $h_{structure}$ = height (m) | Required pre-ballast depth |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility of Load Out Engineering

| Industry Sector | Typical Use of Load Out Method |
|:----------------|:-------------------------------|
| **Oil & Gas (Offshore Platforms)** | Topsides, jackets, FPSO hulls onto barges |
| **Offshore Wind** | Wind turbine jackets / floating substations |
| **Subsea Engineering** | Manifold templates, PLETs |
| **Bridges / Civil Marine** | Bridge segments onto pontoons |
| **Naval / Defence** | Warship hulls from graving dock |

Load out engineering intersects with **naval architecture, structural engineering, geotechnics, and project management** — making it a flagship topic in any offshore engineering syllabus, including the KTU OENST722 curriculum.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are **exhaustive**. Every algebraic transition is shown. No step has been skipped or paraphrased.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Skidding Force on a Horizontal Skidway

**Given:**
- Weight of structure $W$ (in Newtons or tonnes-force).
- Coefficient of kinetic friction between PTFE pad and skidway = $\mu_k$.
- Skidway is horizontal ($\theta = 0$).

**To find:** Pulling force $F_p$ required to overcome friction and move the structure at constant velocity (i.e., kinetic condition).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Free-Body Diagram (FBD) of the structure on the skidway:**

The structure has four forces acting on it:
1. Weight $W$ acting vertically downward.
2. Normal reaction $N$ from the skidway acting vertically upward.
3. Pulling force $F_p$ acting horizontally.
4. Friction force $F_f$ acting horizontally, opposing motion.

**Step 2 — Apply vertical equilibrium:**

$$
\sum F_y = 0
$$

$$
N - W = 0
$$

$$
N = W
$$

*Textual logic:* The skidway supports the entire weight, so the normal reaction equals the weight.

**Step 3 — Apply horizontal equilibrium (constant velocity, hence no acceleration):**

$$
\sum F_x = 0
$$

$$
F_p - F_f = 0
$$

$$
F_p = F_f
$$

*Textual logic:* Pulling force exactly balances the kinetic friction (Newton's First Law).

**Step 4 — Apply the Coulomb friction law:**

$$
F_f = \mu_k \cdot N
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $N = W$ from Step 2 into Step 4:**

$$
F_f = \mu_k \cdot W
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $F_f$ into Step 3:**

$$
\boxed{F_p = \mu_k \cdot W}
$$

*Engineering interpretation:* The pulling force is simply a fraction $\mu_k$ of the weight. For PTFE on stainless steel, $\mu_k \approx 0.05$, so only **5 % of the structure weight** needs to be pulled — which is why hydraulic skidding is so efficient.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Skidding Force on an Inclined Skidway

**Given:**
- Weight $W$ acting vertically downward.
- Skidway inclined at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal.
- Coefficient of friction $\mu_k$.

**To find:** Total pulling force $F_p$ required for upward (against gravity) sliding.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — FBD on inclined plane:**

Resolve $W$ into two components:
- Component **along the skidway (down-slope):** $W \sin\theta$
- Component **normal to the skidway:** $W \cos\theta$

**Step 2 — Normal reaction (perpendicular equilibrium):**

$$
N = W \cos\theta
$$

**Step 3 — Friction force (opposes upward motion, hence points down-slope):**

$$
F_f = \mu_k \cdot N = \mu_k \cdot W \cos\theta
$$

**Step 4 — Apply force balance along the skidway:**

The structure must be pushed **up the slope** against:
1. The gravity component $W \sin\theta$ (down-slope).
2. The friction force $F_f$ (also down-slope, since structure is being moved up).

$$
F_p = W \sin\theta + F_f
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute expression for $F_f$:**

$$
F_p = W \sin\theta + \mu_k \cdot W \cos\theta
$$

**Step 6 — Factor out $W$:**

$$
\boxed{F_p = W \left( \sin\theta + \mu_k \cos\theta \right)}
$$

**Numerical sanity check:** For $W = 10{,}000$ tonnes-force, $\theta = 3°$, $\mu_k = 0.06$:

$$
F_p = 10{,}000 \times (\sin 3° + 0.06 \cdot \cos 3°)
$$

$$
F_p = 10{,}000 \times (0.0523 + 0.06 \times 0.9986)
$$

$$
F_p = 10{,}000 \times (0.0523 + 0.0599) = 10{,}000 \times 0.1122
$$

$$
F_p \approx 1{,}122 \text{ tonnes-force}
$$

*Engineering interpretation:* For every 10,000 tonnes of structure, only ~1,122 tonnes of pulling force is needed.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Barge Draft Calculation (Uniform Loading)

**Given:**
- Total weight on barge = $W$ (tonnes).
- Seawater density = $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³.
- Barge water-plane area = $A_w$ (m²).
- Acceleration due to gravity = $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**To find:** Mean draft $T$ of the barge.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Archimedes' Principle:** Buoyancy force = weight of displaced water.

$$
F_{buoy} = W
$$

**Step 2 — Write buoyancy force in terms of displaced volume:**

The displaced volume = $A_w \times T$ (water-plane area × draft).

$$
F_{buoy} = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot (A_w \cdot T)
$$

**Step 3 — Equate buoyancy to weight (floating equilibrium):**

$$
\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot A_w \cdot T = W
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $T$:**

$$
\boxed{T = \dfrac{W}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot A_w}}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical check:**
For $W = 20{,}000$ t, $A_w = 3{,}000$ m², $\rho_{sw} = 1.025$ t/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²:

$$
T = \dfrac{20{,}000}{1.025 \times 9.81 \times 3{,}000} = \dfrac{20{,}000}{30{,}169.5} \approx 0.663 \text{ m}
$$

*Engineering interpretation:* A 20,000-tonne topside on a 3,000 m² barge produces ~66 cm of mean draft. Quayside water depth must exceed this with adequate **under-keel clearance (UKC ≥ 0.5 m)**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Sling Tension in a 4-Point Lift

**Given:**
- Total lift weight = $W$ (tonnes).
- Number of slings = $n = 4$.
- Sling angle from vertical = $\gamma$.
- CoG located at the geometric centre of sling pattern.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Vertical equilibrium of the suspended load:**

The total vertical component of all sling tensions must equal the weight.

$$
n \cdot T_{sling} \cdot \cos\gamma = W
$$

*Textual logic:* Each sling makes angle $\gamma$ with the vertical, so its vertical lift component is $T_{sling}\cos\gamma$. Summing over $n$ slings gives the total upward force.

**Step 2 — Solve for $T_{sling}$:**

$$
\boxed{T_{sling} = \dfrac{W}{n \cos\gamma}}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical check:**
$W = 5{,}000$ t, $n = 4$, $\gamma = 30°$:

$$
T_{sling} = \dfrac{5{,}000}{4 \cos 30°} = \dfrac{5{,}000}{4 \times 0.8660} = \dfrac{5{,}000}{3.464} \approx 1{,}443 \text{ t}
$$

*Engineering interpretation:* A sling angled at 30° from vertical carries **1,443 tonnes** — almost **15 % more** than its share (1,250 t). This is why **sling angles are kept ≤ 30°** in practice to keep tensions within safe limits.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Barge Stability (Metacentric Height)

**Given:**
- $KB$ = height of centre of buoyancy above keel (m).
- $BM = I_T / \nabla$, where $I_T$ = transverse second moment of water-plane area (m⁴), $\nabla$ = displaced volume (m³).
- $KG$ = height of centre of gravity above keel (m).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define GM (metacentric height):**

By definition in naval architecture:

$$
\boxed{GM = KB + BM - KG}
$$

**Step 2 — Stability criterion:**

For a load out to be safe, $GM > 0$ (positive stability). KTU/industry codes typically require $GM \geq 0.30$ m during load out.

**Step 3 — Numerical check:**
$KB = 1.5$ m, $I_T = 50{,}000$ m⁴, $\nabla = 30{,}000$ m³, $KG = 3.0$ m:

$$
BM = \dfrac{50{,}000}{30{,}000} = 1.667 \text{ m}
$$

$$
GM = 1.5 + 1.667 - 3.0 = 0.167 \text{ m}
$$

*Result:* $GM = 0.167$ m — **below the 0.30 m threshold**. Ballast must be redistributed (lower KG) or CoG must be lowered before load out proceeds.

---

## 3.6 Master Analytical Matrix (for last-minute revision)

| Parameter | Formula | Typical Value in Load Out | Failure Consequence |
|:----------|:--------|:--------------------------|:--------------------|
| $\mu_k$ (PTFE pad) | empirical | 0.05–0.10 | Sticking / binding |
| Skid slope $\theta$ | design choice | 1:20 to 1:12 | Excessive pull or rollback |
| Sling angle $\gamma$ | design choice | $\leq 30°$ | Sling over-tension |
| Min $GM$ | code (API/ISO) | $\geq 0.30$ m | Barge capsize |
| Max barge draft | quay depth | $\leq 80\%$ of depth | Grounding |
| DAF (lift) | code (DNV/ABS) | 1.10–1.30 | Pad-eye failure |
| Max sea state (float-over) | operational | $H_s < 1.0$ m | Misfit / damage |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams use **purely alphanumeric node IDs** (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`) and **plain uppercase labels** (no bold or italics inside quoted node labels) to comply with KTU rendering safeguards.

## 4.1 Master Flowchart — Generic Load Out Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[START: STRUCTURE READY IN YARD] --> B0[WEIGHT AND DIMENSION CHECK]
    B0 --> C0{WEIGHT LESS THAN 5000 T}
    C0 -->|YES| D0[HEAVY LIFT SLING METHOD]
    C0 -->|NO| E0{WEIGHT BETWEEN 5000 AND 20000 T}
    E0 -->|YES| F0{QUAY SLOPE AVAILABLE}
    F0 -->|YES| G0[SKIDDING METHOD WITH HYDRAULIC RAMS]
    F0 -->|NO| H0[FLOAT OVER METHOD]
    E0 -->|NO| I0{QUAY DEPTH SUFFICIENT FOR SUBMERGENCE}
    I0 -->|YES| J0[FLOAT OVER METHOD WITH BALLAST CONTROL]
    I0 -->|NO| K0[USE TWO BARGES OR SELF FLOAT METHOD]
    D0 --> L0[SEA FASTENING AND TOW OUT]
    G0 --> L0
    H0 --> L0
    J0 --> L0
    K0 --> L0
    L0 --> M0[END: BARGE TOWED TO OFFSHORE SITE]
```


```mermaid
    A2[STEP 1: BARGE TOWED TO QUAYSIDE] --> B2[STEP 2: BARGE PRE BALLASTED]
```
