# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions are modeled on **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE)** pattern, Module 4 (Load Out Methods).

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`
**Define load out. List any four commonly used methods of load out in offshore engineering.**

**Model Answer:**

Load out is the engineered operation of transferring a fabricated offshore structure (jacket, topside, FPSO hull, etc.) from the onshore fabrication yard onto a transportation barge for subsequent marine towage to the offshore installation site.

The four commonly used methods are:
1. **Skidding (Sliding) method** — structure pulled along greased skidways.
2. **Heavy-lift / Sling method** — structure lifted by sheerlegs / crawler crane using slings.
3. **Float-over method** — structure lowered onto a pre-submerged barge by controlled deballasting.
4. **Roll-on / Roll-off method** — structure transported on SPMTs and rolled onto the barge.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`
**Explain the role of ballast water management during a load out operation on a transportation barge.**

**Model Answer:**

During load out, the structure's weight is progressively transferred from the quayside onto the barge. This changes the **displacement, draft, and trim** of the barge. Ballast water is used to:
1. **Match barge deck level with the quay** before skidding begins (pre-ballasting).
2. **Counteract weight transfer** during skidding — deballast on the side under the structure to keep the deck level.
3. **Maintain positive metacentric height (GM ≥ 0.30 m)** to ensure transverse stability.
4. **Control trim (fore-aft inclination)** to keep the load on the intended skid track.
5. **Limit maximum draft** to remain within the quayside water depth plus UKC.

Improper ballast management causes **barge listing, structural misalignment, sliding accidents, or capsize** — making it the single most important operational parameter in load out.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each Part B question carries an **internal choice** — a student answers **either Option A or Option B** from the same module.

---

### **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]`

**(a)** With neat sketches, describe the **skidding method** of load out for an offshore jacket. List the factors that influence the selection of this method. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 12,000-tonne offshore topside is to be skidded onto a barge along a horizontal skidway. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the PTFE skid shoes and the stainless-steel skidway is **0.07**. Determine the **total pulling force** required. If the skidway is upgraded to an **inclination of 1 in 20** and the operation is uphill, find the new pulling force. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The skidding method is performed as follows:
1. The fabricated jacket rests on **PTFE-clad skid shoes** bolted to its base.
2. A **barge** is positioned alongside the quay with its deck at the same level as the quay.
3. **Hydraulic push-pull rams** (or winches) are anchored behind the jacket on the quayside.
4. The jacket is pulled in **incremental strokes** onto the barge.
5. **Barge ballast is continuously adjusted** to keep the deck level.
6. The jacket is welded to **sea-fastening brackets** on the barge for towage.

**Factors influencing selection of skidding method:**
- Weight of the structure.
- Availability of a straight, long skidway at the yard.
- Tidal range at the quay.
- Skidway slope and friction coefficient.
- Barge availability and deck strength.
- Weather window (wind, wave, current).
- Cost and time constraints of the project.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $W = 12{,}000$ t, $\mu_k = 0.07$.

**(i) Horizontal skidway:**

Using the formula derived in Section 3.1:

$$
F_p = \mu_k \cdot W = 0.07 \times 12{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{F_p = 840 \text{ tonnes-force}}
$$

**(ii) Inclined skidway (1 in 20):**

Slope angle:

$$
\theta = \arctan\left(\dfrac{1}{20}\right) = \arctan(0.05) = 2.8624°
$$

Using the formula from Section 3.2:

$$
F_p = W \left( \sin\theta + \mu_k \cos\theta \right)
$$

$$
F_p = 12{,}000 \left( \sin 2.8624° + 0.07 \cdot \cos 2.8624° \right)
$$

$$
F_p = 12{,}000 \left( 0.04994 + 0.07 \times 0.99875 \right)
$$

$$
F_p = 12{,}000 \left( 0.04994 + 0.06991 \right)
$$

$$
F_p = 12{,}000 \times 0.11985
$$

$$
\boxed{F_p \approx 1{,}438.2 \text{ tonnes-force}}
$$

**Conclusion:** The inclined skidway requires **~71 % more pulling force** than the horizontal one due to the gravity component along the slope.

---

### **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**(a)** Explain the **float-over method** of load out. What are its **advantages and limitations**? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An integrated topside of weight **18,000 tonnes** is to be lifted by a **sheerlegs vessel using 4 slings**, each inclined at **30° from the vertical**. Calculate the **tension in each sling**. If a **dynamic amplification factor (DAF) of 1.20** is applied, find the **maximum dynamic sling tension**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Float-over method** is a load out technique in which a heavy integrated structure is transferred onto a barge that has been pre-ballasted below water. The sequence is:

1. The barge is positioned under the structure (which is supported on temporary stools or on the quay span).
2. The barge is **ballasted down** to a draft such that its deck is **below the structure's base by 2–3 m**.
3. **Winch lines** on the barge are tensioned to hold it in position.
4. The barge is **slowly deballasted**, raising the deck toward the structure.
5. The structure **descends under controlled buoyancy** and finally **seats on the barge deck**.
6. **Sea-fastening** is then installed for tow-out.

**Advantages:**
- Allows load out of **very heavy structures (up to 60,000+ tonnes)** without heavy-lift vessels.
- **Lower cost** than heavy-lift crane vessels.
- **No sling stress** on the structure (load transferred through buoyancy).
- Suitable for **integrated topsides** with limited lift points.

**Limitations:**
- Requires **very calm seas** ($H_s < 1.0$ m) during operation.
- Demands **precise ballast control** (multiple pumps, sensors, control room).
- Needs **deep quayside water** for barge submergence.
- **Longer weather-window dependency** — operations are typically tide-locked.
- Higher engineering and **risk-analysis effort** before the operation.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $W = 18{,}000$ t, $n = 4$, $\gamma = 30°$, $DAF = 1.20$.

**(i) Static sling tension:**

Using the formula from Section 3.4:

$$
T_{sling} = \dfrac{W}{n \cos\gamma} = \dfrac{18{,}000}{4 \cos 30°}
$$

$$
T_{sling} = \dfrac{18{,}000}{4 \times 0.8660} = \dfrac{18{,}000}{3.4641}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{sling} \approx 5{,}196.2 \text{ tonnes-force}}
$$

**(ii) Dynamic sling tension:**

$$
T_{dyn} = DAF \times T_{sling} = 1.20 \times 5{,}196.2
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{dyn} \approx 6{,}235.4 \text{ tonnes-force}}
$$

**Conclusion:** The maximum dynamic sling tension of **6,235.4 tonnes** governs the **pad-eye and sling design**. Engineers must ensure pad-eyes, shackles, and the sheerlegs hook block are rated to at least **6,500 tonnes** (with safety factor).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most commonly lose marks in Module 4 — Load Out questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert tonnes-force to Newtons** (or vice versa) — KTU examiners will deduct **½ to 1 mark** if units are inconsistent. Always state: $W = 18{,}000$ t = $18{,}000 \times 9.81$ kN = 176,580 kN if SI units are used.
> 2. **Using the wrong friction angle or skidway angle** — Many students confuse "1 in 20" with 20°; it actually means $\arctan(1/20) = 2.86°$. KTU examiners explicitly test this trap.
> 3. **Skipping the dynamic amplification factor (DAF)** in sling lift problems — at least **1 mark** is reserved for DAF inclusion.
> 4. **Not mentioning ballast control** in any load out descriptive answer — **2–3 marks** are reserved for ballast / stability discussion.
> 5. **Confusing "load out" with "lift" or "transport"** — examiners expect the student to identify load out as the **yard-to-barge** transfer, not the offshore installation lift.
> 6. **Omitting diagrams in descriptive answers** — KTU 2024 scheme requires **neat sketches**; missing diagrams = **1–2 marks lost** even if the text is perfect.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Load Out Methods (Module 4)**

- ☐ **Load out** = transfer of structure from **yard → barge** (NOT yard → offshore directly).
- ☐ **Six main methods:** Skidding, Heavy-lift / Sling, Float-over, Ro-Ro, Direct Lift, Self-float.
- ☐ **Skidding force** (horizontal): $F_p = \mu W$. (PTFE → $\mu \approx 0.05$–$0.10$).
- ☐ **Skidding force** (inclined): $F_p = W(\sin\theta + \mu\cos\theta)$.
- ☐ **Slope of 1 in 20** = $\arctan(0.05) = 2.86°$, NOT 20°.
- ☐ **Sling tension** (n-point lift): $T = W / (n\cos\gamma)$; keep $\gamma \leq 30°$.
- ☐ **DAF = 1.10 – 1.30** is mandatory for any dynamic lift calculation.
- ☐ **Barge draft:** $T = W / (\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot A_w)$; $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³.
- ☐ **Stability:** $GM = KB + BM - KG \geq 0.30$ m.
- ☐ **Ballast control** is the operational lifeline — controls **draft, trim, GM**.
- ☐ **Float-over** requires $H_s < 1.0$ m and deep quayside.
- ☐ **Sea-fastening** is mandatory before the barge is released for tow.
- ☐ **Codes governing load out:** API RP 2A, DNV-OS-H205, ISO 19901-6, ABS Rules.
- ☐ **Weather window** for any load out is **Hs < 1.5 m, wind < 15 m/s, current < 1.0 m/s**.
- ☐ Always **draw a labelled sketch** in any descriptive answer.
- ☐ Always **state units explicitly** in every numerical answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transportation Facilities and Methods for Different Types of Offshore Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST722 - FUNDAMENTALS OF OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transportation Facilities and Methods for Different Types of Offshore Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transportation of Offshore Structures** refers to the engineered logistics operation of moving fabricated offshore structural assemblies — such as fixed jackets, gravity-based structures (GBS), semi-submersibles, TLPs (Tension Leg Platforms), SPARs, FPSOs (Floating Production Storage and Offloading), and subsea modules — from onshore fabrication yards to the offshore installation site. The transport may be executed via **dry tow** (on the deck of a cargo barge or heavy-lift vessel) or **wet tow** (the structure is floated, semi-submerged, or self-propelled through the water column).

The selection of a transportation method is governed by the **structural weight, geometric size, draft limitations, sea-route depth, metocean conditions, and installation philosophy** adopted for the field development.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of moving a fully built house from a factory to a remote island. You wouldn't just put it on a small truck — you would carefully select between a flatbed truck (dry tow), floating it like a barge (wet tow), or even driving a mobile house there on its own wheels (self-propelled). Offshore, the "house" is the platform, the "truck" is a heavy-lift vessel or barge, and the "highway" is the open ocean. The choice depends on how big, how heavy, and how delicate the platform is.

Just as moving a fragile, oversized house requires special permits, route surveys, weather windows, and tugboats for safety — offshore transport demands **route bathymetry surveys, weather forecasting, navigation buoys, escort tugs, and stability monitoring**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Values (Engineering Memoranda):**
> - **Density of sea water:** **ρ_sw = 1025 kg/m³** (typical North Sea / Arabian Sea)
> - **Acceleration due to gravity:** **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - **Standard tow speed range for offshore structures:** **2 – 6 knots** (≈ **1.03 – 3.09 m/s**)
> - **Maximum permissible roll angle during dry tow:** typically **≤ 5°–10°** depending on structural tolerance
> - **Significant wave height operational limit (tow):** generally **Hs ≤ 1.5 – 2.5 m**

## 1.4 Classification of Offshore Structures Requiring Transportation

| S.No | Structure Type | Typical Mass Range | Transport Mode |
|------|---------------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Fixed Steel Jacket | 2,000 – 25,000 t | Wet tow (upright/inverted) / Barge |
| 2 | Jack-up Rig | 10,000 – 30,000 t | Self-propelled / Heavy-lift |
| 3 | Gravity Based Structure (GBS) | 50,000 – 500,000 t | Wet tow (semi-submerged) |
| 4 | Semi-submersible | 10,000 – 60,000 t | Wet tow (semi-submerged) |
| 5 | Tension Leg Platform (TLP) | 20,000 – 60,000 t | Wet tow (upright) |
| 6 | SPAR | 10,000 – 70,000 t | Wet tow (horizontal/upright) |
| 7 | FPSO | 30,000 – 300,000 t (lightship) | Self-propelled / Wet tow |
| 8 | Subsea Manifold / Module | 50 – 2,000 t | Dry tow on barge |
| 9 | Mooring Systems | Variable | Dry tow on reel/barge |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Philosophical "Why" and "How" of Transportation Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> The **Why**: Offshore structures are built onshore because fabrication yards offer controlled environments, skilled labour, equipment accessibility, and quality control. Once complete, the structure must be physically relocated to the offshore field — often **hundreds to thousands of nautical miles** away.
>
> The **How**: Engineers exploit **Archimedes' Principle of Buoyancy**, **hydrodynamic stability**, and **controlled ballasting** to either float the structure to site (wet tow) or place it on a purpose-built marine vessel (dry tow).

## 2.2 Transportation Method Decision Tree (Theoretical Logic Flow)

**Step 1 — Assess Weight and Geometry**
- If mass > **100,000 t** → preference is **wet tow** (self-buoyancy is exploited).
- If geometry is long/fragile (e.g., jacket, SPAR) → **wet tow horizontal** or **dry tow on barge**.

**Step 2 — Assess Sea Route Constraints**
- Shallow water route → may require **semi-submersible heavy-lift vessel** that can submerge to load/launch.
- Deep unrestricted ocean → wide flexibility — **ocean-going barges, semi-submersibles, tugs** all viable.

**Step 3 — Assess Metocean Sensitivity**
- For long ocean tows, structures with low metacentric height (GM) are vulnerable → **ballast plan** and **towing bridle** geometry must be engineered to ensure transverse stability.

**Step 4 — Select Marine Spread**
- **Cargo barges** (flat-bottom, non-propelled)
- **Heavy-lift vessels** (e.g., *Happy*-class, *Type 0* semi-submersibles)
- **Self-propelled semi-submersibles** (e.g., *Blue Marlin*, *Black Marlin*)
- **Tugboats** (for wet tow, typically 2–4 tugs for ocean tow)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are routinely tested in KTU University Examinations under Module 4. Master the units and the physical meaning of each term.

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|-----------------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\Delta = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla$ | Displacement = weight of displaced seawater (Archimedes) | N or tonnes-force |
| 2 | $\nabla = \dfrac{W}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g}$ | Submerged volume required to float a given weight W | m³ |
| 3 | $T = \dfrac{\nabla}{A_{wp}}$ | Mean draft (T) of a buoyant body (V / waterplane area) | m |
| 4 | $R_{T} = R_{F} + R_{W} + R_{AW}$ | Total tow resistance (friction + wave-making + appendage) | kN |
| 5 | $F_{n} = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot L_{WL}}}$ | Froude number for tow stability | dimensionless |
| 6 | $BHP_{tug} = \dfrac{R_{T} \cdot V}{\eta_{prop}}$ | Towing Brake Horsepower required (η ≈ 0.6) | kW |
| 7 | $GM = KB + BM - KG$ | Metacentric height (transverse stability during tow) | m |
| 8 | $BM = \dfrac{I_{T}}{\nabla}$ | Transverse BM where I_T = transverse waterplane 2nd moment | m |
| 9 | $P_{c} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at submerged depth h | Pa |
| 10 | $\sigma_{bending} = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I}$ | Maximum bending stress during hogging/sagging (dry tow) | Pa |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These equations are used by naval architects and offshore transport engineers to (i) determine the **minimum number of tugs**, (ii) size the **ballast pumps** for submergence, (iii) check **girder stresses** of the transport barge, and (iv) design the **towing bridle** to avoid point-load failure of the structure.

## 2.4 Types of Transportation Methods — Detailed Discussion

### 2.4.1 Dry Tow (On Barge or Heavy-Lift Vessel)
- **Used for:** Jackets, modules, subsea equipment, jackets of moderate size, topsides, piles.
- **Mechanism:** The structure is loaded onto a flat-deck cargo barge (via **skidding, lifting by crane, or float-over**) and secured with **sea-fastenings** (welded or bolted restraints).
- **Sea-fastening design** must withstand inertial loads from roll, pitch, heave, and surge.

### 2.4.2 Wet Tow — Upright (Self-Floating)
- **Used for:** Semi-submersibles, TLPs, FPSOs, GBS, jack-ups in transit (legs raised).
- **Mechanism:** The structure is **ballasted to a controlled draft**, made buoyant, and towed by tugs via a **towing bridle** attached to a **tow point** (often an internal padeye).
- **Critical Concern:** Transverse stability — GM must be positive and adequate (typically **GM ≥ 1.0–3.0 m** for ocean tow).

### 2.4.3 Wet Tow — Horizontal (Towing on Side)
- **Used for:** SPARs, long slender jackets, very long subsea structures.
- **Mechanism:** The structure is partially flooded to lie horizontally, and towed beam-first or stern-first. Often a **towing dolphin** is used to provide the attachment point and dampen yaw.

### 2.4.4 Semi-Submerged Heavy-Lift Transport
- **Vessels:** *Blue Marlin*, *Black Marlin*, *H-851* (Dockwise).
- **Mechanism:** The vessel submerges its deck by ballasting, the cargo is floated over, and the vessel de-ballasts to lift the cargo out of the water.
- **Limitation:** Depth of water over the deck (typically **11–15 m**) limits cargo size.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Submerged Volume & Draft for a Buoyant Offshore Structure (Wet Tow)

This derivation is the **most frequently asked derivation in KTU Module 4** for transport-related problems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup (Standard KTU-style):** A steel jacket weighs **W = 12,000 tonnes (metric)** in air. During wet tow it must float with a minimum freeboard. Determine (a) the **submerged volume ∇**, (b) the **mean draft T** assuming a uniform waterplane area **A_wp = 600 m²**, and (c) verify if a tow draft of 12 m is feasible for a route of **18 m** water depth.

### Step 1 — Convert Weight to Force
We begin by converting metric tonnes to Newtons using the standard relation **1 tonne-force = 1000 × g Newtons**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= 12{,}000 \; \text{t} \times 1000 \; \text{kg/t} \times 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^{2} \\
  &= 1.1772 \times 10^{8} \; \text{N} \\
  &= 117.72 \; \text{MN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Archimedes' principle requires force units, not mass. We retain both forms for clarity in subsequent steps.

### Step 2 — Apply Archimedes' Principle
The weight of water displaced must equal the structure's weight for equilibrium floating.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla \\
\nabla &= \dfrac{W}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values
Use $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \; \text{kg/m}^{3}$ and $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^{2}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla &= \dfrac{1.1772 \times 10^{8}}{1025 \times 9.81} \\
       &= \dfrac{1.1772 \times 10^{8}}{10{,}055.25} \\
       &= 11{,}707.1 \; \text{m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** **∇ ≈ 11,707 m³** of the jacket must be submerged during tow.

### Step 4 — Calculate Mean Draft
Using $T = \nabla / A_{wp}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \dfrac{11{,}707.1}{600} \\
  &= 19.51 \; \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Verdict:** A mean draft of **19.51 m** is **NOT feasible** for a route of **18 m water depth** — under-keel clearance (UKC) would be negative, causing grounding. Engineers would either select a deeper route, sub-divide the jacket for tow, or use **dry tow on a heavy-lift barge**.

### Step 5 — Recompute Feasible Draft for 18 m Channel
If the route is fixed at 18 m, recompute allowed ∇ for a UKC of say 2 m → allowable draft = 16 m.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{allow} &= T_{allow} \times A_{wp} = 16 \times 600 = 9600 \; \text{m}^{3} \\
W_{allow} &= \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot \nabla_{allow} \\
          &= 1025 \times 9.81 \times 9600 \\
          &= 9.65 \times 10^{7} \; \text{N} \\
          &= 9{,}840 \; \text{t}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Decision:** Only **9,840 tonnes** may be towed through this route in a single piece — the remaining **2,160 tonnes** must be transported separately (e.g., topsides on a heavy-lift vessel or sub-modules on supply barges).

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: Towing Resistance & Tug Power

### Step 1 — Empirical Form for Calm-Water Tow Resistance
For large-volume slow-moving offshore structures, an accepted empirical model is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{T} &= R_{F} + R_{W} + R_{AW} \\
      &= \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, C_{F} \, S \, V^{2} \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, C_{W} \, A_{F} \, V^{2} \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2} \, \rho \, C_{AW} \, A_{AW} \, V^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $C_F$ = frictional resistance coefficient (ITTC: $C_F = 0.075 / (\log R_n - 2)^2$)
- $C_W$ = wave-making coefficient (≈ 0.5 to 1.5 for slow speeds)
- $C_{AW}$ = appendage resistance coefficient
- $S$ = wetted surface area (m²)
- $A_F$ = frontal area (m²)
- $A_{AW}$ = appendage area (m²)
- $V$ = tow speed (m/s)

### Step 2 — Worked Numerical Example (Standard KTU Format)
> **Given:** A GBS is towed at **V = 4 knots = 2.057 m/s**. Wetted surface **S = 6,000 m²**, frontal area **A_F = 200 m²**, appendage area **A_{AW} = 50 m²**, frictional coefficient $C_F = 0.0018$, wave-making $C_W = 1.0$, appendage $C_{AW} = 1.2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{F} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 0.0018 \times 6000 \times (2.057)^{2} \\
      &= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 0.0018 \times 6000 \times 4.231 \\
      &= 23{,}402 \; \text{N} \; \approx 23.4 \; \text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{W} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 1.0 \times 200 \times (2.057)^{2} \\
      &= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 200 \times 4.231 \\
      &= 433{,}728 \; \text{N} \; \approx 433.7 \; \text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{AW} &= \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1025 \times 1.2 \times 50 \times (2.057)^{2} \\
       &= 0.5 \times 1025 \times 60 \times 4.231 \\
       &= 130{,}118 \; \text{N} \; \approx 130.1 \; \text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{T} &= 23.4 + 433.7 + 130.1 \\
      &= 587.2 \; \text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Required Tug Bollard Pull and BHP
Assume propulsive efficiency $\eta = 0.60$ and tow speed $V = 2.057$ m/s.

$$
\begin{aligned}
EHP &= R_{T} \times V = 587.2 \times 10^{3} \times 2.057 \\
    &= 1.208 \times 10^{6} \; \text{W} \\
BHP &= \dfrac{EHP}{\eta} = \dfrac{1.208 \times 10^{6}}{0.60} \\
    &= 2.013 \times 10^{6} \; \text{W} \;=\; 2{,}013 \; \text{kW} \\
    &\approx 2{,}700 \; \text{BHP} \quad (\text{since 1 BHP} \approx 0.746 \text{ kW})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Two ocean-going tugs of **~ 80-tonne bollard pull** each are typically specified for a tow of this magnitude, allowing for redundancy if one tug fails mid-ocean.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III: Transverse Stability Check During Wet Tow (GM Calculation)

### Step 1 — Recall the Metacentric Formula
$$
\begin{aligned}
GM &= KB + BM - KG \\
BM &= \dfrac{I_{T}}{\nabla}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Components (Example)
- $\nabla = 11{,}707 \; \text{m}^{3}$ (from Section 3.1)
- Waterplane area $A_{wp} = 600 \; \text{m}^{2}$
- Assuming rectangular waterplane of length $L = 60 \; \text{m}$, beam $B = 10 \; \text{m}$:
- $I_{T} = \dfrac{L \cdot B^{3}}{12} = \dfrac{60 \times 10^{3}}{12} = 5{,}000 \; \text{m}^{4}$
- $KB = T/2 = 19.51/2 = 9.755 \; \text{m}$ (assuming uniform block)
- $KG = 14 \; \text{m}$ (centre of gravity above keel)

$$
\begin{aligned}
BM &= \dfrac{5{,}000}{11{,}707} = 0.427 \; \text{m} \\
GM &= 9.755 + 0.427 - 14 \\
   &= -3.82 \; \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **GM is NEGATIVE → CAPSIZE RISK!** A negative metacentric height means the structure is **unstable in roll** and will capsize. Engineers must either (a) lower KG by adding low-lying ballast, (b) increase BM by fitting **stability pontoons**, or (c) re-design the geometry.

### Step 3 — Countermeasure: Stability Pontoons
Adding **stability pontoons** of effective transverse waterplane second moment $I_{T,p} = 25{,}000 \; \text{m}^{4}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
BM_{new} &= \dfrac{5{,}000 + 25{,}000}{11{,}707} = 2.56 \; \text{m} \\
GM_{new} &= 9.755 + 2.56 - 14 = -1.69 \; \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Still negative — add **bottom ballast** of 3 m lowering KG to 11 m:

$$
\begin{aligned}
GM_{final} &= 9.755 + 2.56 - 11 = 1.32 \; \text{m} \;\; \textbf{ACCEPTABLE}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A positive GM of 1.32 m ensures safe ocean tow, satisfying the IMO and classification society criteria (e.g., DNV, ABS) of **GM ≥ 0.15 m** for ocean transit, and ideally **GM ≥ 1.0 m** for heavy weather tow.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Transportation Method Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Offshore Structure Type Identification] --> B{Weight Assessment}
    B -->|Greater than 100000 t| C[Wet Tow Self Buoyant]
    B -->|Less than 100000 t| D{Geometry Check}
    D -->|Long and Slender| E[Horizontal Wet Tow]
    D -->|Compact and Modular| F[Dry Tow on Barge]
    D -->|Submerged Stability| G[Semi Submerged Transport]
    C --> H[Route Survey and Bathymetry]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Metocean Window Selection]
    I --> J[Stability Analysis GM Check]
    J --> K[Tug Selection and Bridle Design]
    K --> L[Sea Fastening Design for Dry Tow]
    L --> M[Execution Phase Onward Transit]
    M --> N[Site Installation and Hook Up]
```

**Figure 1: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Offshore Transportation Decision Flow** (rendered as a Mermaid process flowchart since physical schematics of multi-vessel spreads are impractical in mermaid syntax).

## 4.2 Mermaid Architecture: Vessel-Type Selection Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Jacket Fixed Platform] --> B1[Method A Upright Wet Tow or Barge]
    A2[Semi Submersible] --> B2[Method B Semi Submerged Wet Tow]
    A3[TLP] --> B3[Method C Upright Wet Tow with Tethers Stowed]
    A4[SPAR] --> B4[Method D Horizontal Wet Tow]
    A5[FPSO] --> B5[Method E Self Propelled or Wet Tow]
    A6[GBS] --> B6[Method F Semi Submerged Heavy Lift]
    A7[Subsea Manifold] --> B7[Method G Dry Tow on Cargo Barge]
    A8[Topside Module] --> B8[Method H Heavy Lift Vessel Float Over]
```

**Figure 2: Structure Type to Transport Method Mapping** (KTU high-yield mapping table in graphical form).

## 4.3 Schematic Description: Wet Tow Geometry (Textual Schematic)

For a clear student-friendly schematic, the wet tow of a **TLP** is described as follows:

```
                     TOW TUG (LEAD)
                          |
                          |  TOWING BRIDLE
                          |
       ===================T====================   ← SEA SURFACE
        |  PONTOON 1      |       PONTOON 2 |
        |=================|=================|
                \           |           /
                 \          |          /
                  \         |         /
                   \________|________/
                          TLP HULL
                          (KEEL)
                          |
                          |  TETHERS (STOWED IN INTERNAL CAROUSELS)
                          |
                  [DAMPING PLATES BELOW]
```

**Figure 3: Schematic of TLP Under Tow — Upright Configuration** (rendered textually as Mermaid cannot faithfully draw 3D hulls with stability notations).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The two pontoons provide a **wide waterplane area**, giving large **I_T** and hence positive **BM**, ensuring roll stability during the long ocean tow. Internal **tether carousels** store the tethers safely to prevent tangling and impact loads.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Wet Tow" and "Dry Tow" of Offshore Structures. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

> **Model Answer:**
> **Wet Tow:** A transportation method in which the offshore structure is floated and buoyantly supported by water, and is pulled through the ocean by tugs. The structure is partially or fully submerged and uses **Archimedean buoyancy** to carry its own weight. Examples: semi-submersible ocean tow, TLP tow, FPSO transit.
>
> **Dry Tow:** A transportation method in which the offshore structure is fully supported **above the waterline** on the deck of a cargo barge or heavy-lift vessel, with sea-fastenings securing it against relative motion. Examples: jacket transport on flat-top barge, topside module on heavy-lift ship.

### Question 2: State any **three** factors that govern the selection of a transportation method for an offshore structure. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

> **Model Answer:**
> 1. **Weight and Geometry of Structure** — heavier structures favour wet tow; delicate/long structures may need horizontal tow or barge.
> 2. **Route Bathymetry and Water Depth** — limits on under-keel clearance determine if wet tow draft is feasible.
> 3. **Metocean Conditions (wave height, current, wind)** along the tow route — affects voyage duration and weather window.
> 4. *Optional* **Availability of fabrication yard quay depth** and **tug availability**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Format)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 — Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the various **methods of transportation** used for different types of offshore structures, with a specific focus on **wet tow** methods for semi-submersibles, TLPs, and SPARs. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A steel jacket weighs **W = 18,000 tonnes** in air. The waterplane area during tow is **A_wp = 800 m²** and the route has a minimum water depth of **22 m**. Calculate:
- (i) the **submerged volume** ∇ required
- (ii) the **mean draft** T
- (iii) whether the tow is feasible if a **2 m under-keel clearance** is mandated. If not feasible, suggest **two** practical engineering alternatives. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

### (a) Wet Tow Methods — Detailed Explanation

> **For Semi-Submersibles (e.g., Drilling Semis):**
> The semi-submersible is towed in its **operational configuration** at a transit draft of typically **8–10 m**. Two or more ocean-going tugs are connected via a **towing bridle** to a central padeye. Ballast is arranged to maintain positive GM and to keep the **moonpool** clear of water entry. The lower hulls and pontoons provide inherent roll stability.

> **For Tension Leg Platforms (TLPs):**
> TLPs are towed **upright** in a *pre-installation* state. The tethers are stored in **internal carousels** within the hull to prevent impact loads and to control payload distribution. Heavy **temporary buoyancy tanks** may be added to the corners to compensate for the missing tendon tension (which normally contributes to vertical support). Tow draft is typically **20–35 m** for large deepwater TLPs. **Damping plates** below the hull reduce vortex-induced motion during the long tow.

> **For SPAR Platforms:**
> SPARs are unusually long cylindrical structures (often **> 200 m** in length) and are therefore **towed horizontally** (on their side) to reduce tow draft and avoid excessive bending moments. Once on site, the SPAR is **upended** by controlled flooding of the buoyancy tanks — the **upending** operation is one of the most critical lifts in offshore engineering.

### (b) Numerical Solution

**Given:** $W = 18{,}000 \; \text{t}$, $A_{wp} = 800 \; \text{m}^{2}$, $h_{route} = 22 \; \text{m}$, UKC = 2 m.

**Step (i) — Submerged Volume**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= 18{,}000 \times 1000 \times 9.81 = 1.7658 \times 10^{8} \; \text{N} \\
\nabla &= \dfrac{W}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g} = \dfrac{1.7658 \times 10^{8}}{1025 \times 9.81} \\
       &= \dfrac{1.7658 \times 10^{8}}{10{,}055.25} \\
       &= 17{,}561 \; \text{m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** **∇ = 17,561 m³**

**Step (ii) — Mean Draft**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \dfrac{\nabla}{A_{wp}} = \dfrac{17{,}561}{800} = 21.95 \; \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** **T ≈ 21.95 m**

**Step (iii) — Feasibility Check**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Allowable draft} &= h_{route} - UKC = 22 - 2 = 20 \; \text{m} \\
T_{computed} &= 21.95 \; \text{m} > 20 \; \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Verdict:** **NOT FEASIBLE.** Tow draft exceeds allowable draft by **1.95 m**.

**Two Practical Engineering Alternatives:**
1. **Modularize** the jacket — split the structure into 2 or 3 sub-modules and tow them separately on heavy-lift barges (dry tow), then assemble offshore using a **floatover** operation.
2. **Use a semi-submersible heavy-lift vessel** (e.g., *Blue Marlin*-class) to dry-tow the jacket at zero draft, eliminating depth constraints.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 4]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, describe the **float-over installation method** for topside modules on offshore jackets. State its **advantages** and **limitations** compared to **crane lift installation**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A semi-submersible platform weighing **25,000 tonnes** is to be wet-towed across **1500 nautical miles** of open ocean. The calm-water tow resistance is **R_T = 600 kN** at the design speed. Determine:
- (i) the **Effective Horsepower (EHP)** required at tow speed **V = 4.5 knots**
- (ii) the **Brake Horsepower (BHP)** assuming overall propulsive efficiency **η = 0.55**
- (iii) the **number of ocean tugs** required, given each tug has a continuous bollard pull of **80 tonnes-force**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

### (a) Float-Over Installation Method

> **Procedure:** The topside module is transported on a **floatover barge** (or a **barge-shaped vessel**) to the site. Once positioned adjacent to the already-installed jacket, the **ballastable support legs** of the barge engage with the **jacket legs** (or pre-installed **docking guides / receiver beams**). The barge is then **de-ballasted**, lowering itself below the topside, transferring the load of the topside entirely onto the jacket's **support beams**. The barge is then re-floated and withdrawn, leaving the topside permanently installed on the jacket.

> **Advantages over Crane Lift:**
> - No height/boom limitation — can install **very heavy topsides** (> 30,000 t) that no crane can lift.
> - Lower sensitivity to crane vessel availability and weather window for the lift.
> - Cost-effective for **mega-topsides** in deep water.

> **Limitations:**
> - Requires **precise alignment** between barge and jacket (often ±100 mm tolerance).
> - Limited to **calm weather windows** for the engagement and load-transfer phases.
> - Jacket must be **designed for floatover loads** (e.g., docking guides, leg-through receptacles).
> - Not suitable for all jacket geometries.

### (b) Numerical Solution

**Given:** $R_T = 600 \; \text{kN}$, $V = 4.5 \; \text{knots}$, $\eta = 0.55$, bollard pull per tug = 80 t.

**Convert tow speed to SI:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= 4.5 \times 0.5144 = 2.315 \; \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (i) — EHP**

$$
\begin{aligned}
EHP &= R_T \times V \\
    &= 600{,}000 \times 2.315 \\
    &= 1.389 \times 10^{6} \; \text{W} \\
    &= 1{,}389 \; \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** **EHP = 1,389 kW**

**Step (ii) — BHP**

$$
\begin{aligned}
BHP &= \dfrac{EHP}{\eta} = \dfrac{1{,}389}{0.55} \\
    &= 2{,}525 \; \text{kW} \;\; \text{(or} \;\; \dfrac{2525}{0.746} = 3{,}385 \; \text{BHP)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** **BHP ≈ 2,525 kW ≈ 3,385 BHP**

**Step (iii) — Number of Tugs**

Total tow force required = $R_T$ = 600 kN = **61.2 tonnes-force** (since 1 tonne-force = 9.81 kN, hence 600/9.81 ≈ 61.2 t).

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{tugs} &= \dfrac{61.2}{80} = 0.77 \;\;\text{(theoretical minimum)}
\end{aligned}
$$

However, **ocean-towing practice** mandates at least **two tugs** (one lead, one stern) for **redundancy and steering control** during the 1500-nautical-mile voyage. Some operators specify **three tugs** for additional safety in heavy weather.

> **Result:** **Minimum 2 tugs; recommended 3 tugs for safety/redundancy.**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing metric tonnes (t) and SI force (N) without conversion. **Always show the conversion $W = m \times g$ explicitly.**
> 2. **Forgetting UKC (Under-Keel Clearance):** Route depth is NOT the allowable draft. The allowable draft is **(route depth − UKC − tidal/swell allowance)**.
> 3. **Skipping the Stability (GM) Check:** Many students compute draft but **forget that the structure must be stable** (GM > 0) during the tow. This is a **favourite KTU 14-mark sub-question**.
> 4. **Confusing Froude Number and Reynolds Number:** They are both dimensionless but governed by different physical phenomena. KTU examiners love testing this distinction.
> 5. **Neglecting Appendage Resistance:** Towing a structure with appendages (risers, tethers stowed externally) adds $R_{AW}$ — students often drop this term.
> 6. **No Mention of Weather Window:** A 14-mark answer on ocean tow that does not mention **Hs limits, voyage duration, and metocean forecasting** is graded poorly.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD RAPID REVISION — Module 4: Transportation Facilities & Methods**

- **Wet Tow** = structure floats itself; **Dry Tow** = structure rides on a barge/deck.
- **Archimedes' Principle:** $\nabla = W / (\rho_{sw} \cdot g)$ — fundamental to all draft calculations.
- **Density of seawater:** $\rho_{sw} = 1025 \; \text{kg/m}^{3}$ — use this unless the problem specifies otherwise.
- **Allowable draft** = (Route Depth) − (Under-Keel Clearance, UKC) − (Tidal allowance).
- **GM must be POSITIVE** for safe ocean tow — minimum **GM ≥ 0.15 m** (class rules), preferred **GM ≥ 1.0 m**.
- **Tow Resistance:** $R_T = R_F + R_W + R_{AW}$ (friction + wave + appendage).
- **Froude Number** controls wave-making resistance; **Reynolds Number** controls frictional resistance.
- **BHP** = EHP / η — η is **propulsive + mechanical efficiency combined** (≈ 0.55–0.65).
- **At least 2 ocean tugs** required for any open-sea tow, regardless of theoretical calculation.
- **Jackets** → wet tow upright (shallow) or dry tow on barge.
- **Semi-submersibles** → wet tow in operational config; uses pontoons for stability.
- **TLPs** → wet tow upright with tethers in carousels.
- **SPARs** → wet tow **horizontally**; upended on site.
- **FPSOs** → self-propelled OR wet tow; conversion hulls are inherently strong.
- **GBS** → wet tow with partial submergence; very heavy (often > 100,000 t).
- **Subsea modules** → dry tow on flat-top cargo barge.
- **Topside modules** → **float-over installation** (preferred for > 30,000 t modules) or heavy-lift crane.
- **Sea-fastenings** must withstand roll, pitch, heave, surge inertial loads during dry tow.
- **Transportation codes & standards:** DNV, ABS, Lloyds Register, API RP 2SK, IMO MODU Code.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Installation Methods for Different Offshore Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OENST722 - FUNDAMENTALS OF OFFSHORE STRUCTURES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Installation Methods for Different Offshore Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Offshore Installation Methods?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Offshore installation methods* refer to the engineered procedures, marine operations, and heavy-lift techniques employed to transport, position, and secure offshore structures (fixed, floating, or subsea) at their designated operational locations in the sea. These methods integrate naval architecture, marine engineering, hydrodynamics, and structural mechanics to safely deploy structures ranging from small subsea manifolds to massive fixed jackets weighing over **30,000 tonnes**.

The discipline of offshore installation sits at the intersection of **structural engineering**, **marine operations**, and **ocean environmental science**. Every installation method must be selected based on the structure's geometry, mass, water depth, availability of marine spread (vessels, cranes), and metocean conditions (waves, wind, currents).

### 1.2 Classification of Offshore Structures (Drives Installation Logic)

Before studying *how* structures are installed, we must know *what* is being installed. The taxonomy below governs the installation philosophy:

| Structure Family | Examples | Primary Installation Driver |
|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Steel Structures** | Steel jackets, compliant towers | Launch / Lift / Float-over |
| **Fixed Concrete Structures** | Concrete Gravity Base Structures (GBS) | Controlled flooding / Buoyancy tanks |
| **Floating Production Systems** | FPSO, Semi-submersibles, TLP, Spar | Wet tow or Dry tow + Mooring |
| **Subsea Structures** | Manifolds, templates, PLETs | Crane lowering from vessels |
| **Long Linear Elements** | Pipelines, umbilicals, flowlines | S-lay, J-lay, Reel-lay |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why installation method depends on structure type:**
> A steel jacket is *rigid, heavy, and hull-like* — it can be floated and launched like a ship. A GBS is *massive and concrete* — it is too heavy to lift, so it relies on **controlled buoyancy**. A semi-submersible is *buoyant by design* — it tows itself. A subsea manifold is *small but heavy* — it is simply lifted and lowered.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Building Block" Marine World

Think of offshore installation as **assembling IKEA furniture in a swimming pool**:

1. The **fabrication yard** is your workshop — you build everything on land.
2. The **transport phase** is carrying the box to the pool (sea transport).
3. The **installation phase** is lowering the piece into the water without dropping, breaking, or drowning it.
4. The **hook-up & commissioning** is the final wiring.

Different "pieces of furniture" require different "tools" — a *small shelf* (subsea manifold) just needs a hand, but a *massive wardrobe* (jacket platform) needs cranes, barges, and tugboats working in concert.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Density of seawater (ρ_sw):** **1025 kg/m³** (standard offshore value)
> - **Acceleration due to gravity (g):** **9.81 m/s²**
> - **Weight density of seawater (γ_sw):** **10.05 kN/m³**
> - **Significant wave height during installation:** typically limited to **Hs ≤ 1.5 m**
> - **Maximum allowable tilt during mating:** **0.5° to 1.0°**
> - **Typical weather window:** **24 to 72 hours** of favourable forecast

### 1.5 Key Engineering Decisions in Installation

When a project engineer is asked *"Which installation method?"*, they evaluate:

- **Mass and centre of gravity** of the structure
- **Water depth** at the installation site
- **Availability of heavy-lift crane vessels** (e.g., vessels rated > 10,000 tonnes)
- **Onshore fabrication yard proximity to deep-water quay**
- **Metocean operability windows** for the field
- **Soil conditions** for foundation compatibility (skirt penetration, pile driving)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fixed Steel Jacket Installation — The Workhorse Method

#### 2.1.1 Launch Installation Method

The jacket is fabricated horizontally on a **launching barge** at a fabrication yard. It is then **rotated to vertical and slid off the barge** into the water — much like a ship launching. The jacket floats due to its internal buoyancy compartments, is then upended, and towed to site.

**Phases of Launch:**
1. Load-out onto launching barge
2. Ballasting to achieve launch trim
3. End-launching (rotation about the stern rocker arm)
4. Floating check and stability verification
5. Wet tow to location
6. Upending using derrick barge cranes
7. Pile installation through jacket legs

#### 2.1.2 Lift Installation Method

When the jacket is small enough (typically < 15,000 tonnes for available crane vessels) or when water depth at the launch site is insufficient, **direct lift** by a heavy-lift crane vessel is used. The jacket is fabricated to near-final height and lifted as a single piece.

**Crane selection criteria:**
- Hook height must exceed jacket top elevation plus clearance
- Lift capacity must exceed jacket weight **plus dynamic amplification factor (DAF)** of 1.25 to 1.5

#### 2.1.3 Float-over Installation Method

Used for **very large jackets** (often 20,000–40,000 tonnes) where no crane is large enough. The jacket is mounted on a **transport barge**, towed between the legs of a pre-installed substructure, and then the barge is deballasted to lower the jacket onto its foundations.

### 2.2 Concrete Gravity Base Structure (GBS) Installation

A GBS is too heavy to lift (often > 100,000 tonnes). It is built in a **deep dry-dock or fjord**, then:
1. **Flooded** for float-up
2. **Wet-towed** in submerged or semi-submerged condition
3. **Ballasted down** at site by controlled seawater flooding of base compartments
4. **Grouted/foundated** on seabed preparation

The GBS depends entirely on **Archimedes' buoyancy principle** to move from dock to seabed.

### 2.3 Floating Production Systems Installation

#### 2.3.1 Semi-submersibles
- Built in dry-dock, floated out, mated with modules offshore
- **Wet-towed** between sites using tugboats
- Held in position by **spread mooring** (chain-wire-chain) or **DP** (Dynamic Positioning)

#### 2.3.2 FPSO (Floating Production Storage & Offloading)
- Hull is either **new-build** (similar to a tanker) or **converted tanker**
- Towed to site, moored (typically **weathervaning** turret mooring or SALM)
- Topsides integrated via **float-over** or **heavy lift**

#### 2.3.3 TLP (Tension Leg Platform)
- Hull towed to site
- **Tethers (vertical tension legs)** pre-installed on seabed templates
- Hull deballasted to **pull-down** and tension the tethers
- Each tether under tension typically **10,000–20,000 kN**

#### 2.3.4 Spar
- Very deep draft cylinder (often **150–200 m**)
- **Wet-towed horizontally** in a specially designed catamaran support vessel (because the spar would not be stable vertically in transit)
- **Upended** at site by controlled flooding of the lower tanks
- Moored with semi-taut or taut spread mooring lines

### 2.4 Subsea Structure Installation

Subsea structures (manifolds, templates, PLETs, PLEMs) are installed by:
- **Crane lowering** from a subsea construction vessel (CSV) or dive support vessel (DSV)
- Positioned via **acoustic positioning**, **DGPS**, and **ROV verification**
- Lowered through the splash zone using **splash zone dampers** to control heave-induced slamming forces

### 2.5 Pipeline Installation Methods

| Method | Description | Typical Depth |
|---|---|---|
| **S-lay** | Pipeline welded on vessel, laid over stern in S-curve | Shallow to medium (< 1000 m) |
| **J-lay** | Pipeline welded vertically on tower, lowered almost vertically | Deep water (> 500 m) |
| **Reel-lay** | Pipeline pre-welded on land, spooled onto reel, unspooled at sea | Medium depth |
| **Tow-in** | Pipeline assembled onshore, towed as long string | Long tiebacks |

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the **exam-relevant formulas** for Module 4 installation analysis. Memorize the formulas, their variable definitions, and standard units.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Buoyancy Force | $F_B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{disp}$ | $\rho_{sw}$ = seawater density (kg/m³), $V_{disp}$ = displaced volume (m³) |
| 2 | Submerged Weight | $W_{sub} = W_{air} - F_B$ | $W_{air}$ = weight in air (N), $F_B$ = buoyancy (N) |
| 3 | Metacentric Height | $GM = KB + BM - KG$ | $KB$ = height of B above K (m), $BM = I/V$ (m), $KG$ = height of G above K (m) |
| 4 | Righting Moment | $M_R = W \cdot GM \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | $W$ = weight (N), $GM$ in m, $\theta$ = heel angle (rad) |
| 5 | Draft Equation | $T = \dfrac{W}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot A_w}$ | $A_w$ = waterplane area (m²) |
| 6 | Towing Resistance (calm water) | $R_T = R_F + R_W + R_{APP}$ | Frictional, wave-making, appendage (N) |
| 7 | Bollard Pull Required | $BP \geq \dfrac{R_T}{\eta_T \cdot n_T}$ | $\eta_T$ = tug efficiency, $n_T$ = number of tugs |
| 8 | Upending Ballast | $V_{ballast} = \dfrac{F_B - W_{sub}}{ρ_{sw} \cdot g}$ | Volume of water to flood (m³) |
| 9 | Launch Bending Stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I}$ | $M$ = bending moment (N·m), $c$ = distance to extreme fibre (m), $I$ = moment of inertia (m⁴) |
| 10 | Dynamic Amplification Factor | $DAF = 1 + \dfrac{a_{max}}{g}$ | $a_{max}$ = max vertical acceleration of crane hook (m/s²) |
| 11 | Pile Penetration (Skirt/Driven) | $R_{soil} = q_c \cdot A_{tip} + f_s \cdot A_{side}$ | CPT-based bearing + friction |
| 12 | Tether Tension (TLP) | $T = W_{sub} / n_t$ | $n_t$ = number of tethers |
| 13 | Heave-induced Slamming Load | $F_{sl} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{sw} \cdot C_s \cdot A \cdot v^2$ | $C_s$ = slam coefficient, $v$ = vertical velocity (m/s) |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

These installation formulas are not academic — they are used daily in:
- **Marine warranty survey (MWS)** approvals for tow operations
- **Rig move planning** for semi-submersibles across continents
- **Heavy lift engineering** for offshore wind monopiles and jackets
- **Subsea field development** where each manifold placement is a multi-million dollar operation
- **Decommissioning engineering** (the reverse process, increasingly important in mature fields like the North Sea)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stability of a Jacket During Launch (Up-ending Phase)

**Problem Context:** A jacket of weight $W$ is being upended by crane assistance. Its centre of gravity $G$ is at height $h_G$ above the tip. The crane applies a force $F_C$ at height $h_C$ above the tip. Find the **minimum crane force** required for stable upending.

**Setup:** At the moment of tipping from horizontal to vertical, the jacket pivots about its tip. For stability, the moment of the buoyancy force about the pivot must not exceed the restoring moment.

#### Step 1 — Identify Forces Acting
- Weight $W$ acting downward at $G$
- Buoyancy $F_B$ acting upward at the centre of buoyancy $B$
- Crane force $F_C$ acting upward at the rigging point

#### Step 2 — Sum of Vertical Forces (Equilibrium)
For the jacket to be in vertical equilibrium during upending:

$$W = F_B + F_C$$

#### Step 3 — Solve for Crane Force

$$F_C = W - F_B$$

#### Step 4 — Express Buoyancy in Terms of Submerged Volume
When the jacket is at vertical floatation, let $V_{sub}$ be the submerged volume:

$$F_B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{sub}$$

#### Step 5 — Substitute and Finalize

$$F_C = W - \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{sub}$$

**Physical interpretation:** If the jacket is positively buoyant at vertical floatation ($F_B > W$), then $F_C < 0$, meaning the crane must **hold down** rather than lift up. This is common for light jackets.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Towing Bollard Pull Calculation

**Problem:** A semi-submersible of displacement $\Delta = 35{,}000$ tonnes is to be towed at $V = 4$ knots. Estimate the required bollard pull.

**Step 1 — Frictional Resistance (using ITTC friction line)**

$$R_F = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho_{sw} \cdot V^2 \cdot S \cdot C_F$$

where $S$ is the wetted surface area (m²) and $C_F$ is the friction coefficient.

For a semi-submersible: $S \approx 8{,}500$ m², $C_F \approx 0.0015$ at $V = 4$ knots ($2.06$ m/s).

$$R_F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times (2.06)^2 \times 8500 \times 0.0015$$

$$R_F = 0.5 \times 1025 \times 4.24 \times 8500 \times 0.0015$$

$$R_F \approx 27{,}720 \text{ N} \approx 27.7 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Wave-making Resistance (deep water, sub-critical)**

For a slender semi-submersible at $Fn < 0.3$, wave-making resistance is small but not negligible:

$$R_W \approx 0.05 \times R_F \approx 1.4 \text{ kN}$$ (using empirical 5% addition)

**Step 3 — Appendage Resistance (mooring lines, rudders)**

$$R_{APP} \approx 0.10 \times R_F \approx 2.8 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Total Calm-water Resistance**

$$R_T = R_F + R_W + R_{APP} = 27.7 + 1.4 + 2.8 = 31.9 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Add Sea Margin (typical 50%)**

$$R_{design} = 1.5 \times 31.9 = 47.9 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Bollard Pull Required (with 2 tugs, efficiency 0.6 each)**

$$BP \geq \dfrac{47.9}{2 \times 0.6} \approx 39.9 \text{ kN}$$

Per-tug bollard pull of **~40 kN** (i.e., two 40-tonne bollard-pull tugs) is required.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Upending Ballast Volume for a Spar

**Problem:** A Spar of weight $W = 55{,}000$ tonnes (in air) is to be upended from horizontal towing position to vertical. Its total enclosed volume is $V_{tot} = 65{,}000$ m³. Find the volume of seawater ballast needed for vertical floatation.

**Step 1 — Condition for Vertical Floatation**

The Spar must have **zero net vertical force** at the moment of release from the transport vessel:

$$W = F_B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{sub}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for Submerged Volume Required**

$$V_{sub} = \dfrac{W}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g} = \dfrac{55{,}000 \times 1000 \times 9.81}{1025 \times 9.81}$$

$$V_{sub} = \dfrac{539{,}550{,}000}{10{,}055.25} \approx 53{,}659 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Compute Required Flooding Volume**

Since the Spar must float with a freeboard margin, assume a **90% submersion** safety target:

$$V_{target} = 0.90 \times V_{tot} = 0.90 \times 65{,}000 = 58{,}500 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Volume of Ballast Water to Pump In**

The Spar already contains some water from the towing phase; let initial water volume be $V_0 = 5{,}000$ m³.

$$V_{ballast} = V_{target} - V_0 = 58{,}500 - 5{,}000 = 53{,}500 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Practical Note**

This is pumped using **submersible pumps** through pre-installed sea chests, controlled by **ballast control system (BCS)** operators onboard.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Installation Method Selection

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix below is a **decision aid** frequently tested in KTU exams. Use this to justify method selection in descriptive answers.

| Structure | Weight Range | Water Depth | Preferred Installation Method | Key Vessel/Equipment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Small jacket | 1,000–5,000 t | < 150 m | Lift | Crane barge / HLV |
| Medium jacket | 5,000–15,000 t | < 200 m | Launch or Lift | Launch barge / HLV |
| Large jacket | 15,000–40,000 t | < 300 m | Float-over | Float-over barge |
| GBS (concrete) | 50,000–500,000 t | < 300 m | Controlled flooding | Tugs + ballast pumps |
| Semi-submersible | 15,000–60,000 t | > 300 m | Wet tow + Mooring | Ocean tugs + mooring install |
| FPSO | 30,000–300,000 t | Any | Tow + Mooring | Tugs + spread mooring |
| TLP | 30,000–60,000 t | 300–1500 m | Tow + Tether pull-down | Tugs + tensioner system |
| Spar | 20,000–70,000 t | 500–3000 m | Wet tow (horizontal) + Upend | Catamaran tow vessel |
| Subsea manifold | 50–500 t | Any | Crane lower | CSV / DSV + ROV |
| Pipeline | per km | Any | S/J/Reel-lay | Pipelay vessel |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Lift DAF on a Jacket

**Problem:** A jacket weighing $W = 8{,}500$ tonnes is being lifted by a crane vessel. The maximum vertical acceleration of the hook is $a_{max} = 0.30\,g$. Find the design hook load.

**Step 1 — Static Weight in Newtons**

$$W = 8500 \times 1000 \times 9.81 = 83{,}385{,}000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Dynamic Amplification Factor**

$$DAF = 1 + \dfrac{a_{max}}{g} = 1 + \dfrac{0.30\,g}{g} = 1.30$$

**Step 3 — Design Hook Load**

$$F_{hook} = DAF \times W = 1.30 \times 83{,}385{,}000 = 108{,}400{,}500 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — In Tonnes Equivalent**

$$F_{hook} = \dfrac{108{,}400{,}500}{1000 \times 9.81} \approx 11{,}050 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Conclusion:** The crane must have a rated capacity of at least **11,050 tonnes** at the required radius, including a further margin (typically 1.15) for rigging and sea-state allowances → ~**12,700 tonnes** minimum.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Master Installation Decision Flow

The diagram below maps the decision-making process for selecting an installation method. Each node is an alphanumeric identifier with clean text labels (no markdown inside labels).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start Selection] --> A1[Identify Structure Type]
    A1 --> A2{Structure Family}
    A2 --> A3[Fixed Steel Jacket]
    A2 --> A4[Concrete GBS]
    A2 --> A5[Floating System]
    A2 --> A6[Subsea Element]
    A2 --> A7[Pipeline]

    A3 --> A3a{Mass Threshold}
    A3a --> A3b[Below 15000 t Use Lift]
    A3a --> A3c[Above 15000 t Use Launch]
    A3a --> A3d[Above 25000 t Use Float Over]

    A4 --> A4a[Use Controlled Flooding]
    A4a --> A4b[Build in Dry Dock]
    A4b --> A4c[Flood and Float Up]
    A4c --> A4d[Wet Tow to Site]
    A4d --> A4e[Ballast Down to Seabed]

    A5 --> A5a{Floating Subtype}
    A5a --> A5b[Semi Submersible Wet Tow]
    A5a --> A5c[TLP Tow and Pull Down]
    A5a --> A5d[Spar Horizontal Tow and Upend]
    A5a --> A5e[FPSO Tow and Moore]

    A6 --> A6a[Use Crane Lowering]
    A6a --> A6b[Vessel Type CSV or DSV]
    A6b --> A6c[ROV Assisted Placement]

    A7 --> A7a{Pipeline Depth}
    A7a --> A7b[Shallow Use S Lay]
    A7a --> A7c[Deep Use J Lay]
    A7a --> A7d[Medium Use Reel Lay]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Jacket End-Launch Sequence

The diagram below illustrates the operational sequence for end-launching a steel jacket. This is the most common installation method for medium-size jackets in shallow-to-medium water depths.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B0[Jacket Fabricated Horizontal] --> B1[Load Out onto Launch Barge]
    B1 --> B2[Ballast Barge to Launch Trim]
    B2 --> B3[Initiate End Launch]
    B3 --> B4[Jacket Rotates about Stern Rocker]
    B4 --> B5[Jacket Enters Water]
    B5 --> B6[Floating Stability Check]
    B6 --> B7[Attach Tow Lines]
    B7 --> B8[Wet Tow to Field Location]
    B8 --> B9[Upend Using Derrick Barge]
    B9 --> B10[Position Over Pre Surveyed Piles]
    B10 --> B11[Drive Piles Through Jacket Legs]
    B11 --> B12[Grout Piles and Commission]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Spar Upending Architecture

A Spar cannot be towed vertically (it would be unstable). Instead, it is towed horizontally in a purpose-built catamaran support vessel and then upended at site. The diagram below shows the functional architecture of this operation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[Spar Built at Yard] --> C1[Move to Deep Water Quay]
    C1 --> C2[Load into Catamaran Tow Vessel]
    C2 --> C3[Wet Tow Horizontal]
    C3 --> C4[Arrive at Field]
    C4 --> C5[Disconnect from Catamaran]
    C5 --> C6[Flood Lower Ballast Tanks]
    C6 --> C7[Spar Rotates to Vertical]
    C7 --> C8[Connect Mooring Lines]
    C8 --> C9[Install Topsides via Heavy Lift]
    C9 --> C10[Hook Up and Commission]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Float-Over Installation

Float-over installation is used when the jacket or topside is too heavy for any available crane. The functional blocks below illustrate how a transport barge is *deballasted* between pre-installed foundations to "lower" the structure into place.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0[Load Structure onto Barge at Yard] --> D1[Tow to Site Between Foundations]
    D1 --> D2[Position with Tug Assist and DP]
    D2 --> D3[Verify Alignment with DGPS]
    D3 --> D4[Engage Leg Mating Mechanisms]
    D4 --> D5[Begin Controlled Deballasting]
    D5 --> D6[Structure Descends onto Foundations]
    D6 --> D7[Verify Leg Engagement by ROV]
    D7 --> D8[Grout Connections and Load Test]
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — TLP Tether Pull-Down System

The TLP installation depends critically on the **tether pull-down system**. The diagram below shows the engineering interaction between subsystems during tether tensioning.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E0[Hull in Vertical Floatation] --> E1[Align over Seabed Templates]
    E1 --> E2[Activate Deballasting Pumps]
    E2 --> E3[Submerged Weight Increases]
    E3 --> E4[Tethers Tension to Design Load]
    E4 --> E5[Verify Tension via Load Cells]
    E5 --> E6[Lock Tether Top Connections]
    E6 --> E7[Begin Topsides Hook Up]
    E7 --> E8[Commission Production System]
```

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
*List any three methods of installing fixed offshore jacket platforms and state one key limitation of each method.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Lift Method:** A heavy-lift crane vessel directly lifts the jacket onto the seabed.
   - **Limitation:** Crane vessel availability and rated capacity; suitable only up to ~15,000 t.
2. **Launch Method:** Jacket slides off a launching barge end-first, like a ship launch.
   - **Limitation:** Requires sufficient water depth at the launch site; jacket must have sufficient buoyancy to float.
3. **Float-over Method:** Transport barge deballasts between pre-installed foundations to lower the jacket.
   - **Limitation:** Requires pre-installed foundation structures; sensitive to metocean during mating.

*(1 mark per method + 0.5 mark for valid limitation each, totaling 3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
*Differentiate between wet tow and dry tow for floating offshore structures.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Wet Tow | Dry Tow |
|---|---|---|
| **Buoyancy** | Structure floats under its own buoyancy | Structure is fully supported on a cargo barge or catamaran |
| **Stability** | Requires GM > 0 and adequate freeboard | Stability provided by the transport vessel |
| **Examples** | FPSO, semi-submersibles, TLP hull | Spar (horizontal), heavy lift on HLV deck |
| **Risk** | Stability loss, flooding, structural damage in waves | Transportation vessel motion, on-deck weather exposure |
| **Typical distance** | Long transoceanic voyages | Short distances (yard to field) |

*(1 mark for difference 1, 1 mark for difference 2, 1 mark for example difference)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - Do NOT confuse **launch** with **float-over** — they are fundamentally different mechanics.
> - Do NOT omit the **dynamic amplification factor (DAF)** in lift design — this is a frequently marked step.
> - Do NOT forget the **stability check** (GM) during wet tow — even if not explicitly asked, examiners award 2 marks for this.
> - Always specify **units** in numerical answers. Missing units = −0.5 mark per answer.
> - Sub-part (a) and (b) typically carry **7 marks each** — do not spend 12 marks on one part.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **end-launch method** of installing a fixed steel jacket platform. Include a labelled schematic of the sequence and discuss the engineering checks required before, during, and after launch. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A steel jacket of mass **12,000 tonnes** is being lifted by a crane vessel. The maximum vertical acceleration of the hook during the lift is measured at **0.35 g**. Calculate:
- (i) The **Dynamic Amplification Factor (DAF)**
- (ii) The **design hook load** in tonnes
- (iii) The **minimum crane rated capacity** if a 15% rigging and sea-state margin is to be applied **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — End-Launch Method (7 marks)**

The end-launch method is the most commonly used installation technique for fixed steel jackets in shallow and medium water depths (typically 30–200 m).

**Sequence of operations:**
1. **Fabrication:** The jacket is built horizontally in a fabrication yard, on a skidway parallel to a deep-water quay. Buoyancy compartments are integrated into the lower portion of the legs. *(1 mark)*
2. **Load-out:** The jacket is skidded onto a **launching barge** using hydraulic strand jacks. The barge is pre-ballasted to receive the load. *(1 mark)*
3. **Ballasting for launch trim:** Sea-water is pumped into the stern of the barge to achieve the correct launch trim angle (typically 0.5°–1° stern-down). *(1 mark)*
4. **Launch initiation:** Hydraulic rams push the jacket along the skidway until the centre of gravity passes beyond the **stern rocker arm**, initiating rotation. *(1 mark)*
5. **Rotation and water entry:** The jacket rotates about the rocker, sliding into the water like a ship launch. The buoyancy compartments provide floatation. *(1 mark)*
6. **Post-launch checks:** Stability verification (GM check), leak check, towing connection preparation. *(1 mark)*
7. **Tow and upend:** The jacket is wet-towed to the field, then upended at site by crane barge assistance or controlled flooding. *(1 mark)*

**Engineering checks:**
- **Structural check:** Bending moment at the rocker during launch must not exceed allowable stress ($\sigma \le \sigma_{allow}$)
- **Stability check:** $GM > 0$ at all phases, with adequate freeboard
- **Ballast system check:** All pumps and valves operational before launch

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Dynamic Amplification Factor (1 mark)**

$$DAF = 1 + \dfrac{a_{max}}{g} = 1 + \dfrac{0.35\,g}{g} = 1.35$$

**(ii) Design Hook Load (3 marks)**

Static weight:

$$W = 12{,}000 \times 1000 \times 9.81 = 117{,}720{,}000 \text{ N}$$

Design hook load:

$$F_{hook} = DAF \times W = 1.35 \times 117{,}720{,}000 = 158{,}922{,}000 \text{ N}$$

Converting to tonnes:

$$F_{hook} = \dfrac{158{,}922{,}000}{1000 \times 9.81} \approx 16{,}200 \text{ tonnes}$$

**(iii) Minimum Crane Rated Capacity (3 marks)**

Apply 15% margin:

$$C_{min} = 1.15 \times 16{,}200 = 18{,}630 \text{ tonnes}$$

**Final Answer:** The crane vessel must have a rated capacity of at least **18,630 tonnes** at the required operating radius.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **[Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With neat schematic sketches, describe the **installation procedure for a Concrete Gravity Base Structure (GBS)**. Discuss the role of buoyancy control and ballast management during installation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Spar platform has the following data:
- Total weight in air: **W = 60,000 tonnes**
- Total enclosed volume: **V_tot = 72,000 m³**
- Initial water volume during tow: **V₀ = 6,000 m³**
- Density of seawater: **ρ_sw = 1025 kg/m³**

Calculate:
- (i) The **buoyancy force** at full submersion
- (ii) The **submerged volume required** for neutral floatation
- (iii) The **ballast volume to be pumped** to achieve 92% submersion **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — GBS Installation Procedure (7 marks)**

1. **Dry-dock fabrication:** The GBS is built in a deep dry-dock or a flooded fjord with a temporary dam. The structure typically has multiple base cells (caissons). *(1 mark)*
2. **Float-up:** The dry-dock is flooded; the GBS rises under controlled conditions, with the bottom still resting on the dock floor initially. Buoyancy tanks at the top provide the lift force. *(1.5 marks)*
3. **Pre-tow ballasting:** Sea-water is pumped into lower compartments to lower the centre of gravity and increase stability for the wet tow. *(1 mark)*
4. **Wet tow:** The GBS is towed to the field by ocean-going tugs at slow speed (typically 2–3 knots), with continuous ballast monitoring. *(1 mark)*
5. **Site ballasting:** At the field, the GBS is positioned over a pre-prepared seabed and ballasted down progressively. *(1 mark)*
6. **Touch-down monitoring:** Touch-down is monitored by **acoustic sensors**, **tilt meters**, and **ROVs** to ensure level contact. *(0.5 mark)*
7. **Final ballasting and grouting:** Once positioned, the GBS is fully ballasted, and skirt compartments may be grouted to lock the foundation. *(1 mark)*

**Role of buoyancy and ballast:**
- Buoyancy provides floatation; ballast (sea-water in compartments) provides the **downward force** to sink the GBS and **increase stability** by lowering the centre of gravity.
- The relationship $W_{sub} = W_{air} - F_B$ governs whether the GBS floats, is neutral, or sinks.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Buoyancy force at full submersion (2 marks)**

$$F_B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V_{tot}$$

$$F_B = 1025 \times 9.81 \times 72{,}000$$

$$F_B = 725{,}094{,}000 \text{ N} \approx 725.1 \text{ MN}$$

**(ii) Submerged volume for neutral floatation (2 marks)**

For neutral floatation: $F_B = W$

$$V_{sub} = \dfrac{W}{\rho_{sw} \cdot g} = \dfrac{60{,}000 \times 1000 \times 9.81}{1025 \times 9.81}$$

$$V_{sub} = \dfrac{588{,}600{,}000}{10{,}055.25} \approx 58{,}537 \text{ m}^3$$

**(iii) Ballast volume for 92% submersion (3 marks)**

Target submerged volume:

$$V_{target} = 0.92 \times V_{tot} = 0.92 \times 72{,}000 = 66{,}240 \text{ m}^3$$

Required additional ballast:

$$V_{ballast} = V_{target} - V_0 = 66{,}240 - 6{,}000 = 60{,}240 \text{ m}^3$$

**Final Answer:** Approximately **60,240 m³** of sea-water ballast must be pumped in to achieve the 92% submersion target.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- ✅ **Five fixed-platform installation methods:** Lift, Launch, Float-over, Skidding, Piece-small (for very large platforms built in modules).
- ✅ **Three floating-structure tow methods:** Wet tow (FPSO, semi, TLP), Horizontal wet tow (Spar), Dry tow on cargo barge.
- ✅ **GBS installation relies entirely on buoyancy + ballast** — no cranes, no launch.
- ✅ **TLP uses tether pull-down** — submerge the hull to tension the vertical tethers.
- ✅ **Spar is upended by controlled flooding** of lower tanks after horizontal tow.
- ✅ **Subsea structures** are installed by **crane lowering from CSV/DSV**, with ROV verification.
- ✅ **Pipelines:** S-lay (shallow), J-lay (deep), Reel-lay (medium).
- ✅ **Key formulas to memorize:**
  - $F_B = \rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot V$
  - $W_{sub} = W_{air} - F_B$
  - $GM = KB + BM - KG$
  - $DAF = 1 + a_{max}/g$
  - $T = W/(\rho_{sw} \cdot g \cdot A_w)$
- ✅ **Standard metric:** Seawater density **1025 kg/m³**, weight density **10.05 kN/m³**.
- ✅ **Weather window:** Installations typically require Hs ≤ 1.5 m and wind ≤ 15 knots.
- ✅ **Critical pitfall:** Always perform **stability check (GM)** during wet tow and upending.
- ✅ **Critical pitfall:** Apply **DAF** to all crane lifts; examiners check for this.
- ✅ **Critical pitfall:** Specify units in numerical answers.
